### Post by frodon on 2009-10-30
[SIZE="2"][COLOR="Red"]*** Disclaimer for those willing to analyse this poll ***
Most of users voting here are users with issues.
Users with painless experience are not likely to come here.
If you want to compare Karmic release with other releases based on this poll anyway here are the previous polls :
[Jaunty Poll]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1133869&highlight=experience")
[Intrepid Poll]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=963853&highlight=intrepid")

The purpose of this thread is to share your experience installing/upgrading Karmic Koala.

Did it work flawlessly ? 
Did you get problems ? 
Did you manage to solve them ? 
if yes how ?
...
...
.

Feel free to post your experience here and think to explain how you solved the problems you got, it might help other users in your case.

Thank you for contributing :KS

### Post by spuzzdawg on 2009-10-30
I started my own thread but my post was probably better suited to something like this. I wrote:

> I was trying to upgrade my 9.04 Leonovo 3000 C200 to 9.10 and X server appeared to crash early on in the installation. Screen went black like it normally does after a period of inactivity but wouldn't 'wake up' with the mouse or keys. HDD light was flashing like crazy so I assumed that the installation was continuing on without any display. After an hour or so the HDD light stopped flashing but the laptop had not restarted. After remaining in this state for a while it suddenly flashed a "Ubuntu in low graphics mode" error screen listing a bunch of errors in the window. At this point the latop completely crashed leaving an unbootable system.

I've got a 9.04 disk lying around from a linux format magazine so I've booted into that and backed up all my important information onto an external HDD (I know I should have done this before starting the upgrade) and am now downloading the 9.10 install disk via torrents to do a fresh install. Hopefully this fixes the problem.

spuzzdawg

### Post by Dimarchi on 2009-10-30
I had no trouble installing Karmic (clean install, not upgrade). There were problems after that, though. The first was GRUB error 15 on reboot, solved by selecting the other of the two hard drives I have in BIOS settings. The second was screen resolution after opting to install restricted drivers (fglrx, typed sudo aticonfig --initial -f in terminal before reboot after activation). Solved by aticonfig resolution stuff I have just for this purpose. :) The third appears to be printed related (not found): I have not yet spent time with that so it may be a non-issue.

The fourth is what has always been when there are two screens of different resolutions, compiz active, and fglrx in use - although the latter two may have nothing to do with it...doubt that, though. Firefox window does not remember size on the main screen on restart...it appears to resize itself to the smaller of the two screens. Vertically, at least, not sure about horizontal. It does make some sort of sense, however, when you think about it. Annoyance that can be avoided by disabling the second screen, I guess. It does not affect Thunderbird, which puzzles me.

---

### Post by jhenager on 2009-10-30
I jumped in with both feet last night. I did the upgrade using Update Manager, and I had over 1,400 packages to update. I let it run all night. When I woke up this morning, it was ready to remove obsolete packages, so I did that while I was shaving. When I checked back, it was ready to restart. When it came back up, my wallpaper was there, Desktop Effects were enabled - in short - painless.
The only minor tweak I had to make was to install some Language components. I just followed the prompts and again, no problems.
I can't wait to get home and play with it some more. I do think I am going to try to change the theme to something else. That brown is not very attractive. Other than that, I give it an A+ so far!

---

I am running a IBM T43 with 2gb of ram, and ATI x300 with 128mb of ram.

### Post by Charamei on 2009-10-30
I dived in last night and despite some problems with my shoddy 'net connection dropping out, the upgrade process went without a hitch.

However, I have discovered two problems:

1. My WiFi no longer works. I was expecting this - my WiFi always breaks - but now the only drivers I have ever found which would talk to my chipset (madwifi-hal-0.10.5.6) will no longer compile using sudo make. I cannot therefore reinstall them.

2. Nautilus seems to be having problems loading large folders. I have not had a chance to adequately test what went wrong, so can't be sure exactly, but here's what happened: I streamed a ~220 MB video file into my Videos directory and from there moved it into a more appropriate subdirectory. On following it in to check that it had moved properly, I found that the directory would not load; it just sat there with the 'loading' cursor. Moving up a level and back in fixed it, but on moving the file to the next subdirectory level the same thing occurred. This time a reboot was required - although since then the directory seems to be loading without issue. The folder in question is just under 3 GB in size.

### Post by Angus77 on 2009-10-30
I have yet to successfully boot up Karmic.
I've installed desktop-amd64 twice---once via USB, once from a spare partition on my hard disk.  Both times it seemed to install flawlessly, but then Karmic doesn't show up in the Grub2 menu.
Booting into my Jaunty partition, I could see that there's no vmlinuz in /boot on the Karmic partition.
This is so frustrating!  Jaunty was a snap to install.

### Post by johanbove on 2009-10-30
Upgraded using Update Manager last night. The Upgrade process went smoothly and without issues. I chose to remove all the unsupported packages after verifying the list. It didn't look like anything really important was going to be removed.

After the first reboot i noticed something was different though. The usplash screen and login screen had been re-themed to "Xubuntu", with mouse logo and everything. This was strange since I just had "reinstalled" *plain *Ubuntu 9.04 from the CD a couple of days ago (I had messed up my setup by fooling around in the config files).

My "/home" folder, which I keep on a separate drive, probably contained references to Xubuntu which the Upgrade Installer interpreted perhaps? Don't know for sure. 

But after applying the Gnome themes again, I was back up and running!
Great job on the installer programming!

### Post by w00ly on 2009-10-30
I have two systems that i'm going to be going from 9.04 to 9.10. On the slower machine when booting with the livecd I was only able to get a blank screen on bootup (after the two loading screens). I heard the startup noise and everything, but my monitor instantly goes into sleep mode.

On the quicker one I was able to get into the livecd but was unable to mount my home partition because I had chose to encrypt the home partition when installing 9.04 and then wasnt able to get the passphrase command to complete the encryption process to work properly (i dont remember what the problem was but it didnt work). I made a thread about it here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8193460](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8193460) with one guy saying he was able to get it working by using the desktop edition, mounting the partition as home and then at some point in the process inputting the username and password but i'm not sure if this will work for me because mine apparently isnt setup properly. So now I have to figure out how to get that setup right and also whether or not I need to backup /var/lib/ecryptfs. Either way, it seemed like I had quite a bit of work to do last night to get this working so I left it for a later day. I certainly wish the encryption mounting process was more automated like everything else is!!

if anyone has any info on how to easily set this up let me know

### Post by OnlyTim on 2009-10-30
I did an upgrade yesterday from Jaunty 9.04 to Koala. I have two HD's. Windows on the first. (For the Mrs.) and the second EXT3 drive for Ubuntu. The upgrade took over 4 hours to complete,(Yes I held my breath) but everything went fine. My only unsure moment was when the upgrade asked me where to put Grub. sda sdb sdc. ### Post by samwisefoxburr on 2009-10-30
It works and I like it but there are annoyances that really irk me because they did not exist in an out-of-the-box install of 9.04.

1. chattr is broken.  I get "Inappropriate ioctl for device while reading flags on dir/file".

2. My sshfs share called "Files" now shows up in Places twice when mounted.
[IMG]http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3489/4058246102_9e54d4d1e8_o.png[/IMG]

I get this error when I mount my share:
[IMG]http://farm3.static.flickr.com/2772/4058246082_62e90c9954_o.png[/IMG]

Sometimes it is worded differently:
[IMG]http://farm3.static.flickr.com/2688/4058246072_690fe6f2bd_o.png[/IMG]

It does mount and works, but it keeps the unmounted entry in Places.

3. Can't disable Animations in the CompizConfig Settings Manager.  I found a thread that explains the reasoning behind this from an Ubuntu developer but, in my opinion, is a very bad reason to restrict people from changing it:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1280562](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1280562)

4. The right Alt key does not work.  I've tested this on both my desktop and laptop.

### Post by thechanklybore on 2009-10-30
The upgrade from Jaunty worked flawlessly apart from wanting to remove Avant Window Navigator for no reason I can fathom. However I just reinstalled afterwards and it's all fine.

One other little issue which I'm not sure are upgrade related is that the new notifications pop up about 200px below the top panel, rather than flush underneath it as they used to. Looks horrid but not a showstopper.

One other thing that is REALLY very annoying is Epiphany-Gecko has been consigned to the dustbin even though Epiphany-Webkit is a bug ridden, featureless load of rubbish. I use Ubuntu as my main OS and I frankly don't have time to waste watching pages load with incorrect font sizes, grey areas around the GTK widgets, etc. I'm now having to compile the bloody thing from source just to rid myself of this crappy version.

Other than that though, Karmic seems a great performer, and I'm very happy with the upgrade experience as a whole.

### Post by skotos on 2009-10-30
October 30th, 2009:

I yesterday upgraded my wife's desktop, my laptop and an old server running the desktop version.

Everything went smoothly: for the first time after Ubuntu 6.10, no screen, nor advanced desktop effects, nor audio, nor Amarok 1.4 issues!
Ahh...... _**Yes, it was an awaited pleasure after a very long time.**_

Well done Canonical! :D
  
:popcorn:  :popcorn:  :popcorn:  :popcorn:  :popcorn: 


Now, the day after, a few things come in:[INDENT] 1) the **USB mouse cannot be plugged and unplugged at will** as it could be done up to Jaunty inclusive,
2) the **USB drive** cannot be automatically mounted if it has been switched on after login or it takes a few seconds to be correctly mounted...

Humm... is this a **udev issue?**

Now Netbook Remix does not want to be installed over my USB(!) - once again a coincidence ? - stick...
There is *no way to successfully format/resize/erase the USB stick....*

3) **the USB drives/sticks are now [so slooow!!]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1306333")**

Humm... Is this the USB time/release?
[/INDENT]Further feedback:[INDENT] Booting from the live cd gives access to full speed USB devices, thus it seems to be an upgrade only problem.

From scratch installation: perfect! (....almost ...at least)
  The day after the installation from scratch I have just discovered a few new problems even on it:

[LIST=1]
[*]no more automatically found/configured USB HP All-in-one printer device (solved by adding HPlip package from HP in the repositories)
[*]no more automatically found/configured USB hard disk drives/flash memories (still looking for a solution)
[*]a probable regression in the Phinilips/Avermedia DVB card recognition on my Acer Aspire 9815WKMi
[/LIST]
[/INDENT]A few days later:[INDENT] My internal DVB board needs, according to the kernel, the firmware file named *xc3028-v27.fw*.
Unfortunately, this *Philips Semiconductors SAA7131/SAA7133/SAA7135 Video Broadcast Decoder (rev d1)* board has never worked though giving the kernel the file it requested in Jaunty speeded up the boot process of many seconds.
Under Karmic, instead, I am currently leaving the kernel complaining because it does not find the firmware file: in this way - in fact! - any further USB transfer rate problem has disappeared as soon as a **_lucky_** (more on this here below) USB stick is found and configured.
**=>**[COLOR=Black]**The low USB transfer rate was not a USB related problem, but a remote misconfiguration/error**: a similar problem might affect others who have the same symptoms.[/COLOR]
[/INDENT]Up to November 4th, 2009:

[LIST=1]
[*][SIZE=3][SIZE=1][COLOR=Black]The USB automounting/recongnition does not work on both the 32/64 bits versions; on UNR, on the contrary, does.[/COLOR][/SIZE][B][COLOR=Red] 
[/COLOR][/B][/SIZE]
[*][SIZE=1][COLOR=Black]The Karmic **UNR** does not work well, instead,with the **second display** (when it is not mirrored)![/COLOR][/SIZE]
[/LIST]
November 9th, 2009:

[LIST=1]
[*]Kernel upgraded to **2.6.31.15-28**.
[/LIST]
[INDENT]Though still in the *Proposed Updates* repo, THIS IS _THE PARTIAL_ SOLUTION TO THE USB AUTOMOUNT PROBLEMS!
[/INDENT][INDENT]:popcorn:

[/INDENT][INDENT]A few more issues I am currently facing:
[/INDENT]
[LIST=1]
[*]**SSH** has some problems when you have chosen to have an encrypted home, (because when you try a remote connection the keys are encrypted and the credentials exchange fails)
[*]**XDMCP** seems to be stripped off and does not work
[*]the **Login Window** control is missing: no easy way to set up shown/hidden users, xdmcp, local and remote screens/themes...
[*]there still is no easy way to switch between desktop environments in UNR
[/LIST]
[INDENT]
In conclusion:


[LIST=1]
[*] SSH and XDMCP may heavily affect professional environments functionalities, thus it is really important to think of them and decide accordingly before upgrading. **It is better to avoid running users with an encrypted home when ssh is a prereq.**
[*] The current totally missing GDM customization can create further annoyances with the end users and the company which might want to run a specific theme/background/startup color.
[/LIST]
[/INDENT]November 13th, 2009:[INDENT]*After upgrading to the latest proposed kernel* - **2.6.31.15-50** - [I][COLOR=Black]the USB stack went worse:
[/COLOR][/I] 
[LIST=1]
[*]Once X has been started, the mouse cannot be plugged and unplugged at will.
[*]USB hard drives do not get recognized any more
[*]The HP 5610 all-in-one is recognized by the ugly HPLIP only
[/LIST]
[/INDENT]November 18th, 2009:[INDENT][SIZE=1][COLOR=Black][B][SIZE=2]_USB automatic recognition does work again!_[/SIZE]
[/B][/COLOR][/SIZE]
:popcorn:

Simply update from the standard repositories!
On three of four updated computers, no reboot was needed to have USB sticks automatically mounted!
[/INDENT]

---

### Post by pnmoslo on 2009-10-30
Perfectly smooth upgrade (server) except for one problem -- the upgrade script for MySQL seems to have aborted, and MySQL refused to start after the upgrade. 

I posted this as a separate thread -- the 9.04 system was up to date and had been functioning without problems, so I really can't see how this can have happened (an serious error in the script would have had folk screaming by now). I suspect something like the script not finding correct superuser details to make changes for the MySQL 5.0 > 5.1 upgrade.

Solved by doing things you're not supposed to do with MySQL tables. But it seems to have worked.

For the poll, then: perfect upgrade except for one serious problem that the scripts were unable to solve.

### Post by alex2399 on 2009-10-30
My fresh install was a horrible experience, costed me hours. First some info on the system. 

Ati HD3870, Dual screen via DVI, AMD 5600 X2, 4GB Ram, 1TB HD & 500GB HD.
On the 1TB HD resides in a 56GB partition Windows XP 64-bit, 8GB linux Swap and the rest is for Ubuntu. The 500GB HD is unused.

First I downloaded and burned the latest Karmic RC on a DVD. Install went fine. Then after starting the system it would reset or completely shut-off when displaying the login screen. The shut-off of the system was not normal in any way, I had to remove the power completely from the system before it started again. Going into recovery mode and scrolling down with the cursor keys resulted in a completely FUBAR Display where barely readable, a message was displayed with "General mounting Error" or something along these lines. Pressing keys on my Keyboard resulted in only more garbage being put on my screen with things like "root@my_user_name".
Hmm okay, maybe its because its an RC so waited for the Final version of 9.10 Karmic. Downloaded, burned, installed, same damn thing happening with login screen and recovery mode. After spendind hours reading up on possible solutions I finally got to a command prompt, did apt-get update, apt-get upgrade and dpkg --configure -a. Took alot of time to get all in. Praying it would work now.

Well, the result was still the same thing happening at the login screen, reset or shut-off. Thankfully recovery mode functioned ok now, read some more about possible solutions (none found) and installed the package "xorg-driver-fglrx". After this it finally worked.

So my feelings about this install experience are pretty negative, actually I have been outraged. Not only did it not work as it should, the recovery mode which is a very basic thing didn't even function properly.

Regarding my thoughts towards the working system I am satisfied, but this install was one of the worst, time consuming and frustrating installation I have ever done. This includes comparing it to ancient versions of that other legacy OS on shoddy machines.

---

### Post by raychje on 2009-10-30

I did a clean install in Vista.  Initial install went great, no problems.

AND NOTHING ELSE.. .no "click here to get the data necessary", NOTHING!... not one hint on what to do now.  Software center obviously know about the package but gives ZERO help in getting it.

NOTHING.... FAILURE on the developers of software center for not helping the user...  they show the user a package .. then give ZERO help on getting the package... why even show the package if you can't load the software?  

I tried fontForge .. SAME THING... no help at all from the software center.. just "not available in the current data" .  Did not ANYONE actually TEST this thing with their NON-computer friends and notice this TOTAL FAILURE!!!!

### Post by Velvet_Man on 2009-10-30
I spent all day yesterday trying to upgrade. It was downloading at about 50k/s, so it took forever. 

It finally finished downloading last night and started installing. It got to 98% complete on the install when some window popped up saying something about kdesudo failed somethingorother... I don't know. I've never seen such an error before.

So now I've got 98% of the updated packages installed, but it's not done and I'm afraid to turn my computer off because I don't think it'll reboot the way it is. I've been searching on google to try to find an answer for what I can do, so far I haven't found anything. I tried running the update-notifier-kde -u in the alt-f2 dialog that I used to start the upgrade, but it doesn't do anything anymore.

Does anyone know how I can make it resume the upgrade where it left off?

EDIT:

I might have stumbled on an answer. I tried to run something with sudo in terminal and it told sudo was locked because another process was using it. Turns out dkpg was using root and frozen (from the upgrade process?).

So I terminated dpkg and ran "sudo dpkg --configure -a" (terminal told me to run that, I wouldn't have had a clue). Anyway, it started updating alsa-base, which is what the update was doing when it froze. It then did some stuff with pulse audio.

I guess my question now is, did it just finish the installation process? Is it safe to reboot now? What about removing old packages? 

Thanks for the help.

### Post by ST3ALTHPSYCH0 on 2009-10-30
I upgraded two systems yesterday from Jaunty to Karmic, and the only issue to note was painful server speeds when I upgraded my faster system in the evening (around 5:00 pm EDT)
The first system was a Sempron 2100+ (1.57 Ghz) with 512 Mb RAM (32 bit). It updated in about an hour.
The second system was a Phenom II 2.5 Ghz (3.0 Ghz OC) with 6 Gb RAM (32 bit). It took nearly 4 hours to complete.

Three caveats:
First, neither system has, now or ever, personal info (besides appearance settings and programs) saved on the boot partition.
Second, I've got Mac4lin setup on my account on the faster machine, and with the exception of the GDM theme (and I knew this ahead of time) it carried over flawlessly.
Third, I had formatted both machines to ext4 at time of install for Jaunty. I didn't do any research ahead of time on the subject, and just assumed "latest and greatest", I think that worked in my favor for the upgrade.

The reason I chose to upgrade instead of do a clean install is that I wanted to see if I experienced any difficulties. With all personal data on other partitions I was out absolutely nothing if I had to do a clean install from crash.

The only question I have (and I'll search elsewhere, b/c I think this will get lost, and it's not the point of the thread) is can I replace grub with grub2 after the fact? If you can answer this question, please shoot me a PM, b/c as I said, that's not the point of this thread, and I don't wanna post jack.

### Post by maaarcooose on 2009-10-30
Running Ubuntu Studio with KDE installed. Has previously been 
upgraded from 8.04 to 8.10 to 9.04 without problems.

Dell Latitude X300 Laptop 1.6Ghz with 1GB, 60GB HDD, Intel 915 Graphics.

Tried standard Gnome update manager, Kept crashing while trying to find updates.

Installed and ran kubuntu-update and it ran.
1400 packages to download so left overnight.
Rebooted this morning.

Grub was not updated to the new kernels. Did it manually.
Booting new kernels gives 20% success rate on boot up and mostly gives blank screen on kdm starting.

Tried to revert to GDM, same problem.

Cannot switch to primay tty console as it messes up the screen and goes blank when I switch back to X.

Booting older kernel seems to work to a certain degree, but console switch is still hosed.

Touchpad did not work on first boot. Used this fix:
[http://danilogurovich.wordpress.com/2009/10/17/ubuntu-karmic-koala-9-10-beta-buggy-touchpad-behavior/]("http://danilogurovich.wordpress.com/2009/10/17/ubuntu-karmic-koala-9-10-beta-buggy-touchpad-behavior/")

Using KDE is now impossible. As soon as the monitor goes into power save, it crashes the display and have to just power off. That's if it actually loads.

Currentlly backing up my home folder so I can do a clean install.

I do not recommend an upgrade....

!m!

### Post by Steve Hayes on 2009-10-30
Two (possibly related?) problems so far, along with one niggle. This was an upgrade from 9.04 on a Dell Inspiron laptop.

First problem encountered - during first KDE startup after restart and log-in, screen went to brightly coloured flashing squares. Rather alarming and didn't clear. I pressed CTRL-ALT-DELETE and, after a delay, was back at the login screen. Logged in again and it was OK this time. Exact same thing happened again after power down and restart but hasn't happened since then. Hope it's gone.

Second problem - touchpad wouldn't work at all (note - use external USB mouse as a temporary fix.) Google turned up this could be kernel related. Spotted that grub menu was out of date and still loading old kernel. Edited /boot/grub/menu.lst and problem is fixed but a newbie would have no chance.

Niggle - wireless network settings had to be re-entered after upgrade.

I'll have a good play with the machine later and hope to find nice things.

---

### Post by Kaput1982 on 2009-10-30
I did a clean install.  The install itself went very smoothly. 

I've got a GeForce 8400 running dual monitors.  When I went to download the restricted drivers (ver. 185) I received an error (can't remember what it was).  I did an apt-get update and that allowed me to install the drivers.  

After that I had to dink around with the resolution on one of my monitors (the monitor was correctly detected in 9.04).  That was solved by editing my xorg.conf.  

I am running the 64bit version and so I have been getting the kernel error, but everything seems to work regardless so I'm not to worried. 

I'd backed up all my stuff to an external hard drive the day before, so I was up and running in a couple of hours.

### Post by florinn on 2009-10-30
I've installed the beta and then the final release of Ubuntu Karmic (clean install every time).
Everything was fine, now I have some problems trying to configure various things.
For example I cannot get rid of the drum sound of the login screen.
Previously I could mute it from System/Administration/Login Screen Setup > 'Accessibility'-tab
Now there is no such option.
I choosed No sound from Preferences > Sounds > Sound scheme
There is still the login sound and it is annoying.

I do not like so mush the new login screen, I do not want to have a list of users when I login, I want to write down the user name myself. At list I should have an option: choose from list / type user name.

I do not have icons for System submenu (Preferences, Administration etc) only the text.
I do not have icons in Firefox menus.
SOLVED: System > Preferences > Appearance > Show icons in menus 

I like a few things a lot, for example new mouse configuration menu of Gnome (horizontal scrolling, disable touch pad while writing)

Ekiga still not working... Why Twinkle is working like a charm and Ekiga not at all ? Twinkle still has its problems that is why I want to get rid of it.

Another thing, alt+tab for switching between applications:
without visual effects you can move to the next application with alt+tab and to the previous with alt+shift+tab
with all the effects enabled it is not working (in the beta version I installed first it worked)
though in compiz settings manager prev window is set to shift+alt+tab

1. I enabled Application switcher plugin and it is working now, not with alt+shift+tab but with shift+alt+tab
2. Finally I found out the problem with alt+shift+tab, I was using alt+shift to change keyboard layout, after changing the keyboard shortcut everything is working fine

Another issue was Eclipse Galileo
I tried to download and run Eclipse PDT but when I tried to create a new PHP project the buttons of the dialog window were not working (I tried 2 versions of Java with no success)
I solved the problem by installing Eclipse from Synaptic and then installing PDT from Eclipse.

### Post by wm_brant on 2009-10-30
The upgrade process on my 3-month old 64-bit home system from 9.04 to 9.10 went well.  I downloaded the 9.10 upgrade image as a torrent (in less than 30 minutes!), and burnt it to a CD.  I downloaded and applied all pending updates to 9.04.

I booted into the 9.10 upgrade CD and applied the upgrade - no problems noted during the upgrade process.

After booting into 9.10 I found some issues -- Firefox could not connect to the Internet.  Firefox could see my wireless router and my DSL modem, but could not connect to anything on the Internet.

I tried checking my network settings with the graphical tool and found a more concrete problem; I can't change my network settings.  When I click on the 'Edit' button, I get a message to the effect that the program 'Was not granted sufficient privileges'.  At or about that time I see a icon appear on the top panel that states that I have a crash report.

After a while I get pop-up messages that my system is unstable.

So... My path going forward is this:
1.  Get connected to the Internet (I need to identify the command line equivalent of the graphical tool)
2.  Download whatever 9.10 updates there are and apply those, and see if those resolve my problems
3.  If I still have problems, or find more problems, decide whether to attempt to fix them or regress to 9.04 for a while (I knew I was taking a risk trying the upgrade in the first day).

Sigh.

  -- Bill

Update:  After that, I tried a clean install of 9.10 and had the same problem.  I then regressed to 9.04, where I am now.

There are a number of bug reports that mention the DSL-related 'insufficient privileges' problem I mentioned above:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager-applet/+bug/462944](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager-applet/+bug/462944)

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/network-manager/+bug/438771](https://bugs.launchpad.net/network-manager/+bug/438771)

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/432205](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/432205)

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager-applet/+bug/467537](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager-applet/+bug/467537)

### Post by jaycee23 on 2009-10-30
Only a recent convert to Ubuntu. Had 9.04 installed but before I did so, a bit of reading round made me use Ext4 and separate /home partition.

Got to say thank God I did. 

Between installing Jaunty and then Koala, I installed Windoze 7 on a separate partition.
Had a nice 9.04 / Windoze 7 setup going but thought got to get the new Koala,

Did a clean install, (but leaving my /home partition intact) - all went very smoothly. I used the "Save Markings" option on Synaptic therefore fairly straightforward but slow task of reinstalling my packages. Cleaned up any incompatibility issues and then was ready to roll.

Couple of minor issues 
1) Had my wife on as user but when I tried add her again it kept asking me to change the name of her home folder - I didn't want to that was the whole point of not formatting /home!?! Did manage to find a solution but seemed to be unduly complicated for such a simple request.

2) Added Amarok but it seemed to have become dyslexic - it couldn't read the mp3 tags properly so all the songs / artists / albums were mixed. Removed Amarok and using rhythmbox for now. Will try again sometime. 

No problem dual booting either!

All in all - very good but I think I understand why people go for clean installs and not upgrades

:o

### Post by slumbergod on 2009-10-30
Xubuntu - installation went smoothly.

Post-Install Issues:
1. annoying crackling sound (like pulse audio did for me in Jaunty) [EDIT: solved - Intel HDA vs new karmic power manager]
2. Firefox CPU spiking, hanging, and crashing [EDIT: upgraded to FF3.5.4 & installed Flash 10r32 from Adobe]
(3. video performance perhaps still not as good as what I expected)

General Impression:
Nice polished "feel" to it, but feels sluggish compared to Jaunty running XFCE4.61. Booting seems marginally faster (it was always pretty quick though). Shutdown is definitely faster!

This was a smoother transition than the one I had to Jaunty. I hope that the upcoming updates can sort out the nagging issues above, and perhaps make the desktop environment feel a bit more snappy.

### Post by wkulecz on 2009-10-30
The installer wrote grub2 to the wrong drive.  I installed to the first IDE drive (/dev/hda in the good old days) but grub ended up on my SATA drive so the system wouldn't boot after install!  Making the SATA drive boot first in the BIOS was a work around, but its a problem I need fixed.

My first IDE drive is in a hard drive carrier and I want/need it to be self-contained with it first in the BIOS boot order and the boot loader and any multiboot options contained on it.  My other two drives are data installers shouldn't touch!

So I voted the last option, although this is my only problem so far, but I've not done much other than poke around the examples.  Network and sound worked out of the box, it set 1920x1200 display by default to match my monitor, and the restricted Nvidia drivers installed and appear to work fine so far.

Why grub2?  Don't people have enough problems with grub already?  I'd hope there is a real good reason for it -- if it works nobody is gonna care as it only boots the system and then goes unnoticed, but when it doesn't work you are hosed unless you understand it pretty well.  Took me quite a while to learn how to fix the common grub problems, now I'm back to zero, for what purpose?

--wally.

Once I booted "sudo update-grub /dev/sdb" put the boot loader on my first IDE drive and then I could change it back to first in my BIOS as boot as expected.  Good info on grub2 is here:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Grub2](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Grub2)

All things considered, based on the "improvements" list, I'd vote grub2 more trouble than its worth, the link above has instructions for reverting to grub.  Now if the improvements said "boots from raid5" then there may be benefits here, but I don't see anything but potential eye candy (on boot up? we want it as fast as possible, a pox on anything that slows it down!) and perhaps unification of the boot system for other architectures.

### Post by tricia56 on 2009-10-30
I ran into some serious issues with the flashplugin in the upgrade. I got stuck with that, in some endless loop.   Eventually I had to reboot, the upgrade completed okay but my flash was in mess.  I then had no internet, though I was able to ping router, other computer etc.  I had to put in the nameservers, which I had never had to do with 9.04, I only found this out by researching through the forums here.  So, whilst it was now up and running I had serious issues with flash.  I looked around but it all seemed a little beyond my experience to fix.

So, I did fresh reinstall.  That went great.  It took no time.  I still had issues with the internet but that was easily fixed now that I had figured the fix.

My one issue is that my laptop is getting bent out of shape when it is in suspend and always triggers an alert about kernal panic -I have reported to launchpad and there are many others with same prob - so all in all as a fairly newish Ubuntu user  (less than 1 year) I am very very pleased with the ease of everything. I would never ever look back to another OS now!  It is an adventure to figure things out and learn more about my computer and myself along the way I might say!

I feel that I may even put some effort into testing Lynx!

### Post by bluzit on 2009-10-30

 I tried to restore with the cd but it couldn't detect my hard drive. I shut down all power and unplugged the data and power cables to the hard drive and tried again. It booted back to my original system and then restarted the upgrade. After reboot, it worked but the Package Manager was frozen. It just wouldn't work. I found a post to enter a few terminal commands and finally it was fixed. I'll never hurry to upgrade again!:rolleyes:

### Post by Lucky. on 2009-10-30
I spent all last night trying to get a clean install.  My problem was my fake RAID on my MSI P35 Neo 2 motherboard.

On previous versions, Ubuntu wouldn't work with my fake raid.  In order to get Intrepid and Jaunty to work, I had to change my BIOS settings to use the SATA "IDE" mode instead of "RAID" mode.

Karmic on the other hand detected my fake raid and made an attempt to use it.  Things would install just fine, but then it wouldn't boot (got to an initramfs prompt where I was lost).  Tried the alternate CD with a software RAID, nope.  Tried the alternate CD with both software raid and single hard drive installs...nope.  Gah!  Couldn't even get the desktop version to recognize single hard drives...all it wanted to install to was my RAID.

I went back to the BIOS, and started trying different SATA settings.  Changed from "IDE" mode to "RAID" mode, started the install (LiveCD version), and went to bed.

Woke up this morning and Ubuntu boots just fine, and is using my full RAID 0.  Woohoo!

I'm grateful that they recognized my fake RAID and got it working.  My only complaint (as others have mentioned) was the long download wait for what appeared to be a language pack.  I hear that it was due to space constraints for CD format.  I hope I'm not being selfish, but I am ready for a DVD format.  The download added 30 minutes to an hour of time onto the install (probably because the servers were getting hammered with the release).

(I also heard that if I unplugged my network cable, then the download would be postponed until later).

---

### Post by pdadad on 2009-10-30
Have been running Hardy Heron since it's release.  Love it!

Tried the live CD and it worked perfectly.

Installed Karmic on a new partition.  It seemed to go fine.  When trying to boot into the system I get to a login prompt in a console, like when you hit ctrl-alt-backspace, and it flashes and won't accept what I type in for a user name and password.  Tried to start gnome from there and it says it's already running.  Very disappointing.

1st update:
Downloaded and installed from the Alternate version.  Worked wonderfully.  So far I have sound, IM, Ubuntu 1, and NVIDIA drivers all working.

2nd update:
To fix sound fading out, edit /etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base.conf to have the following: 
"options snd-hda-intel power_save=10 power_save_controller=N model=ref"

### Post by DigitalAxis on 2009-10-30
HP Pavilion dv3-2150us, Kubuntu Karmic
Lots of problems, all things that worked in 9.04

* Amarok and KDE itself won't play sound any more; pidgin and the `play' command that comes with sox will (although `play' skips with alsa underruns) so I know it's not the generic problem with HP Pavilion laptop sound.
* knetworkmanager-applet cannot connect to networks protected with passwords any more, as in not hex keys. Gnome's nm-applet (also a frontend for network-manager) still can.
* KDE can't shut down (may be related to the first item, but I found that bug and it was supposedly fixed in June)
* Every once in a while it complains about not finding my various hard drive partitions.  Some times I end up at a recovery prompt (I believe simply exiting the prompt brings up the system), sometimes it still comes up ok.  I've got lots of directories on the root of my /home partition named after apparently defunct UUIDs.

Bugs about all points except the last one have been filed in launchpad.

### Post by Strohfeuer on 2009-10-30

[LIST]

### Post by peakpc on 2009-10-30
I have a compaq v5000 laptop and decided to load Karmic 32bit fresh as I was running Jaunty 64bit.  My problem was the broadcom wireless would not be recognised after install (I dual boot with xp) after one no go install and trying all the options in other posts still no wireless.  I just took a SWAG and I figured out the secret to making it work.
You have to install (enable) the hardware drivers running from the Live CD then (of course you can't reboot to make it stick), but if you install to the HD after that fwcutter will then be available to and you can enable and reboot it get it working. At least this worked for me. 

This was no issue in Jaunty and I am not liking Grub2 or the limited GDM options. My boot time went from 30 seconds to 40 seconds, not a deal killer but I had wished for better not worse.

Bring back GDM options I had to use a command line hack to turn the drum sound off during boot. I find full volume noises during boot unacceptable.  

found: "sudo -u gdm gconftool-2 --set /desktop/gnome/sound/event_sounds --type bool false" to remove drum sound yah!
Now I have discovered that Suspend and Hibernate resume me to a black screen that requires a reboot to fix.  this all worked fine in 9.04. this could be a deal killer if not fixed soon.

Also had a false bad sector alert on a usb HD this is a known bug [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-disk-utility/+bug/438136]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-disk-utility/+bug/438136")

Waiting for bug fixes.;)

### Post by bkreeder100 on 2009-10-30
My experiences with Ubuntu 9.10 Final 64 Bit install.
By 
Brian Reeder

Loaded Ubuntu 9.10 by using basic Duel-Boot load instructions via youtube videos.

Installed Flash Player using the Ubuntu Software Center and it worked but the controls didn't work so uninstalled and it didn't uninstall properly had to try various fixes in the forums but the only thing that worked was.


$ sudo rm /usr/lib/swfdec-mozilla/libswfdecmozilla.so 
$ sudo rm /usr/share/ubufox/plugins/libswfdecmozilla.so

Hopefully I didnt mess anything else by trying other things.

I think I removed Gnash also ... So I'll try reinstalling it if it is needed. But I won't install directly.

Went to Synaptic Package Manager under System/Administration and removed the Niswrapper and the flashplayerplugin.

Then I reinstalled the flash player from the Unbuntu Software Center.


New users that have just installed the 64 Bit version of Ubuntu might just need to only uninstall the niswrapper and flashplayer that might be  preconfigured and reinstall it from the Unbuntu Software Center.

I then decided to install Realplayer - due to I am an account holder.

Installing Realplayer 11

Got 64 Bit version from the following link - I am not sure if it is the full version cause it looks like the old version 10 in windows. - Says Realplayer 11 though.

[http://forms.helixcommunity.org/helix/builds/?category=realplay-current](http://forms.helixcommunity.org/helix/builds/?category=realplay-current)

It wouldn't install due to needed libstdc++5

Had to goto this web page to get libstdc++5

[http://packages.debian.org/etch/amd64/libstdc++5/download](http://packages.debian.org/etch/amd64/libstdc++5/download)

After installation then ran

chmod u+x realplay.bin

I think I tried this command a few times using different switches but it worked.

and then

./realplay.bin

And then it installed properly

### Post by smacfarl on 2009-10-31
9.10 looks like it will be great when I can actually boot into it.

I have a Vista install that has the Windows Boot Loader. I downloaded EasyBCD in anticipation of having to keep the Windows Boot Loader the primary, having had previous ugly experiences with Windows 7 Beta, and Ubuntu 9.04. 

What the hell is the Partition Manager doing? Seems like every upgrade cycle that partition scanning gets slower and slower. Seriously, why should it take longer than 30 seconds to identify where my partitions are and how big they are? Literally it took 20 minutes. I have a 320gb drive with a single 60gb NTFS partition and a second 160gb drive that has a single 160gb NTFS partition. Partition tables are small. Grinding on my drive util the install program screen power management feature blanks the screen indicates there is a serious problem.

Eventually I get to create a new 30gb ext4 partition and a swap partition. My first Ubuntu was 7.04. It was a very fast install and it  instructed me which partitions to create. Rather than responding with error messages after the forward button is presed as 9.10 install seems to do.

Then we get to the copy cycle. Which looks great until we are 80% through and we have to download language packs for god knows what. This takes 40 minutes. Can I skip this? There is a skip button here, but I have no idea what the effects will be of skipping files during an OS install. Why am I downloading anything before the initial install is complete? I just burned an ISO yesterday. What could possibly have changed in 24 hrs that is so important that my base system install can't complete before these language packs are downloaded. I chose USA English why the hell am I downloading language packs?

Finally we are finished and we reboot. Of course Grub completely fails to replace the windows boot loader, so I log into vista and  EasyBCD a new menu option to go to the Ubuntu partion. I reboot select the new Ubuntu option. And I am in a grub command line prompt!!

WTF? The whole point of the graphical installation process is that I should never have to configure grub, let alone boot the system from the grub command line. Did anyone test this at all?

You know I really fell in love with 7.04 because it just worked. I liked 9.04 because it booted quickly. Since I have had to upgrade to Vista the Ubuntu experience has become a real pain in the ***. 

My thought was Shuttleworth has Billions of dollars, the guy can pay 3 or 4 guys to test the crap out of the most common scenarios, I mean it's not like they are doing kernel development here, this is just standard system install stuff. And how much would it cost to get someone to fix the partition manager, so that it doesn't take twice as long as the entire rest of the install? Has shuttleworth ever installed Ubuntu himself? Because I just can't see that he's going to sit there and wait while the little circle cranks for 20 minutes before there is any partition feedback. 

What was the point of upgrading to Grub2 if the 9.10 install isn't able to successfully create a boot menu with it. At one point I used the Live-CD to try and talk to some help people via gnome x-chat. The response I got was - "I am too busy to talk to you, google grub." Really? This is technical support? 

Forget Ubuntu One and the new Installer feature. I don't give a **** about that if I can't boot the damn OS. I am just getting more and more angry the longer I write about this. 

Somebody. Anybody. Help me get 9.10 to boot and then show me to a forum where I can talk to someone who can fix these problems so it never happens again. I will devote time to testing. Whatever it takes.

---

### Post by al_erola on 2009-10-31
Two installations:

One on a desktop homebuilt AMD64 machine went pretty flawlessly.  I kept opensuse bootloader on the mbr and chainloaded to the ubuntu partition.  I would call it flawless.  Set up koala as a ext3 file system.  Kubuntu will be my choice except I can't get the sound working.  Ubuntu still looks so incredibly brown, like in dirt, but the sound does work. :o:o:o:o
Second on a Dell Inspiron E1505 that already had Vista and Opensuse.  I set up koala as an ext4 filesystem.  Again I tried to chainload the ubuntu partition but got hung up on mounting a shared fat32 partition and the opensuse partition.  Linux Mint derivative of 9.04 had the same problem.  Apparently ubuntu and its children can't handle mounting other file systems or distributions.:(:(:(:(

I'll wait for opensuse's new release on my laptop.

over all I give it 2 thumbs up  it works good an other then the two listed problems i havnt had anything else too complain about.

### Post by w00ly on 2009-10-31
Continued from my earlier post: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8195580#post8195580](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8195580#post8195580)
I went ahead and did the fresh install. I mounted the encrypted home directory as /home the same as I did in 9.04's install. Then I told the installer it would have to decrypt and mount the home directory. I used the same username and password as when I setup 9.04. Unfortunately it isnt decrypting and mounting and now I am stuck with an unwriteable home directory which means I have no desktop and thus my system is useless and I cant access any of my personal files at the moment.

### Post by fishears on 2009-10-31
I've been running Ubuntu for nearly 18 months now and in that time have upgraded from 8.04 to 8.1 and then to 9.04 without any problems and all on the same hardware. Then came Karmic.

I ran the upgrade and it appeared to be doing great, very fast progress. Then I got a message telling me the install of Nvidia 180 had failed. The installer still went to the end and then told me to restart. Thinking that the installer knows best,  I restarted the computer and after the white ubuntu logo disappeared I was left with just a flashing shell login.!

That's where the fun really began because the shell would only accept my keypresses when it was flashed in (not out). Typing my username was hard enough but an invisible 10-digit password?! IMPOSSIBLE.

Thank God I had a working laptop to get on the net and hit Google! When I searched there were a huge number of people in the same position. I found that I needed to install Nvidia-glx-185 (don't know why Karmic tried 180). I eventually achieved this by pressing escape at the grub loader and dropping to a root shell. That let me do a apt-get install nvidia-glx-185.

Then I restarted but this time I just got a permanent black screen after the white ubuntu logo.

So.... I decided to do a clean install. This, again appeared to go smoothly (no errors at all). Restarted, and, yes, exactly the same as the upgrade attempt - I got the flashing shell!
I repeated the manual nvidia-glx-185 install from a grub root shell and this time it worked: X started and its all working now.

I'm just surprised something as big as this got through the net and made it to release. I'd tried the latest Beta before and that had worked fine on my hardware - don't know about the RC though...

Finally, like some others have mentioned, what's with the "skip" button on the "configuring APT" process? The process takes soooo loooong but there's no info telling you what "configuring APT" is or what the effects of skipping it would be... And why does it download all the language packs only to delete them during clean up - I already told it my language?!?!

---

### Post by Relysis on 2009-10-31
I, for one it would seem, had a flawless install.  I did a clean install from 8.10 to 9.10, and love it.  Pretty much everything worked for me instantly.  The installation was incredibly fast and simple.  My one complaint with Karmic is that you can't disable the startup sounds.  This is really important, but I'm sure it will be fixed soon.

On a side note, Skype 2.1 works wonderfully now.  It's pulseaudio only, but works out of the box thanks to the new sound profiles in Karmic.  Pulse has given me no troubles so far, which is a huge step up from Hardy.

### Post by emacdonald on 2009-10-31
If you are upgrading from 9.04 to 9.10 remember to dismount any NTFS drives you may have before commencing!

Upgrading from 9.04 to 9.10. - Remember to dismount all drives not required for the upgrade.

I have had to recover the offending NTFS drive, after I left it mounted during the upgrade. Used an other CD bootable Linux Distribution, to help recover the drive, as 9.10 could not see the the drive etc...

All will be well of course it is just a matter of time.

But it seems odd that Parted (from the other dist)  can see the damaged NTFS unit and Parted 9.10 can't. (had to boot up the PC in XP recovery mode to run Fdisk etc). Offending disc is now ext3, and am reloading the files back onto it. 

When the drive is recovered, I plan to disable the HD's from the PC, install a new blank one, and install 9.10 fresh. Reason for this is the PC has been upgraded through, about, four Ubuntu upgrades, it's time to start from scratch and clean up. Hopefully it will clear up some of the niggles that I have had.

The real problem, it seems was that the OS sees the Drive twice once as sdb and also as /dev/mapper/sil....
Cannot format drive as it thinks the OS is using via the /dev/drivemapper/sill.. device.
Do not know to get round this at this time as I cannot get rid of the duplication. Something to do with dmraid perhaps.!

Anyone know how to solve this one?

---

### Post by spacecheck on 2009-10-31

Firefox access was terribly slow, until the patch was installed.

Haven't tested other apps. Or tried booting into XP. Ouch, having just seen the below caveat about dismounting NTFS before upgrade, which I did not do, my next step is to check that.

### Post by Corcorelli on 2009-10-31
I upgraded last night. No problems and very neat process. Took about 2 hours for me. I should explain I'm no techie and I have Ubuntu on my home PC basically as a matter of principle. I do like the way Ubuntu lets me mess about and get things how I like them in a way that Windows doesn't, but my wife / parents don't (how many times has that been written!).

So upgrade process was perfect for me. Comments below are things, for example, that would stop me converting my parents from Windows to Ubuntu:

1) Scary messages about replacing or keeping configuration files. I half know what I'm doing, but really I'm mostly just guessing when I answer these questions. I know there's a way of getting round this with a separate partition for configurable elements, but I haven't had the time to do it yet.

2) Disabling / re-enabling repositories. Although I guess there's some legal issues here.

3) Desktop changes. Where did my launcher for Firefox go? It was on the top panel, now I can only get it through the main menu. Easily fixed (done now), but could be unnerving.

Actually I really like these upgrades and playing around afterwards is the best bit. I could probably get it perfect if I read the upgrade notes BEFORE starting, but that would be dull. Addressing the comments above would detract from my fun, but would make me feel easier about installing Ubuntu for my parents.

---

### Post by ~williahayd~ on 2009-10-31
Upgraded both my laptop and desktop.

Desktop was a fresh install of Jaunty, as I was having some video issues a week before karmic release. Upgrade went flawlessly. Haven't had any issues since.

Laptop was a Jaunty install from a few months back, upgrade went well, but every time I minimized windows GDM/X would crash, I worked out it was the minimize compiz effects that was the culprit, so I disabled them and haven't had any issues since. Also been having issues with Ubuntu one not connecting, so I've filed a bug report on that. (EDIT: Bug report can be found here: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntuone-client/+bug/466285](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntuone-client/+bug/466285))

Other than those minor problems I'm really happy with Karmic. Loving the new notification area, and fast boot-up.

### Post by Valygard84 on 2009-10-31
Hello @all,

I first tried to upgrade, which ended in disaster. After rebooting, X wouldn't start at all (not because of any drivers - I removed those in recovery mode later). 
Even worse, my keyboard had a near-random lag between pressing a key and it being registered, making it impossible to enter my password to get into a root shell.
Also, everything was flickering rapidly.

Not wanting to spend to much time diagnosing everything, I then installed from scratch, nuking my /-partition but re-using my old /home/.
My first impression was absolutely great - Install went very smooth (and looked very nice!), and every component (except bluetooth) in my laptop (!) worked out-of-the-box.

All my settings worked, too.

Then I got hit by the DNS-bug, making surfing and such unbearably slow.
After automatically applying the workaround via shell script, everything worked fine, though.

Except my boot time is now way longer (70 sec measured with bootchart). :(

### Post by 8oluf7 on 2009-10-31
Did a clean install from the Karmic Live CD on a donated 2.4GHz P4 whitebox system with a QDI Platinex-2E V2.0 PCI6 mobo (Intel 845E chipset). The graphics card is identified as a nVidia MX/MX400 and I installed the 96 variant of the proprietary driver. I'm using a LaCie electron22blue display which can handle very high resolutions.

My problem is that on switch-on, the graphics driver is set to 'Auto' and goes for a very high resolution - tiny fonts and sluggish performance. I can open the nVidia X-server settings widget, select and change to a less challenging resolution (1280x960). BUT, when I try to 'Save to X Configuration File' I get the message: 'Failed to parse existing /etc/X11/xorg.conf' and the desired resolution doesn't survive a reboot. 

Usplash also complains that it can't handle the resolution it is given (which isn't the one I set - or AFAICT the one displayed after log-in).

### Post by TaoBoogie on 2009-10-31
> **TaoBoogie said:**
> I've just now completed the upgrade and I'm happy to say the process was completed smoothly and everything appears to be running perfectly.

Due to my internet connection it took about eight hours to upgrade from ver 9.04. Downloading and installing over 1400 packages. I followed the suggestions about removing some eighty odd packages and then upon restart my original configuration of desktop images, short-cuts, Avant launcher and wireless connection were functioning just as I like them, perfectly.

I could have let my hamster in charge while it was upgrading and got myself some sleep. This is the first time I have upgraded rather than going for a fresh install and I'm delighted to say it was a complete success. 

Oh yes, after restart there was a pop up prompt to inform me that some language packs were not complete and did I want to install them now? I clicked "yes" and the system completed the language pack installation.

Well done once again you fellahs. I'm getting some sleep now so I cam wake up early to a "box fresh" Ubuntu.

There's nothin' but good Karma going on here.:KS

I just wanted to add to my previous post (quoted above) to say that my system is a desktop:

AMD Athlon 64x2 Dual Core 4600+
500gigHD
GeForce 8500 GT

Although I have a 64BIT system I have installed the 32 bit Karmic Koala

@neilg4rqn
I thought my sound had failed until I realised the volume had been reset so low I could not hear it. While checking this I discovered my first real oddity with the install. The Volume and the wireless network connector icons are barely visible in the top task bar and actually look like they are greyed out. I'm running Avant launcher so this does not bother me unduly.

Before I upgraded I made sure through Update Manager that I;
(1) Had all the available updates installed
(2) Unmounted all other drives
(3) Closed all running applications.

Still nothing but good Karma here. I'm truly sorry to hear of the people who have had problems. Stick with it folks I'm sure the problems will be resolved given time and determination.

### Post by alpage2 on 2009-10-31
A clean install of ubuntu went flawlessly - except that the Edimax usb wireless adapter (rt73 chipset) that previously 'worked out of the box', no longer worked.

### Post by mizar001 on 2009-10-31
I have a notebook asus x50SR 
-ATI CARD mobility radeon hd3470
-4gb ram
-wireless card (seen with lspci) : Network controller: Atheros Communications Inc. AR928X Wireless Network Adapter (PCI-Express) (rev 01)

Apparently the upgrade from 9.04 to 9.10 got me no problems. But after few tries i found some issues:

1 - audio is snapping and buzzing for some application... and i note some video lags when audio fails.
2 - the streaming online (youtube, megavideo etc) freezes my pc.

Problem 1... even with some reconfigurations the sound has yet so many problems, sounds are almost unusable except for playback applications.
Problem 2 seems completely solved because the network gnome manager actually presents a bug with my wifi adapter, so i installed wicd (wich also automatically remove gnome network manager). See this bug:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/426130](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/426130)

Actually the experience with karmic koala is not as good as i hoped, but the major issues seems to have a solution for now. Hope canonical will release some audio enhancements.

### Post by bowens44 on 2009-10-31
My story.....

I did a clean install of Karmic. I went from a 32bit install of Jaunty to 64bit Karmic.

I did a manual set up of the partitions so that I could keep my /home directory. 

On boot I got the dreaded GRUB ERROR 15. I had not specified where grub should be/start up etc. Once fixing this I was able to boot without issue.

The next thing that I noticed was that I couldn't get lm_sensors to work. 
I followed some instructions I found while googling, still no go so installed lm_sensors 3.1.1. Now all is good.

The next thing that I noticed was that with the NVidia185 drivers, the hue was wrong in any app that plays video. Everybody was blue and green. 
If I started up ncvidia settings, this would clear for the current video. I ended having to install the 173 driver instaed. This works fine.

Now the unresolved big problem for me, no sound from tvtime.

I'm not sure how to fix this but if I can't it's back to Karmic.

Oh well except for the tvtime thing, I'm loving it,

### Post by wortmanb on 2009-10-31
Did an upgrade of a large media server for my home.  Apparently 910 doesn't like my Reiserfs volume because I just lost my entire 200+GB iTunes library.  I'm trying various things to try to get the box to acknowledge the reiser partition but so far it just steadfastly refuses to mount it.

I just added some resier packages and am now seeing this:

[INDENT]```
root@marvin:/var/log# mount -a
mount: /dev/sdb2 already mounted or /media/audio busy
root@marvin:~# lsof /media/audio
root@marvin:~#
root@marvin:~# df -h
Filesystem            Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sdc1              16G  8.1G  7.2G  54% /
udev                 1005M  276K 1005M   1% /dev
none                 1005M     0 1005M   0% /dev/shm
none                 1005M  332K 1005M   1% /var/run
none                 1005M     0 1005M   0% /var/lock
none                 1005M     0 1005M   0% /lib/init/rw
/dev/sdd1             107G   38G   64G  38% /media/pictures
/dev/sde1              71G   58G   11G  85% /media/documents
/dev/mapper/sil_agaedebjfbba1
                      185G  176G  8.8G  96% /media/videos
root@marvin:~# grep audio /etc/fstab
LABEL="audio"   /media/audio    reiserfs defaults 0 0
root@marvin:~# mount /dev/sdb2 /temp
mount: /dev/sdb2 already mounted or /temp busy
root@marvin:~# umount /dev/sdb2
umount: /dev/sdb2: not mounted
root@marvin:~#

```[/INDENT]
Reiserfsck turned up no errors, no corruptions on the filesystem so I'm kind of at a loss as to why it's suddenly unmountable.

Any ideas from the smart people?  ;-)

BTW, apart from this, the upgrade went without a hitch, which is great because this is a headless box and I administer it via vnc so when things go wrong, it's a real headache.

### Post by lotharmat on 2009-10-31
Did a clean install on a Fujitsu Siemens Lifebook; refomatted to ext4 and went for it from a USB stick (created with Unetbootin) Install took about 12 minutes.

[INDENT]+'s[/INDENT]
[LIST]
[*]Faster boot and shutdown times
[*]ext4 *Seems* faster
[*]Atheros AR5001 wireless card worked without the need for madwifi!!!!:D
[*]Battery life longer
[/LIST]


[INDENT]-'s[/INDENT]
[LIST]
[*]That stupid new Hard Disk Manager - reported a few bad sectors on my primary disk! Ignorance *WAS* bliss
[*]When lid was closed and laptop was docked; the external monitor was switched off too - A simple trip to gconf-editior should have done the trick with this - I' need to confirm when I get back to work.
[/LIST]

All in all a painless install and I'm looking forward to having some fun with it!

### Post by efflandt on 2009-10-31
I had only first installed Ubunto (v9.04) on my desktop and laptop a week ago, so I had nothing to backup or lose trying 9.10 upgrade.

Upgrade Manager took hours to upgrade Friday night (servers busy) to 9.10 64-bit on **HP A530n** (circa 2004) AMD Athlon64 3200+ 2000 MHz 1024K cache (different PSU and ATI video).  After upgrade I got notification about critical (but non-fatal) errors with kernel 2.6.31-14 apparently because"

```
OopsText:
 EDAC amd64: WARNING: ECC is NOT currently enabled by the BIOS. Module will NOT be loaded.
     Either Enable ECC in the BIOS, or use the 'ecc_enable_override' parameter.
     Might be a BIOS bug, if BIOS says ECC is enabled
     Use of the override can cause unknown side effects.
 amd64_edac: probe of 0000:00:18.2 failed with error -22
```But CMOS setup does not mention anything about ECC or any memory settings.  My solution, booting alternate kernel 2.6.28-16 runs without errors (nothing about ECC in any logs) and everything works fine with no changes to any settings.

I also had to get rid of the non-Adobe flash pluggin I had installed earlier from Firefox, and get the alpha 64-bit version per sticky in 64-bit forum.

**Toshiba A105** laptop with 1.6 GHz Intel dual core, 32-bit upgrade Saturday morning was quicker and faultless including wireless, with no changes in settings.  It works fine with the 32-bit 2.6.31-14 kernel.

PS: If anyone has sound issues check to make sure that your sound is not muted (speaker icon at top).  The upgrade muted the sound on mine.

---

### Post by brc816 on 2009-10-31
Tried to do a "new" installation of Ubuntu 9.10 on the partitition of my USB drive that had held Karmic Koala RC.

It managed to wipe the partition table off of the USB drive. I am now running gpart (under my normal day-to-day openSUSE 11.1 system (I see I need to update my .sig below!) that knows how to behave itself and not scribble nilly-willy all over whatever platters it finds). Fortunately, I had saved a paper copy of my partition table for that drive.

My experience with the beta and RC versions of KK lead me to believe that while Ubuntu itself is very solid and stable once installed, the installation is a very scary process because so much of what's going on is hidden from view and doesn't permit a knowledgeable user to control it. I have (or maybe now had...) four other single-partition Linux installations on the USB drive that I could select from at boot time. Couple that with GRUB2's prediliction for the same sort of behaviour, and I am going to be extremely reluctant to attempt another installation, and then perhaps only to a 16GB thumbdrive where I can (hopefully) keep it isolated from anything valuable.

### Post by Korrode on 2009-10-31
These are my posts from another thread... i didn't notice this thread earlier, but i figure what i posted should be in here:
> **Korrode said:**
> I have an early Audigy and also am having problems.
Firstly i'll note; i used the 'alternate' CD and did a 'command line only' install, then installed xorg, openbox, alsa-base, etc.

Applications running in X don't seem to be able to talk to ALSA.

eg.
If i run alsamixer from bash when i've first booted, it's fine.
Whilst i'm running X, if i hit ctrl+alt+F2 and goto that console and run alsamixer, it's fine.
If i run alsamixer in a terminal within my X session, i get:```
alsamixer: function snd_ctl_open failed for default: No such file or directory
```

I've tried reloading ALSA while X is running```
:~$ sudo alsa force-reload
```
...and it unloads and reloads fine, but doesn't help my problem.

I also tried using module-assistant to build my own alsa kernel module using the alsa-source package... didn't help.

Considering ALSA is working fine outside of X, i can only assume X itself, or maybe some new "protect the user from themself" technology is the cause.

Any help would be greatly appreciated :(

For the last ~year i ran Debian Testing, but decided the benefits of a 'rolling-distro' were being out-weighed by the downfalls, so with the release of Karmic i've come back to Ubuntu, and so far i'm really happy and impressed, but this sound issue is kicking my ***. :P> **Korrode said:**
> Well i've now fixed it...

My mind can't understand the logic of it, but the solution ended up being simple; I added myself to the 'audio' group.

Usually this would've been one of the first things i'd check, but i figured it couldn't be that because my user account could use ALSA from a console just fine.

Weird.

### Post by Raptor Ramjet on 2009-10-31
I've got 4 machines running Ubuntu here.  A mini-itx server, a PVR running MythBuntu and 2 desktops.

So far I tried upgrading one of the a desktops which now gets "grub error 25" when it boots.  Boring but (probably fixable)

As for the PVR... I was looking forward to 9.10 as my sata DVD drive didn't work under 9.04 (which made it a pretty useless PVR...)  

Originally I'd also had tremendous problems getting HDMI audio to work (it took me nearly a month of googling and trying stuff out) and guess what ?  After the upgrade it doesn't work again.  Even worse I almost know how to fix it as having done various tweaks I've got *most* audio working.  However the last piece of the puzzle I needed last time was to run "gnome-sound-properties" and set the default audio device to HDMI.

But "gnome-sound-properties" doesn't seem to exist at 9.10 and I'm simply too jaded to do any more googling to see how to fix it.

And who had the great idea to set alsa HDMI audio to muted by default ?  Even worse why was it reset to muted during an upgrade ?  That is rubbish.

As for the server it's not getting upgraded anyway as it does everything I need it to at the minute.  The other desktop is also staying at 9.04 as I've had enough of spending *all* my PC time trying to fix things.

I've said this before and I'll say it again.  Audio on Ubuntu is simply dreadful.  Badly thought out, badly implemented and overcomplicated with layer after layer of crap and no centralised GUI to fix everything from one place (not to mention that it's also prone to break at the drop of a hat)

So sadly Ubuntu 9.10 rates a solid "0/10 totally unimpressed" from me so far.

### Post by manzdagratiano on 2009-10-31

Ubuntu it shall be to the grave...
Had been waiting for karmic for quite some time, following newsletters, forum posts (invisibly) and google in general... I decided to upgrade to the release candidate on the 28th as I could not wait anymore; while upgrading to nvidia 185.xx during the process I am told something to the effect of "this driver has no modules in the kernel 2.6.31, installing in 2.6.28"; upgrade completed with problems - graphics are a mess on reboot because artefacts plagued the entire screen, and I would be a few mouse clicks away from freezes and then an inevitable reboot.
Reinstalling nvidia made no difference, so I decided to check if compiz was the problem. Reinstalling compiz made no difference, so I decided to do a clean install of karmic on the 29th (it was out yay!)... ### Post by bblinn on 2009-10-31
Unfortunately, Karmic Koala reminds me of Vista.

I hope that I've missed something that will be obvious to others who are more familiar with Karmic Koala. For me, it's all but unusable.

In earlier versions of Ubuntu, I discovered that I liked the visual effects and turned all of them on. Under Karmic Koala, they were turned off, so I turned them on again. The system told me it could not enable the effects. Then I tried the middle step. No joy there, either. I could enable either set of features on the same machine in the earlier version and Windows 7 has no problem running all of the Aero effects on the same dual-boot computer. I'm guessing that Karmic hasn't found the appropriate drivers for the video system.

GRUB continued to show Ubuntu 9.04 on its menu. To update this, I planned to open KGRUBEditor and edit the file. It was nowhere to be found and it wasn't available via any package manager. I opened the file in a plain text editor (as super user) and manually typed the new information in. Where did KGRUBEditor go and why can't either the package manager or the Ubuntu Software Center find it? I suppose I could download it and manually install it, but I don't have enough Linux experience to trust myself.

What has really surprised me, though is the speed of the system. Saying it's lethargic would be needlessly kind. On an Intel Core Duo 2.26GHz system with 3GB of RAM, Karmic Koala crawls from one screen to the next instead of snapping as it did under 9.04. I'm guessing that this may be related to the video issue.

Firefox is unusable. It's that slow. Firefox is normally somewhat slow on Linux, but taking several seconds to switch from one tab to the next is entirely too slow. Fortunately, Opera is considerably faster.

That's what I've encountered so far. I would be most grateful for any recommendations.

### Post by Donalb on 2009-10-31
I've stayed on Intrepid because of the Jaunty Intel Graphics regressions so I was excited about jumping to Karmic. Decided to do an install rather than upgrade, as my feeling was I'd read more negatives about upgrading over than part few years. Grabbed the ISO  (in less than 5 minutes since the US hadn't yet woken up).

Did my backup. Plonked in the CD and started the install.

Got a "No operating systems present". Odd because I have a dual-boot system so Linux & Wins should be detected. 
Stopped the install and launched the new Palimpsest Drive Utility. Which told me my laptop HDD had 1202 bad sectors...

Called Dell. (I've got an e6400 laptop)

"Can you boot into Windows please sir". "No", I lied, "I only use Linux". "I trust my Linux drive utility & it tells me my drive is dying". "Ok sir let's try the onboard diagnostics". We do that. The drive passes. He says "Ok you've a 3 year warranty, I'll send you out a new drive anyway". 
"Ta very much".

Next I reboot and run GParted, and clone my failing HDD (all 5 partitions) to an external USB HDD. (I've never done this before but 5 minutes reading the basics was sufficient). 
So my HDD is cloned & I'm waiting for my replacement HDD to clone the blank and replace.

My points?
1: Upgrade wouldn't have detected this problem and I'd have a crash somewhere down the line.
2: I LOVE the new disk utility. (I used to work for a Hard drive company in a former life).
3: GParted is great for cloning, if a bit cumbersome compared to Norton Ghost.
4: All this done without command line. I can cut and paste fine, but it should be possible to do everything GUI, and it was so far.

Back when I'm done. But hugely impressed so far.

---

### Post by RTrev on 2009-10-31
Only solvable problems so far here.

1) Setting up the Twinview monitors took a bit of fancy footwork.  The nvidia driver was installed, and it placed nvidia-settings in the menu system.  It is unable to write to the /etc/X11/xorg.conf file since it's not running as root.  If I exit and run it as root, it then has some other error when I attempt to save the settings.  What I had to do was:
```

cd /etc/X11
sudo rm xorg.conf
sudo nvidia-xconfig
sudo nvidia-settings

```

2) VirtualBox, as installed from the repositories, has problems.  It shows no option to use USB devices, and it can't install the Guest additions.  Adding the Sun repository and downloading the slightly later version from them solved all problems.

3) Pan news reader crashes after any use of the spell-checker.  I haven't figured out any solution for this yet, short of not using the spell-checker at all.

Seems really solid and nice!!

Bob

### Post by PhilGil on 2009-10-31
[FONT=ARIAL,][SIZE=2][COLOR=Black]My vote was for [/COLOR][/SIZE][/FONT][I]Install - worked but had few things to fix, nothing serious though

[/I][FONT=ARIAL,][SIZE=2][COLOR=Black]I did a clean install of the RC so I could reformat my drives to the Ext4 file system.  I then upgraded to final release using the Upgrade Manager.

Had the following problems after the install:

1. Permissions on my 2nd hard drive were assigned to root after reformatting.  Had to change them back to myself.

2. The shared NTFS partition on my boot drive wasn't assigned as a separate drive (it was in Jaunty).  It took me a few minutes to hunt it down in the file system.

3. I was hit by [this bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/417757"), which made web browsing very painful until I switched my DNS Server to Open DNS (instructions are in the launchpad thread and [here]("https://store.opendns.com/setup/device/ubuntu/")).  The workaround functions fine, but I believe this bug should be addressed by the devs.  

4.  Had to spend some time fiddling with VirtualBox to get USB support working again (yes, I installed the correct version).

On the plus side, my sound functioned correctly out of the box (a first), the default theme is more attractive and some rendering problems I was having in DigiKam seem to be resolved.

### Post by oldHat on 2009-10-31
I started by running a dist-upgrade.  I made the mistake of downloading and burning the distro instead of the other disk.  Wish it was possible to do an upgrade from *that* CD instead of doing a network upgrade.

Problem #1:  no sound after the upgrade.  LSMOD showed that no module was loaded for my VT1708/A (it seems to use snd_hda_intel).  I couldn't get it going, so I did a fresh install off the CD.

My mistake:  I probably should have inventoried my software add-ons before I wiped them out (maybe there is a tool to do that).  It will probably be a few weeks before I finish re-installing the stuff I've forgotten.  Afterwards, I referred back to [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1181327](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1181327) in order to get many of the codecs and apps I wanted.  I'll look forward to seeing a karmic upgrade to this documentation.

Problem #2:  Grub Error 15 on boot after the install.  I have a separate boot partition and two hard drives; perhaps this confused the installer.  I reinstalled a few times, but this didn't fix the problem.  Somebody posted a link to this howto:  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1195275](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1195275) .  I followed the directions on "Reinstalling from the Live CD."  This got me going again.  However, I'm still using Grub Classic now, not Grub2.

Issue: /dev/sda is now /dev/sdb and /dev/sdb is now /dev/sda.  This has happened before, and I mostly use my UUIDs, so this wasn't a big issue and didn't come as a complete surprise.
