### Post by rbsteinbach on 2009-10-31
I had two machines with Ubuntu on them. 

 The IBM TP-G41 upgraded flawlessly over existing 9.04 running from an NTFS partition on the hard drive.  In fact, I had been unable to get the wireless adapter on the TP to work under 9.04 but it works under 9.10 with no input needed by me.  Great! :p

My other PC has been a disaster. :(  I had lost the original 160GB PATA drive a few weeks ago (which had 9.04 on it and working fine), and put in a new drive imaged from the old one, but only had used WinXP since.  When I tried to boot to 9.04 for the upgrade it failed to load 9.04.  No big deal, so I decided to repartition and set up a small (10GB) dedicated Linux partition after the last Win partition.  I repeatedly got "invalid parameter" errors from trying to install over NTFS, even trying 9GB and 8GB Linux sizes.  I then booted from the CD (ISO checksum is okay), and tried to make it a real Linux partition.  Seemed to work, but no entry in Boot.Ini and no GRUB for startup.  Tried that a few times and it never worked.  Tried making the desired partition larger, but still no good.  Finally I deleted the "new Linux partition" and merged it back into the last NTFS partition on the drive so that I could let Ubuntu take what it wanted out of that for Linux.  Working from the CD again to install from Windows, it STILL failed with "invalid parameters".  SO, I tried just invoking WUBI.EXE with no CD, but in the same directory where the ISO resides, and it seemed to install just fine.  Rebooted to "complete the installation", and Ubuntu came up at the end as expected.  Then I shut down and upon attempting to reboot into Ubuntu, it hangs at "Try hd(0,0): FAT16.  Hacking the wubilder.mbr it looks like it can't recognize the partition being used, even though its fine in WinXP...

I have uninstalled and attempted to reinstall Ubuntu 9.10 over and over and no matter what this PC just can't get it to work.  There is really nothing special about this custom built PC, and 9.04 installed from the web originally just fine.  Now I can't install 9.10 nor reinstall 9.04 (same "invalid parameter" error) from the CD, so I'm totally stuck.

Bummed out to the max! :(  

SUMMARY:  One laptop upgraded flawlessly, one PC now totally hosed for Ubuntu (but still okay for windows).

### Post by immrlizard on 2009-10-31
I have been using Ubuntu since the release of 6.06 with very few difficulties


I started using this latest at alpha 3 and have been really impressed.  I always do clean installs.    I have had some difficulty in the past with upgrades, so I don't really think that is the best way to go.

That being said, I think that the folks that make the decisions on what to include in the releases may want to consider moving to a dvd install.    I have had several installs fail (all alternate versions) because it has to download a large number of files from somewhere.   It gets to 91% and freezes for hours.   I did get the non alternate version to install though.   I did have to tell it to skip the language packs.

I consider this a severe error on their part when an alpha install goes without a flaw and then a full release has such dramatic fails.   I do realize that the servers are getting hammered because of the installs going on, but experiences like this aren't going to go well for folks that are experiencing it for the first time.

In contrast, I set that exact laptop up in 23 minutes with windows 7

Just something to think about when deciding what to include and not include in the default setups and iso images.    Color me unimpressed with what I have seen so far on this release.  I am probably going back to 9.04 and avoiding new installs until things improve.   It is ridiculous to worry so much about layout and design when the basics are ignored

---

### Post by Phenomena on 2009-10-31
Upgraded from Jaunty with alternate CD. The upgrade process went smoothly but after restarting I was welcomed with the flickering black screen of death (FBSD). No GUI to boot. I have a nVidia GeForce 7300. So after searching for a solution, many people suggested re-installing the driver. I removed the driver (with --purge), then switched to nv driver. Now GUI works but of course low quality. Reinstalled nVidia driver and now good quality but on both drivers I get a VERY slow boot up and the login screen takes ages. It takes around 5 min to show the users list. Then after I select the user, it takes around 30 sec to show the password prompt. Then another 30 sec to show the desktop.

The only work around for this horrible slow process is to enable auto-login which I don't like very much. Anyway, I don't seem to be able to change the style of the login window from System -> Administration -> Login Screen

System freezes suddenly on some occasions altho not so often after doing the partial upgrade via Update Manager.

I am shocked that so many people went through similar problems. Jaunty was a breeze. I think this release needs to be withdrawn for further investigation.

RAM: 1 GB

All in all not bad, but not as easy as 8.04 to 9.04.

### Post by imaca on 2009-11-01
Had a few problems with the alpha, and for me Beta desktop would not install at all.
Finally all up and going.
Two main issues are:
1.Sound.
Control now seems to be a tangled mess.
ALSA Sound volume is turned down at every reboot.
I only managed to get Tvtime (my only app which doesn't output via spdif) sound working by installing ALSA mixer and turning the volume up after every reboot. I would imagine there are a lot of people with no idea why they have no sound anymore because of this.
Also Amarok2 occasionally has little micro stutters (not CPU load related). Never had any problem with Amarok 1 in Hardy.

2. DVDs
If I am logged in and insert a DVD, Karmic insists that I have no rights to access it. Consequently, I have to reboot every time I want to watch a DVD. Again, I imagine there are many people scratching their heads over this one.

For me, Karmic has been a bit of a multimedia disaster. By far the most issues of any version I have previously used (started at 5.10)

### Post by aaronp on 2009-11-01
I started with Hardy. I had my home on a separate partition so decided rather than following the upgrade path via Jaunty and Intrepid, I would format my root partition and install Karmic RC over the top.

It worked well for me. The initial issue was a freezing mouse and keyboard after choosing to install from the livecd menu. I figured out this was the absence of 'noapic' option that I've needed to add to all of my boots since getting this PC.
After that, it went into the installation and everything went off without a hitch.

My main concern was ensuring I integrated my home partition correctly. When it came time to do partitioning I ensured that I told the partitioner to mount my home partition as /home in my new system. I had a 'back of my mind' feeling that I might be telling it to format that partition and mount it as the fresh, clean new /home for my install. I had my home backed up elsewhere anyway so wasn't too concerned.
When I got to the next screen I then easily saw it was not going to format the home partition so got some relief.
I was still unsure about permissions etc. so chose the same username on my new system as I had on Hardy.

The install then finished pretty quickly and smoothly after that. The much complained issue with 15 mins of language pack downloads only took about 2 mins on my system and within about 30 mins I was ready to boot.

Upon booting, my first experience again was loss of my mouse and keyboard as well as the new dark brown 'spotlight' Karmic login background just sitting there with nothing else on it.
Oops...forgot the 'noapic' option again. Hard reboot and then realised I have no idea how to get to the grub2 menu so I can add the noapic option to the menu.
Had a couple more hard reboots hitting different keys hoping it would give me the grub menu. Then I decided to look it up on the other computer - fount out the key is SHIFT. Press it when it says grub loading and it presents the menu.
OK, so now I've added the noapic option and my mouse light is still on as it boots up - a good sign.
Pretty fast boot, about 20 seconds from grub to login screen - but this is not overly relevant to me as I never turn my PC off or restart it.

About to login and my next question is whether the /home partition will be easy or hard to integrate into the new install.
As I login I see the picture of my son on the desktop and all of my old launchers still sitting there on the desktop - relief! (albeit the underlying targets for all the launchers are all absent, sitting the repos waiting for me to download them all again!)

Loving Karmic so far. UbuntuOne was a bit of a big one for me and that is pretty buggy so far, so that's disappointing but hopefully won't be long before it's working for us all.

I noticed the package upgrade from the RC to the real release actually happened the day before the real full release of Karmic came out.
I found this a bit strange, but it was very easy (97mb of packages to upgrade) and I managed to get out of downloading the entire ISO while millions of others tried to as well.

Aaron

---

### Post by angelus13 on 2009-11-01
Howzit guys, 

Did an upgrade about 2 nights ago, flawless upgrade and install. only problem is that it is not working like ubuntu anymore, its more like i am using windows now. Reason being that there are so many new problems that were not there before. Nautilus has been dying 2 or 3 times and I restart it, but still dies again. Then I need to restart the laptop. Jaunty never gave me these problems, also the login screen, albeit its nice to have your name there, but linux was developed for security, and just providing your name right there, comon now. Also Auth Keyring, for mounting fat/ntfs filesystems. seriously, not something I need at the moment. Its as if I am test running Vista for the first time.

Thing is that the team worked hard, I would give them that. But you are not sticking to Ubuntu/Linux's image of being user friendly, at the moment, I have had more stress using Ubuntu, than I have in the last say 3 or 4 years. I am using linux for the last 10 years now, even with coding into all the other packages, I never had these irritations that I am experiencing now. 

I am reverting back to Jaunty and when the next update is out, I hope it is better refined than this one. 

Sorry guys, good work, but not your best effort.

### Post by Martin_sensei on 2009-11-01
Hello all,

Well today things are going much better than yesterday:

At this time yesterday morning I had just finished the install and was looking black screen with some flickering white text asking me to login.  Although the keyboard input was also very erratic so I could not do anything.  There are a few comments in this thread on how so solve this, which I did by updating the NVIDIA drivers from a recovery session, but after a bit of success and a few reboots, I was presented with a white screen after logging on, and had no real GUI at all!

However today I installed again, from scratch (this time straight from the CD without a live session) and it all just worked... What happened yesterday?!

I do agree with the previous comment though, why are we showing user names on the log on screen?  I liked that extra bit of security...  However I will still with 9.10 for now as pulse audio seems to be finally working properly with Skype (never got it to work in 9.04).  Also I like the Ubuntu one integration, and the foriegn keyboard support seems better.

Cheers

Martin

### Post by tastyracer on 2009-11-01
I'm a recent Windows-to-Jaunty convert. I love it, but have been a bit mystified why it is so hard to get file-sharing working properly. Anyway, I have three boxes to play with so decided to upgrade one to new Karmic.
Download & install went fine, but can't run Compiz as it's unstable and leaves desktop background black & empty (eg no mount icons) - switching to no Visual Effects works fine. Further tests showed this also happens with fresh install, and LiveCD, and only on my machines with ATI Radeon cards.
This is annoying, particularly when 9.04 was ok and I see from Googling that this bug seems to have been present through beta. I now can't upgrade my son's box as he loves the effects!
Also, yet again the only way I can file sharing to work is to install Winbind and manually edit /etc/nssswitch.conf as detailed in []("https://bugs.launchpad.net/debian/+source/libdrm/+bug/348332")[this]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1169149") life-saving post.
So....
Thumbs-up for new look, layout, ease of installation (will never go back to Windows)
Thumbs down for file-sharing for non-tecchies, and ATI/Radeon/Compiz compatibilty

---

### Post by Eberbachl on 2009-11-01
I've done a couple of Karmic installs:

Firstly, a clean install if i386 9.10 Desktop on my friends HP Compaq nx5000 laptop. Absolutely painless. All devices working beautifully. Slick and fast. Nice!

:D

Secondly, a UNR 9.10 install on my MSI Wind U100+ netbook. Install painless enough, but sadly I'm suffering from the well documented Brightness Flicker Bug, and Webcam/USB bug. Unfortunately, both very major bugs! I tried the nomodeset workaround for the Screen Flicker bug, which stopped the screen flicker - but it made the netbook unusably slow. I can stop it by setting the brighteness to zero in power management, which of course results in a slightly dim screen (still not terrible). I cannot adjust brightness without the flicker coming back though :(

Hopefully the above two bugs will be resolved soon, as they're affecting a large audience, and would certainly be enough to stop many from using and/or switching to Ubuntu.

I will install 9.10 AMD64 on my desktop before long, but it requires a more significant backup before blowing it away. :D

### Post by etxkesa on 2009-11-01
I upgraded from Jaunty to Karmic a few days ago. I was hoping a few of the bugs which I found irritating with Jaunty re sync with Windows CE might have been fixed:(
The upgrade took all of the morning (I don't have that many packages installed). After the installation when I did the reboot, I noted a cleaner boot screen. My existing XP disk was picked up correctly. Te only major problem I had was that my Ethernet didn't get detected. I had to manually define a connection by entering the address of my router so that DHCP could do it's magic. Once over that most things work OK, though the syncce functions are not at all ready. I got a kernel error last time I docked my Palm Treo. I have reported both this error plus the one with the missing Ethernet. The only major problem I have, which is really only an irritation, is that I haven't been able to change the boot priority. I did a grub-install -v and got 0.97 as the answer, so I guess I must still have Grub 1. Whatever, changing the old /boot/grub/menu file doesn't do anything.
IN general I'm happy. The new Firefox version is much closer the the one I run under Windows and that is a big improvement. Open office seems to be an improved version too.

### Post by M0WYM on 2009-11-01
Being relatively new to Linux at the time of the 8.04 LTS release I stayed with that until now. The box I use is pretty old but very reliable and consists of an AMD Athlon 1250MHz CPU, 512Mb RAM and a FX5600XT 128mB video card. It has a second 20GB drive that once held held Windows but is now used for media storage.

The initial instal on this box was 7.10 from a Linux Magazine DVD and all other updates have been from downloads. There was a bit of a problem with the video drivers on 7.1 but once sorted I have never looked back.

So this weekend I sauntered down the upgrade path from 8.04 to 8.1 (Friday evening) and then from 8.1 to 9.04 followed by the final upgrade to 9.10 (Saturday).

Along the way I was asked to make a couple of minor decisions that I was totally unqualified to make but went with updated version of whatever it was.

Each upgrade went well although download speeds were poor but I doubt that I was the only person downloading! So far I have had no problems and am very pleased with the Koala! Just one minor issue is that on the two boots that Ive done so far a pop-up says that I don't have the full English language package installed but as far as I can tell I do. Not even a niggle ;)

So, a massive thanks to a lot of hardworking and clever people!!!!


Charlie.

### Post by dreamingdarkness on 2009-11-01

My peeves:
Ubuntu Software Center is not a good replace for add/remove programs. Do not remove Synaptic as well until this software is an adequate replacement for ALL the functions for ALL types of user (newbie to power-user or sysadmin)

NetworkManager restarts itself? I manually kill that task for a reason sometimes, thankyouverymuch. I also managed to drive it into failure by trying different passwords for my wireless, since I forgot the WPA2 key.

Volume icon on taskbar: Can't double-click on it to mute anymore. Seriously, that's small but important here. Doubleclick on volume icon == mute. Universal. K thx.



Likes:
Better notification icons

Boot speed improvement
Shutdown speed improvement
Grub2: new options, though we'll see once I play with it how I feel. I wants my password locked boot menu!

### Post by gersoid on 2009-11-01
I thought I'd upgrade from my dual boot Vista + 9.04 to Win7 + 9.10

Win7 - Installed OK, annoyingly had to download the Nvidia drivers, but now nice to use - much snappier than Vista.

back to  Win7. Life too short.

a) Interesting that biggest category here is "I installed and had major issues"

### Post by frisket on 2009-11-01
A few buglets, most notably not being able to generate umlaut, grave, or circumflex characters from the (apparently correct) keyboard setting (see my post elsewhere on this). Stunningly, 9.10 fixes the earlier inability to support the nVidia Geforce4 card, which I simply didn't believe would ever happen, and which had prevented me from using 8.10 and 9.04 (not even the Live CD would work). Well done someone!

The rest *aren't Ubuntu's fault*, although perhaps **Ubuntu should bitch about these things louder**. Individual users' voices tend to get ignored by developers, who always believe they know better.

[LIST=1]
[*]KPDF and KDVI have gone missing: 
[LIST]
[*]KDE has bundled KPDF into KDEgraphics as Okular, which is OK but rough around the edges, and badly missing the "Current" [page] option in Print;
[*]KDVI seems to have been dropped entirely, which very careless, as it's required for Kile and anyone using standard [La]TeX.
[/LIST]
**Solution**: install the missing dependencies libpoppler2_0.6.4-1ubuntu1_i386.deb and mediamanager_3.5.10-0ubuntu3_i386.deb by hand, then kdvi_3.5.10-0ubuntu1_i386.deb and kpdf_3.5.10-0ubuntu1~hardy1.1_i386.deb by hand. Updated version of these two essentials should be constructed and added to the repos until Okular matures a bit.
[*]Emacs 22's "Buffers" menu does not show buffer filenames any more, only entries to let you cycle between buffers, meaning you have to remember all your currently open files by name. This is an extraordinarily silly design decision, and does not appear to be reversible in the Options.
[/LIST]
Later edit: Emacs bug fixed by adding "export GDK_NATIVE_WINDOWS=1"

I still couldn't hand 9.10 to my mother, though, without a lot of post-install tuning. The usability increases with each release, but it still requires a significant foreknowledge of computing and networking technology.

### Post by msaul on 2009-11-01
I'm using older 32-bit pentium IV (DEll) with following hardware items:

- Hauppauge HVR1600 Dual Tuner card
- Nvideo GEForce 7600 video graphics card

I upgraded, but clobbered many settings, didn't work correctly.
I did new install, but when setup system hung at "Starting NTP" prior to graphical login.

Here is how I solved that problem:

1. edited /etc/default/grub file
   (btw, now notice you can't edit /boot/grub/menu.lst from
    fresh install)

   Changed the line that reads:
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash"

to

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet nosplash vmalloc=256MB"

and needed to run

update-grub




When I upgraded, the system didn't recognize ATSC portion of my Hauppauge HVR1600 dual tuner card, but when I did fresh install, it had no problem detecting all the cards.

I was impressed with the faster speed of scanning for NTSC (cable) and ATSC (OTA) channels. One thing I notice is that cable scan only displays up to channel 59, but higher channels are included and can be later viewed...


When running frontend, I noticed that pressing y key doesn't work, but when you bring up menu, you can select to switch input. Perhaps a newer table of keys or instructions to map keys for Mythbuntu 9.10 would be in order here...

Frontend didn't display my OTA channels, but I followed old post I made that got it working for 9.10. Here is a link for those that may be experiencing the same problems (with the same hardware):

[http://murraysaul.wordpress.com/2009/07/11/mythtv-part-vii/]("http://murraysaul.wordpress.com/2009/07/11/mythtv-part-vii/")

So, I have it working, but I notice some possible bugs:

1. Although I can record a program, I can't seem to delete it from
   the "Delete Recordings" from the "Manage Recordings Section".

   It looks as if it just goes to "View Recordings" screen
   Is this a bug?

2. I will have to check into this, but I can't seem to run 
   'update manager' - it started to run, but stops...

This is my experience...

In general, very nice, but needs some more tweaks, but I'm running the newer system... Good.

### Post by djchandler on 2009-11-01
I tried a fresh install on some spare hardware I have, Athlon 64 3400+, Asrock K8upgradeNF3, 2GB OCZ DDR 400, Samsung 40GB IDE drive, several different optical drives. I was getting crash reports about [kernel problems, something about ECC not being set properly in BIOS.]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/kerneloops/+bug/422536") Motherboard doesn't suppport ECC, and RAM isn't ECC. Very discouraging outcome so far. Luckily I didn't try anything with live, working, data. I use my laptop running 9.04 for most daily tasks. Karmic may not ever go on it as things look now, nor am I willing to risk my Mythbuntu 9.04 backend server to Karmic yet.

The releases between LTS releases need to be recategorized IMHO. They feel like betas at best. LTS releases I've used, Dapper and Hardy, have been rock solid. It seems the last release before the LTS is especially problematic. Gutsy almost drove me away from Ubuntu after Edgy and Feisty had gone so well.[ I posted something similar about the intermediate releases on ZDNet almost a month ago.]("http://talkback.zdnet.com/5208-12554-0.html?forumID=1&threadID=69891&messageID=1337897") This is a response to an [Adrian Kingsley-Hughes article on his Hardware 2.0 blog]("http://blogs.zdnet.com/hardware/?p=5726"). I'm sad to say that so far my "gut" says this is a 7.10 experience all over again for me. I hope I'm wrong.

BTW, there's a troll on ZDNet that made [comments even then about the color scheme]("http://talkback.zdnet.com/5208-12554-0.html?forumID=1&threadID=69891&messageID=1338937"). I thought he was crazy then, but not so much now. BTW I really screwed up GRUB/PC, which was what installed by default on the test machine MBR,  trying to change the opening splash screen with [startupmanager]("http://packages.ubuntu.com/karmic/startupmanager").

Word to the wise--don't start hacking the GRUB/PC scripting, even trying to modify it using a tool from the current repository. I ended up with an unbootable computer, and I may file a bug report on launchpad over that bit of trouble. Could boot with SuperGrub, bu gave up on rescuing the MBR or whatever it is that fouled the boot-up process.

### Post by ColJep on 2009-11-01
I've had mixed results. I did a clean install on my main machine after making a copy of my home folder. This went flawlessly or as near as makes no difference. I am glad to see that my Intel graphics now work perfectly. I just have sound or lack of it to sort out.

I have another computer with Nvidia graphics that I use for testing other distros and anything new that might cause problems on my main machine. I first tried the AMD64 Ubuntu and could only get 800 x 600 graphics and no offer of Nvidia. There are the drivers on the CD but these effectively gave me a Black screen of Death with and Out of Range message on the screen. Second attempt and I could not get the Monitor recognised.

Gave that up and tried i386 Kubuntu. Similar problems but no sign of Nvidia drivers or updates.

Finally put Ubuntu 9.10 on and installation went perfectly. Soon had updates and all software available. Nvidia driver installed and monitor recognised.

Make your own conclusions!

### Post by mrdak on 2009-11-01
I did a fresh install of 9.10 and everything went smooth. "almost"
Dualbooting Vista 64bit, and Karmic 64bit on two seperate SATA drives.

So.........grub loads, and I can boot vista, but can't boot linux. 

I was getting this error....the initrd is too big... press any key. 
Pressing any key sends it right back to the grub screen. Same thing when I tried recovery......No joy.

I found the fix over at ubuntu-bugs, in their mail archive. 
I needed to disable memory hole, and memory hole remapping in my bios. Now this mofo boots just fine. ;)
Using a DFI-LP UT 790FX board. 
Now...... if I could get more than just my system sounds working, I'll be jammin. My Xonar D2X worked perfectly with 9.04 so I'm sure I'll get it working today....... fingers crossed....

---

### Post by geoffm on 2009-11-01
I upgraded from 9.04. After reboot, my display was a white screen, only the cursor appeared. I suspected window manager, turns out the problem was compiz. I could uninstall it blindly using the console (thanks tilda!), then I rebooted and launched metacity.

Fn up/down does increase/decrease brightness by 20%, which is quite unconvenient when you use it all the time.

More importantly, I experience a **severe lockup** when I suspend. I have to remove the battery. After reboot, bug manager says here was an important kernel crash, etc. This happens when I run kernel 2.6.31-14, when I run kernel 2.6.28-16 it suspends and wakes up fine, but audio doesn't work anymore.

I can't even run through the System testing app, it crashes after the audio tests. All audio tests but one fail, but audio works with rhythmbox, youtube and VLC.


EDIT:

Now I reinstalled from scratch. It's flawless: compiz, suspend, and audio all work out of the box with the 2.6.31 kernel.

### Post by Bao2 on 2009-11-01
My problems are:

Ubuntu Software Center: Just annoying. I liked in 9.04 check one app, and then check another one, and check another one and when I finish check all the apps click the icon to install them. Doing the same now is impossible, you need a whole day to check, click the annoying arrow and then click the Install, and the same for the next program and the same for the next one. Annoying. And that is not all: you will be asked for the password every two minutes or so, so if you are installing 10 apps you will be asked for your password around six times or more. Annoying. I will have to do installs in the terminal now instead or I am going to go mad.

Ubuntu One: I just click on it and nothing happens. I wonder if some day I will see an icon flashing or what this is supposed to do (perhaps open a browser?). Incredible.

Sound: When I entered ubuntu 9.04 a personalized sound was playing that I configured in the sound dialog. I can't find where I can configure now the sounds. I would love to play that sound again when Ubuntu 9.10 starts

MeTV: Worked in 9.04. Now I am without TV in 9.10

Disks: I have four hard disks. In ubuntu 9.04 I installed the Authorizations app and changed to be able to mount them automatically. So in Nautilus just click on a hard disk and I could access to it. In 9.10 I am asked of password and installing the Authorizations app is not solution anymore: now there is absolutely nothing to configure anymore in that app. I don't like the idea of automounting the disks because I don't like that, I would love the Authorizations app to be able to configure that again.

Backup: I did a backup of Ubuntu 9.04 before installing 9.10 incase it goes bad and I must to go back to 9.04. Well, I decided wait for next release and go back. I recovered from the backup and when I start the computer it gives me an error "No such file" so I don't know what 9.10 did with the MBR or whatever it be but I couldn't go back to 9.04. Luck that I decided to backup my files also "the normal way" copying them to another disk without using systemrescuecd.

### Post by John Harrington on 2009-11-01
I had multiple problems trying to upgrade 32-bit Ubuntu on system with 1.5 GB Ram, Nvidia GEForce4 MX 4000 video driver, and AMD Sempron 3000+ processor.  Upgrade seemed to go smoothly until reboot.  Boot up said that about 600 packages needed to be upgraded, and then stopped with a login prompt and a flashing console, making it impossible to enter a password, as others have reported.  This happened regardless of whether I used the new or old kernels, and regardless of whether I used normal or recovery mode.  I was able to get a non-flashing login prompt by pressing <alt> <sysrq> and the first few letters of reisub, but then I would usually get a message that the nvidia module could not be loaded and come back to a login prompt.  Occasionally, using recovery mode, I was able to get to the selection screen offering options including normal boot, but was never able to make a selection because the terminal would overwrite the screen with other text, which, as I recall, said something about being unable to mount filesystem.

Apparently unpredictably, I got to the gdm login screen a few times, but after entering my password, always ended up with an illegible screen of static.

I was unable to connect to the internet because all interfaces were reported as unconfigured.

Following suggestion on another forum, I downloaded the nvidia driver from the nvidia website (using a live cd), but when running it got an error message which, as I remember, said something about my kernel headers not matching what the module required, and asking if I wanted other kernel headers to be downloaded.  I couldn't download anything because of no internet connection.  It then asked if I wanted to go ahead with my existing headers, but although I answered affirmatively, it gave another error message and would not go further.

I gave up before trying the suggestion which I later saw on another thread about editing xorg.conf to change the driver to vesa.

I then tried a fresh install using the 64-bit install cd.  (I figure if I were going to reinstall I might as well go with 64-bit).  I got hit with the problem others have reported when it told me to insert the karmic cd (which of course was already inserted).  I got beyond that problem but then got hit with another problem, which I've now forgotten, but which I was unable to solve.  Why is there no live cd?

Finally gave up and installed the current 64-bit Crunchbang 9.04.01.  Figure they'll go to 9.10 when they've tested it sufficiently themselves.

### Post by eg0n on 2009-11-01

1)I couldn't use my second installed language

Everything else works fine (yet...), hope the same for you.

### Post by cboteros on 2009-11-01
Hello everyone, 

This is my experiences with Upgrading to Karmic Koala (from Jaunty Jackalope) on a Macbook 2,1

The upgrade process went "smoothly", this means that during the upgrade there were no error messages.

I then restarted and was very impress with the new look (I like it!) but got dispointed to see that the 3D acceleration (and with that, all the compiz visualizations) were gone...

That, and that the touchpad lost its behaviour of scrolling with 2 fingers (mac way) which I just had to re-adjust in the system preferences and that's it.

Besides that, eveything seemed to be working just fine, wireless, sound, etc.

I'm still having problems with the 3D acceleration on my macbook 2,1 with Karmic Koala, so if someone could lead me on where to find the solution to this, I'll be very greatful :-D

### Post by pgodfrey on 2009-11-01
I just mentioned in another thread regarding some issues that I had yesterday after the upgrade.

Upgrading from 9.04 to 9.10 on my Lenovo X61 tablet caused all sorts of problems and I wasn't about to sift through zillions of pages trying to find a fix.

I downloaded the ISO and started from scratch.  After installation there were a few things left to do such as setting up screen rotation abilities and configuring the wacom drivers.

After all of that was done I'm left with a machine that I can use again.  I'd like to thank everyone who has contributed either by writing the software or providing solutions to common (and not so common) problems on the forums.

The only thing I have left to resolve is getting Evolution 2.28.1 to remember my password.  It has been an on going issue in previous distros and there doesn't seem to be a fix as of yet.  It works sometimes, other times it just won't accept the password I have.


Overall, I'm happy with this release as I have been with previous releases of Ubuntu.


Phil

### Post by jcobban on 2009-11-01
> **frodon said:**
> The purpose of this thread is to share your experience installing/upgrading Karmic Koala.

Did it work flawlessly ? 
Did you get problems ? 
Did you manage to solve them ? 


The first time I tried the upgrade it hung 411 files in.  I assumed that the server was just overloaded, so before going to bed I restarted the upgrade.  It correctly detected that the 411 files had been downloaded and ran to completion.

I am not fond of the appearance of the initial splash screen: the ubuntu logo in white on a black background.  Quite unattractive as a default.

There is a small deviation from the advertised features.  Koala includes Oo 3.1, not the advertised 3.0.  Not an issue for me as I was already running 3.1 on my Windoze Vista system and prefer it to 3.0.

I am really unhappy with Firefox 3.5.  I tried it a couple of months ago and had no end of problems, which do not seem to be resolved yet although I reported them through Launchpad.

[LIST=1]
[*]When I authorized 3.5 to import my settings from 3.0 the entire system hung.  I had to manually power the system off and reboot to continue.  The settings were correctly imported and the hang has not recurred.
[*]3.5 fails to handle some very popular web sites that 3.0 has no difficulty with.  For me access to familysearch.org is a sine qua non.  I now have to revert to 3.0.
[/LIST]

### Post by chuckp on 2009-11-01
I upgraded from 9.04 (amd64) to 9.10 last night, using the upgrade option.

I had two errors during software install, both were with modules in OpenOffice.  One was emailmerge, the other I don't recall right now.

When the Installation portion of the upgrade finished (or at least I think it did) the Upgrade installer crashed and all windows closed.  I was left with my normal 9.04 desktop.

Cleanup and restart did NOT run.  When I manually restarted, it came up with a monochrome Ubuntu icon and after a moment restarted again.  The second time the monochrome Ubuntu icon came up again, and then the 'select user' prompt came up.  My password was accepted and Gnome came up, with an error.  The Gnome-FastUserChange module didn't load.

The major problem, though, is that the applications pulldown menu icon has vanished.  Which means, for me, that I have no means of accessing any of the administration functions.  I can't even open a terminal.  The individual application icons that I had placed, specifically Firefox and Thunderbird, do remain.

What are my options here?  Is there a way to get the applications menu to return by logging in on another console?

Should I do a complete re-install, perhaps from a live cd?  That's how I installed 9.04 and that worked flawlessly, if slowly.  

I have no way at this time to backup my home directory other than burning cd's, which I'm seriously considering.

My system is an HP DV7 laptop, purchased in May.

One final comment and question.  If there is _*any*_ way to get the original Debian applications menu, instead of the quite limited Ubuntu version? I really do prefer having that direct link to every application, rather than the limited set.

### Post by rbsteinbach on 2009-11-01
> **rbsteinbach said:**
> I had two machines with Ubuntu on them. 

 The IBM TP-G41 upgraded flawlessly over existing 9.04 running from an NTFS partition on the hard drive.  In fact, I had been unable to get the wireless adapter on the TP to work under 9.04 but it works under 9.10 with no input needed by me.  Great! :p

My other PC has been a disaster. :(  I had lost the original 160GB PATA drive a few weeks ago (which had 9.04 on it and working fine), and put in a new drive imaged from the old one, but only had used WinXP since.  When I tried to boot to 9.04 for the upgrade it failed to load 9.04.  No big deal, so I decided to repartition and set up a small (10GB) dedicated Linux partition after the last Win partition.  I repeatedly got "invalid parameter" errors from trying to install over NTFS, even trying 9GB and 8GB Linux sizes.  I then booted from the CD (ISO checksum is okay), and tried to make it a real Linux partition.  Seemed to work, but no entry in Boot.Ini and no GRUB for startup.  Tried that a few times and it never worked.  Tried making the desired partition larger, but still no good.  Finally I deleted the "new Linux partition" and merged it back into the last NTFS partition on the drive so that I could let Ubuntu take what it wanted out of that for Linux.  Working from the CD again to install from Windows, it STILL failed with "invalid parameters".  SO, I tried just invoking WUBI.EXE with no CD, but in the same directory where the ISO resides, and it seemed to install just fine.  Rebooted to "complete the installation", and Ubuntu came up at the end as expected.  Then I shut down and upon attempting to reboot into Ubuntu, it hangs at "Try hd(0,0): FAT16.  Hacking the wubilder.mbr it looks like it can't recognize the partition being used, even though its fine in WinXP...

I have uninstalled and attempted to reinstall Ubuntu 9.10 over and over and no matter what this PC just can't get it to work.  There is really nothing special about this custom built PC, and 9.04 installed from the web originally just fine.  Now I can't install 9.10 nor reinstall 9.04 (same "invalid parameter" error) from the CD, so I'm totally stuck.

Bummed out to the max! :(  

SUMMARY:  One laptop upgraded flawlessly, one PC now totally hosed for Ubuntu (but still okay for windows).

To follow up my earlier experiences -- I wiped the non-functional 9.10 off the NTFS partition and then downloaded ISO and installed 8.04 LTS with little problems (so its not my hardware  or partition table as the problem).  So I tried to upgrade from the 9.10 CD used earlier and it reported 127 updated packages.  Cool, right?  But all that did was update 8.04 (whacking my video settings), and NOT upgrade to 9.10 as I had expected.  So, I tried installing 9.10 over 8.04, which it wouldn't do unless I uninstalled 8.04 first.  I discovered wubildr and wubildr.mbr files on my C: (2GB-Fat16) boot partition and also on the F: (25-GB-NTFS) partition from 8.04, but that 9.10 only put them on the C: boot partition.  So I tried to mix and match different combinations of those two files in both locations after attempting to install 9.10 one more time using the same J: (20GB-NTFS) partition and it still failed on reboot, as it had earlier with the "Try hd(0,0): FAT16" message.  The wubildr.mbr files are different, and the wubildr files are even different sizes between 8.04 and 9.10.  I really believe this is the area of the problem...  So -- still stuck and no fix in sight...

### Post by plain_jim on 2009-11-01
> **wallshuff said:**
> I wish I could go back to 9.04.

Ian

That's exactly what I did.

I downloaded both the 386 ISO and the netbook ISO.  Copied the netbook ISO to a USB drive using the administrative startup disk creator tool - it got stuck halfway thru the install, leaving my GRUB toasted; I couldn't even boot into Windows (on my Acer 150).  Made ANOTHER USB install/boot drive, and that one got stuck, too.  I had to kludge together a Master Boot Record-fixing tool onto a bogus XP recovery disk.  Finally, I burned the IOS to CD and stuck that in a USB CD drive, and I was able to install on the netbook.  

Well, that seemed to go OK, so maybe the deal is to install from a CD, right?  So I burn the 386 ISO to CD, and start to install that on my home-built PC.  This time, it was the CD that got stuck halfway through. I broke out a mostly-crippled old Windows laptop (no wi-fi, but the ethernet still works) and burned another CD, and that one worked.  So now I have Karmic on both computers.  I always install a gajillion things, so I started that process.

One of the things I use is jpilot with my Palm Z22 (dagnabbit, I don't WANT a networked device, I just want my calendar and my contacts, and I don't want to have to pay for five copies of Outlook for my home and work computers!).  I've posted elsewhere about Karmic's failure with that; nine out of 10 times I connect the Palm to either Karmic computer with the USB cable, the Palm locks up - sometimes with the most attractive checkerboard/houndstooth design.

The other thing is my HP Laserjet 1018 printer.  Now, admittedly, this printer is dodgy with any Linux install... but I couldn't get Evince to drive the printer at all for some of my documents, using either the hpijs or foomatic drivers (although another pdf viewer finally worked reasonably reliably).

I decided to go back to Jaunty; I still had the CD.  So I copied everything to a 2nd hard drive (now formatted in ext4), reinstalled Jaunty from the CD from six months ago, copied everything from the 2nd hard drive to a folder in my home directory, reformatted the second hard drive to ext3, and I've just spent the last couple of hours reinstalling, re-logging-in so I get my browser cookies set up, getting the eleventy-one Firefox extensions I use, tweaking Thunderbird so it picks up my old settings...  and now I'm back to a working Ubuntu box.

### Post by yeats on 2009-11-01
I've upgraded two stations to Karmic, and both are successes.  One was an upgrade from a fairly fresh image of Jaunty and I did a fresh install on my netbook.  My Dell Vostro A90 (the business-class Inspiron Mini 9) needed an explicit install of bcmwl-kernel-source to get wireless working, and an addition of the line

options snd-hda-intel model=dell

To:

/etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base.conf

got my sound working (this was true for the netbook in Jaunty and Intrepid as well).  I'm quite pleased all told.

---

### Post by walthehippie on 2009-11-01
Upgrade on my old crappy Pentium 4 with Xubuntu.
Let it run all night on my half speed Comcast.
I had major sound problems so I whacked PulseAudio.
```
sudo apt-get purge pulseaudio
```
The sound worked fine after that.
Had some problems logging in on user accounts. Reinstalled xfce and it seems to be fine now.
Not too bad.

---

### Post by czljxt on 2009-11-01
The installation went without a glitch. Funny how quickly one starts taking it for granted. 

But eventually I decided to go back to 9.04 for ver non-geeky reasons:

The new boot process shows a black screen for quite a while. It made me almost think something was wrong.  I did not like the splash image it showed eventually.

The login window looks plain.

Worst of all I was not able to figure out how to change all that. I am guessing that it has something to do with a usplash->xsplash transition and that there were no boot splash images etc to choose from. 

Maybe it could wait.

---

### Post by TheReverendFlea on 2009-11-01
The main problems I'm having are with the volume control and the side panels.

Volume control - What happened to the mixer?  First the volume control is in the notification area.  Second it increases the volume from 0-15%, stays the same between 15% and 65%, then increases from 65% to full volume.  The old volume control from 9.04 and before allowed me to set the control on the mixer to where it was more gradual on my volume buttons on my laptop.

The (autohidden) side panels - They do not appear with Compiz Fusion and got so frustrated that I removed both side panels from my desktop machine.

---

### Post by whoop on 2009-11-01
My upgrade from 9.04 to 9.10 had two hiccups.
I tried to figure out what caused them, and figured out it was due to the fact I had installed grub2 when still using Jaunty. I devised workarounds for both hiccups and posted bug reports for both issues (including the workarounds off course):
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/apt/+bug/445653](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/apt/+bug/445653)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/update-manager/+bug/440633](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/update-manager/+bug/440633)

---

### Post by samsamros on 2009-11-01
everything works perfectly smooth it even improved a lot of stuff HOWEVER the bootup seems MUCH longer than before in 9.04.... it's not looking pretty

---

### Post by czljxt on 2009-11-01
Yes, I also find the new audio control panel unclear.  The "old" volume control(9.04-) was better.

---

### Post by godspiral on 2009-11-01
haven't installed yet, but live cd doesn't seem as good as 8.041

it boots fine with network, sound and good resolution, and usb drive detects. but:

cd churns a lot more than 8.04 did
slower boot
adobe flash doesn't install with permission errors (8.04 did)
printer not recognized (8.04 did)
hardware test doesn't detect screen resolution


I'm unsure which version to install because of this.

---

### Post by CallsignBaron on 2009-11-01
Updated my Jaunty install in VBox on my iMac. Had a few problems getting the update going but changed my source and all went well. I think the main repo was just overloaded because I upgraded just hours after the official release. System runs great! Karmic is well polished, I'm loving it! Thinking of ditching OSX. :D

---

### Post by CCC999 on 2009-11-01
> **CCC999 said:**
> Well, the live CD works. Did the upgrade from 9.04 using bitorrent file burned to DVD. Have been upgrading from Hardy with no issues, but this time I can not boot. See 'Spreadahead main process (2673) terminated with status 1 Mountall:/proc: unable to mount: Device or resource busy' 

I've seen a few other posts with the same issue. For me Karmic was not ready. I have a simple box that dual boots with Vista. Will keep searching for an answer (I prefer the upgrade to a new install).
Update - found fix for my boot issue here - [http://apcmag.com/how_to_dualboot_vista_with_linux_vista_installed_first.htm?page=4](http://apcmag.com/how_to_dualboot_vista_with_linux_vista_installed_first.htm?page=4)
Seems upgrade does not update the configuration file (menu.lst) used by easyBCD.

### Post by TheForumTroll on 2009-11-01
Like many others I did not have any sound after upgrading. This was caused by me saying No to update the menu.lst and then running the old Jaunty kernel. Except for that both the upgrade and the fresh install I have done worked flawlessly :D

Only thing I do not like about 9.10 is the new Grub. Again like many others I have a GRUB menu that takes from ½ to 2 minutes to load! That is unacceptable in a Final release when it was know beforehand (as far as I can tell it is in debian too).

I think there is a problem with upgrading though as many seems to have problems and others tell users to avoid upgrading and go the format-install way instead. One of the reasons I changed to Linux was to get away from a OS that couldn't even be upgraded properly. I don't know if Ubuntu is at fault or the users who tell others not to upgrade is wrong though.

### Post by biglarryk56 on 2009-11-01
> **biglarryk56 said:**
> I did the upgrade on my HP dv9005 laptop...upgrade process went smoothly, but I do have four issues that I haven't solved yet:

[1] Whenever I adjust the master volume control or playback any audio or video, the internal speakers pop. This is mostly noticeable in my gxine setup. I haven't found a workaround in alsa yet. I did not have this problem in Jaunty **UPDATE: I found the bug fix for this **[alsa-driver Bug #456022] [B]and I was able to get rid of the pop:
[/B]Comment out the options snd-hda-intel line in
/etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base.conf, and either reload the sound drivers or
reboot..

 
[2] I have a mixed-mode home network [my HP laptop, a Dell 2400 Windows machine], connected through a Linksys WRT54G wireless router. Samba still won't display UPnP devices [also have a Linksys NAS200 as a media hub, and I can't see it either, although I can connect and mount its Windows shares manually]. This has been a recurring problem for me even when I did a clean install with 9.04 32-bit. **UPDATE: Apparently, the problem was the WPA2 encryption with my Linsys WRT54G router. I switched back to WEP** [64-bit hex] **and that solved the problem.**

[3] Software Sources crashes or fails to finish reloading depositories. [B]UPDATE: Itraced this back to the slow response generated by an improper install of the nVidia 185 X driver. After I corrected that, sources updated without any trouble.
[/B] 
 [4] Recurring issues with the builtin webcam...lsusb yields the following:

Bus 001 Device 005: ID 1058:0704 Western Digital Technologies, Inc. **[my WD 500GB Passport drive]**
Bus 001 Device 004: ID 05ca:1870 Ricoh Co., Ltd Webcam 1000 **[the webcam]**
Bus 001 Device 003: ID 05ac:1262 Apple, Inc. iPod Nano 3.Gen 
Bus 001 Device 001: ID 1d6b:0002 Linux Foundation 2.0 root hub
Bus 002 Device 002: ID 1131:1004 Integrated System Solution Corp. Bluetooth Device **[USB Bluetooth dongle]**
Bus 002 Device 001: ID 1d6b:0001 Linux Foundation 1.1 root hub

Searching the forums hasn' thelped me either...I like to use Skype in Windows to talk to my nephew in Switzerland, but since I have the webcam problems, I haven't installed the Linux version yet.

**These same things happened when I switched from Jaunty 32-bit to a clean AMD64 Karmic install, but I was able to solve the problems as I outlined above. I also had a lot of problems with my nVidia GeForce Go 6150 X drivers [185 series], but a tweak of xorg.conf took care of that.**

All in all, I think the clean install of Karmic AMD64 went as well as I could expect...at least I didn't have to go through the hassles of upgrading from WinXP MCE to Win7 [plus, I saved the $130 cost of Win7 Home Premium!]

HP Pavilion dv9005us
2GB RAM, 80GB Fujitsu SATA HDD, nVidia GeForce Go 6159 (rev. a2), Broadcom BCM4311 WLAN

### Post by yester64 on 2009-11-01
i think i will downgrade again, since i can't adjust my nvidia drivers. Plus the also drivers are not working really either.
To sad, since it looks so nice.
I wait for the next update. Maybe then.

I was digging a little more. 
It occured to me that there is no Services under Admin anymore. In 9.04 you were able to change certain privileges like can install drivers etc..
Now there is nothing. Also if i go in Software Center i can not, i like to stress this point, i can not install any adminstrative tools. The system simply does not let me do that.
I wonder if thats a punishment or something else. I installed the system like everyone else and i cant change things like user rights.
I tried to get it with the synaptic package manager, but instead of having a full list, i only have user permissions but not detailed for certain tasks.
If i may humbly ask, why was that changed? Or did i press a wrong button during the installation?:(
So far no one can give me an answer, which is bad. But if can not solve this soon, i will switch back since this is just silly.
With this i like to take my comment of the web.

Thanks;)

### Post by ermeyers on 2009-11-01
First problem encountered was a complaint about another program having the lock during the Update Manager upgrade, so the update didn't complete.  I opened a terminal after rebooting, and I did an 'apt-get update' and 'apt-get dist-upgrade' to finish the upgrading.  Second problem is the graphical boot screen (with the progress bar) just cycles continously, and I eventually can get a single graphical screen on Ctrl-Alt-F7 thru Ctrl-Alt-F9, with some button pressing attempts.  The third problem is that there are no text screens at Ctrl-Alt-F1-F6.  Then the fourth problem was with Network Manager and my two ethernet interfaces being swapped around.  That all I know of so far.

### Post by Rumpty on 2009-11-01
Did a clean install, using the Alternate i386 iso, downloaded via torrent. On a home assembled desktop computer (Gigabyte board, AMD CPU, NVidia graphics)

All went well, except I couldn't install the grub bootloader to a floppy disc - error message "cannot find a GRUB drive for /dev/fd0", so didn't install the bootloader at all. Presently booting all the OSystems using Jaunty's menu.lst in another partition. Will stay with that for now. Had to manually change the UUID in menu.lst for the Karmic partition, as would be expected.

No great problems otherwise. No audio, that was just the volume turned right down.

### Post by Sky Aisling on 2009-11-01
[CENTER]Lost My Beautiful Screen Resolution
when I upgraded to 9.10
[/CENTER]

This is my first post to this forum.  I am a beginner to Linux.
My machine is a **Toshiba Satellite 2805-S401** running **XUbuntu**.
Screen1 display is set at 1024 x 768. 
Yesterday, I upgraded from Jaunty Jackalope 9.04 to Karmic Koala 9.10.
The upgrade went fine, no issues.
However, with the new system, I have lost my beautiful screen resolution.  The images on screensaver, wallpaper and thumbnails in the picture folder are degraded.  The exception to this is when I use 'Image Viewer' to view pictures.   Image Viewer gives me clear crisp pictures.

The Karmic Koala system seems to work faster than Jaunty Jackelope but the imaging is so poor, that I'd rather have my beautiful pictures back than the speed.  

How to I get Jaunty Jackelope 9.04 back onto my computer?  Or is there a fix to the poor screen resolution in Karmic Koala 9.10?

Thank you for your consideration of my questions,

Sky Aisling

### Post by dazlari on 2009-11-02
I followed the online the Upgrade path from 9.04 to 9.10 (Mythbuntu). This was completed successfully inside of two hours. Boot time is approximately the same if a little quicker. The login interface is much improved. ;)

Only one gripe. After a reboot, while I still have network connections (browsing is fine), the System Update tool (and Synaptic) both fail to find their online update sites. Not sure if this is because of traffic (tried several times over 12 hours), or because of a hole in the install. I suspect the latter. The Update tool believes I need to perform a "Partial Upgrade", however, without the connection to the repos, it no like it. 

It turns out that my "Download from server" (see System | Software Sources) was set to the Australian Server (me being an Aussie), so I pointed it at the Main Server and all went well. No reboot required. Just Brilliant!

Perhaps there's a delay in getting the Aussie server fully mirrored (as you'd expect).


Daz.

---

### Post by rbsteinbach on 2009-11-02
> **rbsteinbach said:**
> To follow up my earlier experiences -- I wiped the non-functional 9.10 off the NTFS partition and then downloaded ISO and installed 8.04 LTS with little problems (so its not my hardware  or partition table as the problem).  So I tried to upgrade from the 9.10 CD used earlier and it reported 127 updated packages.  Cool, right?  But all that did was update 8.04 (whacking my video settings), and NOT upgrade to 9.10 as I had expected.  So, I tried installing 9.10 over 8.04, which it wouldn't do unless I uninstalled 8.04 first.  I discovered wubildr and wubildr.mbr files on my C: (2GB-Fat16) boot partition and also on the F: (25-GB-NTFS) partition from 8.04, but that 9.10 only put them on the C: boot partition.  So I tried to mix and match different combinations of those two files in both locations after attempting to install 9.10 one more time using the same J: (20GB-NTFS) partition and it still failed on reboot, as it had earlier with the "Try hd(0,0): FAT16" message.  The wubildr.mbr files are different, and the wubildr files are even different sizes between 8.04 and 9.10.  I really believe this is the area of the problem...  So -- still stuck and no fix in sight...

Happy to report that I finally determined what at least one problem was:  During yet another attempt, to install 9.04 (which had previously been working), upon reboot from initial setup start, the screen cleared to black with a single blinking "dash" symbol in the upper left corner.  I thought perhaps it was just working thru something in the background, but it never got further.  I did a power-off shutdown (all that I could do), and upon reboot again the blinking dash was back...  But I noticed that I was getting an occasional flash of the hard drive light (primary IDE), simultaneous with a flash on the legacy IDE Zip drive light (secondary IDE on the same cable).  Just for yucks I put a blank Zip disk in the drive, and suddenly the installation re-commenced as it should have.  Apparently Ubuntu 9.x thought the Zip drive was another hard drive and was waiting for a response.  That could have also been the problem with the earlier 9.10 install attempt ("Try...").  I did NOT go back to that again, as 9.04 completed installation just fine. I was then able to use the Update Manager to do the rest of the upgrade to 9.10. AND, I don't need the blank Zip disk in the drive for normal operations. SUCCESS!  :p

Now the only minor problem seems to be that when I attempt to Shutdown or Restart, the 9.10 system doesn't do it, and I have to manually hard power off.  I think I can fix that later, but the main thing is that I now have two systems that upgraded okay.  One flawlessly, one with extreme difficulty.

Best of luck to others.

---

### Post by ptm on 2009-11-02
Not ready for Primt Time. 

Compaq EVO N610c laptop.  I jumped in with both feet and did the upgrade using Update Manager, and I had over 1,400 packages to update. When it was ready to remove obsolete packages I did.  When I looked up it was ready to restart. When it came back up, my wallpaper was there, Desktop Effects were enabled - in short - painless.

The only minor issue noted was that I had to install some Language components. I just followed the prompts and the computer locked. I had to do a power off reset to get back my keyboard.  Now the hard drive is toast.  I am reformatting the HD and installing from a rev 8 iso disk.:guitar:

### Post by rockney on 2009-11-02
I've upgraded several Ubuntu desktops and servers over the last few years. Most went very well - even a sequence of 3-4 upgrade hops to new distro versions.

However - 9.10 Karmic has been a disaster.

Upgraded a newly-built 9.04 server + DTC over this weekend.

The upgrade --

 1) is utterly glacial ... 14 minutes to boot to XWindows, 9 minutes to login and see the desktop, 4 minutes 12 seconds to open Firefox.

 2) trashed our runlevel boot arrangement with this thing called "Upstart" that appears to do it ONE way.

 3) trashed a DTC setup with 4 web sites, email server, etc. where MySQL and other services won't even pretend to start.

 4) now generates hardware errors for devices that don't exist.

 5) and (my boss's pet peeve) the Gnome login dialog lists all accounts on the box with no sign of being able to disable that.

It seems someone is trying to make packaging and approach decisions for everyone while removing features and options ... sounds like "The Micro$oft Way".  Are all Linux distros going this way?

But - it's my fault. After such good luck with previous upgrades I didn't go read the release notes, known issues, or other documents. Maybe these changes and breakages are discussed somewhere and it wouldn't have come as such a nasty surprise.

---

### Post by luctor on 2009-11-02
Ubuntu 9.10 amd64 on Dell Inspiron 531

I did install the release candidate. Only some minor software problems so far, hardware is working fine
[LIST] The rt-kernel wouldn't boot properly when using Nvidia driver. Latest updates solved that for me and is working great.
[/LIST]
[LIST]
 Couldn't create mp4 due to (IMO) borked ffmpeg (no aac encoder). Had to compile from source.
[/LIST]
[LIST]
Kino still gives me troubles exporting to mp4,but I'm ok with exporting to dv and then use Handbrake to create mp4
[/LIST]

Had to compile Handbrake from source, but that's not Ubuntu's fault cause it's not in the repos :-) 
... ah just checked [http://handbrake.fr/snapshot.php](http://handbrake.fr/snapshot.php), there are 9.10 debs now

### Post by destroyedlolo on 2009-11-02
For me, it was a nightmare (but not only due to Ubuntu).

I've tried to install 2 PC.

_**Netboot problem [ubuntu related]:**_
I've tried to install one by netbooting.
No problem w/ PXE, no problem to start the installation but I wasn't able to complete Aptitude stage : it found a missing dependancy on x11-common (even if checked to be installed), and I wasn't able to manually install it as Aptitude said a lock file already exists. 
So, I give it up.

=> Perhasp someone as to test again  the net install ;)

[U][B]Unetbootin [bad luck related]:
[/B][/U]On the other PC, I have m$win-2000 installed and my wife wanted to keep it for kids' games.
So I tried a net installation using **Unetbootin**.
Everything started find ... slowly (as ubuntu servers were busy) but it crashed due to R/W error on the disk.
Rebooting ... m$win restarted once but only once because when I tried to restart the computer the day after, it said the disk is dead :cry:

=> I fact I was able to resurrect it (but M$-win is gone, RIP) : 1 booted from a CD, I was able to got access to this disk and this partition but very very slowly.
I trying to install Ubuntu on it, and the partitionning was very very slow (I guess because it's checking disk health and relocating bad sectors).
But the installation succeed and now I don't encounter problems anymore.

_**CD install [bad luck again]:**_
I took the .iso from the Torrent (very very fast way to got it :D:D).
But the first burn attempts was ... unsuccessful ](*,)

Second was fine and I have now Ubuntu 9.10 installed on my both environment =D>

So, but the netboot issue, my ^$&@^# was due to ... bad luck !

Bye

Laurent

### Post by fantan on 2009-11-02
Hi for All!

I did a clean install of Karmic (9.10) on my computer after the new version came out. After clean installation in the new system I got a lot of problems accoring sound.
My configuration is as follows:
- motherboard: Asus
- processor: Intel(R) Pentium(R) 4 CPU 3.20GHz, 3199.901 MHz
- memory: 1 GB/666 Mhz
- HDD-s: SAMSUNG HD161HJ 140GB
	 Maxtor 6L080LO 80 GB
- Graphic video card: GeForce 7300 GS
- Sound card: CMI8738

On my computer I have four partitions. 
On 1. of them installed Linux Mint 7 Gloria (9.04) Hungarian
On 2. of them installed Linux Mint 7 Gloria (9.04) Russian
On 3. of them installed Kiwi Linux 9.04 with LXDE desktop
All three old systems used the same hardware and are working wery well, without any problem, especially according to sound and video possibilities.

I made a clean install of the new version (9.10 Karmic) on the 4. partition. Of course before installation I checked the md5sum of downloaded ISO file (ubuntu-9.10-desktop-i386.iso), checked the burned CD two times. The burned CD is without any defects and errors.

The istallation and the boot process went without problems.
The problems began when I tried to play an mp3 file with Rhytmbox. Rhytmbox downloaded the needed codecs and other packages, after that the playback has a lot of problems. 
The playback starts well, but abruptly the sound gets stumble in one's play, cracks, and at the same time the processor uses 100%  resources. Of course after some time the sound stops or the system totally freezes.
This situation happens again independently which program runs: audacious, qmmp, moc, exaile, amarok, mplayer, vlc, mpg123, mpg321, music123, etc. 
I tried a lot of setup variations in multimedia system setup: Pulseaudio, Alsa, OSS, the result is the same. I uninstalled the pulseaudio totally, but theese problems are abiding.

I can't understand, why theese problems aren't present in my other systems on the same hardware.
I don't know at all, how to resolve theese problems, because of that I need all the help from you.
Please help me slve theese problems.

Thanks in advance!

Fantan

### Post by astalavista2000 on 2009-11-02

### Post by Xanthomryr on 2009-11-02
[x] Upgrade - worked but had few things to fix, nothing serious though

We did an upgrade on one of our Jaunty 64bit servers and the only problem we encountered, was that proftpd was removed and needed to be reinstalled.
But that was no surprise for us as the proftpd package changed to pftpd-basic some time ago in Debian. So no harm done. :D

We will now monitor this server for a while and wenn no problems occur we upgrade our other Jaunties as well.

Befor we upgraded to above mentioned server, we did an upgrade on a not so critical server and that showed no problems too.

/edit: The servers run apache2, mysql, postfix and now vsftpd.

### Post by davidU on 2009-11-02
Since the documentation for 9.04 isn't fully worked out yet, and writing as a Linux newbie of several years, I don't see much point attempting to upgrade unless you have plenty of time and the patience to spend lots more time working out why stuff doesn't work straight from the tin.

If you just want to get on and do some work with your PC, this upgrade is not for you.
Even if the community rate it as stable, I have to disagree strongly with that rating.
Just read the posts in this section !

I don't understand why 9.10 should be released to the general public and rated as a stable release when there are still so many problems with the previous release 9.04.

As a newbie, am I supposed to assume that those bugs in 9.04 are now not trendy and will not get attended to !

If so, this is getting to be a bit like Microsoft, releasing Windows 7 (with all the bugs) just 'cos Windows Vista is too broken to run, which was released only 'cos Windows XP was too hard to secure and the huge number of monthly updates was giving Microsoft a bad name (HA!).

Sorry if I sound like I am griping.  I am not intending to slag off anyone, but please do not advertise or even imply that this is gonna be a straight forward job.

Many people moving away from Microsoft Windows just want to get on with some work, not waste a day or two trying to work out why the "recommended" Seamonkey security upgrade - just doesn't !  When under Windows it just does ! 

If you are looking for the same kind of user base that Windows has, Ubuntu needs to realise that Windows users are not developers first.

Regards

DavidU
;)

---

### Post by zasf on 2009-11-02
After 3-4 dist-upgrades (since Edgy I guess), I decided to do a clean install. Now that everything works I'm really happy with karmic, but installing was a mess mainly due to my old cd reader and inexperience.

I wanted to create a raid array in order to keep my data safe, so I backup up my data in a usb drive and delete all partitions after burning the desktop cd. Unfortunately the cd was burnt at high speed and this resulted in cd problems when installing. After a while I also realized that in order to install on a raid system I needed the alternate cd.. it is not easy to burn a cd when you're running on the livecd :) So I needed another comp to do the job. After booting with the alternate I found that the cd was broken or something, since it didn't install the whole system. So I installed grub and I was able to boot the computer, update sources.list and install ubuntu-desktop from the internet. 

Now I have karmic up and running. Two things I noticed: the boot is really quick and skype works great (both audio with pulseaudio and webcam)!

Matteo

### Post by wbarido on 2009-11-02
I tried twice to upgrade to 9.10 from 9.04.
Both times the install failed to fully download.
I downloaded the iso and installed Karmic using Wubi.
Sound did not work. Turns out it was muted by default.
I had no video. Downloading Adobe flashplayer didn't work either.
Finally, I used the Terminal command:
"sudo apt-get install flashplayer-plugin"
Now, everything works. It's just that I have two Ubuntu installations on my hard drive.
I've been with Ubuntu since Dapper and I still had problems making my installation work. 
We need to do better if we want to grow the Linux base.
Newbies are terrified of the Terminal.

### Post by Sunflower1970 on 2009-11-02
2nd fresh install has been done on my laptop. Thinkpad R40 with Radeon 7500 M6 LY grqaphics card, installed ext4 for home and root partitions.

Overall, I'm happy with it. I will swear it's running cooler than Intrepid did, which is a huge plus. I'm having one odd graphics problem when running Compiz. Across the top of the screen I get these lines--kind of looks like a scrambled TV signal. It only happens on the desktop portion--not in any program, and when I rotate the cube it stays on the 4 desktops as well. When effects are off, it goes away. I'm still trying to figure out how to stop it, but it's not a showstopper. 

Everything is more responsive. I don't notice any difference in booting up--it feels the same--but programs open faster.

### Post by Roger Allott on 2009-11-02
Clean install of Xubuntu 9.10 onto my old Windows 2000 PC.

The first glitch found was at install page 4 or 5, where it reported that, according to it, there was no other O/S on the disk.

That was clearly wrong, and could have been a major issue for someone, but I took the plunge and accepted that I'm not so unhappy about losing W2000 after all these years.

Other than that, the install went fine, except that upon first boot up, my screen size (1024x768) wasn't autodetected so I had to suffer 800x600.

9.10 has done away with an xorg.conf file in /etc/X11/, so after a fair bit of long distance support from a linux-savvy friend, I managed to create one and manually amend it.

I think the decision of the Ubuntu Team to do away with a configuration file for X is asking for trouble. Users surely need a better interface to their X settings, but Ubuntu seems to be moving further and further away from that.

I haven't yet upgraded my main PC from 9.04 to 9.10. I'll wait a couple of weeks or so to do that.

---

### Post by Nintendo01 on 2009-11-02
I successfully upgraded to Karmic, but the madwifi drivers that I had going in Jaunty (using the Hardware Drivers applet to enable them as restriced drivers, not manually installed) weren't there after the upgrade. The ath5k driver that it used (after I un-blacklisted it) instead would take forever to connect to my network, and then it would be so slow as to be unusable. I tried a couple of different manual installs of madwifi and they would never work. I did re-blacklist ath5k and add ath_pci to /etc/modules. The drivers did load, but it could never connect to the network at all. I ended up having to go back to Jaunty to get it working again.

I'd like to know why the madwifi drivers aren't included in the install like in Jaunty and are actually removed during the upgrade if they are installed. 

For the record, I have an Atheros AR2413 card that works flawlessly with Jaunty's madwifi restricted drivers, but barely functions with ath5k in either Jaunty (before activating the restricted madwifi drivers) or Karmic, and doesn't work at all with manually installed madwifi in Karmic.

### Post by sloggerkhan on 2009-11-02
So I have a netbook acer aspire one. It's worked perfectly with 9.10 since beta. 
Love it. Went perfectly.

Desktop, on the other hand, was painful. I did a fresh install and then copied over a few important config files. Getting latest nvidia up and running with vdpau for smplayer was a pain. Had to mess around with some ppas and do the nvidia driver install to get a combination that worked.

Similarly, there's some sort of meaningless kernel error on boot that doesn't cause issues and appears every boot. Really long thread on it on launchpad.

I keep having audio issues. I am thinking of removing pulse audio to seeing if it's any improvement. With relative frequency random apps will lose audio out capability. Some programs, such as glx version of nexuiz (sdl works fine) are unusable with pulse audio.

Likewise, sometimes quitting nexuiz lock up the xsession and I have to do services gdm restart from console.

Empathy won't import my old pidgin contacts (it offers to, then doesn't), so I just installed pidgin. It seems to have more features than empathy anyhow.

Flash also seems to be performing worse. I might try the beta 64 bit flash from adobe if I get around to it.

Basically, 9.10 is great for my netbook, but it's got lots and lots of small irritating issues on my desktop.

I was planning to upgrade my server-router machine, but I will probably wait till the lts, especially after hearing about people having major problems with MDADM software raid with an upgrade.

### Post by bobnutfield on 2009-11-02
I upgraded (no fresh install) my netbook (AAO), my desktop and my main laptop.  All the upgrades went fine with a couple of hiccups on the main laptop because it is a triple boot with two other distros (Fedora and Slackware).  Both the desktop and the main laptop have been upgraded since Intrepid (8.10).  So far, I have noticed pretty much the same performance on all three as there was in Jaunty, except boot is considerably slower and shutdown is considerably faster.  Go figure.

Just like all of the previous releases. there are going to be people who expereince problems to some degree, but I expect that most if not all will be resolved in furture updates as they have always done.  The wireless signal is weaker on all three, but that is about it for me.  

So for me, it was pretty much a wash....no harm, but no improvement either.

### Post by JoseRijo on 2009-11-02

### Post by welshmike on 2009-11-02
I had a dual boot Windows XP Home and Ubuntu 9.04 system and did the upgrade to Ubuntu 9.10.
The upgrade went well until I was prompted about changing the?boot configuration?". I replied that I wanted to retain my existing one as I suspected that my menu.lst file would get changed and I would not subsequently be able to boot Windows XP. The prompt then repeated and I could only procede by accepting the modification to menu.lst so that 9.10 would be a subsequent boot choice. Eventually when 9.10 was up and running I looked at menu.lst. No sign of being able to boot Windows XP. Fortunately I had printed out the contents of menu.lst when I was running 8.04 and was able to add the lines to boot Windows XP. Maybe I had modified menu.lst on 8.04 so that 9.04 install would not modify it without deleting the Windows XP boot lines. Below is the last set of lines in my menu.lst. Did I bring the install glitch on myself?

## ## End Default Options ##

title		Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition
rootnoverify	(hd0,0)
savedefault
makeactive
chainloader	+1

title		Ubuntu 9.10, kernel 2.6.31-14-generic
uuid		d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.31-14-generic root=UUID=d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d ro quiet splash 
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.31-14-generic
quiet

title		Ubuntu 9.10, kernel 2.6.31-14-generic (recovery mode)
uuid		d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.31-14-generic root=UUID=d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d ro  single
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.31-14-generic

title		Ubuntu 9.10, kernel 2.6.28-15-generic
uuid		d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-15-generic root=UUID=d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d ro quiet splash 
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-15-generic
quiet

title		Ubuntu 9.10, kernel 2.6.28-15-generic (recovery mode)
uuid		d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-15-generic root=UUID=d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d ro  single
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-15-generic

title		Ubuntu 9.10, memtest86+
uuid		d0ed199c-f022-40fa-99d8-26096d45b19d
kernel		/boot/memtest86+.bin
quiet

### END DEBIAN AUTOMAGIC KERNELS LIST

# This is a divider, added to separate the menu items below from the Debian
# ones.

# This entry automatically added by the Debian installer for a non-linux OS
# on /dev/sda1

(end of menu.lst lines)

My future plan, if 9.10 does not intermittently freeze like 9.04 did, is to use the partition occupied by Windows XP for Ubuntu, install a Virtualizer and have Windows XP running as a guest of Ubuntu for the few rare Windows applications that I have not found the equivalent of on Ubuntu.

### Post by radam on 2009-11-02
I spent the weekend attempting to clean install Karmic on my system. I do have it installed but I haven't had a chance to even use it; I temporarily removed my newer Nvidia 9800 GTX graphics card and replaced it with my older 8600 GT card to avoid the black screen situation I run into during installs. I haven't been able to log in due to the X Server constantly crashing. The card switching trick worked with previous versions.

I believe in exhausting the resources available to me & I usually glean valuable help after browsing the Ubuntu Forums, but trying several tips this time hasn't helped. I've been using rescue mode to try everything from installing Envyng to update the Nvidia driver at the commandline, editing the X-Server configuration & changing BIOS settings to no effect. 

It's frustrating. It's like owning a new car without the keys to drive the blasted thing.

### Post by PippoFranco on 2009-11-02
I made a fresh install of 9.10 on a old Acer TravelMate 370, 500 Mbyte, 1.5 Ghz Pentium, 50GB disk. So far I have not experienced any problem. Suspend and hibernate work out of the box, sound is OK, network manager OK with cable, wireless and 3G from Vodafone (little problem I had also with 9.04: I must connect twice to get a proper setting of the DNS). SD card reader works, external monitor OK. Function keys work fine. After the installation of the restricted extra package I can ply music and DVD. Graphics work much better than 9.04 which was ugly.

Altogether for me 9.10 is a great improvement in comparison to previous versions (I use Ubuntu since 7.04).
