### Post by gej on 2009-11-02
In a word -- frustrating.  This being my fourth flavor of Ubu' I thought most of these bugs would have been worked out by now.  9.04 wasn't nearly as painful.

The "upgrade" as advertised did not work - period.  I suspect the US mirrors were overwhelmed which would explain the poor d/l performance I experienced (13 hours was initially indicated but it actually only took 20).  Tried at least 6 times to use this method, all failed miserably.  The "upgrade" route was chosen because I didn't want to hassle with backup/reload of data.  During all 6 false starts to upgrade the system froze at various stages, none making it past "installing packages."  My system froze to iceberg proportions with no easy way to reclaim control, recover or restore.  Attempts to cancel were met with more frustration as the system indicated it had reverted but then would not boot because GRUB2 froze.  A clean install took care of the problem but, of course, I now had to reload my backed up data.  

Post installation performance has noticeably degraded with choppy video, stuttering sound, an occasional hum or beep out of the speakers when attempting to adjust volume, folders and files which take an eternity to open, Opera 10.1 which causes some sort of Gnome crash, I could paint faster than pictures open in F-spot, the list goes on.  Windows XP, long since removed from this old box, had better performance than 9.10 Karmic.  No plans to return to Win but reverting to 9.04 Ubu' or another distro is a real possibility.  Regardless of path, the downgrade will be almost as painful as the upgrade because of the hundreds of updates required to get back to safe, secure operating mode.  Win at least offers SPs which one can d/l and save to disk.  Why Ubu' doesn't offer a similar set of consolidated patches?  I'm not sure.

Oh yes, and that "boots faster" mantra... Takes at least 2-3 times or longer to boot now than it did with 9.04.  GRUB2?  Ugh!

I almost feel as if I'm stepping back several generations with this "upgrade."  Karmic probably works fine on newer hardware but if you prefer older hardware don't bother.

Just my experience.  I'm still sold on Linux and Ubu' still runs on this box .. For now.

System:
Dell Dimension 8100 desktop
1.5Ghz Intel
1G RAM
128MB NVidia
320GB HD

---

### Post by krash1220 on 2009-11-02
> **smacfarl said:**
> 9.10 looks like it will be great when I can actually boot into it.

I have a Vista install that has the Windows Boot Loader. I downloaded EasyBCD in anticipation of having to keep the Windows Boot Loader the primary, having had previous ugly experiences with Windows 7 Beta, and Ubuntu 9.04. 

What the hell is the Partition Manager doing? Seems like every upgrade cycle that partition scanning gets slower and slower. Seriously, why should it take longer than 30 seconds to identify where my partitions are and how big they are? Literally it took 20 minutes. I have a 320gb drive with a single 60gb NTFS partition and a second 160gb drive that has a single 160gb NTFS partition. Partition tables are small. Grinding on my drive util the install program screen power management feature blanks the screen indicates there is a serious problem.

Eventually I get to create a new 30gb ext4 partition and a swap partition. My first Ubuntu was 7.04. It was a very fast install and it  instructed me which partitions to create. Rather than responding with error messages after the forward button is presed as 9.10 install seems to do.

Then we get to the copy cycle. Which looks great until we are 80% through and we have to download language packs for god knows what. This takes 40 minutes. Can I skip this? There is a skip button here, but I have no idea what the effects will be of skipping files during an OS install. Why am I downloading anything before the initial install is complete? I just burned an ISO yesterday. What could possibly have changed in 24 hrs that is so important that my base system install can't complete before these language packs are downloaded. I chose USA English why the hell am I downloading language packs?

Finally we are finished and we reboot. Of course Grub completely fails to replace the windows boot loader, so I log into vista and  EasyBCD a new menu option to go to the Ubuntu partion. I reboot select the new Ubuntu option. And I am in a grub command line prompt!!

WTF? The whole point of the graphical installation process is that I should never have to configure grub, let alone boot the system from the grub command line. Did anyone test this at all?

You know I really fell in love with 7.04 because it just worked. I liked 9.04 because it booted quickly. Since I have had to upgrade to Vista the Ubuntu experience has become a real pain in the ***. 

My thought was Shuttleworth has Billions of dollars, the guy can pay 3 or 4 guys to test the crap out of the most common scenarios, I mean it's not like they are doing kernel development here, this is just standard system install stuff. And how much would it cost to get someone to fix the partition manager, so that it doesn't take twice as long as the entire rest of the install? Has shuttleworth ever installed Ubuntu himself? Because I just can't see that he's going to sit there and wait while the little circle cranks for 20 minutes before there is any partition feedback. 

What was the point of upgrading to Grub2 if the 9.10 install isn't able to successfully create a boot menu with it. At one point I used the Live-CD to try and talk to some help people via gnome x-chat. The response I got was - "I am too busy to talk to you, google grub." Really? This is technical support? 

Forget Ubuntu One and the new Installer feature. I don't give a **** about that if I can't boot the damn OS. I am just getting more and more angry the longer I write about this. 

Somebody. Anybody. Help me get 9.10 to boot and then show me to a forum where I can talk to someone who can fix these problems so it never happens again. I will devote time to testing. Whatever it takes.

I am so with you on this one. I have spent four days trying to get Karmic going. Grub2 totally sucks. I have done numerous clean installs and Grub2 will not load. I get Grub Read Error, Reboot and Select proper boot device etc etc. I have tried reinstalling it with the help of all the documentation online from the livecd with no result. I tried to uninstall Grub2 and install grub legacy with no result. I have tried installing it without installing a bootloader and then trying to install grub2, no result, and the same with grub legacy. I tried to install grub to another partition with no result. I finally tried changing the bios from ahci to ide and karmic will install and grub2 installs fine but I have two sata drives and don't want to go back to ide because it's slower. I have finally given up and went back to Jaunty. I'm thinking about just doing an upgrade but I really don't like doing that. I have loved Ubuntu up until this release but this one sucks. If grub2 is suppose to be so great then why won't it install and run correctly on my sata drives. I certainly hope the next release of Ubuntu is better than this one. Oh and I forgot to mention how long it takes to install Karmic. I loved Jaunty because of the quick install but Karmic takes forever. I finally started skipping the dreaded apt config and the language install which takes 20 minutes and I didn't want anyway.

### Post by joewski on 2009-11-02
I have 17 machines running Jaunty. I have only upgraded 2 of them and both experienced problems with the upgrade process.

In Australia we are charged by the MB downloaded in advanced so we tend to be savy with our internet usage.

So what I wanted to do is use the unofficial bigpond repositories to download the bulk of the karmic files and then switch repositories backtop ubuntu-mains for the rest of the upgrade.
This process mostly worked but required experience to perform. Its a pitty ubuntu or linux in general doesn't have a manually configurable prefered repository setting, with if fails reverts to the other repositories. Its a common problem by the way I find that repository management is not as automatic as it could be.

Anyway I was able to install and get to the desktop however since I run mulitple desktops kde, gnome and xfce I ended up with the xfce login screen. This created a problem in that the gdmsetup program had changed from jaunty to kamic making it impossible to change to the ubuntu login screen. Seems to me ubuntu wants to keep its login branding.

Another problem has been the ubuntuone fiasco in which beta tested found themself with problems being unable to connect to ubuntuone. It turned out someone decided that the final release would be 1.02 while the beta was 1.1. This problem could easily be fixed if the final release was renamed 1.2.

Overall despite the problems with the startup process being a total mess the desktop is nicer overall once logged in. The white startup theme is frankly boring and I have switched back to the colour theme.

The final aspect which I am not happy about is the Ubuntu software centre which frankly slows the process of installing software right down. You could check multiple packages to install and simply hit apply. Now you have to click one at a time. 

If Ubuntu wanted to improve the add/remove application center they should add simply run button, and add a wiki link for the descriptions. I think the description files are still pretty lame.

### Post by eagleendtime on 2009-11-02
To answer the questions at the beginning of this thread:

Did it work flawlessly ? 

No, absolutely not.  Worse upgrade I've ever done with Ubuntu.

Did you get problems ? 

Yes gobs of them.

Did you manage to solve them ? 

Some but there are still major problems left.

if yes how ?

The ones solved:
First Wine worked fine under 9.04 with the upgrade forget anylinks in the like of Folio, and using search unreadable fonts.  Notepad had illegible fonts too.  Solution totally remove wine (including config files), deleted entire pseudo C drive.  Reinstalled everything, and they worked fine.

Compiz: of the three settings of compiz, I was using normal under 9.04 without any problems.  Forget using compiz period for 9.10, background goes black on first desktop, first program loads fine, then any others painfully slow, and won't display properly.  Solution: No special effects and everything works fine except gnome-system monitor. Ha

Gnome-system monitor will only display properly under Compiz and than only for a while.  Without compiz - System monitor display is totally unreadable.

VM Ware needed to update itselfs for new kernel, that went without a hitch (only thing wonderful).

Didn't like the new software center: it is good overall, but Add-Remove is better for multiple actions, Solution: reinstall Add-Remove from advice of another thread.

Login display is not good very limited choices.  I prefer to type in my user account for my laptop (just an extra security step - though a very small one) for desktop at home having login account displayed is no problem.  I do have a boot up password for the Laptop. 

Bootup from Grub to login seems a lot slower than 9.04 so much for a better Login display.

Conclusion I will not be upgrading any other machines with 9.10, not worth the headache, or lost time.

I need a solution to Compiz and Gnome-System monitor.  My laptop is Toshiba Satellite 1110, 512 mb, 1.8 Ghz.  Everything worked fine for 9.04 but NOT for 9.10

Googleearth blinks, and display acts incorrectly.  Worked just fine under 9.04.  Trying a reinstall after a complete removal, that helped somewhat, but still no solution.

Considering display problems it may be incompatibility problems with the ATI card, when I get the time I will do a clean install of 9.04 again, and ditch ever doing 9.10, unless a solution is found quickly.  Maybe it's time for a new system, other than ubuntu.

---

(.04 ran fine, but I want the faster boot time of 9.10 on my laptop, I might just re-install 9.10 and replace grub2 with grub. ### Post by neilbu on 2009-11-02
I've tried it on a few systems with nowhere near the problems reported, though it has been the roughest release yet.

My main issues were Gnome panel locking up for 30 seconds after login, which turned out to be the kernel no longer recognising the Sony flash card reader, causing USB device enumeration to stall. This also lead to the PC locking up on shutdown. I fixed it by removing the card reader, not a big deal since I don't use it anyway.

I also had popping audio which was caused by the sound hardware going to sleep after 10 seconds and Flash crashing, but Flash from the Ubuntu repos always crashes and I always fix it with a manual install.

I've got more detail on my issues on my blog:

[http://blog.burlock.org/component/content/article/44-ubuntu/124-review-of-karmic-koala-ubuntu-910]("http://blog.burlock.org/opinion/124-review-of-karmic-koala-ubuntu-910")

---

### Post by absolutepressure on 2009-11-02
Horrible!
I was running 9.04 as a dual boot, upgraded to 9.10, and now all of a sudden it says my hard drive "has many bad sectors," that it could crash, and I should replace the hdd. I booted up in windows, and it didn't think anything was wrong, so it must all be in my linux partitions. Well, ubuntu crashed today when I was on the irc trying to get help. Also, after the upgrade, if it goes into suspend, it won't come out. And, I still can't figure out the keyring password reset.

I'm pretty close to becoming a mac user. I hate windows, and now Ubuntu is giving me just as much, if not more grief.

I am very disappointed with the Koala.

Update:

Well, after looking at all the problems people are having with 9.10, I followed some sound advice and did a fresh install of 9.04 and will wait for a newer version that works, if I ever want to switch, that is. I'm back to 9.04, and it's smooth as ever! The fresh install to 90% of how I had it before the update took less time than trying to fix one of the 9.10 problems. Oh, and reformatting the hdd linux partitions got rid of the "bad sectors" deal. Those, I believe were definately caused by 9.10. My advice, unless it went perfectly smooth for you, don't upgrade. If you haven't upgraded yet, DON'T. Not until the problems have been fixed.

-I'm out, peace!

### Post by Miguel Angel Da Vila on 2009-11-02
The nVidia driver is very slow. If the Desktop efects are enabled it is impossible to play a game like Torcs.
It is required to disable the Sync to VBlank in NVIDIA X Server Settings to have a reasonable performance.
The glxgears originally returned a frame rate of 80 frames in 5 seconds, after disabling the  Sync to VBlank increases to 14,000-15000, and after disabling Compiz jumps to 23000-25000. Winth a pair of GeForce 9500 GT arranged in SLI

---

### Post by wijit on 2009-11-03
I've got different results on upgrading to Karmic on two boxes, Acer Travelmate 4720 and ECS Green 773. Acer was successfully upgraded with minor problems (detected by appearance of Apport which can collect the information but failed to send out), but ECS encountered application problem (found on Firefox, app-get and Synaptic). This reply was posted from the Acer box, which I believe having no further problems, to report problems on the ECS. At a cleaning-up stage, the pop-up asked me to remove or keep unnecessary packages and I chose removal. I did the same on both machines which have slightly different set of applications. After a reboot for completion of the upgrade, on the ECS, Firefox could not connect to any web sites. On the status bar it showed 'Connecting to ...' and a spinning dots but no others like 'Waiting for ...' or 'Transferring Data From ...' until time-out error hit. At first I thought this was a network problem as suggested by the error page. I then tried pinging, ftp (command line) and gftp and found no problem. I tried uninstall and install back Firefox but got no better result. Re-installation also gave no help. Note that while there appears elder version of Firefox shown on the list of Synaptic, they all point to the latest version. I actually want to try installing v3.1 which works fine on Jaunty but the Koala's repo gives me no chance. If we take the different between the two sets of network-related (internet) applications into consideration, there must be some packages that one set rely on while the other does not. I did try Firefox to connect to the web server and MySQL server on localhost. Firefox worked but at very low speed. This strengthens my assumption stated above. That is the end of my ability. I cannot go deeper than this. I hope that someone who read this can help. However, at the moment, I am downloading the alternate CD for another try. I forgot to say that two different things between the two boxes which are the CPU and the size of RAM. The Acer has a dual-core Intel, 1G and the ECS has a Celeron, 256M (before partially consumed by VGA). I'll subscribe this thread for coming back next time to see the progress and to give more information.

### Post by tau-lepton on 2009-11-03
I upgraded three machines, Lenovo R61 Thinkpad, Dell Optiplex360, and custom system with ASUS AMD MB.  

I also installed onto a VirtualBox VM.  

All installs were 32 bit except for the Dell Optiplex360, which was also the VirtualBox host.  The two issues that I had were with wireless on the thinkpad and a GTK issue with Eclipse.  Both were easily resolved; removing the old madwifi and configuration on the thinkpad, and setting GDK_NATIVE_WINDOWS=true for the Eclipse 3.5 issue. (Discussed here: [http://forums.opensuse.org/pre-release-beta/424021-eclipse-doesnt-accept-mouse-clicks-buttons-2.html](http://forums.opensuse.org/pre-release-beta/424021-eclipse-doesnt-accept-mouse-clicks-buttons-2.html))
Envyng is also not running on one machine, python library error; not a big deal.

### Post by Stephen_at on 2009-11-03
I voted Install - no problems. It was that or upgrade - no problems.

I'd already partitioned my disk with /home as a separate ext4 partition when I'd installed Jaunty so it really made sense for me to not do an upgrade but to do an install instead. Admittedly I then had to re-install a few extra packages that I'd added on but it worked perfectly for me. I've got a Medion S5610 laptop which Jaunty had a couple of problems with the sound on. Its got an ATI video card and Intel wireless and Karmic just worked, and because it worked out that /home/steve already existed and my new user was also steve and thus just changed the permissions it picked up my customised desktop, my wireless settings and so on. I do wonder if it would make sense for Ubuntu to create /home as a separate partition using most of the disk by default.

Karmic even sees the HDMI output, where Jaunty didn't.

Boot times, excluding the grub delay but including me selecting a user and entering a password, to a working connected desktop is 56 seconds. Shutdown, excluding that 60 second countdown, is 6 seconds.

So I'm actually quite happy with it. The only odd thing is when you use apt-get the "reading database" seems to take longer than I'd expect it to.

### Post by brunovecchi on 2009-11-03

### Post by thrudd on 2009-11-03
Jaunty -> Karmic beta worked fine for my core2duo.  No problems.    Fresh install on a Dell Satellite Pro 6100 with a Geforce 4 420 Go graphics card not so good - the edid reported from the monitor is *wrong* (bad hardware).  Had to create a hacked edid file and make up a xorg.conf to load it and get the resolution right, and use old nvidia drivers.  Hope there is a work around for future releases.  OTOH could be new old laptop time :P    Here's how i did it, props to original author:  [http://blog.freshnewpage.com/2008/04/26/nvidia-geforce4-420-go-on-ubuntu-804-hardy-heron/](http://blog.freshnewpage.com/2008/04/26/nvidia-geforce4-420-go-on-ubuntu-804-hardy-heron/)  Or you could boot off the cd after install, mount your HDD, and rename your /etc/X11/xorg.conf to something else and run with the safe drivers.

### Post by cfbmoo1 on 2009-11-03
Installer locked up when I tried to do a full blown install from a downloaded image. I'd see the Ubuntu friends symbol and then a black screen and no other activity on my EEE 1000 40G. That was the 9.10 NBRM USB fresh installer I made.

A few days later I saw the distro upgrade option in my update tool and since I had my home directory already backed up I took the plunge and it plowed away installing everything. When it was finished everything seemed to work perfectly. 

I'm guessing there's some configuration stuff on my Netbook that 9.04 had and 9.10 just picked up on and made things painless at that point. A fresh install just flat out bombed when booting so I don't know what to say, I really had better luck doing the distro update.

Since I'm reading about a a lot of other people having fresh install problems I'm going to hold off trying things on the desktop. That and my 9.04 experiences with Pulse Audio on my desktop have me sticking with Windows 7 for desktop stuff. It's a shame because I love what Ubuntu does on my netbook.

My only peeve with 9.10 after installing is the fact that I have two buttons on my task bar instead of one for things like Shut Down, Restart, Logoff, Suspend, etc. I like having all those options as one button on a 1024x600 screen. Screen space is a valuable resource on a netbook and I don't like wasting it for something that was originally one button.

### Post by ChrisWebb on 2009-11-03
I have two machines upgraded from Jaunty. I haven't tested either exhaustively, but so far I have the following:

x86 version: Is suffering the most. Movie Player doesn't run, Mplayer can't output video, Nautilus is apparently crashing a lot and the Software Center has never worked. Software Center issue seems to be related to GStreamer, but haven't been able to trace fault yet (a lack of time as much as anything). Re-installing anything GStreamer related does not help, nor does re-installing Software Center or even the ubuntu-desktop package. Pidgin and Empathy also crash at start.

x64 version: Haven't had any flickering displays on my GeForce 6600 and dual screen support seems OK. However I've noticed a performance drop in 3D rendering. SkyRocket running on defaults is a typical example with sometimes a very noticeable drop in render speed. Have also had some problems with audio stutter in Amarok.

All in all the x64 PC has faired much better.

My guess is that the proprietary nVidia driver is at fault in both cases.

### Post by gallafent on 2009-11-03
Problem: squeezeboxserver 7.4.1 package from slim devices repository at “deb [http://debian.slimdevices.com](http://debian.slimdevices.com) stable main” has broken mysql dependencies for ubuntu 9.10.

Solution: switch to slim devices “testing” repository at “deb [http://debian.slimdevices.com](http://debian.slimdevices.com) testing main” to obtain 7.4.2, in which this problem is fixed.

Reference: [http://wiki.slimdevices.com/index.php/Debian_Package](http://wiki.slimdevices.com/index.php/Debian_Package)

Worked for me.

### Post by SilverWave on 2009-11-03
If you are risk averse wait until 9.10.1 at least. D'oh!

The installation was smooth and quick.

Clean Install, but keeping /home, is the way to go IMHO

==========================================

I do a clean install but reuse my /home drive leaving all the .files in place. If I have any problems I can just reinstall 9.04 again. Oh and, of course, back up home and etc to an external USB drive.

I have separate partitions for "/" (root) 10GiB; /home 100GiB; swap 8GiB; /windows; /backup.

Set your partitions up from a LiveCD with Gparted then go the manual route in the installer options. You may want to have a run through using a virtual machine first ;)

Small issues I have had:

1. Flash is not recognizing mouse clicks in multiple situations:

Fix: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/flashplugin-nonfree/+bug/410407/comments/143](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/flashplugin-nonfree/+bug/410407/comments/143)

2. Video is tinted blue after update

Fix: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gstreamer0.10/+bug/409640/comments/16](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gstreamer0.10/+bug/409640/comments/16)

3. Karmic tries to load rt2870sta AND rt2800usb. Results in no WiFi

Fix: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/460323](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/460323)

Yes there are some bugs but as its FOSS you can fix them in double quick time yourself or just wait for the fix to be pushed by Ubuntu.

One tip:
Once you have burned the ISO to CD boot into the CD and choose:
Check disk for defects.

Making sure the CD is not corrupt saves you a lot of pain.

### Post by sveterv on 2009-11-03
I am upgrading from 7.10 consequently through every single release till 9.10 and always had some minor issues, but always was able to correct them in a few minutes. 

My system is really customized (many additional packages, many system wide "important" settings are changed) 

I've made last upgrade (from 9.04) on the day Karmic Koala beta became alive :) I love upgrades, you never know what is going to happen ;) 

What I can advise to people is one thing ALWAYS change your display drivers to open source ones (like nv) this gives 99.9%  guarantee you'll be able to get graphics mode (GNOME) working OK after upgrade. 

What else? Hmm, backup your data, I always have full backup of my system (simple tar.gz) on another hard disk, so at any time I can restore my system in 10 minutes. 

I love Linux, Ubuntu especially :)

Ahh, I forgot, I always made upgrades using alternate CD and "cdromupgrade" command on it.

### Post by Mugsy323 on 2009-11-03
I downloaded Karmic Koala (64bit Ubu 9.10) and created a Boot CD.

When I select the "Try Ubu" option, it eventually stops at the Login screen asking me for a username and password. (WTF?) No matter what I enter (including leaving it blank) works.

The .iso image of 64bit Ubu 9.10 (desktop version) is a tad too big (690M) to fit on a standard CD ("leadout" adds about 25M). I had to enable "overburn" to get it to fit. Someone needs to look into this.

So I put Ubu on a DVD and it boots just fine. I then used it to create a Boot USB flash dive, which works as well.

I've already wasted two CD's trying to create a working CD of Ubu 9.10. I'm not going to waste another half dozen CDs looking for a solution. (The CD is not for me. It's for someone else.)

Anyone else experience this and/or know of a fix?

PS: Also, the Network Manager is still flakey. Entering all my login info under the DSL tab in the NM... even though it detects "eth0" and sets it to "auto"), I can not connect to my DSL through it. I must instead run "pppoeconf" from the terminal and enter all my login info there to get online via the Boot CD.

### Post by mattgreen on 2009-11-03
Had problems with my upgrade from Jaunty (9.04) to Karmic (9.10) - I had the 'flickering login prompt' issue as reported by many others - took about a day to fix.

My graphics card is an Intel i945GM - I deleted /etc/X11/xorg.conf and all is working well.

Also, compared to Jaunty, the slow scrolling problem is fixed.

### Post by stevebuck on 2009-11-03
I let my Lenovo S10-2 netbook (N280, 2G/250G) with 9.04 Remix proceed with 9.10 upgrade via the upgrade manager. Everything went normally. When asked if I wanted to keep my menu.lst file, I choose the default shown, use my previous one.
Upon rebooting, Grub still showed 9.04 (I guess that's expected since it is the same menu.lst file) and the computer froze with a blank screen before any desktop was shown. After pressing the power button until the system powered off, subsequent power on allowed the computer to boot to login prompt. After logging in, the desktop no longer looked like it was designed for a netbook and the mouse pad did not work. The icons are HUGE, making the upgrade look like a totally different distro. I was so disappointed in the look and lack of mouse control. I plugged in a USB mouse, couldn't find any "quit" icon and eventually found a shutdown by right clicking on my user name.
Now my netbook is sitting in a bottom drawer and here I am on Unbuntu forum, using Windoze. I am not a happy camper.
I think somebody compiling releases messed up and let a normal Ubuntu desktop take over my netbook's Remix. It doesn't look good on  1024x600 10.1" screen:(

Update: I had some time to take another look at my Lenovo S10-2. Besides the track pad not working, there also was no sound. I opened up a terminal and did a 'uname -a' and found the kernel loaded was still the old 2.6.28-16. Looking in the /boot folder I could see the newer kernel 2.6.31-14. So it looks like the default action during upgrade of leaving the menu.lst file untouched is wrong. You definitely want the upgrade to write out a new menu.lst file. I quickly edited menu.lst with the new kernel number, saved, rebooted and now it looks like everything is working again. What a relief. But I think 9.10 is slower and definitely takes longer to boot up. This really concerns me because it is taking 50 seconds to get to login prompt and 60 seconds for Windows 7 to reach login prompt. I think Remix should be better optimized for netbooks. Remember, these netbooks only have a little Atom CPU with single core and HT, and 512k cache.

### Post by cutterjohn on 2009-11-03
First time that I upgraded Ubuntu so soon after a 6m release(I usually wait a month or so for last minute bug mopup and to allow server traffic to die now a bit), and it went fairly well again this time. (Ubuntu 9.10 x86-64 + catalyst 9.10)

The only problem that I encountered is that my lowest two brightness levels are brighter than the minimum brightness level, i.e. using the brightness control function keys, the popup brightness meter with no bar and the 1st segment of the bar is brighter than the 2nd segment of the bar which appears to be the actual minimum setting.  It almost seems that the brightness level are slightly offset by two settings for some reason now.  (This is not a major problem, more like an irritant as it's something that should work out of the box, but at least my birghtness function keys work unlike some others that I saw when doing a quick search through the various bug reports...)

MSI GT725

Some clarifications, etc. or so I hope...  Actually I would've tried the beta, but AMD is so slow at getting catalyst support for newer kernels that by the time that the special Ubuntu build for 9.10 appeared I figured that I may just as well wait for the final release...

### Post by Stormspace on 2009-11-03
I tried an upgrade on my Pentium Dual-Core E2180 with an Asus P5N73-AM Motherboard and after the upgrade got the Ubuntu X server failure. I tried several different ways to reset the x server configuration but nothing would work, so I opted for a fresh install to the see if that would work. 

Hardware config, first time through:
2 IDE Hard drives connected to the single IDE port, 1 IDE CD-ROM drive connected via an IDE raid card set to operate as an IDE add in board.

Booted to install, answered language prompt, changed to install Ubuntu and pressed enter. White Ubuntu logo displayed and ghosted after a few minutes. (Basically two logo's on screen, one to the upper left of the first in a slightly muted color.)

Tried again..No logo issues, but after a few minutes the screen went blank. Hit the space bar and saw an initfs something prompt.

I tried several different hardware configs with the IDE raid controller in the machine. I finally removed it and with 1 drive and CD-ROM connected via IDE tried again with the same results. 

One interesting thing to note. I got 9.04 on this box by moving my hard drive into it with the OS already installed. This is my first attempt to install Ubuntu on this box. 

BTW, Windows would not install either until I moved the CD-ROM drive to the integrated IDE controller. This box is currently running windows, but I'd prefer it run Ubuntu so any help would be appreciated.

### Post by DenysT on 2009-11-03
I did a clean install of the 64-bit release on a machine I just built and it went off without a hitch. The nVidia graphics worked correctly unlike the problems I had with 9.04. On the down side I haven't been able to get extensions into OpenOffice, may have something to with 64-bit?

On my Compaq Evo N610c laptop I first attempted to do an in place upgrade. Well, it had major graphics hiccups with Mobility Radeon 7500 chip. It upgraded with Visual effects set to Normal. Well it worked somewhat on 9.04 but as soon as I tried to open any application (Writer, Package Manager, etc) the screen locked up. Hard reboot required. Set the Visual Effects to None and most applications would start but some system apps, e.g. System Monitor, would be nothing but a window that looked like oily cheesecloth. I figured something didn't upgrade correctly so I did a clean install (home data reside on a different partition) but got the same results. Went back to 9.04 on the laptop and it works fine again. What is the diff between the video driver from 9.04 to 9.10 that this is happening?

I have one more machine to I'm going to play with and it has a nVidia graphics AGP card (kind of old but 128 M RAM, so it's a good little card). Honestly, the ATI graphics seem to be as flaky in Ubuntu as they were back in the Windows 95/98/2000/early XP installs I used to do.

### Post by Mr_Leo on 2009-11-03
I few days ago, upgraded from 9.04 using update manager on my 64-bit intel dual-core. Download of nearly 1800 packages took a while, but install went ok.

On first boot, splash screen was a little wierd (simple white logo; text disappeared), then went to a console login that was rapidly flashing and I wasn't able to enter name and password. I logged in using another computer (ssh) and stopped gdm. the flashing stopped and I was able to log into the console. I use nvidia proprietery drivers, so needed to reinstall the driver and restart gdm. Then I logged in to my desktop. Ok, not too bad.

[U]Most things worked well, but I did have to fix a few minor problems:
[/U]
boot splash screen (mentioned above) - fixed after installing grub2 and usplash-theme-ubuntu-color.

Audio works, but there is some stuttering distortion on ubunutu login and startup music. Also volume of the login "drumbeat" is very loud and startling. Sounds works well on most everything else.

Aside from that, the only other problem I had was that one of my games, UT2004, wouldn't start; complaining "could not find shared library libstdc++5". fixed it using "wget http://fr.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/universe/g/gcc-3.3/libstdc++5_3.3.6-17ubuntu1_amd64.deb".

I was a bit disappointed to find that remote login via xdmcp was unavailable, but I've been playing around on the system for a couple days now and have found it to otherwise work pretty well.

### Post by mahy on 2009-11-03
I always had a problem with Ubuntu and laptop hotkeys on my Toshiba A300, I also filed a bug a long time ago, and about 2 months ago they told me it had already been fixed in Karmic (then Alpha 5). I tried the Karmic Alpha 5 live CD, and everything worked!! But now, having clean-installed Karmic final, laptop hotkeys DON'T work again! That's the biggest disappointment of them all with 9.10. Another one was a failed (frozen) 'alternate' installation with selected /home encryption. I had to repeat the whole process without selecting encryption to get things done. To sum up, Ubuntu 9.10 works fine for me, but it's nowhere near as polished as a serious MS and Apple competitor has to be. But what else would you expect for €0 ($0)?

I can stop kvm_intel with the command

sudo rmmod kvm_intel

The only problem is that this only works in the current session, and kvm reloads on every re-boot.

I've tried to remove the package with apt-get, but apt-get doesnt recognise that it even exists.

The question is, what is triggering the package in the first place. I'm starting virtualbox from a clean boot without loading any apps save for compiz, awn, and a couple of applets 

any ideas on how to disable this annoying little beast permanently?

### Post by abrianb on 2009-11-03

Popped in a copy of 8.10 I burned last year, installed, upgraded twice, now I 9.10. Next day I tried the Desktop. I started Upgrade manager, 3 hours later it wanted me to insert a copy of the 0.10 disc. I had not done any thing to this point with a 9.10 disc other than burn it on this machine, twice and opened it to check files on it. When I did that I told the system I did not want to upgrade. My first install was way easier ( 6.06).

### Post by bjfs on 2009-11-03
So I upgraded from 9.04 to 9.10 on desktop and server and here are the results:

** 1. Server (HP Proliant)**
Little to download, nothing got broken (at least I can't see it coming) and I have to get used to the *byobu* instead of the *screen* command.

** 2. Desktop (FSC Amilo)**
Much to download, as one can expect from desktop environment, but then things just got worse...
- sound "clicks" from time to time for no apparent reason
- system fails to restart, as described by people (me included) in [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/459162](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/459162)

Well, at least my xscreesanver works faster and this Ubuntu One thingy looks kind of cool.
Can't get used to Ubuntu Software Center, yet. Oh, and my friend on IRC told that he can't get his CD unmounted :P

### Post by Samilong on 2009-11-03
Upgraded on Saturday (31st October) using Update Manager. What a disaster! The upgrade seemed to go fine, but froze as it came to the reboot. One manual reboot later, and computer failed to boot. I know why it failed - the install was not on the primary (C:\) drive [to use the old PC terms] - but nothing I could do would get the computer (and grub) to recognise the installation.

Eventually, I installed a copy of 9.04 onto the primary drive (wiping the last vestiges of Windows from the machine), downloaded an .iso of 9.10, burnt the disc and upgraded this new installation. The computer booted into a sparkly new Karmic Koala version of Ubuntu. 

After all that, I was deeply unimpressed. Its handling of sound is woeful; I have spent three days trying (unsuccessfully) to get my microphone to work. Rhythmbox crashes every time it tries to read my music collection. The 'Ubuntu Software Centre' seems to be missing almost all the really useful software (System Monitor, for example) and does nothing about system dependencies, the new version of Firefox hates Yahoo mail, and everything is just so much slower than it used to be. 

Would I recommend KK to friends? Hell, no! This is by far and away the most problematic Ubuntu release I have used to date.

---

### Post by mcastaneda68 on 2009-11-03

### Post by m3topaz on 2009-11-03
I have a Dell Studio 1537 laptop, stock apart from 1920 screen and Dell Bluetooth module. Jaunty to Karmic 32-bit upgrade worked just fine, I needed to enable the Broadcom STA proprietary driver to make the wireless work (the B43 seemingly wouldn't work). Palimpsest reports bad disk sectors - this was news to me - ID 197 "Current Pending Sector Count" which has crept up to 532 sectors now. New disk ordered - I do believe the Samsung HM251JI is toast...
I put my old Dell Inspiron 6400 Ubuntu/WinXP HDD in the studio - and not only did it boot into Jaunty but everything worked! Very impressive. Then I flattened the first partition (bye-bye XP) and put 64-bit Karmic on as a clean install. That all worked fine too, from a brief inspection - including grub dual-boot allowing Karmic or Jaunty. I'll look harder at 64-bit over the weekend and report back how that works out as a clean install on my newly ordered disk next week.
Overall: Karmic is another step up. I know there are some concerning stories but I've succeeded with three Jaunty upgrades now; one of the two desktops had a graphics problem now fixed (I just posted that to another thread).

### Post by ubunny on 2009-11-03
the final page of the install has a list of packages that you want to keep.  As it happens I luckily picked madwifi but it then when and rebooted.  

I would have liked a checkbox listing here to allow me to select all, some or none rather than only one.  if I wanted more I probably needed to Ctrl and select.  But this isn't needed, just improve the interface, the objective.

With that, karmic is fine

BUT

now firefox crashes all the time.  if I have any addons at all it dies.  I've resorted to use epiphany until ff or ubuntu has an update.  

as well, the bootup means that it boots up twice.  I see the boot message then a ubuntu logo, some flash of text which I can't find logged (where is it?) and then it reboots again, THEN it loads.  what??  Adds about twice the time for every boot up.

other than that, upgrade was much smoother and since i saved the madwifi initially I didn't have to struggle with Internet connectivity right away.

Some icon changes, and I returned the brown theme ;)  

PROBLEM 

oh one thing that will bother you is the new login.  It has my login name in a list like windows, I hit enter, then type the password.  I would have preferred that the login name is not listed.  Oddly this is not an available option, so I have to stick with it.  Really dumb.  

If you're going to change something I'm going to see every single day I login, then at least make the attempt to give the the previous options.  If it ain't broke don't waste my time.

:(

### Post by ProphetOfDoom on 2009-11-03
Thought I'd just add my 2p's worth. 

jaunty to karmic = from bad to worse. Under Jaunty I had frequent system freezing that many suffer with no resolution. At least one forum thread on that topic is 70+ pages long and no one has nailed the issue. So moved to Karmic in the hope that the problem would have gone. But it's still system freezing and worse, with certain radeon cards you can't enable compiz without your desktop going black (bug reported and confirmed). Downgrading mesa to jaunty version resolves that temporarily but the logs are now full of graphics related memory issues. And boot time is longer than jaunty.

Ubuntu used to be stable and reliable, but I feel it's become a mess. The twice yearly release schedule is to blame. Too fast and not enough testing. I'm looking to jump to fedora or arch now. :(

---

### Post by dld on 2009-11-03
I have been upgrading Ubuntu for years without problems.  Times have changed.
I upgraded a secondary machine first.  There seemed to be no problems, but
I did run janitor and then begin to find Ubuntu hanging after 5 to 10
minutes.  I will get back to that eventually.  I turned to my main machine.
Downloading was going well.  When I got back to that machine the screen was
black.  I assumed that the download had completed and install had begun.
There was a bit of disk activity for awhile.  Then nothing, so I kill the
machine. (That may have been a BIG mistake.)  No more booting Ubuntu. The
new kernel had not been installed, and the old one was broken.  (Is there
any way to run apt-get on a non-running install?)

I had 70+ GB unformatted on the HD, so I did an install of 9.10 there.  Nautilus will mount the old partitions, so I was able to copy files, and
after a few days of scratching again figured out how to do configurating.
Very painful.  I still would like to be able to heal the broken 9.4.

Probably the greatest dislike I have is the gdm logon.  Its slow and
unattractive.  I would be willing to type user and password to avoid the
current screens.   Why don't we see an abstract KOALA?
   Don

### Post by ausmuso on 2009-11-03
I did a clean install. Not only because of bad experiences with updates in the past, but also because it's such a good opportunity to do some housecleaning.

Like always there were a few installation problems but nothing serious. With wireless networking the network manager steadfastly refused to recognise the password for the keyring. First I thought I had been sloppy in my typing somewhere. I reinstalled from scratch, being very careful with my typing but the same thing happened again (a bug?).

Luckily Karmic is running on a desktop so I always log in to the one wireless server. The simple solution was to do away with network manager altogether and use wicd instead. Now the wireless network gets up and running at boottime, ready for use.

The wireless connection was slow because the driver for my Ralink rt2500 wireless card didn't properly negotiate a connection speed (this is an old problem). I fixed this by adding the command "iwconfig wlan0 rate 36M" to the /etc/rc5.d/rc.local bootscript.

Karmic didn't find any proprietary drivers for my NVidia chip. I should have added the Medibuntu repositories to Apt-get  before searching, of course. By the time I had twigged this I had long since installed a proprietary NVidia driver "the oldfashoned way". I grabbed the build-essential and kernel-header packages, downloaded driver
package 190.42 from the NVidia webside and installed it by running the shellscript. The driver runs fine.

From hereon everything seems to be going fine and I keep adding bits and pieces to make Karmic the perfect desktop.

---

### Post by Shooter40 on 2009-11-03
I have an older Dell computer.  Ubuntu was running on it for several months flawlessly.  I tried the upgrade to 9.10 and it seemed to go OK until the end when it gave me several warnings that the 20GB hard drive had many bad sectors.  I reinstalled 9.04 and got the same warnings.  Both of the programs seemed to run alright.  I then reformated the disk with DOS and ran scandisk.  It reported that the disk was OK with no bad sectors detected.

Reinstalling 9.10 after the formatting gave the same warnings again.  I am a newbe to Linux, but have run Ubuntu 8 series and 9.04 with no problems on this computer.  I will use DOS again to reformat the disk and try the 9.04 installation again unless someone has any better ideas.  Why would DOS Scandisk not find any bad sectors when this program says I have over 150 bad sectors?

### Post by second.exodous on 2009-11-03
Wow, I'm surprised about all the problems people are having.  9.10 installed for me no problems, sound and video even worked better than it did in 9.04.

I went to installed Handbrake and it didn't work, had to use the SVN, but that is a well known 'bug', the SVN will turn into the new release and fix this.

I did have a problem with my 1TB drive formatting in an installer, but every distro I try has this exact same problem, so it's not Ubuntu.  I just format it with gparted in a working install then install on it.  I haven't tried installing Windows or OSX on this drive so I wouldn't know if it's Linux specific or not.

Also, maybe more people are using it and it's time to do the service pack thing, maybe release stable for servers/power users, and then in 3 months have it released for 'everyone else'.  Still  would have a 6 month release cycle but have a release between that would be 'really works for everyone' release.  So still have *.04 April and *.10 October release for stable but have a 'really ready' *.045 release in July and a *.105 release in January.

### Post by recluce on 2009-11-03
> **Shooter40 said:**
> I have an older Dell computer.  Ubuntu was running on it for several months flawlessly.  I tried the upgrade to 9.10 and it seemed to go OK until the end when it gave me several warnings that the 20GB hard drive had many bad sectors.  I reinstalled 9.04 and got the same warnings.  Both of the programs seemed to run alright.  I then reformated the disk with DOS and ran scandisk.  It reported that the disk was OK with no bad sectors detected.

Reinstalling 9.10 after the formatting gave the same warnings again.  I am a newbe to Linux, but have run Ubuntu 8 series and 9.04 with no problems on this computer.  I will use DOS again to reformat the disk and try the 9.04 installation again unless someone has any better ideas.  Why would DOS Scandisk not find any bad sectors when this program says I have over 150 bad sectors?

Because 9.10 will give you SMART warnings while scandisk will only find bad sectors after the drive runs out of spare sectors, which is rare. DOS and Windows XP do not monitor SMART. 

Read up on SMART on wikipedia.

---

### Post by eriktheblu on 2009-11-03
I did a CD install with Hardy as my primary OS; the upgrade option seemed a little unreasonable as I would have had to go through Intrepid and Jaunty. Prior to install, I resized my partitions to make room for new EXT4 partitions. I had hoped by leaving them in place the install would recognize 8.04 still on the system and allow me to select it from Grub (didn't happen).

Install went without a hitch.

Video drivers (proprietary Nvidia) work just fine.

My Linkstation NAS didn't mount using the same procedures as with Hardy, but I eventually got it to work by replacing the server name with the IP.

WoW through Wine had some bugs and would crash in certain situations with 8.04. I'm not sure if it's the new video driver, new Wine version (when I used the current release in Hardy, X crashed) or the distro as a whole, but WoW no longer crashes.

I'm having some issues with Flash still, specifically video player controls not responding. That will probably get corrected soon enough.

Overall, I'd say it's been an improvement over Hardy. The stuff that gives me grief with Karmic is the same stuff that gave me grief with Hardy.... only the solutions are just a little different.

### Post by bmorency on 2009-11-03
I had only one issue when I upraded from kubuntu 64-bit 9.04 to 9.10. It was minor to fix as well. I walked away while it was doing the upgrade and when I came back it didn't ask me if I wanted to reboot. I'm not sure if it was supposed to do it by itself or not. It was sitting at the desktop. So i rebooted myself anyway. When it came back to the desktop sound did not work. 

So I checked to see what kernel it was using and it was still using the old kernel (2.6.28-11). It did actually install the new kernel but did not add it to the grub menu. So i reinstalled the new kernel so it would add it the the boot menu. Once it did that I rebooted and selected the new kernel sound started to work and have not had any other issues yet.

### Post by Kazriko on 2009-11-03
Only minor problems, and it actually fixed some issues I had with Jaunty. 

1. It needed too much space. My /var drive was too small. I deleted all my archives and had about 1.9 gigs free, but it ran out of space before it finished. I deleted the archives again and reran the configs and everything went fine.

2. The hardware drivers kicked back from FGLRX to Nvidia. I deleted fglrx and reenabled it and it worked perfectly.

3. The sound defaulted to the wrong card. A little fiddling in preferences had everything working again. (I have about 3 things it considers sound cards, but it used the internal motherboard card instead of my addon.)

4. I ran a local DNS server (So I can test sites before we make them live on the global DNS,) but early in the upgrade it was shut down and suddenly the system couldn't find the ubuntu archive. I switched to a Hurricane Electric server and finished the install.  (IPv6, Yay!)

5. Jaunty had left this glitch in my header file dpkg that wouldn't go away no matter how I tried. The upgrade to Koala got rid of this odd installation problem with depmod and I haven't seen it since.

---

### Post by ddaviszx on 2009-11-03
upgraded desktop and laptop, no problems at all, is running great

### Post by pearl298 on 2009-11-03
I have now upgraded 4 machines from 9.04 with only minor problems! There seems to be a problem on the first bootup with getting the screen to work properly, it takes two or three reboots to get a visible screen. After initial boot and setting the various display options everything works fine.

Hardware: 1920x1200 display, both ATI and Nvidia graphics cards. I use a KVM switcher to select display, keyboard and mouse between the four systems. One 64bit I7-920, one 23bit Athlon 6000+, one 32 bit dual Intel P4, one 32bit Athlon 1700+ all working fine now.

The only other problems are with third party add ons: Wine has no karmic repository yet, and my webcomic reader buoh is no longer supported (abandoned in 2005) Comic Aggregator is an upgraded replacement.

I had trouble with an encrypted system in that I wanted to add an extra harddrive in /etc/fstab, it needs a couple of extra steps that are not documented anywhere that I could find. I hope to contribute a tutorial when I get it figured out. (I have two more to do!)

All in all a "happy camper" and Vista refuge! The XP->Vista upgrade was the reason I migrated to Ubuntu in the first place!

### Post by bill516 on 2009-11-03
My machine is a System 76 Pangolin PanP4.  2 Gigaherz dual-core, 3 Gigs Ram, 160 Gig HD, nvidia 9300M video, Intel 5100 wireless card.

Everything works, configuration once installed was easy.  No issues.

9.10 Live CD worked fine as a Live CD.  (MD5Sums did check)  The install process hung repeatedly and left me with an inoperative 8.10 and a blasted GRUB.  Not good.

I reinstalled 8.10 (which has been rock-solid, by the way) and then upgraded to 9.04 just long enough to upgrade to 9.10.  That was the long way through the woods to grandmother's house!  As a result I have ext3 (really fine with me, frankly), but no GRUB2.  I must install GRUB2 and set about learning it.

One suggestion for the Live Install?  Do put a button on the desktop that takes new people to a special page on the website where they can get a walk-through of just what is going to happen when they do the process and what decisions they will have to make.  Do this for both full-disk single system situations and a dual/multi boot scenario.

Can I suggest a separate home partition by default?

But let there be no doubt I think the team has done a fine job!  My thanks to one and all.

My installation blow up?  I really just do not know.  I have never had anything like that happen, going back to 7.04.  The error message seemed to suggest the possibility of bad media or a bad reading head in the drive.  Perhaps, I just do not know.  (I have had no other problems with the drive.)

I went the upgrade route becaue I need my machine urgently.  So the multi-step upgrade,  while time-consuming, suddenly seemed safer.  (I usually prefer a fresh installation)  In any event it did work.

### Post by plogston on 2009-11-03
I tried installing Karmic on my old junk laptop (Sony P4 2.4Ghz laptop, 512K, Radeon Mobility 7500) to check it out. In general, it's been rough. In all cases, I did a pretty much vanilla fresh install.

My first attempt was installing from Kubuntu 9.10 i386 Desktop. There were a lot of graphical problems with the installer and the resulting install booted, but the KDE environment was really unusable (the plasma and window borders were corrupted). I think that my experience maps to bug 434376 with the ATI driver.

I then reinstalled fresh using the Ubuntu 9.10 i386 Desktop CD. The install process looked good. After the install, I did see some graphics corruption in Gnome, but that mostly went away when I disabled desktop effects. I seem to be affected by the notify-osd bug that seems related to the one that affected KDE (I think that this is the same issue: 416001). Also, System Monitor shows the same kind of corruption.

Outside of those issues, Karmic runs great (using it to post this comment). Boots fast and I think that it is much more responsive in general. I'm holding off installing on the other computers until I get a read on how it does with other systems.

### Post by axel_2078 on 2009-11-03
My experience? Here goes...

I worked on trying to get this installed for three straight days and came to the conclusion that this is a sorry excuse for the "best" Ubuntu release yet. No matter what I tried, grub would not boot the system after the installation. Many others in the forum are having the same problem, but no one seems to know what's causing it or how to fix it. I did manage  to get 9.10 installed by reinstalling 9.04, then installing all the updates via update manager, then upgrading via update manager. The process went smoothly but some things broke afterwards. Sound and wireless still work great; that's always a plus. However, my video is just adequate. Although the system recognized that my video card is an ATI Mobility 9000, there is no driver installed for my chipset (generic open-vga driver). That means I have to set my visual effects to "none" just to be able to see my desktop (it's black otherwise). Screensavers don't work either. The resolution is the right size though. Still annoying. By going the upgrade route (vs clean install), I also missed out on the ext4 file system and the ability to encrypt my home partition until login. I still can't believe this has been so difficult. You shouldn't have to figure out how to hack grub for find a "work around" to get it to boot on a new release.

I have to say, I think this particular release is the Microsoft Vista equivalent....a total disaster that was released before it was ready. It makes me embarrassed to say that I'm a fan of Ubuntu. There's no way I would hand out 9.10 discs to friends or recommend it to anyone.  I realize that each release will have it's share of problems, but this is just ridiculous. I really really hope that 10.4 will redeem Ubuntu.

Edit: I have now reinstalled 9.04. The problems in 9.10 were too numerous and I don't have the time to mess with it when 9.04 works great.

---

### Post by tonymaro on 2009-11-04
I think I'm going to have to go back to Jaunty.

I'm running a dual-core AMD 64 bit X2 5600 with a GeForce 8600 GTS.  My boot time has more than quadrupled compared to Jaunty.  1 out of every 3 times I log back in through the screensaver, Compiz crashes.  Periodically Emerald crashes leaving me with no window decorations at all.  Several times a day Xorg just restarts for no apparent reason, dumping me back on the login screen.

To me it sounds mostly like video driver issues, but I definitely had none of these problems in Jaunty.

The other issues I've had are silly things like Empathy periodically disconnects from my Jabber server and doesn't bother telling me.  I've already had to switch back to Pidgin to keep that from happening.

Also, I use LDAP authentication on our domain, and I don't want a list of all of those users on the login screen - I want to TYPE my login name each time.  I could care less about trying to set a background or theme for GDM, but the fact that you can't disable the user list at login is very annoying.

All in all, a painful experience, and I hate admitting it.  Still better than Vista though ;-)

### Post by JPSant on 2009-11-04

Good bye MS....My last Linux distro was over 1 year ago..Mandriva...  Last version of Ubuntu 9.04 WOULD not install properly on this HP laptop...9.10 EXCELLENT...way to go!!
SORRY others have issues..

### Post by alawson on 2009-11-04
Sharing my experience.....

I decided to upgrade my media box. It's a Core 2 Duo with 2Gb of mem and a recentish NVidia card, been running 64bit Jaunty for ages, previously upgraded from Hardy. It was running low on disk (I only had a 40Gb and an 80Gb in there) so I popped out and bought a 500Gb drive for it.

I swapped out my old drives as a fallback, and put the new drive in. Got through POST and booted to the usual Ubuntu install screen.

After I'd selected to install, I was faced with a delay of several minutes with just a white Ubuntu logo on a black background. My CD drives activity LED is concealed within the fascia of the case, and the HD LED was constant. Even though I suspected the box had crashed I left it running and went away to do something else. When I came back  after 30 mins or so it had progressed and I picked keyboard and location etc.

I selected to create a 100Gb partition to install the OS, with a 10Gb swap partition and leave the rest as a big space to drop MPEG2 TV recordings from my TV decoder card.

After install, the system would complete POST, but then just stop. I played with the BIOS boot config for a while and got cross. I went away and killed some zombies in Left 4 Dead for a while on my gaming box. Much restored, I came back and attempted a second install before giving up.

Second time around, I let the OS partition the drives as it saw fit, and managed to boot into the OS after install.

I use Kaffeine to watch DVB TV, and it wasn't happy. It tuned the channels okay, but then complained about a codec when I tried to watch them. After installing all the codecs I could find in the package manager, it started to work.

I was still faced with the same video problem I had when I installed Jaunty, as detailed at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1168458](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1168458) But the same fix worked too, which was nice. And this is mostly my display's fault because it lies about is geometry.

Also, my diNovo Edge keyboard has bombed twice during the writing of this post. Which is annoying, 'cos I thought I'd fixed that :(

So all in all, not 100% smooth. I certainly wouldn't advise those less technically able to install it. If previous versions of Ubuntu hadn't been great, I'd have already moved on to another distro.

### Post by SteveDee on 2009-11-04
A simple fresh install on a test pc appeared to be OK, so I took the plunge and upgraded my desktop...

...problems when upgrading;

[COLOR="Blue"]>>>Initial boot[/COLOR]
 Took me into Xubuntu! OK, I admit I installed Xubutu a few months back, tried it, then switched back to Ubuntu. But don't know why this should have become my default after upgrade. Eventually used this: [http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/puregnome](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/puregnome) to get back to pure Ubuntu

[COLOR="Blue"]>>>Wifi[/COLOR]
 signal level was 85-100% on Jaunty (and 54Mbits/s), now 20-25% (and 1MBit/s). Believe this to be a problem in Linux kernel...tried compiling & installing 2.6.32-rc5 (on a test pc) but got error messages about "Android"...[COLOR="Red"]I NEED A FIX FOR THIS![/COLOR]

[COLOR="Blue"]>>> Video Problems[/COLOR]
Tested with mpeg:-
* smplayer plays sound from video but no picture
* avidemux; picture shrunk horizontally (bad aspect ratio) and no sound.
* totem movie player; picture ok, no sound.
* mplayer; picture shrunk horizontally, sound ok. Errors; "unsupported pixelformat -1", "mvs not available"  
* vlc; error: "VLC can't recognize the input's format", otherwise sound & picture OK
AVI files also a problem (e.g. jerky)

[COLOR="Blue"]>>>Power Management Problem[/COLOR]
Get a problem when computer is woken from sleep (report posted)

[COLOR="Blue"]>>>Wikidpad[/COLOR]
Would not load my wikis; Error: Required data handler compact_sqlite unknown to WikidPad.

A quick fix (found here: [http://tech.groups.yahoo.com/group/wikidPad/message/5615](http://tech.groups.yahoo.com/group/wikidPad/message/5615) ):-

Below Wikidpad installation directory, in file
"lib/pwiki/Sqlitethin3.py", starting about at line 96, there is:-

pyver = tuple((int(s) for s in platform.python_version_tuple()))
if pyver >= (2, 5, 0):

Replace this by:

pyver = platform.python_version_tuple()
if pyver >= ("2", "5", "0"):


[COLOR="Blue"]>>>Misc crashes[/COLOR]
 trapped by system (some reports sent via report mechanism)

[COLOR="Blue"]>>>Seem OK;[/COLOR]
 Thunderbird, Sunbird, Firefox, Open OfficeCalc

Loads of apps are yet to be tested.


...and another thing, network printing don't work!
I have to stop then restart samba before I can print from a remote pc.

### Post by jthill on 2009-11-04
update-manager upgrade from Jaunty. Two big problems:

1) Reboot dropped me into busybox with a readonly root partition, and wouldn't let me remount until I made enough sense of what I was looking at to manually remount it rw,data=writeback to match the new default data= option.  Why mount cares what the journaling option was on a ro mount is beyond me, but once I ran tune2fs to specify data=ordered in the filesystem rather than letting it take the new default of writeback, that fixed that.

2) The text consoles were inaccessible - black screen.  Yanking splash from the boot options in menu.lst solved that.

One minor problem: kacpid is eating as much cpu as X and there are ACPI error messages spewed all over the logs.  kern.log and messages are 76M each, syslog is 15M, syslog.1 26M.  That's 200MB of logs in a few days on a light-duty desktop system.

But the major breakage is repaired, and the system feels more responsive and smoother than jaunty.  Kernel modeswitching if that's what it is very nice, back-and-forth to the text consoles doesn't feel like I'm abusing anything anymore, doesn't make the display squeal as it resets.

Openoffice went nuts once and started eating every pageframe in sight.  It was halfway through my swap space before I noticed the problem.  Good thing I'd already solved the text console issue, good luck even getting an xterm to respond let alone opening one under that kind of memory pressure.  Good old top on a text console found it and fixed it.

### Post by dillandriskell on 2009-11-04
I've looked forward to the release of Ubuntu 9.10, and I'm impressed with the new features.  I did suffer from some problems, only one detrimental.  I recognize that the system has only just been released however, so I'm not in the least worried about these problems remaining an issue.  Multiple updates have already been released, the recent of which fixed my worst problem.

The detrimental issue I had was with my Desktop (Dell Dimension 5100), where shortly after logging on to the graphical environment (absolute beginner talk = the place you usually log on as appose to using only the terminal) my system would freeze.  I still had choppy mouse movement but nothing worked and I was forced to dirty shutdown every time.  This seemed to be related to my CPU usage, as if I opened any more than 2 windows it crashed.  I knew it was a hardware incompatibility issue and not a corrupt disk as Ubuntu 9.10 worked almost flawlessly with my Laptop (Lenovo SL500 ThinkPad).  However there is a fix for it if anyone has that issue, but the only problem is getting to the fix without freezing up.

The trick is to avoid the cause of the problem, which is the graphical environment.  In my case I turned on my computer, and waited for it to finishing booting.  Once at the log in screen I didn't log in, but entered a console mode by pressing Ctrl+Alt+F1.  This is just a terminal for the unfamiliar, without the graphical environment, and it can be accessed by Ctrl+Alt+F1, F2, F3, F4, F5, or F6.  All of which open a different console, but you only need one, and you can return to the graphical interface by Ctrl+Alt+F7.  Then I entered my log in name, then my password.  Once it finished logging me in I typed the following code;

```
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade
```After entering my password again as prompted, this code installed all the new updates, including the fix for my graphical interface.  Once completed I finished the update with;

```
sudo reboot
```Problem solved, no issues thus far with my graphical interface.  I also went into my **System > Preferences > Appearance** then selected **Visual Effects** and selected **None**.  Just a precaution, I'm not sure if it's necessary.

Other than that I am suffering some minor issues, all so far on my Laptop (Lenovo SL500 ThinkPad).  I've listed them below and haven't found any fixes for them as of yet.

- OpenOffice crashes often
- Alt + Tab lags terribly and in some cases has forced me to reboot the system to restore performance.  At this point I've just stopped using it.
- Windows completely ignore my attempts to close them, neither clicking the close button or Alt + F4 work.

Again, nothing serious, these are things I can work around until a fix has been developed.  Other than that it's another amazing distribution and I always look forward to any updates.

### Post by der_joachim on 2009-11-04
I've been using Kubuntu since its first version, 5.04 or something. I did several clean installs and many, many urgrades. ATM, my only Kubuntu box is my work laptop. My other PCs have moved on to another distro, but I do not want to go offtopic.

Since I do not have the time to do a clean install, I decided to do a dist-upgrade. It was a disaster.

As with earlier versions, the recommended way to upgrade is to use the KDE update manager. As with earlier versions it crashed horribly. Note to developers: please do not recommend using an unstable upgrade tool! 

The first boot was a complete and utter failure. My root partition was a bit of the Schroedinger persuasion: it did not know whether it existed and whether it was writable (it decided not to be to be sure). A fsck did the trick though. After a few runs, my root partition mounted and I continued setting up all half-installed packages.

Xorg worked perfectly! Oh no! It did not! At boot, X starts in low graphics mode. The xorg log file shows only one warning about an invalid font path, and the rest appears to be perfect. After logging in on the console and restarting KDM, xorg works swimmingly! 

Sound worked OK, but only after having discovered that it is amplified 4x or something. Now, my volume level is at 30%-ish and the sound is acceptable. 

My fonts were screwed up badly. The KDE fonts were tiny, whereas the GTK fonts were huge. 

Subversion suddenly needed a gnome-keyring password. I never installed any gnome stuff and I never asked for yet another security (?) layer. I uninstalled any keyring-related stuff that never should have been there in the first place. 

Two things that are missing in my Kubuntu install, but are not particularly missed: a decent startup screen (still the old blue-logo-on-black-background), and grub2. 

/var/lib/dpkg/status was borked and it refused any installing, deinstalling or upgrading. Nice work! I had to manually hack it.

All in all, almost every problem was solved, but it took me a lot of time. I still have a few nagging problems, and maybe a clean install will be a better idea next time. It won't be a Canonical distribution though.

---

### Post by Duncan J Murray on 2009-11-04
I did a clean install of karmic, which seemed to go find until I hit this pretty major bug:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/403408](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/403408)

which meant that it would not boot at all.

I was forced to reinstall jaunty, and download the alternate cd, reinstall karmic and then it all worked again!

Apart from that major problem, karmic has been a pleasure to use.

Plusses:
1. Sound works very well, and seems to be given higher priority - no skipping.
2. Graphics are better - slicker on my laptop.
3. boot up is quicker.

Negatives:
1. This problem with the grub
2. flash seems to be a bit slow again (a problem which was sorted out on jaunty)
3. occasional crash when I started using it, now seems to have stopped.

Overall, I'm happy.  But I could see how others might be tearing their hair out!

Duncan.

### Post by mlnease on 2009-11-04
*mmap_min_addr *in KarmicHello,

On the fourth day now of a fresh install of Karmic, everything going smoothly after the first few bumps posted earlier.

This morning in Slashdot, I read about a vulnerability ("null pointer dereference flaw...making virtually all production versions in use at the moment vulnerable.").  A response to the article ([http://linux.slashdot.org/story/09/11/04/0320254/Bug-In-Most-Linuxes-Can-Give-Untrusted-Users-Root](http://linux.slashdot.org/story/09/11/04/0320254/Bug-In-Most-Linuxes-Can-Give-Untrusted-Users-Root)) recommended running sysctl -n vm.mmap_min_addr in a terminal window to check vulnerability.  Here's my output:

mn@ubuntu:~$ sysctl -n vm.mmap_min_addr
0
mn@ubuntu:~$ 

--meaning, as I understand it, that I'm vulnerable.  The previous response recommends an easy fix that I won't post here as it's over my head--those interested can go to the article or maybe a more skilled Ubuntu user can comment here.

Sorry if this is off-topic or should've been posted elsewhere (or not at all?).

mike

p.s.  Another response says that "Ubuntu sets this to zero if you have wine installed."  I do have Wine installed; I'll post again if changing this setting affects Wine.

the problems after upgrading from 9.04:

1. can't turn off the stupid login sound at the login screen anymore - the menu option in prefs to do this is gone!

2. usernames are listed on the login screen.  this is a security problem.

3. on shutdown one of my laptops gets an error writing to some loopback device or something (iirc) and never shuts down

4. my laptop that connects to an external monitor has problems with the menu panels (they only show up on one of the two screens and i cant figure out an obvious way to fix that)

5. if i let the laptop that connects to an external monitor suspend when it resumes the settings for the monitor setup are lost.

6. first boot after upgrading resulted in an unusable system on one of my machines, fortunately after a poweroff/poweron it worked the next time.

the upside over 9.04:

- the nvidia driver seems to be a lot more stable than the one in 9.04 was, and you can do mpeg4 playback in hardware if you know the right command line mplayer incantation.

### Post by timzak on 2009-11-04
Overall a decent experience.  I like the upgraded packages and the default theme choices are much better.  I really like the new icon theme.

However, I had a number of issues, most that seem related to configuration options being taken away.  I hope Ubuntu doesn't dumb-down the OS so much that we are unable to tweak it like we used to (remember how they took the Updates icon away in 9.04?).

The first problem:  grub error 15.  This was on a clean install with two hard disks.  I installed on sdb, and had sda as a data drive.  It seems that the installer put grub on sda instead, and I had to follow a howto to transfer grub over to sdb.  Now I get a message each time I boot up telling me there's a problem with my swap partition.  I haven't been able to fix this yet, but it hasn't affected me other than seeing the message on bootup.

Other problems:
-Don't like that Services is gone. That was where I'd shave my memory footprint by disabling services I don't use.

-They completely changed Sound Preferences and I can't figure out how to get to all the settings I used to be able to control (like bass and treble) on my Soundblaster Live!.

-They disabled mousewheel control for changing desktops with Compiz. It took me a while but I figured out how to turn it back on.  It was a setting in compiz configuration settings that I never would have guessed without help.

-I did not like that there was a constant mail/envelope icon in the Indicator Applet. Apparently this is related to Evolution and Empathy, neither of which I use.  First I disabled Evolution's mail checker and that still did not get rid of it so I checked Empathy's settings.  I disabled the autostart feature in Empathy but it did not get rid of the envelope icon. Finally I had to uninstall Empathy as there seemed to be no setting in Empathy that turned the envelope icon off. I don't use Empathy so it was no loss, but annoying that I had to uninstall the app to get rid of the envelope icon.  Likewise, I got rid of the logout icon because it's so IM-centered it's silly.  I simply replaced with a regular old shutdown icon.  Why does Ubuntu assume everyone IM's all the time?

Those are my issues, but like I said in my first paragraph, overall I'm happy with it.  As long as they allow me to configure the defaults I don't like I will use Ubuntu.  If they ever force me to use it the way they think I should, (which it seems like they are moving towards based on the how things have been progressing since 9.04), and don't allow me to configure back to a sane setting for me, I'll have to find a new distro.

---

### Post by ohiomoto on 2009-11-04
> **Stufu said:**
> The live CD made problems (desktop was not displayed, or froze). I installed anyway, from the Alternate CD. First start: Login not possible to be frozen. 've Given up.

Notebook AMILO M7400 (Fujitsu Siemens)I had a similar issue with a clean install on of 9.04 on my Acer.  The system came with Vista and I was setting up a dual boot.  I ran the live CD and installed UNR.  Everything went perfect until I rebooted the system.  It wouldn't boot Ubuntu. I would just hang there.  I switched the SATA driver mode from AHCI to IDE in my BIOS and it ran perfectly.  (Of course Vista wouldn't boot then, so it reinstalled it under IDE.)  Seems like there might be an issue with AHCI driver support.  You can easily switch modes in your BIOS to find out if that is keeping Ubuntu from booting.

### Post by r-senior on 2009-11-04
I've voted flawless because the upgrade was - pretty much. Just pressed the button and let it go. It asked a couple of questions about overwriting config files: one was something I'd not heard of so I let it. MySql I kept the existing one because I knew I'd changed it.

Cleared out unwanted packages and everything came back up smoothly.

My only gripes are that:

(a) Alternate login screens are no longer available and there's no xdmcp remote login which I used to access a headless box that I've not upgraded.

(b) I use the Dust theme and had to upgrade the Firefox theme because the old one wasn't supported but I'm not going to class that as detracting from a flawless upgrade.

(c) I tried the beta Ubuntu One and eventually got parts of it going but it seemed so flaky that I've uninstalled it. Liked the idea though so I've installed Dropbox as an alternative until Ubunto One settles down and comes out of beta.

(d) It doesn't seem quicker to boot, despite that being cited as a benefit of Karmic. In fact it's probably slower than Jaunty. Suspend is still not working on my Toshiba laptop but I can live without that.
