### Post by RingoFyre on 2009-11-04
Stats first - 2.6.28-16-server (#55-Ubuntu SMP Tue Oct 20 20:37:10 UTC 2009) x86_64), Intel(R) Core(TM)2 CPU 6320 @ 1.86GHz, 3807MiB ram, nVidia Corporation D9M-20 [GeForce 9400 GT], Marvell Technology Group Ltd. 88E8001 Gigabit Ethernet Controller, Intel Corporation 82801H (ICH8 Family) HD Audio Controller, 3 sata (vista, storage, /home), 1 ide (/) and dvd (720gb all up).

I tried the dist-upgrade on Tues. night - apt gave me lots of dpkg errors after and my update was 859mb after I had apt-cdrom added both desktop and server cds! No dice as I've been shaped by my isp till Sat. So; clean install (I know I could've waited and updated but nearly a gig after adding cds and nearly 4 gig of backedup apt cache seemed a bit exessive).

On Wed.I installed to / (formatted to ext4) & kept /home. After reboot grub error 15 - being new to grub2 I fixed but then couldn't find menu.lst to edit later! I got no x at all (I even tried startx in single-user mode) so I tried my old xorg.conf but still to no avail. @ which point after a fwe contemplative beers I gave up.

So back to Jaunty. The nice thing being, after fresh install with backedup xorg.conf, sources.list, menu.lst, some home-made install scripts & said apt cache it took me less than 1/2 hr. to get back to where I was. Thank you rsync! On reflection - I think Mark and the Canonical team need to reconsider 6 mth (.10 buggy) releases and go back to an lts model @ least once a year - from memory hardy was excellent and intrepid not so good. I waited a long time for Lenny (Debian) but the wait was worth it as it installed correctly and hasn't stopped running since.

### Post by Pollywoggy on 2009-11-04
I upgraded my HP netbook from Jaunty to Karmic a few days ago with only minor problems.  Since it went fairly well, I backed up a few things on my desktop machine today, but did not back up all important files.  That was a mistake.  I took a chance and I lost some data because my home directory was encrypted with ecryptfs and during the upgrade, the installer crashed and I was left with an unbootable system.  I was unable to rescue it, so I reinstalled and left my home directory intact.  Still, I was unable to use the encrypted home directory and ended up having to do a full reinstall.  My most important files were backed up 2 weeks ago but I lost some data.  I should have taken an extra day and backed up everything.

So just because one system is upgraded easily, don't assume your other systems will upgrade gracefully.  That is the lesson I learned today.

### Post by luvr on 2009-11-04
> **ubunny said:**
> oh one thing that will bother you is the new login.  It has my login name in a list like windows, I hit enter, then type the password.  I would have preferred that the login name is not listed.  Oddly this is not an available option, so I have to stick with it.  Really dumb.
[SIZE="7"][COLOR="Sienna"]**Aha! Disabling Login User List!**[/COLOR][/SIZE]

[B]Fedora users are apparently struggling with this very same issue, and a Fedora forum user named *"daten"* posted [a workaround.]("http://forums.fedoraforum.org/showthread.php?p=1122586#post1122586")
If you run the following command, then the user list will no longer get displayed:[/B]
```
sudo gconftool-2 --direct --config-source xml:readwrite:/etc/gconf/gconf.xml.defaults --type bool --set /apps/gdm/simple-greeter/disable_user_list true
```
Quite the command--but at least, it works!

### Post by Sky Aisling on 2009-11-04
> **Sky Aisling said:**
> [CENTER]Lost My Beautiful Screen Resolution
when I upgraded to 9.10
[/CENTER]

This is my first post to this forum.  I am a beginner to Linux.
My machine is a **Toshiba Satellite 2805-S401** running **XUbuntu**.
Screen1 display is set at 1024 x 768. 
Yesterday, I upgraded from Jaunty Jackalope 9.04 to Karmic Koala 9.10.
The upgrade went fine, no issues.
However, with the new system, I have lost my beautiful screen resolution.  The images on screensaver, wallpaper and thumbnails in the picture folder are degraded.  The exception to this is when I use 'Image Viewer' to view pictures.   Image Viewer gives me clear crisp pictures.

The Karmic Koala system seems to work faster than Jaunty Jackelope but the imaging is so poor, that I'd rather have my beautiful pictures back than the speed.  

How to I get Jaunty Jackelope 9.04 back onto my computer?  Or is there a fix to the poor screen resolution in Karmic Koala 9.10?

Thank you for your consideration of my questions,

Sky Aisling

_Update on situation 11/04/09 pdt_
Apparently the upgrade changed the screen default depth from 24 to 16.  To correct, we forced xorg.conf back to screen default depth of 24.  All is well now.  Koala 9.10 is running faster than Jackelope 9.04 on this machine.  Images are beautiful again.
Sky Aisling

---

### Post by Sky Aisling on 2009-11-04
_Update on situation 11/04/09 pdt concerning losing image quality when changing from Jackelope 9.04 to Koala 9.10_ (See my previous post.)

Apparently the upgrade changed the screen default depth from 24 to 16. To correct, we forced xorg.conf back to screen default depth of 24.  Images are beautiful again. Koala 9.10 is running faster than Jackelope 9.04 on this machine.

**Toshiba Satellite 2805-S401** running **XUbuntu**

### Post by kelvinator on 2009-11-04
I had two experiences. The good one was an upgrade on a Dell Latitude D600 formerly running 9.04. Because of the size of the HD I did not have many non-9.04 applications installed so the upgrade was a breeze.

My desktop was another story. With a larger HD I had installed many, many applications over time. As a result the upgrade set was much larger and it bombed mid-stream leaving the HD in read-only mode. From the forum I found the command (which I now can't recall) that allowed me to restart the install from the command line. After the installation completed the machine still gave problems - forcing me to boot in recovery mode. One of the boots forced a HD check but I had to run startx from the command prompt to start X Windows. After two reboots the machine sorted itself out.

Lessons Learned
1. BACKUP BACKUP BACKUP. I now own and have used an external 
   backup device.
2. Clean up your installation prior to an upgrade by removing
   applications that you do not use or need. That way there will
   be fewer things to upgrade/configure.

### Post by eMJayy on 2009-11-05
I moved to Ubuntu 9.10 last weekend using the upgrade path. It took a while to download the packages but once that part was done it was smooth sailing. I was worried that it might change my tv card configuration settings that I had manually set, but it didn't.  Everything works, it's fast, and the new sound applet and settings have made this version awesome for me. It now allows stereo audio to be played back in 4 channels in a 5.1 surround sound setup (the two front speakers and the 2 rear speakers) instead of just 2 front channels. Not even Windows can do that without third party software. I haven't tested all of my software yet, but so far the ones that I got through PPA work just fine. A few months down the line, I'll do a fresh install to start using Grub2 and ext4.

### Post by ChrisWebb on 2009-11-05
I seem to have resolved my issues with nautilius, empathy, totem and software-center crashing (among others).

Either launch Synaptic (System - Administration - Synaptic Package Manager) and REMOVE "ubuntu-desktop", click "Apply", then INSTALL "ubuntu-desktop" and click "Apply" or

```

sudo apt-get remove ubuntu-desktop
sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop

```It seems you need to remove, then install. Marking for re-installation doesn't work.

---

### Post by mikechant on 2009-11-05
Did a fresh install of 9.10 yesterday alongside my existing 9.04.

PC: Dell Inspiron 530 2.2Ghz Core2Duo with 2Gb RAM, Nvidia 8300GS graphics card, Internal 500Gb and 1Tb SATA HDs.

Installed from 32 bit desktop CD.
First: Backed up existing root, home and data partitions in case partitioning screwed up (even though I'm not touching these partitions at this point!)

Install process - no problems, 12mins from clicking 'install' to being ready for initial boot. Installed initially to single ext4 partition, will switch in existing /home later after testing completed. Installed grub2 to new partition NOT MBR as I'm not enitrely happy with some of its differences. Manually updated menu.lst for legacy grub in 9.04 install to add 9.10 options.

Testing:
First boot fine. Applied all maintenance and activated Nvidia 185 version driver.

Rebooted OK, desktop effects now enabled. Set effects to extra, wobbly windows etc. working.

Wired network connection to Cable Modem via router working - tested with Firefox OK. (No wireless card on this machine).

Printer - HP 880C inkjet autodetected and working OK for Firefox print.

Scanner - CannonScan N620 autodetected and working OK with Xsane.

SD card - Plugged in directly in SD slot and indirectly via USB adaptor - automounted both partitions in both cases OK

External harddrive - Plugged in, automounted, verified workiing at full USB2.0 speed.

Installed flash plugin with synaptic - youtube OK including sound (note sound was muted initially but this was shown in the status bar so it was pretty obvious).

Installed Gparted, verified secondary harddrive recognized and partition layouts exactly as expected.

Boot time (grub selection to logon screen) : 9.04 25s 9.10 20s
- 20% speed up.

Tests still to do:
CD/DVD ripping/burning
Video/Audio playback for various formats incl restricted stuff.
Suspend/Resume
NTFS partition access.

Summary:
Perfect so far apart from volume being set muted initially!
:D

---

### Post by Svartalf on 2009-11-05
Well, as an update:

NBR install to a eeePC 701: Success.

My install "problem" as reported earlier in the threads was due to me having an SD card in the slot (causing it to exceed the count of total drives by one, since I had to boot from something USB stick or portable CD/DVD burner...)- NICE.

Whatever they've done to cause it to boggle with more than two disks installed (something that appears to have been reported in the betas, mind...) and then not correct the problem makes me wonder what else slipped through the cracks there...  

It's fast and fairly responsive so far- ***after*** it boots.  Boot times from 9.04 to 9.10 have slipped back close to the 8.10/eeeBuntu boot speeds (over a minute, where 9.04 did it in 30 seconds, only slightly longer than the stock ASUS install took.).  There's another one that I've got to wonder what they did and what they let slip past their quality process there.

### Post by jelofson on 2009-11-05
Performed my fresh install over top of 9.04 on an older dell dimension desktop (P4). I installed after running the live cd for a bit to make sure everything was working properly. I used the Install Ubuntu icon on the desktop. 

Install was quick. The slideshow was a nice touch. There were hints  that certain codecs etc could not be installed, but no explicit mention to that fact or how to solve it. I think the community documentation has that issue covered anyway. 

After install, I restarted to my new 9.10. Update manager found a bunch of updates and I plowed through those quickly. Everything worked perfectly. I am not running wireless, nor am I running the proprietary nVidia driver for my graphics card. In the past, the proprietary driver has had problems detecting the native resolution of my monitor, so I haven't bothered trying again. Ubuntu did alert that closed-source drivers were available.

Empathy works well, from what I can tell. I haven't given it a workout yet. 

The notifications are nice, though they vanish when I put my mouse over them. Not sure if that is intentional or not.

I set up Ubuntu One. That was easy. I think the Ubuntu One web page could be slicked up a bit (fonts and layout). I set up Notes synchronization to Ubuntu one. This worked quite well, but I had to type in the name of the computer I was syncing to. It didn't detect that automatically for me. 

Suspend works well. Haven't tried hibernate. 

Firefox installed the Adobe flash plugin nicely. Gave me three plugin options. 

The icons and interface are very nice. Best yet. I also like the new login screen. 

Software Centre worked fine for me. No complaints. 

Overall, very happy with it.

---

### Post by muecker on 2009-11-05
This was a smooth, easy upgrade on the two machines I have upgraded so far.  I have been very impressed with the results.  I do have three mostly minor issues on Karmic that I have yet to find a satisfactory solution for.

First is VMware 2.0.2 server on x64.  There are workarounds to most of the issues but I blame VMware, not Karmic because they still haven't added support for the latest kernels.  A couple of months ago I upgraded my kernel from 2.6.28 to 2.6.29 and it still isn't supported under VMware server 2.0.2, which was released a week ago.  The workarounds on the web have been available for months.  Now there are additional problems with GTK added to the mix and I cannot restart vmware without restarting my machine because one (or more) of the modules will not unload from the kernel.

The second issue is the notifications on my laptop.  When running Empathy on my laptop yesterday I got crash notices any time somebody signed on or off.  I don't get that on my x64 desktop.  There is a bug that has been open for a couple of weeks and not yet fixed in Karmic.

Third, Evolution is still just as unstable as ever when connecting to Exchange.  I am lucky enough to be on a server supported by the older Exchange plugin, but that is still a hacked solution that sometimes works.  Evolution crashed when I tried to enable the MAPI connector and I had to manipulate the data files directly to get it to even start again.

### Post by Pollywoggy on 2009-11-05
I have experienced considerable difficulty installing karmic.  I am going to download another iso and try again but it appears the best way to do it is NOT to use update-manager.  It appears the best way to get a system that does not break is to upgrade the packages on the command line with apt-get or aptitude.  The graphical utility for upgrading the system broke my installs several times.  The only error message I saw (one time) was that sudo had crashed.  I was then left with an unbootable system. ### Post by magnificent_tears on 2009-11-05
I installed Jaunty a few months back.  After some tweaks I was relatively happy with it, though there were some issues that extremely disappointed me, considering that these things worked on my previous linux install of Fedora Core 3 (I've been using FC3 for 5 years... in 2008 I built my own kernel to leverage newer video drivers so I could use Skype w/ a webcam).  So when I installed this brand-new Ubuntu and all these things that used to work on my hideously old distro were now broken it was disappointing, but i either found a fix or got used it to it.

I was very happy with Jaunty's boot time; amazed at how quickly my computer was up.

I upgrade to Karmic and now am very sad.  It's so slow.
Boot takes 2, maybe 3 times as long.  I usually only boot once a day, in the morning, so that's the kind of thing I can overlook.  But the worst thing ever is the unresponsiveness of my X.  It's great that my GL screensavers stopped their hideous flickering, but when I press Alt-Tab it takes a few seconds for the window-switcher to show up, then it takes even longer for it to go away.  Or worse, sometimes it doesn't go away until I press a key (which then gets sent to the app I should have been switched to).
That is absolutely the worst.  Talk about a productivity killer.

My audio is flaky, now, too.  Sometimes I hear sound from multiple apps, sometimes one takes over.  Sometimes I lose all my sinks and I can't hear anything.  I have three different mixers I have to control now b/c they all seem to be separately muted everytime I boot up.  And the notifications (which were beautiful on Jaunty) suck here too.  If I press my volume up or down a few times, it'll be 5 minutes before all the notifications have eventually displayed (b/c one waits for the next).

And despite the fact that I use a downloaded firefox instead of the apt version I have extensions that complain and I still have that dumb ubuntu extension.

Oh, and one day my X session was essentially hijacked b/c it started sending the enter key indefinitely.  I had to ssh in with a remote machine and kill gdm (and then of course i had other problems and had to reboot).

Years ago when I was on windows I frequently wished i had an OS "undo" button.  I don't recall ever needing one of those in Linux until now.  If I could "undo" and go back to Jaunty I would.

I guess what that means is that I'll try a fresh Karmic and see if that's any better.  If not I I'll do a fresh Jaunty (again).  As if I had the time to install 2 new operating systems.

Until I have the time for that I'll /try/ to get something done.

---

### Post by witeshark17 on 2009-11-05
I see that there were issues using the GUI (update manager) to upgrade to 9.10 from 9.04. I chose that method, and the only issue I found was failed to boot, having this on screen:

Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic (recovery mode)
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.28-16-generic
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.28-16-generic (recovery mode)

I get this error:error: unknown command 'initrd'

type "e" to edit to find (and write down, no cut and paste!) a number like:

UUID=73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333  <(example)

then


Ctrl-C (it may be just C !!) to enter the GRUB command line
2. Type in ```
*insmod linux*
``` and press ENTER

*```
linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.30-10-generic root=UUID=
```*```
73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333
``` where  **_73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333_**   < is unique to your system (found after hitting "e" to edit in the boot options list)

then

```
initrd /boot/initrd.img-_**2.6.31-14**_-generic
```where 2.6.31-14  <is your current (latest) kernel

```
boot
```   And after booting doing a 

```
sudo grub-install dev/sda
``` (where dev/sda denotes your target drive)

I will post more details and corrections as I put this together after a lot of Google!

Please post any inaccuracies as since I can no long recreate the scenario, I post this from memory!

### Post by mmand on 2009-11-05
There needed to be a poll option between "worked but had few things to fix, nothing serious though" and "got many problems that i've not been able to solve".

I had a LOT of problems with my install, but I've fixed most of them.

I made six coasters trying to get a working CD. Downloaded the image four times on two different computers (and three different OSes). First and third time I downloaded the image I used browser and torrent (on Ubuntu using my dual-boot machine), respectively. The second I used someone else's Windows computer to download via browser. The last time I downloaded via browser on Windows using my dual-boot machine. None of the six burned CDs worked. I ended up downloading the DVD image and FINALLY got a working disc.

During install I nuked my old 8.10 install, so it wasn't an "upgrade". I've had enough problems with Ubuntu's "upgrade" procedure, that I don't ever do it again.

After installing (which took twice as long as normal, it seemed...) I had no problems until I started loading the software I use every day. The Ubuntuzilla version of Thunderbird gave me all kinds of errors, but it seems the version in whichever repository doesn't. There was that problem fixed, I guess.

The other problems I have I guess are more of a personal-usage type than anything else. I ended up ditching Empathy for Pidgin. Empathy probably works for 99% of users, but I like having more control over my IM software than I was able to have with Empathy. Same goes for Evolution and Transmission. Ditched for Thunderbird and Deluge, respectively.

One problem I haven't found a fix for is how to turn off the "shutdown/restart/etc." notices. I just TOLD the system I want to restart/shutdown/logoff, I don't need to confirm, and if I walk away, I DON'T want to come back halfway through a 60 second timer. I also cannot change the way my name displays on that applet. I don't need to know who I am logged in as, I'm the only one that uses this computer. I also know my own name, thank you very much.

Overall, I should have just upgraded to 9.04 instead of 9.10. At least my 9.04 desktop doesn't have as many problems as this 9.10 install does.

I'd give Karmic 4 out of 10 stars, because at least it looks pretty while being crap.

---

### Post by the_maplebar on 2009-11-05
Clean install on my Dell D820 with NVidia graphics and Broadcom wireless.

Install is working but had some problems I had to fix.  I tried the Live CD a few times and everything looked great, recognized all my hardware and proprietary drivers.  After I did a clean install 9.10 did not find the drivers for my video card or wireless network.  Frustrating thing was that I had no wired network to fall back on.  I booted back into the Live CD and searched around, figured out I could install the wireless drivers using apt-get install bcmwl-kernel-sources from the apt-repository on the CD.  After getting the wireless working, and doing updates 9.10 finally recognized my nvidia card and prompted me to install the NVidia driver.

Overall it wouldn't have been too bad except that I didn't have a network connection and it was frustrating that the install did not work as well as the Live CD.

### Post by taygan on 2009-11-05
BEST UPGRADE EVER!  Super smooth.  Followed standard procedure and removed 3rd party repositories, updated everything on 9.04 and then got the alternate install disk off bittorrent (save the servers and get it quicker!).

Rebooted 9.04, stuck the 9.10 alternate install cd in the drive, and let it access the internet.  It pulled an additional 350 Megs of files - I'm assuming it updated all the extra stuff I had installed that was not on the original CD.

Rebooted, adjusted the sound to analog output and EVERYTHING has been working for a week!  (well, I guess my firefox search engines were out of order.)

Load times are maybe a little faster, but shutdown only takes a few seconds.  Wow.

(Gateway M-1625 dual boot with secondary Vista home)

### Post by skyiscrying on 2009-11-05
Not impressed at present.  At first I upgraded from Jaunty and the main problem there was that the system would not turn off the computer on **shutdown**. So when the official release came out I downloaded the CD and I am a bit suspicious about this because it might NOT have been the right one. 

It burned ok in K3b and then I did a complete install giving it the whole 80 GB hard drive. Karmic went ok for a while. it did seem mighty faster than the ugrade I had been using. But then it crashed when I started to use Devede. Then it seemed that all went haywire. 

I thought it might have been the Ext4 file system. I re-installed, using Ext3 and doing my own partitions  - just the three - 10 GB for root at the begining, 2 GB for a swap area at the end, and the remainder was /home.

Once again the Devede program caused havoc. Well, it went into a panic and said I was running out of room. So I have to ask myself did I utterly confuse the hard drive somehow?  Or did I not re-format the partitions when changing to EXt3?  

So at present I am using LinuxMint -Gloria.  I never lost any vital data. I will give it another go starting with a fresh downloaded ISO from Ubuntu's download site.

### Post by Piscium on 2009-11-05
Good Karmic things first. 

Compared with Jaunty, Firefox leaks way less memory, though still leaks, but perhaps it is not Firefox that leaks but some of the plugins like Flash, who knows.

With Jaunty I suffered the infamous Intel graphics woes. The situation improved hugely with Karmic, though it is still not as good as Windows XP playing Flash.

Bad things.

After waking up from suspend I was getting every time an Apport report of a kernel failure. This was "fixed" a few days later with the update that disables reporting of kernel oops. So the issue was not fixed but it is just not reported. Still I am happy that that kernel issue seems to be harmless.

Grub 2 is quite bad, that is, the version that comes with Karmic (1.97 beta4). I had been using Grub 2 with Jaunty for 3 months (1.96), so I was quite used to it and knew what was normal and what was not. 

Grub 2 in Karmic definitely has issues. Quite a few people have problems. In my case there was a lot of disk thrashing between the BIOS splash screen disappearing and the Grub menu appearing. 

My solution to this problem was uninstalling Grub with Synaptic, then going to the Grub website and downloading the latest release version (1.97). Then I built it and installed it and it works fine. It would be good if Ubuntu would update the Grub 2 package. 

Mind you, I don't know if the issue in the Karmic version is in the upstream beta4 or in the packaging done by Debian or Ubuntu. What I know is that the version from the Grub website installs files in different directories and has different templates and it worked for me without making any change to the defaults.

### Post by ebozzz on 2009-11-05
I always use the upgrade method first on one PC, try that for a few days and then I make a decision on whether I should install the new release on all of my PCs. I guess that i just want to see how much progress has been made with the upgrade process first. 

The upgrade went well but as in previous upgrades, some things did not work as well after it was completed. On this one I had some problems with flash that were remedied by performing a re-installation of flash.

After playing with it for a few days, I decided to perform a clean install on the same PC as well as one other one as a dual-boot configuration (XP). The installs went went well but would not boot into Ubuntu. I received an error (Grub: Error: No Such Device Grub Recovery) and decided to try again. This time I used GParted and configured the partitions that were targeted for Ubuntu prior to initiating the install process. This time everything completed just fine and I successfully booted into linux on both machines. The funny thing is that I could access XP on both after receiving the error. 

The only other quirk that i have noticed so far after the clean installs is a problem that I had changing the password of one of my users. I setup the users via Users and Groups (System > Administration > Users and Groups). All attempts to change the password using that GUI failed and the old password was retained. I then opted to use the terminal (Applications > Accessories > Terminal) to attempt the changes. I ran the following command...

```
sudo passwd *username*
``` 

and I was able to successfully complete the needed changes.

### Post by lian1238 on 2009-11-05
Fresh install.

Got things working after installing the nvidia drivers from recovery mode. After the install, the screen was flashing at a black screen login prompt.

Overall, the software improvements are great, but the nvidia driver really messed up my install.

I like the following:
The icon theme is great!
The software center
Option the set ctrl+alt+backspace in keyboard options
Google gadgets!
The (almost) latest Cairo-dock in the software center, woohoo!
(excuse me if I listed some things already in 9.04, I had to skip that upgrade.)

I have the following problems: (excuse me if it sounds like a rant.., it IS, but not in any particular direction)
Had to install nvidia drivers from the commandline.. a new user would've been put off by this.
Was greeted with a six-screen problem.. needed a line in xorg.conf
cannot end the session without restarting.. the screen would be shaking after a logout, X restart, etc.
Sound cracks when choosing just the left or right channel completely.
Some program added to Startup keep disappearing after a reboot.. (what's the point of "startup applications", again?)
The screen would freeze for 5 seconds once in a while.

Minor problems:
The pointer capture applet would sometimes release the pointer (while still "locked").
Double-clicking on the sound icon doesn't open the preference window.. that IS a big deal!
All downloads going to "Downloads".. I prefer the Desktop.
Rhythmbox keeps showing the wrong album cover with in the.. what do you call it.. osd-notify? (and how the heck do you configure it..?)
Screen brightness applet is inconsistent in getting the brightness.

System (Laptop): Lenovo Y550
NVIDIA GT 240M (works with minor annoyances with the latest drivers)
Network controller: Intel Corporation PRO/Wireless 5100 AGN [Shiloh] Network Connection (works well)
Audio device: Intel Corporation 82801I (ICH9 Family) HD Audio Controller (rev 03) (works perfectly)

### Post by QIII on 2009-11-05
Fresh install on several machines went flawlessly.

Separate /home partitions required a bit of realigning of the Firefox preference folders, since they were previously in the Shiretoko folder.  Similar with Thunderbird stuff.

I would say that things like that might frustrate newer users, though.

I was glad to read that Catalyst 9.10 had been included, because one of the machines has an ATI GPU.  Instaling CCC from Synaptic worked fine, and that machine has better graphic performance than it has ever had.

I would like to see the separate /home partition be the default for a new installation -- while allowing the user to change that, of course.  That would make the experience of a fresh install easier for a newcomer and possibly avoid some of the aggravation some people seem to be having with the upgrade.

### Post by mnoyes on 2009-11-06
Did two upgrades and one install:

Upgraded from 9.04 to rc2, then to 9.1 on a dell m1330 laptop. Had to fix a few things -- get sound set up right for skype, enable ibus instead of scim, remove network manager and install wicd, things like that. Basically a pretty easy upgrade.

Going from 9.04 to 9.1 remix on my dell mini9 was terrible. Tried upgrading but had trouble with wireless and touchpad preferences and suspend, etc. So, I tried a clean install from a usb stick.

Install was easy but problems persist -- have spent days scouring the forums and trying out solutions but still no wireless and computer freezes when I shut the lid and later reopen (used to sleep like a baby with 9.04). 

My conclusion after reading through the forums entries on networking and wireless is that this release was premature -- or should have come with clearer warnings: i.e., don't upgrade/install this if you are using a dell mini9...(or specific hardware -- broadcom wireless card, etc.)

I would not recommend upgrading for anyone but the tech savvy -- I'm no expert, but I can't imagine how frustrated a new user would be -- it would be enough to turn them off ubuntu forever.

### Post by JHCKX on 2009-11-06
My upgrade from 9.04 to 9.10 went badly, my computer (a Packard Bell EasyNote MZ38) wouldn't boot *unless* I attached an external keyboard and mouse. Even then the system was unstable, programs kept freezing. But a subsequent clean install didn't work either, I had the same problem with booting. Just my hardware I guess but it's a very common laptop, at least in the Netherlands. I re-installed 9.04, it always worked just fine.

I've read that there are often problems with new versions of Ubuntu. But in that case, Synaptics should not offer the upgrade option for the first few weeks until most of the bugs are ironed out. What would also be useful are instructions for test installing. The first step isn't hard, install a new version on a new partition (the install program does that by default). Then you can select which version to use using Grub.

Copying files and settings to the test version is fairly easy, cp -R, then chown -R

Installing programs, that can be done using dpkg --get-installed

The biggest issue is when you want to keep just the new install and reclaim the space on the partition for the old install. That's not to hard in itself, boot an install cd, use gparted, delete the old partition and enlarge the new. But that will mess up grub, that's not hard to fix but you need to know what to do.

Some good instructions along these lines could help people install without burning their bridges behind them. You could try a new (or beta) version, trash it if it doesn't work, switch safely if it does.

### Post by steff123 on 2009-11-06
After upgrading to 9.10 the boot-disk disapeared from '/dev/disk/by-uuid' and the internet went down the drain. Well, wireless is working and the home-network is visible, also the gateway but dialing out is not possible.

What I did:

I could re-configure GRUB to use /dev/sda1 instead of the UUID (I suspect UDEV is the misdemeanant), but I'm still working on the internet. I suspect that AVAHI bothers me quite bad because 'resolv.con' doesn't take manual configurations (like /etc/hosts) because changes disappear after boot. Don't know yet if I'm gonna reckon that as a feature or a bug!

Alas!

Cheers,

### Post by SteveF48 on 2009-11-06
The upgrade went smoothly, except that it forced me to repartition my hard drive. Why couldn't it just overwrite 9.04?
It wasn't a big problem, because to be honest I don't use my Sony Vaio with Ubuntu for any serious work. That may upset a few Linux fanatics, but I've only been using Linux for a couple of years and find it too difficult to sort out problems.
For example:
I managed to persuade wireless networking to work, for a while, then Ubuntu displayed "enter password for default keyring to unlock". My login password doesn't do anything, so I'm completely stuffed. I only installed 9.10 on Thusday, so I'm hardly likely to have forgotten. I never put a password in for any keyring.
Tried just pressing return, that didn't work. Googled and searched the forums, for answers. There are something like 2800 results.
The first few suggested using a password manager accessory, but that didn't display the option they mentioned to change the relevant password, but they were dated 2005-6. That indicates that this issue has been around for a very long time. Others suggested killing the daemon and deleting default password, so I tried that.
What a mistake, now wireless networking a) doesn't see my network, just all of my neighbours networks b) sits there forever doing nothing.
Oh well I'll re-install 9.10 (there's nothing Karmic about it!) and see whether one of the other 2799 results has a better effect.

I'd love to be able to convert to Ubuntu on all my PCs and persuade my friends to use it, BUT it seems to have picked up all of the vices of Windoze Vista in its latest incarnation.

### Post by nadian on 2009-11-06
I've been a Windows user for over 12 years and finally got the confidence to try a liinux distro. Everyone recognises Ubuntu and the one to start with so I installed 9.10 on a partition with Windows 7.
I wanted to write my experiences from 2 points of view. I have taught ICT for over 10 years and have lots of experience supporting users so I'd like to give my perspective and the perspective of my semi imaginary friend [COLOR=Navy]Bill[/COLOR]. [COLOR=Navy]Bill[/COLOR] is an average windows user who can use the internet, install progams but no expert.
[B]
WEEK 1[/B]

Installation-
Me: little confused by partitioning process due to different labeling of drives in ubuntu, stumbled through this and install went ok

[COLOR=Navy]Bill: Wouldn't know what partitioning is but could probably do a fresh install from cd[/COLOR]

Setup -
Me: First issue no idea where to find 'terminal' or how to install/unistall programs luckily I am enough of a gerdneek to trawl forums for help which got me started

[COLOR=Navy]Bill: Stuck, would not trawl through forums (hasn't ever used them and would not have the patience). Needs installed guidance and wizards to get him started. Would be confused by need for password to mount drives access synaptic e.t.c

I thought about how this could work and created a web page to illustrate what could be done (the page is very simple and only covers 1 function in ubuntu - also various technical problems mean it lacks sound and annotation on the video)
[http://www.gwilkinson4.btinternet.co.uk/](http://www.gwilkinson4.btinternet.co.uk/)
[/COLOR]
Issues - 
Me: No sound, lots of forum trawling and trying sudo this and that until i found a page that worked 
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1043568](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1043568)
most programs work well but looking for a replacement for mediamonkey proved tricky - tried songbird but ended up having to learn how to write a script file that would allow it to load. Settled on Amarok after various others, but no coverflow, no good tag editing album cover download facilities (album cover download crude compared to mediamonkey) otherwise ok.
Spent a long time looking for good screen recorder tried xvidcap, recordmyscreen,wink and then video editing packages to get what I wanted see these threads for how I'm geting on
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1316096](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1316096)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1315689](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1315689)
(apologies for crossposting)
Tried komposer for editing HTML and CSS but ...settled on quanta plus instead. However not totally happy with its CSS editiing and upload features, it'll do for now.
[COLOR=Navy]
Bill: Sound issues, wouldn't have any idea where to start (might even go back to windows!!) However if the was some kind of bug/issues email that he could sign up for when he first installs Ubuntu that might help. (i.e weekly of bugs and issue discovered and links to forum solutions). Bill would not be comfortable with 'terminal' interface, he would expect and GUI wizard that takes him step by step through issues, solutions and suggestions.
Music he would use default media player, but would be disappointed with its features (he's used to itunes)
He wouldn't want to screen record of create webpages so he would be OK there.[/COLOR]

[COLOR=Black]Thats it for now.[/COLOR]

### Post by pbrane on 2009-11-06
I upgraded from Jaunty to Karmic. The upgrade went flawlessly. The only thing is I use nVidia graphics driver so I edited my xorg.conf to use the vesa driver before upgrading. After the upgrade I removed the nVidia driver, which I should have done BEFORE the upgrade. But after the upgrade I installed the latest nVidia driver using envyng and everything went smoothly. The only issue I see is the DNS lookup seems to be slow on some types of routers. My home router (Cisco/Linksys) works fine. My work router (2Wire) has problems, VERY slow lookups. The difference is in /etc/resolv.conf. With the Linksys router the only entries are the nameserver IP addresses, the 2Wire nameserver IP is the router itself. I started using OpenDNS to get around this. Not sure what changed in Network-Manager, it worked perfectly on Jaunty. 

I'm very happy with this new release. I have been using GNU/Linux systems for over 10 years now and since about 1999 I haven't had any other OS on my personal computers. I started with MSDOS around 1991 and moved to Windows after that, I was writing software at the time (signal processing app) and wanted a better graphical system to use. When I came across a GNU/Linux system I pretty much dropped slow, clunky Windows.

### Post by decoherence on 2009-11-06
I have upgraded to Karmic on four machines. Three of them went flawlessly. The fourth hit a hiccup when I switched the KVM it is attached to to another computer. When I switched back, Ubuntu wouldn't pick up the mouse or keyboard.

With no way to provide input (ssh wasn't running, either) I had to hard-reset it. It booted in to a command line at which point I ran dpkg --configure -a, finished the dist-upgrade and rebooted. Everything is working fine, now.

I wonder what we can do about these aborted upgrades? Sure, it's a fringe situation, and nobody really expects a computer to work properly if it gets killed in the middle of an upgrade, but surely there can be some kind of automation as the fix is so simple.

I'd also like to know about the USB not getting picked up when I switched my KVM. Unfortunately I haven't got the time to try and reproduce it, I didn't take very good notes when it happened and (of course) it works perfectly now.

Anyway, there is always room for improvement, but the Karmic upgrade for me has been pretty pain-free.

### Post by jstalnak on 2009-11-06
My upgrade was a disaster (I'm typing this logged into a Win7 computer). The upgrade started fine. But after one of the prompts related to restarting apache2 the usb subsystem failed and I had no input from keyboard or mouse. I had no option but to hard reboot. DISASTER!!! Next boot failed at mounting partitions.  I eventually had to boot from a LiveCD, use a terminal to mount my partitions (and note that my /home dir is an encrypted partition), mount -o bind /dev, etc. to get a working /root, then chroot to get the upgrade to run to conclusion. Of course it was completely hosed (I got a bazillion dependency errors) since I was doing a kludged chroot environment. I was eventually able to get to a normal boot process with the new karmic version, but the prompt in the boot process that asks for the encrypted partition's passphrase is behaving oddly -- I get the prompt, but seconds later I get console output a mount in /dev/fstab cannot yet be mounted and the system goes into hyperslow mode. The cursor disappears and I cannot use the keyboard to enter text for minutes at a time. Very frustrating. After nearly a dozen reboots the last time, just a few minutes ago, the passphrase prompt acted as it used to and I was able to type it in and press RETURN and the boot continued normally. I get finally to a working desktop, but I get continued program crash reports (dbus, gnome-settings, etc.) Networking seems to be broken as I cannot send the crash reports getting a 'can't connect to crash database' error.  I just logged out and into a failsafe term and ifup/downed eth0 and networking seems to be restored doing so.

I'm online in Win7 looking to see if there is a way to force a reinstall of the upgraded packages.

NOT a pleasant process and it surely did not 'just work' :-\

### Post by Lateralis on 2009-11-06
I've had a variety of problems on my laptop after upgrading from Jaunty to Karmic; some have been solved, one in particular hasn't.  

I'm generally very happy with Karmic.  Cosmetically it is a little better than Jaunty, the Bluetooth support for my laptop has improved and lots of little things seem better.  

But, that being said, I have experienced problems such as

* USB flash drives and external hard drives did not auto mount despite them doing so quite happily in Jaunty.  Latest udev upgrade from the proposed repository has (apparently) sorted that out. 

* I had some intermittent sound card driver issues which I now believe to be sorted.  After I upgraded, my sound sometimes worked... sometimes didn't.  It seems happier now, but time will tell.

* My display options are borked.  In Jaunty I could happily dual monitor display with my laptop and an external 19" monitor.  Jaunty was flexible enough to allow me to configure it the way I wanted it.  Now the options for the external 19" monitor are rubbish and makes it unusable.  This is still an ongoing issue and no solution - satisfactory or otherwise - has been proposed.  For me this is a big issue.  

* I did a fresh install of Karmic on my mother's PC over the weekend.  Whilst we were configuring everything for her, I noticed that she didn't have all of the screensavers that were available under Jaunty and available on my laptop and desktop post-upgrade from Jaunty to Karmic.  Why have some of our favourite screensavers - spirograph and GLeidoscope for instance - been removed?  

Finally, what I don't like is the subtle rearrangement of GUI menus and options that I had gotten so used to in Jaunty.  In this respect, I feel that Canonical have done a bit of a "Microsoft" by faffing around with menus - moving and getting rid of options - that were fine as they were before.  It is a trend that has crept in in the last 5 years or so and I *detest* it.  I fail to see why there is a need to change things for change's sake; if it ain't broke, don't fix it.    


But that all aside, I am quite happy with Karmic.  As I've said elsewhere I am always astounded that such good operating systems are available to the public for free and that people from all over the world contribute to the development of Linux distros out of love and passion than for the salary.  I will forever be grateful to all of the developers involved in making Ubuntu but there still exist a few peculiarities and issues that sometimes makes the Ubuntu experience just a tad irritating.

---

> **witeshark17 said:**
> I see that there were issues using the GUI (update manager) to upgrade to 9.10 from 9.04. I chose that method, and the only issue I found was failed to boot, having this on screen:

Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic (recovery mode)
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.28-16-generic
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.28-16-generic (recovery mode)

I get this error:error: unknown command 'initrd'

type "e" to edit to find (and write down, no cut and paste!) a number like:

UUID=73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333  <(example)

then


Ctrl-C (it may be just C !!) to enter the GRUB command line
2. Type in *insmod linux* and press ENTER

*linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.30-10-generic root=UUID=*     73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333

where  **_73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333**_   < is unique to your system (found after hitting "e" to edit in the boot options list)

then

initrd /boot/initrd.img-_**2.6.30-10**_-generic

where 2.6.30-10  <is your current (latest) kernel

boot And after booting doing a 
sudo grub-install (target drive)

I will post more details and corrections as I put this together after a lot of Google!

Please post any inaccuracies as since I can no long recreate the scenario, I post this from memory! Here is my Grub issue solved, maybe it will help

### Post by silvagroup on 2009-11-06
gianlucawork - Try installing the Nvidia drivers. That will probably relsolve your problem.
I haven't done a complete up grade yet, because I noticed that many of the kernels from 2.6.27-11 had problems with my laptop hardware.

Tried LivCD of Keramic and still had some issues on my laptop.

I suggest one try the LiveCD first make sure you can get your system working on the LiveCD, and if that works go for it. If it doesn't wait a few months and most major bugs should be worked out by then.

I typically just wait six months before doing an upgrade and it is pretty painless with just some minor twaeks to do.

### Post by Johnr123 on 2009-11-06
Upgraded yesterday. It went fine except for a few minor glitches. The upgrade was fairly fast, having waited for a few days before attempting :-)

**The only glitches were:**


[LIST]
[*] ThunderBird wouldn't work because Karmic deleted a required file for ThunderBird. Found the info with a bit of Googleing, installed the file and solved that issue.
[*] The restart after upgrade failed with a black screen. A manual restart solved that.
[*] Suspend works (the swap file is a good size), but the suspend (sleep) keyboard button does not any more. I've tried resetting it, but no go. All other keyboard shortcuts work.
[*]My Logitech quickcam pro 9000 doesn't work now. I haven't sorted that out yet.
[/LIST]
[B]Noticeable improvements:

[/B]
[LIST]
[*]I now have full support for my HP Deskjet 930c printer and HP scanjet 5300c scanner. I had this support in 8.10 (I think that was the number), but it vanished in Jaunty. I could never set hplip utilty up properly with those pieces. The printing was super-slow. now it's normal and I also have a good "draft" setting again.
[*]Performance seems faster on the net, though some of this may be due to my upgrading FireFox using the UbuntuZilla python script so I can use FireFox 3.5.5 (and get update notices for FireFox and ThunderBird to keep them at the latest Mozilla version)
[*]I like the new splash screen and the desktop options. I usually use my own photos, but right now I'm using that new orange screen. I like it.
[*]I'm thankful that my apache2 server, mysql, and php are still all getting along well together and serving up my local sites flawlessly.
[*]I haven't yet dug into all the features, but I'm sure I'll find more to enjoy.
[/LIST]
Highly reccomended,

Best,
John

### Post by tz1 on 2009-11-06
I did a long blog post and many launchpad entries, but here is a summary:

1. The upgrader is still very stupid.  It will spin for 20 minutes and tell you it needs  4 more megabytes but doesn't let you retry.  If the network goes down (like the wireless at my hotel) it will end up with a broken upgrade which can neither be completed or restarted properly.  apt-get dist-upgrade from the command line seemed to produce a limping system that eventually finished.  But it should do the tests, and allow retry at any portion (The network came back up quickly, but not before hundreds of packages were "not found").  Also the upgrader seems to want to go to the network even with the CD in the drive and when I ask it not to slipstream updates.

2. Grub2's engine is great, but the underlying utilities are still alpha quality.  This would not be bad if they didn't try to use them.  For example, it will attempt detect EVERY os on every device even if you don't want it to and add them to the boot menu.  It won't find DOS partitions and some of what it does it won't work since the BIOS enumeration might be different (hd0 and hd1 might swap - getting my C:\ DOS partition on my usb sdhc drive to boot as DOS took over an hour - Grub2 isn't even documented properly - for the curious, all you need do in the menu entry is search for the correct UUID from "sudo blkid" and just do chainloader +1 - two commands but so obscured that it was nearly impossible to figure out).

3. X is half the speed and things like disabling don'tzap no longer work.  The theme changed and became either almost invisible, ugly, or both.  Bitstream Vera fonts disappeared (loads of fun - I found nice replacements though).

4. Another round removing the eye-candy, annoying graphics and animations.  They need a "console-me" metapackage.

5. Still has the network driver deadlock (You need a proprietary network driver!  Oh, let me download it now.  Oh, I can't because you don't have a network driver; let me check for a proprietary one which might fix This...).  Fortunately I did burn the CD.

6. Polkit-1 is broken - when mounting devices I had it set not to ask me under jaunty.  The annoyboxes popped up under Karmic.  The link under details does not open anything which allows it to be set, and it took me over an hour to find the magic file (/usr/share/polkit-1/actions/...) and edit it via sudo vim and on the 3rd attempt the boxes went away.  Any user can do it (if they had my phone number and their IP address)

On the plus side, more hardware is supported "out of the box" and it seems faster and smoother except for X.

For me, the big problem is even if I did the beta, I doubt they would have fixed ANY OF THIS.  They generally can't delay releases, don't have the resources to fix something like the grub2 utilities (or find out what is wrong with polkit), HAVEN'T FIXED THINGS WHICH STILL GO WRONG FROM EDGY!!!

They really need to stop so Lounging Lizard or whatever it is going to be called simply fixes everything outstanding - first in the upgraders - then in every package, utility, whatever, and only then Mushy Mouse can start again with the bleeding edge stuff.

For now, I would stay with / go back to Jaunty except for clean installs on simple, plain, vanilla setups, or hardware reasons.  Or be prepared to maybe have to spend 8 hours fixing and tweaking it after you do the install/upgrade.

---

### Post by aseriesoftubes on 2009-11-06
My Karmic experiences have been pretty poor so far.

On my netbook (MSI Wind U123), the version of netbook-launcher in Karmic is so slow as to make the machine unusable. I previously had UNR Jaunty installed on this machine, and it worked without a hitch. I'd like to try a clean install of Karmic, but I haven't had a chance to do that yet. 

On another machine (HP d530 midtower), internet access was painfully slow out of the box. I could ping external servers without issue, and I can use wget and apt-get with no problems, but if I fire up a web browser (I've tried Firefox, Epiphany, and lynx) pages load molasses-slow, or not at all. Installing bind9 and resolvconf helped a little bit, but speeds are still nowhere near what they were in Jaunty.

I have two more machines that are running Mythbuntu 9.04. Wife Acceptance Factor is critical with these machines. The buggy nature of Karmic, coupled with the fact that Mythbuntu 9.10 includes a pre-release version of MythTV, means there's no way in hell I'll be upgrading these two boxes (at least not for a while).

### Post by monton on 2009-11-06
Lets see, I installed KK - fresh install not upgrade - from 9.04 on a Gateway ML6230 laptop, Celeron M 1.6, 1.5GB RAM, and failing hard drive. New hard drive on the way but couldn't wait!
No problems what so ever. Noticeable increase in speed, quicker boot times (40sec from power on to wireless connect!) and better wireless connection 100% where the best I got before was 93%. Very pleased!
THEN new hard drive arrived I installed it and did a fresh install. BTW I am installing from a thumb drive. Flawless installation. Performed updates, installed wine, and when prompted activated**** the proprietary driver for the SmartLink internal modem.
Some time later I realized I had no sound and no hardware recognized. For three days I've tried various fixes suggested here to no avail. As I combed the threads someone, sorry I don't where the thread was and who posted it but they commented on a conflict with the moden driver. I gave that a whirl and deactivated the modem. Sound is back! Karmic Koala is great on this computer and made a noticeable performance boost - and rock solid so far.
Thanks to all those that work so hard on this fine product and helping people having problems.:D

### Post by ashinshanghai on 2009-11-06
My upgrade from 9.04 went smoothly, but now I am experiencing a number of problems that I never had before. I am on a Lenovo 3000 Y310, Intel Core 2 Duo 2.2 GHz, 2 GB ram, nVidia graphics card w/ version 185 proprietary driver, 160 gb hard drive.

1. Firefox 3.5 is freezing and crashing and hanging a lot. And Java seems not to be working sporadically.

6. The Ubuntu Software Center crashes frequently.

Otherwise, I like the new look and a number of the new features.

Would love to hear if anyone had and solved similar issues.

### Post by infidel on 2009-11-06
Upgrade from Jaunty on two machines, desktop and laptop. My results are disappointing on both platforms.

Boot-up takes *much* longer on both machines under 9.10 than under 9.04; in fact, it's the longest startup if any release that I can remember. Likewise (and particularly) the time between login and a working GNOME. On both machines, I receive notification that Nautilus has crashed as soon as I have a working mouse pointer. Once I have a session underway, I find that GNOME is actually a bit snappier than it used to be, but this scarcely matters on the desktop machine: I'm getting intermittent, spontaneous logouts back to the login screen. (This was also an issue for me a few versions ago; I forget which release. It's worse this time around.) 

On the laptop, no USB storage devices will auto-mount. Flash drives and my external hard disk are being seen and configured, but they can only be mounted manually. Management of the laptop's optical drive is also twitchy. I'm reading both media and data discs (CDs and DVDs), but volume names and properties aren't being updated when discs are changed. For example: cdrom0 was still called "WATCHMEN" after I'd finished playing the movie and moved on to a CD-R of archived files. I could read and manage files on that disc just fine, but Nautilus still insisted that it was a DVD movie in the drive. The issue persists after logging out and back in, so (complete non-expert that I am) I suspect the problem lies somewhere deeper down than GNOME.

The spontaneous logouts are a serious problem and would be more than enough to put me off the distribution if I weren't already so fond of it. The USB mounting problem is very annoying but not completely unmanageable. I would consider fresh installs on both machines, but from what I'm seeing there may be more problems coming from that path than the one I followed. I'm not having much luck resolving these issues by combing the forums. With all due respect to one of the finest support communities in Linuxdom, it's becoming very difficult -- for me, at least -- to sift out pertinent information here. In other words, any direct guidance would be greatly appreciated.

### Post by musarraf172 on 2009-11-06
I am having a very bitter experience with ubuntu 9.10.on my IBM R51 2887 AE4 laptop.
 
9.04 worked flawlessly with any problem. Sound , wifi , booting time 
everything was very good. But after 9.10 upgrade I had the following issue.

1. Can not boot with 2.6.31-14 kernel. The boot process does not end 
while premounting local file systems.

2. Can boot with old 2.6.28-16 but synaptic touchpad  does not work. It has conflict with ps2 mouse. ps2 or usb mouse is working. Sound hardware is not detected.Tried manual probing but without success.

3. Total system is jerky. Firefox stucks. Boot time is longer than 9.04..

4. If the default uspalsh theme is changed system does not boot.

5. got touchpad working by this work around : "echo options psmouse proto=exps > /etc/modprobe.d/psmouse.modprobe"

6. X server hangs when system is idol for a long time. If we change the console and again comeback tty7 then x is working again.

### Post by tkoco on 2009-11-06
Wierd startup screen - chased this one down to conflict with usplash. Removing that package fixed it.
Next, no sound - chased this one down to a incorrect kernel. During the upgrade process, I was asked if I wanted to retain the "menu.lst" or replace it. Since I do not constantly upgrade my system, I had elected to not change it. It would have been nicer if the message had indicated that a kernel was being upgraded, and would I like to update "grub"?
To solve this dilema, I installed grub2 and de-installed grub. The new "menu.lst" now selects the correct kernel and beautiful _sound_.

On a laptop, I had the screen blank out after idling for a while. No amount of key presses or mousing would bring back the "dead" screen. Chased this one down to a conflict in the screen savers (I had 2 different ones installed). I de-installed all screen savers. Then I installed in only _one_ screen saver!
Strangely enough, the usplash worked fine on the laptop. No wierdness.

### Post by chicom9 on 2009-11-06
Very disappointed with the upgrade from 9.04 to 9.10. The issues were endless from one step block to another &#8230; here is the break down: 
Since this was my week off from work (11/02/09) I decided why not upgrade Ubuntu to the latest release &#8230; been that it was out for a few days.  So my current hardware configuration is the fallowing:

Motherboard: M2N-SLI DELUXE ASUS M2N-SLI DELUXE ACPI BIOS Revision 0307 (07/14/2006)

CPU: AMD Athlon(tm) 64 X2 Dual Core Processor 4200

Memory size: 5GiB

Video card: Dual with SLI Nvidia 7600 GT

 Dual Dell monitors 1701FP

To save myself from all the troubles I decided that the best idea will be to do a clean installation. I started by booting from the CD and reformatting the hard drive. I waited for about 20 minutes when the lovely installer for 9.10 will start downloading the language files. So I said lets skip that and just continue. Really Ubuntu should have not included that option in there. The installer should have been kept as in Jaunty... but any how once the installation finish I reboot it my system and there it was Karmic at it's best.  So I was pretty happy I had achieved the installation with no problems. I know Ubuntu and I knew that all their releases are awesome. So now I moved on to start restoring my desktop and customizing it the way I like it. Here is the list of software I use:

Firefox
Gnome-mplayer
K3b with mp3 support
Java JRE with firefox support
Amarok mp3 support
Compiz and emerald theme support
Conky
flash
Avocent kvm software 
Vmware workstation 7

So the Installation of each of these software was pretty smooth except for the part of installing Compiz.
From my past 3 years of experiencing with Desktop effects I know that Compiz can get a little frustrating. But I feel confident so I moved on... 
Here are the steps I took to install Compiz: 

1.I did all system updates and reboot it.
2.Installed restricted drivers for Nvidia 180.xx 
3.Reboot it and No Video all I got was a black screen 

Now the black screen nightmare &#8230; I know it was a problem with the restricted drivers for Nvidia that I had just installed. So I removed them to see if X will come back. However, that was no dice and the black screen remained.
Now I had to pull out my bag of goodies and start to troubleshoot this &#8230; so here...

1.Open up my xorg.conf and replace the references to for Driver &#8220;nvidia&#8221; to &#8220;vesa&#8221;
2.Now with the vesa driver I knew i will get X back  and yes that actually worked. 

Having X back I was a success, but now I needed to figure out why the restricted drivers messed up my xorg.conf. Yet I started to ran out of patience so I took no further troubleshooting steps. It was time to go back to my backup and see if I could use my xorg.conf file:

1.Reinstall restricted drivers for a second time rebooted and no X again &#8230;
2.No more patience picked up my original xorg.conf from the backup of Jaunty and restored to my new Karmic installed. 
3.Reboot it my machine and sure enough X was back and I had successfully installed the restricted drivers for Nvidia.

OK so now trouble free from X and my GUI back. I needed now to install Compiz. So I took the usual steps:
1.[http://www.howtoforge.com/enabling-compiz-fusion-on-an-ubuntu-9.04-desktop-nvidia-geforce-fx-5200](http://www.howtoforge.com/enabling-compiz-fusion-on-an-ubuntu-9.04-desktop-nvidia-geforce-fx-5200)

I know this above guide is for Jaunty, but it shouldn't have varied that much from Karmic approach. The installation was  partially successful some of the plug-ings in Compiz did not work like 3D windows plug-in. I was a bit upset but I knew I can live without it until I researched more. So approach to Compiz installation was 80% completed; but my new experience with Karmic was already 20% of aggravation. So I moved on I installed my next application:
1.Installed Vmware Workstation 7 (successful)
2.Installed conky partially successful some transparency stop working there must be a new way of configuring the .conkyrc; howerver I wanted my workstation back so no time to further troubleshoot 30% aggravation
3.Avocent a breeze no hassle installation successful

Now at this stage I said I can live with a 30% aggravation if some things don't work I can just continue to work and as I learn more about Karmic I'm sure I can get all those little details to work. However, I was wrong It was time for me to got to bed after a long day of reading and troubleshooting Karmic. 

Day #2

So in day 2 I knew things didn't work so I open up Firefox and what! Google doesn't come up try other sites nothing. I go to my other desktop running Debian Lenny sure no problem. Now I know it was early in the morning but what the hell! Now my aggravation went from 30% to 80% how in the world was this happening. I started to read about Karmic and slow Internet speeds and all articles pointed to IPV6 enabled. So I had to try I went through a list of crazy steps: 


[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/glibc/+bug/417757](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/glibc/+bug/417757)


So its a bug,  Ok so maybe Karmic shouldn't be stable release. I just ended by going back Jaunty. Very disappointed, but sometimes thats how Linux can be I know from past experience from Fedora to Ubuntu. I know now not to just upgrade as soon as a release has been done neither Ubuntu or any other distro... 

I am not putting down Ubuntu at all I just think for a stable release the community and developers should really take their time Ubuntu is neat and stable, so let's not concentrate in how fast in can boot. Let's concentrate on usability. 

My 2cents

---

### Post by MarkusAurelius on 2009-11-06
I have 6 laptops running Ubuntu.

First, I put in an early release a couple of weeks ago on a Dell D600 to shake things a bit with Karmic. Of course, there were some issues, but no show stoppers. When the Karmic Final Release came out, I installed it afresh on the this machine.  I had done the previous one with ext4, but I will stick to ext3 for now...

That leaves me 5: 3 Linux only and 2 dual-boot. I did a fresh install on two Linux only machines (Dell D410 & HP6220). It was a breeze to install Karmic on them: everything detected just fine... and working just fine, just as with Jaunty.  The only minor thing, was the lacking of Icons in the Main Menu:  a quick search found the solution: "System/Preferences/Appearance/Interface/Select Menu Icons."  This is indeed minor.

The next two (Dell 6400 & Dell D610) however, were dual boot with Jaunty and WinXP.  Since I am a proponent of fresh installs and I do my best to avoid upgrades, I was debating if I should try an upgrade.  But this time, I decided to be bold and do an upgrade on the 6400 first...  That little "Upgrade" Button in Update Manager was tempting....

I was pleasantly surprised: the upgrade was flawless, even though there were many additional 3rd party PPA repositories!!  Excellent! Nothing "broke" and it rebooted just fine, all applications working... Wow!  This was certainly not the experience I had from 7.10 to 8.04 ...

I felt so good about the upgrade, that I upgraded to GRUB2 on the 6400 and it rebooted just fine...  So I went ahead and "finalized" the GRUB2 upgrade, but, failing to read the Caveat in the notes, I had not "Selected" the Boot device with the "spacebar".  Consequently, I was toast: GRUB Error 15! But, with LiveCD on a thumbdrive, I was able to follow the instructions on how to fix it and all is well. This was completely my fault...

I then upgraded the other dual-boot, the D610.  Same as the 6400: it upgraded perfectly, INCLUDING doing GRUB2 properly this time.

I have one left: an older Mac PowerBook G4 with Jaunty on it... I will keep that as is for now...

On a side note, the only thing that is not working too well on Karmic, is a script to turn the video off: "xset dpms force standby".  The screen turns off OK, but a few seconds later, it gets turned back on again! This script worked exceptionally well in Jaunty on all these machines... However, having an even older laptop (Dell C610) that is used as a local server, I normally use "vbetool dpms off" to remotely turn the monitor off.  So I created a couple of keyboard short-cuts to turn the screen "off" and "on" on all the laptops: this solution works even better than the previously used script...

So, yes, I'm a Happy Camper with Karmic....

Regards.

---

### Post by undfined on 2009-11-07

### Post by 64bitguy on 2009-11-07
> The purpose of this thread is to share your experience installing/upgrading Karmic Koala.

Did it work flawlessly ? 
Did you get problems ? 
Did you manage to solve them ? 
if yes how ?Long time observer, first time Ubuntu installation.
**Test Unit:**  HP Pavilion model DV8301NR Laptop w/ AMD Turion64, ATI Radeon Express 200M, 17" Display - Brand New 80GB 7200RPM EIDE.

Installation:  Appeared to operate without any substantial flaws.  Noticed a few errors on initial boots; however, they cleared themselves up after updates/tweaks.  Used all default settings for installation on a brand new unpartitioned 80GB Internal Drive and booting from KK 9.10 Installation CD.

Issues:
1)  **System sees ATI Video as Intel Video** and didn't recognize ATI Video even after installing ATI catalyst drivers (via USC).  Not really a problem other than not being able to utilize some of the catalyst advanced features.  Video does display all modes without problems, though I would like more controls.  **[COLOR=Blue]*(Unresolved, Investigating further)*[/COLOR]**

2)  **Installation failed to recognize Internal Broadcom Wireless** Networking and it was somewhat of a hassle to get wireless operating.   I thought given how widespread these BCM43xx setups are, not having this work (and the processes that had to be completed to get wireless functioning correctly) was rather disappointing.  [COLOR=Blue]***(******Unresolved, Problem appears to exist in 9.10 installation impacting all Broadcom Wireless based systems). ***[/COLOR]

3)  **Touchpad**.  I have posted my comments on this in the issue specific forum and will only comment here by saying for me "personally" this issue is an inconvenience that I am dealing with using gpointing-device-settings after every reboot; however, in terms of business deployment, not being able to have a simple mouse settings checkbox to "disable Touchpad when any other mouse is present" **(automatically)** is a show-stopper.  (See [my other post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1313224") for details).  The current situation would simply not work in an Enterprise scenario. [COLOR=Blue][I][B](Unresolved, Enterprise Deployment Show-Stopper).
[/B][/I][/COLOR] 
4) ** Firefox 3.5.4 versus Firefox 3.0.15**.  I have been having a great deal of issues with FF 3.5.x on all of my machines (whether they be Windows or any other OS) and have found that Firefox 3.0.15 fits all of my needs and doesn't lock-up, fail to respond or have any of the other (MANY) problems that I have been experiencing with FF 3.5.4. Additionally, certain add-ons required are not available for 3.5.4. [COLOR=Red] **However, there is no option to install Firefox 3.0.15 in Karmic Koala's Ubuntu Software Center _or_ Synaptic Package Manager. **[/COLOR] Installing Firefox via other methods has proved to be challenging, especially in terms of being able to install add-ons after the fact (including associating Java correctly with FF 3.0.15).  This was attempted after finding that Karmic Koala's version of Firefox 3.5.4 behaves with all of the same problems experienced on this same computer (when it was using XP and FF 3.5.4).  **In a nutshell, 9.10 should offer the ability to downgrade FF to 3.0.15 though USC or SPM. **[COLOR=Blue]***(Unresolved, Enterprise Deployment Show-Stopper).***[/COLOR]

5)  **Java**.  For some reason, updating Java isn't as straight forward as I thought it should be.  Ubuntu Software Center and Synaptic Package Manager both fail to have the latest versions of Sun Java which (given the security issues) is surprising and disappointing.  [COLOR=Blue]***(Unresolved, Enterprise Deployment Show-Stopper).***[/COLOR]

6) **Missing Services Management (services-admin) GUI**.  (See:  [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-system-tools/+bug/433701](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-system-tools/+bug/433701))  The discussion here covers what happened, but I think this tool is really needed! [COLOR=Blue]***(Unresolved).***[/COLOR]

7) ** Documentation**.  The real trouble is that most of it points to and regards older versions (in fact, Karmic Koala screens and commands rarely exist and there was not even a documentation root for KK 9.10 on the day I downloaded and installed it after official release.).  I found on-line documentation to be also weak in even listing, never mind demonstrating this version.  I realize that over time, this issue will improve; however, I think sections dealing with 9.10 installation and Ubuntu unique command line operations really needs to be reviewed to reflect how things are done in this version (especially since most people will be looking for information NOW!). [COLOR=Blue]***(Unresolved, but should gradually improve over time).***[/COLOR]

8) **Ubuntu Forums**.  In closing, I wanted to mention that while I see there are a ton of forums (and contributors) here, the organization of information seems to be very loosely structured with confusing, misleading or outright vague forum and sub-forum names (and/or intents).  **Due to current forum structures making no attempt to segregate content or even distinguish content uniquely by Ubuntu Version (and having sub-forums from there) **"new" Ubuntu users will find the existing structure very frustrating (in attempting) to search out Version specific answers, when (a) no Version specific forums exist and (b) instead (as a result) must page through huge amounts of unrelated data.  

To add insult to injury, in some cases (actually with KK 9.10, in most cases), the answers provided regarding a problem in one place may directly conflict with the way things are done in an older (or newer!) version, creating even more problems.   While I love the intent and widespread use of Forums (speaking as widely recognized CMS/Forums software developer, administrator, and multi-site moderator myself), I think Ubuntu moderators should discuss the current structure situation and look at the Ubuntu Forums from a "New Ubuntu User" perspective.  [COLOR=Red]**[COLOR=Black]At present, Ubuntu forums appear to be (from the top level on down) in a word,[/COLOR] "overwhelming"**[/COLOR].  Additionally, by not segregating forums by Ubuntu Version number, huge overhead is wasted by [http://ubuntuforums.org](http://ubuntuforums.org) database Servers, as users are forced to perform forum searches in sections that the user could care less about (and in fact, doesn't want to search).  

So in a nutshell, not only do forum searches of these unrelated sections slow down the entire server (and experience for everyone using this website), but the results of these searches are also revealing unwanted information that could actually do more harm than good, and at minimum, waste the time of the user unnecessarily.  

From a new user "Installation" perspective, well structured, Version oriented forums to resolve issues and obtain targeted version support is critical.  From the perspective of experienced users, moderators and "fix" forum contributors, a version oriented structure also aids their efforts as they can concentrate on areas of knowledge versus being forced to surf the entire disorganized domain for problems that they can actually address and solve.  [COLOR=Blue][I][B](Unresolved, Critical).

[/B][COLOR=Black]I hope someone finds this useful.  Provided for constructional purposes only.[/COLOR]
[/I][/COLOR]

### Post by raananb on 2009-11-07
Updraded from 9.04 on Asus F5 Laptop with double boot - Vista & Ubuntu.

Upgrade went on flawlessly, but with a hitch: I was asked 3 or 4 times what to do with menu.lst. I kept unswering "use existing ...", since menu.lst has my double-boot config.

I ended up with a non-working touchpad, which was retrieved once I manually corrected menu.lst to contain the latest version of the kernel.

I dont see why this question is raised more than once, and I dont see why the install process does not update menu.lst with the latest kernel version by default, without asking - after all, is a user upgrades, it is to have the latest version.

If a user is technically saavy, then it would not be a problem to modify menu.lst if for any reason this is necessary. For the end user who simply wants to upgrade, a fully automatic process with no questions is preferable.

### Post by Cloggsy on 2009-11-07
Sorry guys & gals, this was a disaster for me which eventually took me a week to unpick :mad:. Granted, if I actually knew what I was doing it might have been less painful but the point about Ubuntu is that it's Linux for people who need to be able to use it "out of the box" as an alternative to W - well, you know who!

Did a wubi install but found that it wouldn't recognise the microphone (which I use for VOIP) or my mobile broadband dongle or give me meaningful information about the state of my battery. Tried a clean install on a partition next to the upgrade; same as the wubi. (Yes, FOUR operating systems on the one machine now!). At this point I tried to remove one of the Ubuntu installations and managed to stuff GRUB; Four operating systems on the one machine and NOT ONE of them able to boot. :mad:

Finally had a Basil Fawlty moment and reloaded both Windows Vista and Jackalope from scratch and have nearly got the machine back to where it was before the upgrade. What I saw of Koala looked good but it just didn't work on my machine - especially the bits I rely on to keep in contact. To be fair, this is the first upgrade to go Tango Uniform in my experience and I've been doing them since Gibbon. I think I will have to give Koala a miss. This is on an Acer Aspire laptop; my desktop is also dual-booting into Jackalope but I don't dare now upgrade to Koala as it's an old machine with both my wife and daughter as users on it.

As an aside, I saw that the Add/Remove package had changed as well as GRUB - currently no KGRUBEditor for Koala? I find this really useful to keep the boot menu from overflowing.
