### Post by mbscad on 2009-11-07
Upgraded from 9.04 via net, worked well.
Only problem that I found was "Places" in top menu bar didn't work (ie showed places like home, desktop etc but didn't show files within those folders).
Searched the community answers and found the solution.
I needed to edit the mimeapps.list file.
Mine had an entry from the upgrade of "inode/directory=mount - archive.desktop;nautilus-folder-handler.desktop;"
Just editted it to read "inode/directory=nautilus-folder-handler.desktop;"
On restart, all was well. Thanks to all the hard working developers for this.
I hope this may help anyone else who has this problem.

---

### Post by Phillip Spencer on 2009-11-07
My experience has been fairly dire! I started using Linux with 8.04 and by 9.04 had migrated a desktop machine from Vista and two XP laptops... things were humming and I was recommending Ubuntu to friends as a serious alternative to MS.  Not now!

The desktop and one laptop were "installs" to i386 9.10 - to clean everything up -  while the other laptop, my main work one, was an upgrade to AMD64 9.10.  The two installs went fine until I tried to add extra programs from Ubuntu Software Centre when I had repeated msttcorefonts problems (see bug # 431217).

The upgrade reported that it failed at the end of the process - due to msttcorefonts - but so far has allowed me access to the machine despite this.

However, worse is that since moving to 9.10 internet response time has been lousy.  Someone pointed me at the IPv6 DNS bug #431217.  At the moment I am not clear if this is the cause... I have problems both with wireless and wired connections with all three PCs, I have a brand new router too.   It is this that has brought me to Ubuntu Forums... I had previously used Ubuntu "out of the box" and been happy.  Sadly, those days appear to be over.

There are many people reporting slow internet connections on 9.10... there appears to be something radically wrong with this release... looking at the discussions, the IPv6 was a known problem in Beta and still it went live. What I find staggering is that the bug is only rated "high" not "critical".  Isn't decent internet access a fundamental requirement for most users?  Not sure how long I can stand these poor response times before considering re-installing 9.04.

### Post by dougalkerr on 2009-11-07
I downloaded the .iso at work and installed Karmic at work on Dell Optiplex and decided to install the ATI driver recommended by EnvyNG which appeared to be the latest upon checking the version number. Bad idea coz the screen res changed and I couldn't get my 1680 x 1050 back no matter what I tried. I had to do some work and the quickest way for me to get the screen normal again was to reinstall. I must admit it doesn't take long.
I next installed Karmic on my home laptop, a Dell studio 1537 again with an ATI card. I made a balls up of trying to install the ATI driver manually and had to reinstall but, I was having a bit of trouble with the boot menu and couldn't figure out how to edit it so I reinstalled 9.04 meantime. After a couple of days I 'upgraded' to 9.10 - bad idea, don't do it. Everything seemed to go well but, I had no pointer to use, atleast I had a pointer but, couldn't move it. I managed to get into the menu to shutdown and chose this. I rebooted and installed using the CDROM I had previously burned. Everything seems to be working so far. I chose the correct method for installing the ATI driver and got special effects working in graphics. It is such a shame though that once again the window resize delay problem has not been taken care of. I had the problem with 9.04 but not before that. 8.10 worked flawlessly with Compiz and my ATI card but, 9.04 and now 9.10 has this awful resize delay. I have read of some people getting a fix for it but, I have never succeeded. It is so annoying that I reverted to installing CrunchBang Linux in protest. If I can't use great special effects completely then what is the point. I'd rather have a very fast system like CrunchBang and do without them. So to summarize - install with the CDROM and don't upgrade. Use the correct method for installing the graphics driver and don't bother with special effects graphics. Otherwise no other problems to note.

---

### Post by hasimir44 on 2009-11-07
I'm adding my experience in case anyone else does what I did by mistake.. 

I've done quite a few ubuntu upgrades and unfortunately I got into the habit of expecting several config file prompts popping up and asking if I'd like to keep the old version or change to the new. I always keep my old version and mistakenly selected that for grub's menu.lst :).  After the upgrade and reboot, X11 crashed because it couldn't find my video driver (nvidia), my wireless card didn't work, no audio, etc.. At first I thought it had something to do with the upgrade stopping in the middle due to an earlier complaint about "unable to upgrade vim.tiny" (which I uninstalled for the full vim a long time ago).  

I finally realized that I was using an old kernel by comparing "uname -r" to the initrd files in /boot.  I then manually edited /boot/grub/menu.lst and rebooted.  Now everything is working fine.

### Post by Xavier Oddmon on 2009-11-08
A few days ago, I installed Karmic Koala 64bit on my iBuyPower Battalion LX750 (a re-branded MSI EX700), and it was flawless.

Best install yet. Not only did I not have to fix any issues, but several things that never worked before worked this time out of the box:

Webcam. I expected this to fail horribly. In Jaunty, the best I could come out with after hours of fooling around was a very dark, blue image, with all kinds of weird distortion. So I was quite surprised when i started up cheese, and there I was!

Sound card. Usually, this worked with a minor exception: when plugging in headphones, the speakers didn't mute. Now they do. Unfortunately, I'll still have to figure out how to set up surround sound again, but that's not usually a big deal. Although I do get a bit tired of doing that...

Bluetooth audio. Incredible! I connect to my headphones, and they just work! (Show up as a separate output device in the sounds preferences dialog). 

Touchpad. I have a synaptics touchpad, but it's never shown up as one. Previously, I had to write a script to enable circular scrolling, which looked a bit like this: "xinput set-init-prop "ETPS/2 Elantech Touchpad" 250 32 100 100", repeated several times with different values for different options (how obvious!). Now, I install "touchpad", and it works. 

Karmic Koala has, so far, surpassed all of my other Ubuntu installs (Jaunty was an absolute nightmare for the first month or so, and I had to keep running fsck as root).

The only thing that doesn't work is standby. It has never worked, except under windows (dual boot). It's something i'd like to have, but it's not a big deal.

### Post by 1clue on 2009-11-08
I installed Karmic onto an existing RAID1 array, with RAID10 alternate mounts.  This is on AMD64, an Intel i7 920 with a P6T motherboard and an nVidia GT200 (GeForce GTX 260) video card.

Contrary to what has been said earlier in this thread, if you are installing onto an AMD64 with an existing RAID array, you need the alternate installer.

If you have done this sort of thing before with Jaunty, you will find it extremely familiar.  I went through it without instructions and it worked fine.

I haven't done much yet, but I have found out that sound configuration is a million times easier (no need to go through 5 control panels to get it to work) and my webcam worked right out of the box, and it didn't work at all on Jaunty.  The only issue is that it doesn't track me when I move, it's a LogiTech QuickCam Orbit AF and it should point at me as I move.

That's not really a complaint, I assume that since it didn't work at all in Jaunty and it works fine video-wise in Karmic that the tracking software simply isn't working yet.

My biggest complaint so far is that I can't dual-boot back to the Jaunty installation.  As far as I can tell, it should work fine but I can't get the menu to show up during boot.  I'm not a Grub guy, I've been using Lilo until relatively recently.

If you're doing a new install, I think it would go pretty much flawlessly.

Thanks.

### Post by LuxAeterna on 2009-11-08
Just like joshuajon, I had a broken Compiz after upgrading my Dell XPS M1330 laptop, which crashed Gnome just after displaying the desktop.

This problem did not exist on my desktop PC, however, which is also running Compiz. Both were Ubuntu 9.04, 32-bit, and upgraded through the update manager.

Disabling Compiz solved the problem on my Dell. I did that by using the failsafe Gnome. I believe that Compiz is creating so many problems for users that Canonical should still consider it experimental and not include it with Ubuntu by default. It would probably reduce the number of error reports by users to half. And even if it won't, you should also consider that many people read about and fix the problems without writing entries in the ubuntuforums. Compiz is a real Linux killer.

Good advice to others when upgrading: Disable Compiz BEFORE upgrading, to avoid trouble.

### Post by witeshark17 on 2009-11-08
> **tonyuk123 said:**
> well tried an upgrade from 9.04 to 9.10
all went well until reboot, then it would not start
new kernels dont work with my motherboard, but i still had a kernel that worked before upgrade, that had a pretty good go at starting, but dropped to a command line with errors about missing directories and the like.
was told on here to fresh install, which i did, then realised it would only have the one new kernel ! so no way to start
got and initrd error only
so back to LTS versions for me now initrd error, that's familiar. Here's what I did, I'm not sure is this is exact for a second boot partition since I know windows needs to be on the first, but it has to be worth a look!

 Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic (recovery mode)
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.28-16-generic
Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.28-16-generic (recovery mode)

I get this error:error: unknown command 'initrd'

type "e" to edit to find (and write down, no cut and paste!) a number like:

UUID=73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333  <(example)

then


Ctrl-C (it may be just C !!) to enter the GRUB command line
2. Type in *insmod linux* and press ENTER

*linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.30-10-generic root=UUID=*73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333

where  **_73b2b033-654b-4efa-8e11-17138c4d0333**_   < is unique to your system (found after hitting "e" to edit in the boot options list)

then

initrd /boot/initrd.img-_**2.6.31-14**_-generic

where 2.6.31-14  <is your current (latest) kernel 

boot And after booting doing a 
sudo grub-install (target drive)

### Post by ebozzz on 2009-11-08
I just identified a new issue on yesterday. After successfully logging in, all my users were reporting that they got the following error...

> Unable to update the ICE Authotity

or something to that effect. I checked the permissions of that file in my home folder and found that it was owned by root. All of my research indicated that should not be the case as the specific user should own it. I was able to resolve that matter using the following and substituting the actual user name where the word user appears in the code. 

```
sudo chown *user*:*user* /home/*user*/.ICEauthority
```

After that I entered the following.

```
chmod 644 /home/*user*/.ICEauthority
```

I then used the same method to make the necessary changes for all of my remaining users with one exception. Use the sudo command prior to the chmod code.

```
sudo chmod 644 /home/*user*/.ICEauthority
```

Every user now owns that file in their home folder and no more errors.

### Post by francesco44 on 2009-11-08
I made a post few days ago about difficulties with Karmic on two Thinkpad X31:


The notification window isn't readable...
AbyWord is not usable....
Different display problems


The solution is very simple:

gksudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Under the Section "Device"


you just write:

Option "RenderAccel"   "off"

I suppose the graphic card of the X31 do not support this option...I think this problems happen with three to four years old laptop....spread the message

### Post by Peregrin on 2009-11-08
Gave in today and agreed to "upgrade" my Jaunty AA1 (netbook-remix) to Karmic. It went smoothly and I don't see any serious problems, but still I'm rather disappointed with the result.

The reason is that the Look'n'Feel changed completely from a nice, warm, soothing pastel appearance to an ugly, cold, metallic monochrome one. The yellow-and-red Ubuntu logo has been replaced by some kind of pale skull thingy. Some usability genius apparently also decided that gray on gray is a suitable color scheme for the once-colored mini-icons in the taskbar.

I don't understand why such radical theme changes, which are apparently very much a matter of personal taste, are simply forced on users, rather than offered as an option.

There are also some minor issues: The login screen and the logoff menu now feel a need to remind me of my full name. I really have to wonder how they came up with this idea; I cannot believe that forgetting one's name was a frequent user complaint that needed to be adressed.

It seems that Karmic also lost some user settings. Firefox now has a toolbar which I'm pretty sure wasn't there before, and the time format of the clock applet changed to something I don't like.

I'm not looking forward to turning this ugly thing on again.

### Post by bhold on 2009-11-08
Karmic desktop fresh install into separate partition (ext4), so I could keep my 9.04 installation intact. Vanilla Dell Dimension E520 hardware.

I did not like how the installer blank-screened immediately after entering the hardware configuration phase. (OK, whats happening now???) I waited about 20 minutes, then moved the mouse to see if I could interrupt into a message or activity of some kind. Immediately received an "installation complete" message and felt much better. Rebooted successfully into Karmic.

I like how grub2 correctly integrated the new 9.10 boot info and the old boot info for 9.04.

I next installed my remote Windows printer. I experienced font problems with simple text output (never seen that before following a fresh install of any Ubuntu release going back to 7.04.) 

I also kept getting an annoying remote print authentication prompt, no need for this as it is only myself and my wife on this small office network. I get the authentication prompt not only printing from Firefox, but also from the command line with lp. To get around the authentication prompt in 9.04 I had used the workaround of printing through LPR. This does not work in 9.10, I kept getting a message that lpr cannot run due to permission problems.

During this remote print setup and testing I had a root terminal running so I could restart cupsd. Periodically I would get thrown out of root back to normal user with a user signal 1 message, I have never seen that before.

Another thing I noticed was slow remote access to disk files on the Windows system. The initial access to the shares seemed to take a minute or two on Karmic, as opposed to a few seconds on 9.04.

Anyway, after only a few hours on Karmic, I found 3 or 4 problems with basic functionality that had worked fine on 9.04. I found nothing that would have prevented me from staying on 9.10, but the number of minor problems I found after only a few hours worried me. 

These days I actually use my linux environments for real tasks, so I have decided to stay with 9.04 and / or Debian 5.03 for stability for a few months longer, then will try Karmic again.

### Post by locketine on 2009-11-08
Upon restarting and logging into my computer after upgrading from jaunty to karmic my computer worked for anywhere between 1 and 30 seconds before completely freezing. Even music stopped playing if I managed to open pandora.com. Apparently this problem was caused by my laptop display being on in addition to my main screen.

To fix this problem I had to switch to a virtual terminal (Ctrl-Alt-F1), log in and then run the command: ```
sudo metacity --replace & killall compiz compiz.real
```
After that I switched back to my xwindows session (Ctrl-Alt-F7), closed any open applications since they were all on top of my System menu, and then I opened Sysem->Preferences->Display. I turned off my laptop display. 

Finally, I opened a terminal window and ran the command: ```
compiz --replace
``` which started up compiz, free of any freezing problem.

Make sure to start compiz before restarting as this apparently makes ubuntu remember that compiz should run without a second display. If you just restart after disabling the second display ubuntu will still turn the second display on upon restart.


When I opened Amarok I got about 11 KDebug dialogue boxes informing me that KBuildSysCoca Segfaulted. Apparently this was caused by a bad file and removing it fixed my problem.
```
rm ~/.local/share/mime/globs
```. I got this bizarre fix from this [bug report]("https://bugs.kde.org/show_bug.cgi?id=199300").

It's certainly possible to get a bad .iso file, and just as possible to get a bad burn to CD.  You need to slow the transfer rate down to as low as you can stand, maybe 3X or 4X, to insure a good burn, and this goes for any .iso, not just this one.  Bad burns are unfortunately rather common.

### Post by papikondalu on 2009-11-09
Ubuntu upgrade from from Jaunty Jackalope to karmic Koala was a breeze. Here is what I did:
1) Updated to latest packages on Jaunty.
2) Downloaded the alternate cdrom iso on my Dell core-2 duo laptop, mounted as /cdrom, ran /cdrom/cdrom-upgrade. I've selected download updates from internet.
3) Had to wait for all downloads to happen (150+MB of additional downloads).
4) Reboot the machine and I have karmik koala in place
5) After login, I was asked about installing language pack, I said yes. The installation is complete.

Few impressions about the new release:
1) My GDM theme was reset. No way to get back to that !  (except for changing the background image using some hack)
2) Did not find any change in other aspects - everything looked similar to Jaunty. I'm not sure if I could justify the 800+MB download for no significant change (except for the loss of GDM theme).
3) The only positive aspect I've seen is that my system does a full powerdown. For some reason the Jaunty version goes through the entire shutdown sequence except the last 'powerdown' step.

Alright on my [previous post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8228291#post8228291"), I had a lot of issues developed after the install.  Most I suspected was with my ATI and KK.

[SIZE="1"]*Disclaimer* - when installing a new OS, I **always**:

[LIST=1]
[*]MD5SUM the .iso file before writing to CD
[*]Verify the CD after writing
[*]Check my memory has no faults
[*]Check my hard disk has no faults
[*]Do a Clean-Install, not an upgrade
[/LIST]
_**It's the only way.**_
[/SIZE]

### Post by rrashkin on 2009-11-09
I meant to install 9.10 when it first came out but first I was prevented from doing so by the weather, then I got spooked by some of the tales of woe and intrigue I saw here.  I should know better.  Every time a new version is released there are horror stories and praise in equal measure.

I have an Everex desktop with a VIA chipset.  The only peripherals I have are a printer (HP Photosmart) and a Compact Flash/Secure Digital card reader (Targus).  Which is to say, I don't have a lot of things to go wrong.  I also don't have any software that isn't part of Ubuntu, with the exception of my own app's in Python.  I appreciate that there are people with more extensive requirements but that's my setup.  I installed 9.10 on Saturday.  Even though I had seen the complaints about the screen going black, I still found it disconcerting.  Never the less, The system came back, my Ethernet was found, my video was High-res, my printer worked, and I think the boot was a little faster than it had been.

I like that my user name is "pre-typed" but I kind of don't like that the screen flashes when I hit enter, before the password prompt appears.  I don't really have an opinion about the background: orange is fine, brown was fine, too.  If only Firefox could handle ActiveX controls, I'd never have to go work at all (which I do occasionally in order to use Windows).

### Post by MysteriousHoatzin on 2009-11-09
I can't get the wifi to connect on my Thinkpad T30. Worked just fine in Intrepid, in Jaunty I had to restart network manager every other time I turned the machine on, but it would get it to work eventually. This time around I changed over to wicd, successfully tracked down and downloaded missing firmware, looked at all the troubleshootings here, but neither WEP nor WPA work.

Also, the new notification looks like a black box with junk pixels akin to those that flicker on before the os fully loads. I suspect this is because fancy blurring and dropshadow is not working correctly on my older hardware. In the end, the inflexible nature of the notification styling makes it utterly useless and simply distracting. I wish I could just globally turn them off.

I dunno. This was a really weird update. On one hand the interface seems snappier and more responsive, on the other the notifications are **** and there is no wifi.

### Post by brianfactors on 2009-11-09
Before, I had major problems with my sound volume and multitasking. Karmac made a major update to Pulse that really made my life a lot easier. So I'm very happy about that. I'm also liking the new look... not bad.

I like the update to Do. Seems to be working reasonably well so far.

A few weird things I fixed...
-The Search bar in firefox would not respond when I clicked on it, so I completely purged firefox (aka: manually deleting settings, etc.), reinstalled, and it seems to work fine.
All except for flash. While flash is just fine in Opera, it does werid things in Firefox when Compiz is enabled. [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1313366](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1313366)
-Skype needed to be reinstalled. This time, I installed it from Medibuntu, and it work fine as far as I can tell.

One thing that still bugs me...
What in the world? Why does it take so long. I have a Core2 Duo 64bit, 2.0 GHz, 4 GiB RAM, and a fast hard drive. The only thing really slowing me down might be the graphics card.
It takes it a stinkin' 1:27 min to get going! What's up with that? Once started up, it only uses 450 MiB of RAM, so I have no clue why it has such a slow boot.

Even more, it's a full 30 seconds just to get from GRUB the opening Splash. That's as long as I expect the WHOLE boot process to take.

Some time, I'll run a clean install, but for now, I can't understand ANYTHING (besides Vista, which is actually faster) taking that long to start up. It's completely ridiculous.

Oh, and were in the world did the option for the Login screen go? All I want to do is turn off that annoying login sound. Where'd it go?

So, yeah, overall an improvement, but still annoying in many ways...

---

### Post by unix fossile on 2009-11-09
Karmic Koala (32bit) is the worst disappointment I have had in the last 4 years. I have a large productive desktop system with a Intel Corporation 82915G/GV/910GL Integrated Graphics Controller (rev 04), plus a Dell latitude laptop (multiboot with WinXP, Ubuntu, FreeBSD, OpenSolaris). Once every two years or so I do a fresh install instead of an upgrade. So I did a fresh installation of Karmic on my laptop (I always keep a spare partition for such exercises). The installation went smoothly, but when I wanted to boot the other OSes, grub had forgotten them, and with this terrible Grub2 it was not possible to fix this situation. Uninstalling pc-grub and installing grub-legacy did not fix the problem.
Grub2 is a dog, don't use it on existing systems!
So I upgraded my existing Jaunty laptop environment. The upgrade went OK, grub was not messed up, but I see no real added value in Karmic :-(
On my productive desktop system, I was warned by my laptop experience about doing a new installation, so I upgraded from Jaunty to Karmic. I didn't use Jaunty a lot, since the screen would freeze once in a while, so I hoped that Karmic solved this problem (I run Ibex normally). How naive I was! The upgraded system is soo slow, it takes minutes to start up a firefox window etc. It's like 15 years ago when the first W* systems came up.

Is it my graphics chip on the motherboard that is causing this problem? Why did nobody note?
Anyway, I may have to look into other Linux distributions to stay current...

### Post by brucenduane on 2009-11-09
I am writing this using Barry's Puppy 4.3.1 because it works!!!

I installed Ubuntu 9.10 on an external USB hard drive on an ext3 partition sdb6 that I had previously formated using Ubuntu 8.04

That meant that Ubuntu 9.10 did not need to format the partition.
It did not like that!

I have installed Ubuntu 7.10, 8.04, UbuntuStudio 8.04, and 9.10
on this external hard drive using my notebook.  I have the repair disks SuperGrub Sept2008 and Gparted 0.3.9.4 ready to fix the laptop so it will work after an Ubuntu install.  9.10 was no
exception.

What surprised me was that it made sda2 my linux swap partition bootable on the notebook.  Must be that people using microsoft computers designed the install scripts.   By the way, external USB drives are (hd0) when booted and (hd1) when linux start up. 

I had a very slow network connection <<600 bits/sec when I did the install.  There needs to be an option on install script to install Ubuntu with no internet -- saving the internet activities for later.  There are many place in USA where there is no internet. Probably more than in Africa today.

The Grub2 (alpha software in a production release!!) is different.  My old super grub disk repaired my laptop and I modified my 8.04 /boot/grub/menu.lst to boot 9.10.  I felt safer doing it that way rather than use alpha software on my computer.

I have used 9.10 for 2 days.  When it rebooted my computer the second day, it ended my work with 9.10.  My overall impression is that this is not an OS I will use.  

It is not user friendly.
The scripts are hidden.
There is no choice.

I have installed Breezy 5.10, Dapper 6.04, Gutsy 7.04, Hardy 8.04
and now Karmic 9.10.  You taught me not to upgrade when I did the upgrade from Breezy to Dapper.  Since then I only do fresh installs.  I avoided 8.10 because of what I read on the forum.

If I were to rank 9.10 I would put it before Breezy 5.10 for user friendliness and ability for the user to easily configure the OS for their use.  It seems a giant step backward.

When someone wants to try linux, I give them a Puppy Linux disk or sell them a configured Puppy USB flash.  I used to pass out CDs of Ubuntu 5.10, 6.04, 7.10, and 8.04.  I will not pass out Ubuntu disks again until find one that is friendly and works.

Ubuntu has a lot of packages.  The update and synaptic package manager are great.  It is what attracted me to Ubuntu.  Not the bling, or lack of choice.  Being like MS should not be a goal or even a direction to head in.  Linux is about choice and things that work!  

Alpha and Beta software have no place in a production release!
No exceptions!!!

-Bruce.

---

### Post by AFarris01 on 2009-11-09
4PCs upgraded... 

2 PCs: Desktops, AMD processors w/ nVidia graphics--
    - Nearly flawless upgrade on both
        * Biggest issue was the icons for firefox didn't automatically change over on the accounts from within which the upgrades were triggered. Simple fix.

1 PC: Dell Laptop W/ Intel graphics--
    - Flawless upgrade, except for the issue with the firefox icon

1 PC: Desktop, AMD processor w/ ATI Graphics--
    - Numerous issues, some major some minor. flash-plugin installer failed on removal, and remains broken.
    - ATI's fglrx drivers failed to rebuild for the new kernel, and had to be removed manually, along with the old xorg.conf
    - System failed to launch X automatically on reboot. had to reboot in recovery mode and launch gdm from a root terminal
    - System uses a logitech g15 keyboard. On reboot, g15daemon fails to start automatically

All in all, experience is about the same as normal...ATI always causes me issues and these are a little more serious than usual, and as usual, all the AMD/nVidia-based computers received flawless upgrades.

I must say though, the open source Radeon drivers have improved drastically since I last used them. I will probably be switching to them for the time being, provided that I can get some sort of 3D support from it.

### Post by downsj on 2009-11-09
* No LILO in the alt installer; Grub2 failed to produce a bootable system with RAID+LVM.
* Grub2 still fails to load it's menu using a /boot outside of the RAID (the very fact that having a separate /boot makes the RAID pointless is beside the point).
* ifup runs before resolvconf during boot, preventing /etc/resolv.conf from being configured.
* Audio is... crap.  Can't touch the mixer, if I do the audio is toast, and audio from VirtualBox under Vista/Win7 is a lost cause regardless.
* There is a deadlock between statd and automounter during shutdown; if statd is running the system hangs.  So no locks in NFS for me.

All in all, a seriously craptastic release.

---

### Post by Tanker Bob on 2009-11-09

### Post by ffaker on 2009-11-10
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2009/11/03/karmic_koala_frustration/](http://www.theregister.co.uk/2009/11/03/karmic_koala_frustration/)

Quite interesting how this Register article is missrepresenting the results of this poll: "Only around 10 per cent of those upgrading or installing reported a completely flawless experience."

Although this figure is now incorrect, I can believe that the numbers have changed since this article was published a week ago (Ubuntu fans flocking from the Reg to vote up Ubuntu). 

So it's not a lie exactly, but it is a missrepresentation, because it does not reflect key realities:

1) Because there are so many options in the poll, the results are split, which means 10% is not as low as it seems when quoted out of context.

2) Just as many people reported a flawless install or upgrade anyway.

3) Most people posting here are posting because they have had specific problems: those with flawless experiences are not likely to bother posting. Why would they: they're not having problems!

---

### Post by mikewhatever on 2009-11-10
I've done a clean install of Karmic about a week ago, having partimaged Jaunty, in case I want to go back later. The hardware in question is an HP Pavilion DV52xx, Intel based with nvidia graphics. The experience has been very positive so far:

_Sound_
Worked out of the box, speakers + microphone, no configurations needed. Skype worked out of the box. This is the first release since Hardy, that didn't make me remove pulseaudio.

_Graphics_
Nvidia geforce 7400go, nvidia-glx-185 driver installed and works as expected, Coompiz running, no manual configs done or needed.

_Wireless_
Intel 3945abg, open source driver, works as expected on the home wireless network with wpa2 encryption. Sharing the Public folder with another computer running Jaunty was successful. Planning a more comprehensive samba setup later on.

_Grub2_
Worked as expected, had to read some documentation to get rid of unneeded Windows entries in the menu (used to comment them out in the old menu.lst). Karmic boots noticeably faster, and shuts down much faster then Jaunty.

_Synaptics touchpad_
Works as expected, two finger scrolling and disabling when typing are much welcome.

_Multimedia buttons_
Volume up/down/mute, brightness fn+f7/f8 worked out of the box.

I'll test and update on suspend/hibernate shortly.

---

### Post by scottuss on 2009-11-10

Apart from that, meh, it's another release... hopefully Lucid will bring some real improvements.

### Post by djchandler on 2009-11-10
I have several computers, and I finally found one that karmic works on flawlessly. It's also the newest build of the ones tried so far.

Maybe some of us should get to vote more than once, because this experience certainly more than balances my first post in this thread.

BTW, the color scheme looks great on this LCD. But it sure looked bad on the old CRT that I first tried Karmic on. It was admittedly old, whereas this LCD is close to state-of-the-art.

Advice for those wanting some: Use karmic on newer hardware. If you have somewhat older hardware, consider Debian 5.0. Debian makes compromises for stability and consistency. Ubuntu is pushing the envelope, especially with the last intermediate  release before the next LTS release. That's the pattern I am recognizing. YMMV.

One gripe. This is a dual boot system with Windows 7 RTM (for evaluation purposes). I had over 300GB of unallocated space on my hard drive ready for this clean install. The Ubiquity partitioner defaulted to cramming Karmic into free space on the Windows 7 partition, which only provided 141GB, and would have had to move the Windows data around and re-partition. The partition routine needs to be smarter than that, and should have asked to use unallocated hard drive space first, not subdivide the ntfs partition.

### Post by srcshelton on 2009-11-10
I've had a number of issues with UNR 9.10, which I've documented here:

[http://blog.stuart.shelton.me/archives/487](http://blog.stuart.shelton.me/archives/487)

Neither "*Install - worked but had few things to fix, nothing serious though*" nor "*Install - got many problems that i've not been able to solve*" really describes the situation: I've hit some pretty serious problems which I think are probably outside of the abilities of the average user to fix... but I have nevertheless been able to fix them (and I freely admit that I may be underestimating the skill and perseverance of the average Ubuntu user - but this thing **must** be simple enough that my parents could install it without running into problems).

One of my colleagues had an issue where 9.10 Desktop failed to install because his boot partition wasn't large enough to hold the existing kernels as well as the 9.10 kernel.  I'd imagine that this is one of the more common edge-cases, and certainly when this issue arises the installer should be intelligent enough to ask whether earlier kernels should be archived/removed rather than immediately failing and rolling back.

### Post by Tanker Bob on 2009-11-10
> **KeesM said:**
> What would be the risk of the upgrade doing the same (and my data not really backed up)?

You risk all your data. Redundant backups are key to a good night's sleep.

If the LiveCD doesn't run correctly, there's a very high probability that a full install won't either. That's not 100% certain, but it's pretty close to it. That's one of the reasons for the LiveCD concept.

Another approach would be to dual boot with your current setup and a karmic installation. You'll have to resize your current partition with GParted or an equivalent from a CD first to make room for another installation. Once you get things sorted out, you can delete the second partition, expand the original, and upgrade the main partition to karmic.

---

### Post by blueridgedog on 2009-11-10
I did a clean install in order to migrate to ext4 and 64 bit.  After installing I activated the nvidia driver and then:

```
sudo wget http://www.medibuntu.org/sources.list.d/$(lsb_release -cs).list \
 --output-document=/etc/apt/sources.list.d/medibuntu.list &&
sudo apt-get -q update &&
sudo apt-get --yes -q --allow-unauthenticated install medibuntu-keyring &&
sudo apt-get -q update &&
sudo apt-get install libdvdcss2 &&
sudo apt-get install w64codecs &&
sudo apt-get install build-essential && 
sudo apt-get install gnome-color-chooser &&
sudo apt-get install conky &&
sudo apt-get install lm-sensors &&
sudo apt-get install vim
```

I then restored the lines in /etc/fstab that mounted my documents drive and backup drives when hot swapped, put in a backup drive and restored my /home/USER/Desktop, /home/USER/.mozilla folder and /home/USER/.virtualbox folder.  At that point everything worked and I was more or less up and running.

I had one problem.  I had to extract an old 32 bit library for Floola to work and I found out that the current version of handbrake is busted for Linux, but I did not save the install file for the last version.

On the whole I am very satisfied.

---

### Post by bliffle on 2009-11-10
I had a really dumb problem with partitions. I'm sure it's some little thing I've overlooked, and I should know better since I've done at least 6-8 installs over the past 3 years. The install actually went all the way through but I didn't end up with the partition sizes I wanted.

I started with a new Thinkpad X60s (what a sweetheart!) with a good XP on it, and exercised the XP while awaiting 9.10 so that I could dual-boot the two systems.

I wanted to endup with the XP shrunk down to 20gb and the new Karmic in the remaining 40gb, since I mainly use ubuntu with only occasional XP use.

So I first ran good old GPARTED and shrunk the XP down to 20gb, allocated a 32gb EXT3 partition (primary) to receive the Karmic and a 4gb linux-swap (primary). I thought that would be right, but maybe the two new partitions should be'extended'? Anyway, the partition map looked good to me: 3 partitions in the desired sizes.

I torrented the 9.10, checked the CD, loaded from CD to run a few tests, then hit the "INSTALL".

The partition screen was confusing. I expected partition to ask me if I wanted it to load Karmic into that nice big 32gb, but no! Partition asked if I wanted to install side-by-side with XP or manually allocate partitions. 

I tried manual, but eventually it complained that I didn't have a 'root' partition. So I used GPARTED to unallocate those two new partitions, figuring I'd allocated them wrong. 

Then I went back (with XP in it's 20gb and the rest 'unallocated') and tried a 'side-by-side', since that seemed reasonable. Then the install ran and produced a new allocation with 32gb for XP, 22gb for Karmic and 1gb for swap.

That system works pretty good, but it's not at all the allocation I wanted.

I probably took the wrong path in partitioning, but it wasn't at all clear what to do.

I think that what I'll do is run GPARTED, shrink XP down to 20gb, unallocate the Karmic partitions and retry the install. I don'tknow what that'll do to GRUB, though.

### Post by stevebuck on 2009-11-10
This is actually a follow up to my previous thread
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8232497#post8232497](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8232497#post8232497) 
My netbook is about one month old and I was able to purchase it without OS. I used an OEM license to install WinXP Home in the first partition. This required downloading and installing drivers for just about everything (LAN, WLAN, BTooth, VGA, etc.) I downloaded Remix 9.04 image and used the image tool to write to a 1GB USB flash drive and then booted the USB. I first ran in 'live' mode and within about 20 minutes of playing decided I wanted this on my harddrive. Even in live mode, all the hardware was working. Installation was easy but I did need to take care doing partitioning because I wanted to leave WinXP and also leave space for another OS. The first try failed because I had not created a swap partition, but after that, smooth sailing. 

Over the course of one month, the update manager would pop up and inform me of available updates. I would select them all and let it do its thing. I even had a kernel upgrade that went without a hitch. So when 9.10 was released, and my upgrade manager popped up showing the new release I went ahead and let it rip. BIG MISTAKE.

Obviously this version was not well tested in upgrade mode. Many people have made the same mistake of not letting the install modify of grub menu.lst because we are using multiple boots. This lead to the new kernel not booting and many incompatible problems like no sound, touch pad not working. OK, so even getting the new kernel booting seemed OK, but after a week of use, things just didn't seem right. Colors missing, icons running off the screen, several programs requiring reinstall. It really looked like the upgrade did not differentiate between normal desktop and Remix edition.

So, last night I downloaded the netbook-remix image, put it on a USB flash and booted in live mode. Sure enough, there were subtle differences, but differences there were. Next, I proceeded to re-install 9.10 on clean partitions. I only needed to re-install Thunderbird which I use for my personal mail account and my necessary tools like PuTTY, Filezilla, Wireshark, and Zenmap. I feel sorry for anyone that has spent hours and hours tweaking their system and accumulated loads of data and then needs to do a re-install. For most of us, that cannot go into the system to find these compatibility issues created by the upgrade, re-installing is the only option.

After a complete re-install, the netbook looks like a 'netbook' again. In the future I will be very careful not to do an upgrade to a major release unless I see many positive results on this forum.  The netbook is running fine now, and I'm typing on it at the moment. But I'll edit this post if I find any more problems and I'll continue to read the other posts. It looks like these releases need to be spaced further apart and given more test time. More care needs to be put into recognizing the Remix release as there must be some differences in the desktop compared with a normal non-remix build. 

Production releases should be extremely stable, otherwise Ubuntu will start to be as unattractive as Micro$oft.

```
Hardware:
Lenovo S10-2 'Sailing' (Manufactured Sept. 2009)
Atom N280 (1.67G 667M-FSB)
2GB DDR2
250GB WD HD
Realtek LAN and Audio
Broadcom WLAN and Bluetooth
```

### Post by bousozoku on 2009-11-10
I updated on November 8th from 9.04 and it went well.  Having seen this poll, I was a bit nervous about upgrading within the first two months.  It sounded as though there were quite a few problems and my previous upgrades did not go well.

This upgrade was surprisingly smooth.  Both my Broadcom-based WiFi card and my nVidia 7600 GS graphics card were handled correctly, unlike the upgrades to 8.10 and 9.04.

9.10 even recognises my mobile phone as a modem and will connect, though I've yet to get it to actually work with the browser.  This is better than 9.04 as it only recognised it and it's certainly better than Mac OS X Snow Leopard, as Apple went from perfect usage to no capabilities at all.

### Post by slooksterpsv on 2009-11-10
Ok to reply to the main thread's topics before I state my other comments, here we go:

Upgrading was flawed, for a few reasons, which were ultimately fixable:

Problem: Upgrading my NetBook - Acer Aspire One 751h - from UNR 9.04 to UNR 9.10 made it to where I couldn't use my trackpad.
Fix: Grub didn't update the menu.lst to contain the latest kernel because I'm dual-booting Vista and UNR and chose not to update it - easy fix open pico or nano and open /boot/grub/menu.lst and change a line to the latest kernel version.

Problem: Upgrading my Netbook also made the graphics slow down.
Fix: I installed the latest GMA500 drivers from a 3rd party repository and recreated my X11 configuration.

Problem: Sound wasn't working after upgrade.
Fix: Had to modify the alsabase.conf to make it load the snd-hda-intel to reflect model=acer-aspire

Other than that I'm very happy with Karmic Koala. My Netbook I'll have to reformat cause I don't think it has EXT4 and I love EXT4.

I installed Karmic Koala on my Desktop, ended up dropping my Win 7 RC drive and couldn't be happier. The only thing I need now is for NetFlix to work on Linux then it's bye bye Windows - and no I'm not going to run an XP Virtualized image nor Vista Virtualized Image, I want it to work natively.

Best OS I've used, I've been using it all day, haven't booted into Windows once.

EDIT:
One last thing, since this is working so well and is so nice, I've even signed up for the 2GB Free Space to transfer between my big PC and my Netbook.

---

### Post by pullmoll on 2009-11-11
After installing Karmic Koala I couldn't get DSL to work, so I had no internet to look out for problem solutions. DUH! Fortunately I'm not a newbie and so knew the *apropos* command, and *apropos pppoe* then told me there was a *pppoeconf*. Guess what? Running that tool worked and I'm happy now.

For a release like 9.10, which is otherwise the best distribution I've seen so far, I would have expected some serious testing of the elementary internet connection thingies like ppp/modem, pppoe and DSL before the final **GO**.

### Post by anglican on 2009-11-11
So far I've upgraded on two Netbooks (AAO ZG5 and D250) with no problems. BIOS upgrades fixed problems with fan control (ZG5) and power monitoring (D250 hibernated when AC either connected or disconnected). I also updated an ancient desktop (650 MHz PIII) which gave no problems. I use xubuntu without NBR on all of these.

Like many here I didn't like the greeter offering me usernames, easily fixed with the downside that you have to click to get a username prompt. Booting is measurably slower but still nowhere near as bad as XP (which two of my systems dual-boot into).

Otherwise I'm very happy, especially with the improved xorg driver for the on-board Intel graphics on the AAO's. Now I can use the SVGA, e.g. for presentations, without having to first switch off compiz :D

### Post by OM NOM NOM on 2009-11-11
First - A sincere and heartfelt THANK YOU to Ubuntu and the GNU/linux community for helping us free our systems! :-) 


  To begin, LOVE Jaunty. It has been such a stable and strong release for me that it convinced me to make the switch over to Ubuntu on my laptop, desktop and home server. Printer sharing - flawless. Auto detecting all the hardware - perfect. 

When Karmic came around (and being impetuous) I immediately did a fresh install on my home "server", which is a P4 2.8 Hp D530. one 500GB SATA drive (home), one 60GB ATA drive (/ and swap). 

 Under Jaunty everything seemed to work perfectly. From scratch install to  permissions configured, remote access, the works in under an hour. It detected my two drives flawlessly. with Karmic however, it was almost the exact opposite:

- My ATA drive was undetectable by Karmic. Not able to explain that.

- it recognized my printer, but would not accept print jobs from other users. Again unable to explain, it was almost instantaneous in Jaunty.

- I couldn't get the proper file and samba permissions to take, even from a command line. I thought in might have something to do with ext4, so I reformatted and went back to ext3. Same issues. 

- Writing to a shared drive - other machines would intermittently pause while writing, greatly extending the time to write files over a network. Quick as lightning in Jaunty. 

- My karmic server then began randomly dropping off from the network, and dropping the USB connection to my backup drive. 

So I reverted my machines back to 9.04 and all is well. I have to say though that the whole experience was very disheartening. I love FOSS and Ubuntu: The freedom, the community, the fantastic social experience. I've been so much happier with computing since I discovered Jaunty and have found so many great uses for it in mine and my family's life. 

But maybe a new revision every six months is pushing a bit too much? Or perhaps these releases are for the cutting edge folks, and I should stick  with the LTS versions?

---

### Post by owenrees on 2009-11-11
I upgraded and have had a lot of networking problems which are probably related to IPv6. I have made some progress with some of them, but the current killer is that Synaptic seems unable to be coerced into using only the IPv4 that will work with my ADSL modem/router (which I am not in a position to replace right now, even if I could be sure of finding one that does IPv6 correctly).  I have read a lot of the suggestions for disabling IPv6 or otherwise working around the problems but none actually worked completely for me. I was surprised and disappointed when, after apparently successfully disabling IPv6 in the boot phase, Synaptic reported connection errors quoting IPv6 addresses!  My focus on Synaptic is, of course, because it will be more difficult to find and install anything that fixes any of the problems when that is what is broken.

### Post by Zoot7 on 2009-11-11
I did a clean install. No problems at all.

The only issue I had was sound from flash with pulseaudio - I wouldn't get sound from flash if I'd another audio application was open on account of pulseaudio hogging alsa and flash wanting to use alsa. 
What sorted it for me was to download and compile the source of the pulse audio flash plugin flashplugin-nonfree-pulse as it doesn't seem to exist in the repos.. :confused: This way flash uses pulseaudio directly rather than looking to alsa, oss or esound.

For anyone that has the same issue, here's what I did:
```
sudo apt-get install checkinstall
sudo apt-get install libpulse-dev
wget http://pulseaudio.vdbonline.net/flashplugin-nonfree-pulse_0.1~000.tar.gz && tar xvfz flashplugin-nonfree-pulse_0.1~000.tar.gz
cd flashplugin-nonfree-pulse-0.1~000
make
sudo checkinstall
```

### Post by gej on 2009-11-11
Well, it's been a few days.  New problems have arisen and old ones go unresolved.  The sound problem (buzzing, humming) persists during playback.  Changing the volume stops the humming/buzzing temporarily but crops up when attempting to open any app which requires another adjustment to the volume --- UGH!  What a pain!

Tried to load Amarok through the SPM, it doesn't work at all.  No sound whatsoever but I can hear a quiet hiss through the speakers.  I tried several songs from my collection -- none worked through Amarok.  This one baffles me since it was freshly installed from the pkg manager.

Video of any sort is outrageously choppy regardless of source.

Gnome crashes/freezes for no reason whatsoever (kernel panic?).  There is no way to recover/reclaim control.  I tried every possible keyboard combination to get me anywhere, even a CLI.. Nada.  Ice cube.  A hard shutdown/cold reboot was necessary.

Boot time still runs in the 2-min+ range.  9.04 would boot in about 45 seconds but this GRUB2 seems to take a very long time.

Hibernate/Sleep sometimes locks the machine.  Fix is hard shutdown using power button on box.

So far, 9.10 has been nothing but utter disappointment.  I keep chugging along trying to work out issues but will be loading another distro on this old box very soon.  I hope the bugs get worked out in 9.10 but I simply don't have the time to continuously tweak my system.  I was sure this 'upgrade' would help solve problems but it actually created so many that it's practically unusable.

Fare thee well Ubu community. :popcorn:

### Post by BavarianPH on 2009-11-11
A** clean** install of official Karmic Koala 9.10 did away with many problems that the beta version had.

I am optimistic, that keeping up with updates (I update once or twice / day, including proposed updates) will eventually repair most problems.

Just today's update eliminated the Main Menu's 8 second delay, probably due to the newest kernel updates, including linux-2.6.31-15.50.

Grub2 problems were fixed by a clean install of 9.10.

However, the related **power-management** problem remains.

**Suspend** happens regardless of settings;

the Monitor goes to sleep within 15 minutes, which is very annoying

when one is trying to watch a movie;

And if I wait too long, I have to push the PC suspend button to awake the PC. -

I am still waiting for updates to **xserver-xorg-video-intel**.

Resolutions and refresh rates remain lower than they were in Jaunty.

WINE still has a long way to make most Windows software work well.

**Virtual CDs & DVDs with _drive letters_** is not supported in Ubuntu,

and WINE is not accepted by virtual CD or clone CD windows software.

In Windows I had up to 15 CDs running with Magicdisc 1.6, 

anything from Encyclopedias to Chessmaster. -

Still, I believe, Ubuntu is far superior to Windows in many ways!

Ubuntu is much faster in booting up, in shutting down, in running software.

Ubuntu does not lock up, unless I try to run certain Windows programs.

Ubuntu security is better; ext4 is superior to ntfs.

Linux has all the apps one needs, and more.

Most importantly, Ubuntu is a peaceful community, with plenty of free support.

Linux software is free, Ubuntu is free.

For the most part Ubuntu is driven by love, not by greed and false promises.

BavarianPH, 

**Ubuntu forever!**

---

### Post by 5au1 on 2009-11-11
May experience with Karmic can be summed up into one sentence: I just reinstalled my Jaunty backup.

The first problem I've faced was after reinstalling winxp, although it's in part win's problem, I couldn't recover Grub as I used to, namely by booting a live-cd and entering some commands into Grub's interpreter. Instead I reinstalled grub with the command "grub-install /dev/hda". It recover grub but wasn't able to boot win. Finally SuperGrub disk did the magic, don't know how, but Grub was Ok.

After that, the first thing that bug me was the booting time. It took at least three times more than jaunty, but the real problem came when I tried to modify fstab file in order to mount my win partitions at boot. Don't know exactly why but the fstab that worked fine since Hardy suddenly didn't work anymore. Apparently the user/users option was part of the problem...

I' wasn't happy with the way I had to mount the partitions, but hoped it would be fixed soon, however other things started to happen, for instance, a group created with the GUI option could only be deleted through the console, otherwise it appeared the next time you enter the GUI.

Well, I know these are no serious problems and I should stick with Karmic for a couple of weeks more, however, after reading some comments on the web I realized that there is no hurry on installing every new version that comes out. I'm really happy with my Jaunty installation.

Cheers

### Post by petaramesh on 2009-11-12
My overall Kubuntu karmic upgrade report :

- So far upgraded 7 machines (3 laptops, 1 netbook, 3 desktops) to Kubuntu Karmic, most of them from Jaunty, some from Hardy.

- I have to say that it's my best Kubuntu upgrade experience so far, as *all* the machines upgrades succedeed and *none* really badly failed in progress. With previous versions I always had had a number of machines for which upgrade badly failed and necessitated a lot of manual work before being able to boot again. But not this time :-)

- Here are the small issues I however noticed :

1/ On one machine upon 7, upgraded from Jaunty, after upgrade the machine still booted to the old hardy kernel (no sound - no Synaptics touchpad...) ; new kernel was installed but missing from the menu.lst and running update-grub didn't help. had to manually add the new kernel to menu.lst.

2/ On 2 machines upon 7, upgraded from Hardy, the upgrade of "flashplugin-nonfree" failed because the PREVIOUS installed version failed uninstalling (trying to remove symlinks that didn't exist). This b0rked the end of the whole upgrade process until I manually edited the old "flashplugin-nonfree" "prerm" script in /var/apt...
That's a pity that failing to remove properly an *old* package can make apt so unhappy that it will fail finishing install and config of a number of other new packages until this single thing is solved...
(However in the Karmic upgrade, this didn't prevent the dist upgrade procedure from finishing without crashing, even somewhat incomplete, and the machine from booting properly. Only a number of packages weren't fully configured/installed until I fixed the Flash plugin issue).

After upgrade, didn't notice any major issue on any machine, most of them running on encrypted LVM.

A lot of things got much improved, a few regressions, such as the Wi-Fi card (ath9k driver) on my EeePC that will work in a very flaky way after a machine suspend / resume until I manually rmmod then modprobe the driver.

Besides that, my upgrade experience is quite positive.

---

### Post by Pantcon1 on 2009-11-12
My 'upgrade' from x64 Kubuntu Jaunty to Karmic failed midway and I couldn't re-boot. Unfortunately as I'm still a 'Newbie', technically speaking, I simply switched to a fresh 'install' method instead. However, I then found the nVidia x64 driver was crashing at the initial Kubuntu start-up panel. All I had working was a command line session and not a clue how to fix things. As I had also burnt a 32 bit LiveCD for an old machine of mine, I then attempted an i386 install. This time there was no nVidia problems and I had the wonderful KDE GUI back on my screen, but I then hit other snags. Internet performance problems on Firefox and Konqueror highlighted dreadful DNS performance (20sec+ BBC home page). Yet my old standby Jaunty PC running on the same network/router was fine. Tried various suggestions that have been posted and it was disabling IPv6 in the Kernel that finally fixed it. (Is IPv6 support new in this release?). My ISP is France Telecom (ADSL2+ with an Orange 'Livebox' router).
Karmic has certainly been a hassle to implement which is a shame because both my previous releases on the same hardware went without a hitch. At least I have learned a lot more about Linux and now want to improve my knowledge and skills.
I remain very grateful to the 'Open Source' community for providing an escape to non-proprietary PC 'Freedom'. Long Live Linux!

### Post by randlieb on 2009-11-12
I had a painless upgrade with no problems.

Post upgrade is another story.

1. It boots up ok but when it gets to the screen with the little Ubuntu logo I get text at the bottom of the screen saying something to the effect 'file system check happening' (then a file path shown) then 'press escape if you want to bypass the file check'. And all the time this text is blinking off and on with the file path remaining the same. I hit escape and can go to the desktop no problem but the blinking text is a puzzle. It seems to be trying to check the files but is hung on the one file.

2. System is slooooow. Moving from workspace to workspace there is a definite pause if I have an application open. Takes a few seconds to fully bring the app into focus. Sometimes the processor is working 100% just to get to a new workspace with an app already open in it (Thunderbird being the biggest, but not the only, culprit).

3. What the heck happened to totem-xine? It shows it's installed in Synaptic Package Manager and the new Ubuntu Software Center. However I click on the menu item and it says "Failed to execute child process "/usr/bin/totem-xine" (No such file or directory)". And sure enough I look in usr/bin and totem-xine is no where to be found. Totem with gstreamer is there however. I have other options for playback of course but really prefer totem xine for dvd's.

4. I really don't like the new volume control interface. One thing that was simply made more complicated.

Well those are my issues with Karmic. Nothing that stops me from using my computer but it isn't nearly as smooth and painless running Karmic as Jaunty was. These issues were not occurring with Jaunty.

I have been running Ubuntu since 5.04 and each release seems to be a couple steps forward along with a couple of steps back! Some things are smoother/easier to use and others it's 'what the heck were you thinking'?! Still I guess it wouldn't be Linux if it all went smooth!;)

### Post by papikondalu on 2009-11-12
> **randlieb said:**
> I had a painless upgrade with no problems.

Post upgrade is another story.

1. It boots up ok but when it gets to the screen with the little Ubuntu logo I get text at the bottom of the screen saying something to the effect 'file system check happening' (then a file path shown) then 'press escape if you want to bypass the file check'. And all the time this text is blinking off and on with the file path remaining the same. I hit escape and can go to the desktop no problem but the blinking text is a puzzle. It seems to be trying to check the files but is hung on the one file.


I think you should let the filesystem check go through. The long wait in one state might be due to filesystem inconsistencies in a large contiguous block.

It might be one of the reasons for system slow down due to problems in  filesystem by the application you are using (say a browser while disk caching a page).

-PK

### Post by papikondalu on 2009-11-12
> **whitey_1 said:**
> Hello,

I havent been able to install Ubuntu after many attempts and hours of attention.

The forum members have been very helpful, one person in particular has been great, but the trouble is that my problem persists, it has not changed.

It wont install, it should be just a basic operation, i followed all the instructions, but the problem is still there, wont install, and now no one is replying to my original thread at all.

Any chance someone can help at all?

If you are doing a fresh install, are you able to run it in the live CD mode? For running in the live CD mode, boot your system with the Ubuntu CD. When the menu is shown, instead of selecting install, select "try Ubuntu without any changes to your computer". This will boot you into Ubuntu. If you are able to run Ubuntu fully (display, network, sound, etc) then go for a full install. This is a quick sanity check for any install.

-PK

Overall though i think i'm happy with it, although for me Jaunty was working perfectly, but i was always gonna upgrade regardless.

### Post by davidmb on 2009-11-13
Hi,

Last night I decided to give Kubuntu Karmic a try despite all the recent complaints and I must say it was a great and almost perfect experience.
Since Hardy, I've been following the same installation process and it has always worked, though I remember having issues with the ATI driver in Jaunty (who didn't).

Let me explain it...

My hard disk is split in 3 partitions: 2 small system partitions and 1 large for /home.

The system partitions store my current and previous Kubuntu.
When a new Kubuntu arrives, I install it on the oldest system partition.

But before that, I switch to console mode (Close session -> Go to console mode) to backup all my config files (all dot files and folders in my home).
And then I delete all of them  except the ones I want to keep across versions (just .aMule in my case) to let the new Kubuntu recreate a default config.
I think this method results in a cleaner install and the loss of preferences can be seen as a chance to discover new stuff. ;)

Then, on to install: it was very quick and easy even though the user interface was a bit sluggish, most probably because of the CD-ROM.
I chose Manual partitioning to set the right mount points: the old system partition on / (root) and the large partition on /home. Formatted the system partition only.

Then reboot and voilà! It works out of the box!
The first thing I did was a full system update before installing anything else.
Then, I went to System preferences to adjust my Kubuntu.

Here I found the first problem: after enabling Desktop effects, kwin and plasma crashed.
Then I realized that after the huge update I hadn't reboot, so I did it and everything was now fine.
The new open source ATI driver performs incredibly well; never ever has the proprietary driver worked so well. Congratulations to the team behind it!

So, after that, it's just styling, preferences and extra packages (firefox, kubuntu-restricted-extras and vlc being the most important for me).
And then I found the second problem: it's not possible to install Superkaramba widgets as plasmoids from the user interface. Luckily, Google helped me find the solution [here]("http://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?id=77470"):
```
plasmapkg --type=superkaramba -i Datei_Name.skz
```

And that's it! :)
Thanks if you made it this far!

### Post by BrMBr on 2009-11-13

This should solve.

### Post by s_raiguel on 2009-11-13
Since I chimed in here earlier to say that I had tried Karmic, only to go back to Jaunty, I thought it appropriate to add that a second attempt at installation went quite well. The system is a Dell Dimension 9200 desktop, with dual operating systems, but no dual boot. There are two hard drives, a RAID 0 array running Windows, and another running Ubuntu; each drive has its own boot record, and the boot disk is selected using the BIOS boot options, rather than a GRUB boot menu. My first installation was an upgrade from Jaunty. The upgrade per se went smoothly enough, but most flash videos would no longer play in Firefox, and encoded DVDs would generate errors and refuse to play. On the positive side, even non-repository applications, such as Google Earth and IOQuake, and problem hardware, such as a videocam and wireless headphones, worked as they did before the installation. I was pleased to see that the black-on-dark-gray text problem with Open Office/New Wave Theme had been fixed, and that certain hardware tweaks necessary to get hardware to work properly (e.g., using export LD_PRELOAD to get a Logitech webcam to work with Skype) were no longer necessary. 

After two or three days of trying this and that, making sure I had all the right libraries, etc., the DVD and Flash problems proved to be intractible and a deal-breaker for me, so I reinstalled Jaunty and reconfigured Jaunty to its previous state - a task made much easier by /home and /etc backups, and reinstalling packages using dpkg --get-selections and  dpkg --set-selections. I should add that when I do an Ubuntu install, I physically unplug the Windows drive to make sure that the installer doesn't monkey with the Windows boot loader or try to put GRUB on that disk. This keeps booting simple, and eliminates many possible issues that others have experienced attempting to install a dual-boot. 

Eventually, however, I decided to give Karmic one more try, this time as a fresh installation rather than an upgrade. Again, I made backups and saved the script of installed/deinstalled apps on the computer using dpkg. I reformatted and reinstalled, and the install went perfectly. After installing the necessary codecs, all media would now read flawlessly. Within a half a day, I had everything running under Karmic as well or better than it had been under Jaunty. On the basis of my experience, I would therefor recommend a fresh install, rather than relying on the upgrade. The install may take slightly longer than the upgrade, but using the dpkg commands to reinstall packages minimizes this disadvantage, not to mention the time saved trying to get broken applications to work properly after an upgrade.

---

### Post by MZ250Supa5 on 2009-11-13
Installed through upgrade, though somewhat dubious about this as have had problems in the past.  However, went really well, even if it did take several hours!  Fortunately on upgrade day I'd been working the night before, so was ready for some sleep...

After upgrading, everything seemed fine, and working well. Not sure about the new 'brown' theme, still a bit on the dull side, but it could be worse and be blue.  Most things seem to be working quite well, though some files seem to have been lost in the upgrade, though I haven't yet searched for them.  Annoyingly the system now has a tendency to freeze, though this bug will no doubt be fixed pretty quickly.  I have a major gripe about MythTV though.  The theme seems much better, and some of the 'useability' is much improved, but sadly, on my system I am having major problems in getting the system to remember my TV card, as I seem to have to re-install and retune all channels everytime I boot into the system - a real pain.  I'm also finding that video is freezing, both 'live' and recordings.  I suspect these are related, as I know that 'live' on MythTV is actually on a slight delay.

Ending on a positive note, I am pleased to see that the Welsh language localisation is so much better than in Jaunty, which was a bit of a joke to say the least.  Some of the spelling is a bit suspect, but that's probably due to the first-language translators making the usual silly mistakes ;)  Fair play though, a very good job done here, and though I am really nitpicking about the spelling, I can see what a daunting task translation is at times, not least the translation of technical terms that will be understood by speakers unused to technical terms in their own language - I'm one of those, and sometimes do spend some time scratching my head before understanding.  All good clean fun!  

I'm particularly looking forward to trying the new proprietory ATi drivers, and hoping they work, as then I don't have to buy an Nvidia graphics card to connect my system to my projector thus allowing me to get rid of another few boxes that currently clutter up the living room.

I was expecting much more of a struggle with Karmic, especially as I had a bit of bovver with Jaunty before getting everything working well.  Only another five and a half months until the next upgrade...

---

### Post by cornholder on 2009-11-14
/moved from a different thread

I did the update from 9.04 to 9.10 through the Update Manager, in the hopes it would solve my problems customizing the usplash and GDM login screens. Facepalm.jpg; after trying and failing even harder, I read around here and found out that they took away a lot of the options we had to play with those screens in the effort to reduce boot and shutdown times. 

When I first updated, it had my old usplash but it was all squashed into the middle of the screen at a smaller resolution. After changing the usplash to a different one, it decided I was only going to get the verbose text of the processes starting up. So currently on startup I get that in tiny font on a black screen, then somehow it gave me a Mythbuntu GDM theme that looks like grey corduroy, then it switches to my desktop wallpaper with an ugly Windows95-grey login box, then after login it switches back to the Mythbuntu corduroy while it plays my login sound and then a few seconds after that, I'm in. Synaptic says to remove the Mythbuntu package, it will also have to remove xsplash and ubuntu-desktop, which doesn't sound like something I want to do. Total login time is around 30-35 seconds, probably 10 seconds longer than Jaunty took. Shutdown is definitely a few seconds faster, however.

I am currently backing up all my movies and junk - which is taking 15-20 minutes per movie because the update to 9.10 didn't magically install the usb2 drivers that I lost last time I reinstalled 9.04 a few months ago - because I bring my laptop all over to show friends stuff, and I think it'd be cool to turn it on and have it say something like "HEATING PRIMARY REACTION CHAMBER" or "SATURATING PLASMA FIELD" while a status bar builds. That in itself is worth doing a clean install of 9.04 to me.

### Post by Don_Nadie on 2009-11-15
Dell Inspiron 6400 laptop
Intel Core Duo T2300 @ 1.66GHz
2GB RAM (maxed out)
120GB HD, 12GB wubi folder
wubi 9.04 to Ubuntu 9.10: all Ubuntu upgrades/wubi installs FAIL

I tried to upgrade wubi 9.04 to Ubuntu 9.10 using the Update Manager. (Sorry, I don't have wubi installed anymore and don't remember what the app is called.) After getting almost all the way through, my laptop froze up when I clicked the button that said something like "Restart Now." By froze up I mean there was no disk activity, there was no response to my keyboard and the cursor didn't move when I moved my mouse.

I forced my laptop off by holding down the power button. I booted into wubi/Ubuntu 9.10 and found that my touchpad buttons acted right-handed even though I had the left-handed button checked in my mouse settings. When I plugged in my USB mouse, it acted correctly, i.e., left-handed. I couldn't fix this problem by checking one box, then the other, rebooting, etc. I didn't look for other problems with this upgrade.

I thought I'd try a clean install of wubi 9.10, so I uninstalled wubi, then got wubi.exe 9.10 and waited an hour or so for the Ubuntu download. With 6 minutes left, I got a fatal error pop-up which said "An error occurred: Permission denied. For more information, please see the log file ..." (See the attachment, assuming it shows up.) I uninstalled wubi and tried again. Same problem 2 more times, first uninstalling wubi each time before double-clicking wubi.exe.

The part of the log file I think is of interest reads like this:
______________________________________________
ERROR  TaskList: Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "\lib\bittorrent\RawServer.py", line 229, in listen_forever
  File "\lib\wubi\backends\common\btdownloader.py", line 70, in error_callback
DownloadError: Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "\lib\bittorrent\RawServer.py", line 221, in listen_forever
  File "\lib\bittorrent\Rerequester.py", line 96, in fail
  File "\lib\wubi\backends\common\btdownloader.py", line 70, in error_callback
DownloadError: Problem connecting to tracker - HTTP Error 400: Bad Request
______________________________________________

I don't think I'm going to bother with Ubuntu anymore. Operating systems seem like religions to me and I'm not very religious. The internet abounds with discussions of what's wrong with Micro$oft Window$. Linux is less resource intensive and more robust and secure than Window$. However, Window$ is light years ahead of Linux as far as hardware support goes and, because it's so ubiquitous, it has much a much larger help community than Linux.

I'm posting this for Ubuntu fundamentalists to ponder over. I don't think I'll be back here again. Hardware/peripherals work so much better in Window$ than in Linux and, despite its myriad shortcomings, I find it much easier to accomplish tasks in Window$ than in Linux, even though Window$ runs more slowly than Linux.

Examples of what I'm talking about:

[LIST]
[*]Synaptics touchpad functionality in Linux is a poor subset of subset of what the Synaptics Window$ driver provides
[*]When I installed the Ubuntu driver for my printer (Epson C79), I got an ink monitor for a 6 color/cartridge printer even though my printer is a 4 color printer
[*]US International Keyboard: Dead key support for multiple languages only needs 2 keystrokes in Window$; nobody in the Ubuntu forums could come up with dead key functionality using less than 3 keystrokes in Ubuntu
[*]Installing new hardware in Window$ is just a matter of double-clicking on setup.exe; in Linux installing new hardware leaves you with a mere ghost of the same hardware's capabilities available in Window$
[/LIST]
The list is far from complete. Window$ isn't wonderful or anything. It's just that I find Linux more off-putting than Window$.

### Post by Lateralis on 2009-11-15
Don_Nadie.

I'm sorry to hear that you've had such troubles with Ubuntu and will be leaving for good.  I'm not a Linux fundamentalist, insofar as I'm not fussed which operating system you chose to use and will not try and "convert" you, but I'd just like to add my two cents...

For me, installing new hardware on Linux has been pretty straightforward and actually requires me to do *nothing*; not even a simple "click setup.exe" was required.  There have been a couple of instances when things haven't played ball but I find it difficult to blame Linux distros for that.  A lot of the blame, and indeed responsibility for hardware support, must lie with the hardware manufacturers.  Companies such as Intel, HP and others do actually have very good Linux drivers and support for their hardware.  Other companies just don't give a rats ***, and that isn't the fault of Canonical.  Or Red Hat or anyone else, except for the hardware manufacturer themselves.  Unfortunately, this state of affairs won't change much unless consumers, ie. you and I, and companies such as Canonical and Red Hat start badgering corporations to actively support Linux distributions. But ultimately, the buck lies with the manufacturers; we can badger as much as we like but if they bury their head in the sand and refuse to do anything then that's that.  We just have to vote with our feet and go to corporations which *do* care.

Secondly, I'm not a big fan of Wubi installations, although this is a personal preference.  I normally suggest to people that they try out Wubi for a short time to give the operating system a "dry run", if you will, to see if they'll get on with it.  If they don't, then they don't and nothing has changed with their hard disk.  They can uninstall it like any other application and all is dandy.  If they do like it, then they can do a disk partition and install it properly.  


In any eventuality though, I wish you success in your future operating system-related endeavours.  If you do decide to try out a future version of Ubuntu - and I hope that you do, for it is updated every 6 months; Ubuntu now is vastly improved compared to Ubuntu 2 years ago - then you know where to find a community that will gladly and openly help you at every step of the way. =)
