### Post by ofb on 2009-11-15
Hmm. This poll needs a choice between *"worked but had few things to fix, nothing serious though"* and *"got many problems that i've not been able to solve"*

My show-stopper was the still-mysterious UUID bug that Karmic has introduced.

I was able to get around mine with a kludge, and I'm waiting for the good folks at launchpad to sort the various complaints down to the source issue, and create a true fix.

Otherwise the upgrade was almost flawless, with only very minor issues.

----

The most relevant UUID bug report I've found:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/428318](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/428318)

This is easy to see on my ext3 machine. Run 'ls /dev/disk/by-uuid/' on both the Jaunty and the Karmic LiveCDs. Karmic only lists four of my five partitions.

My temporary kludge is to use the device path (/dev/sda1) in my fstab instead of the UUID.

But I'm lucky, as my missing partition was /home and not /, like some. And yet other folks are getting strangeness like the wrong UUID placed in fstab. This is a serious problem for those it afflicts.


the minor issues:

At boot, volume is often muted and turned down.
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/453776](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/453776)

SMPlayer audio quality is poor, and the player has freezes, because it defaults to alsa and not pulse.
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/smplayer/+bug/414171](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/smplayer/+bug/414171)

Ctrl-Alt-Backspace behavior is changed yet again.
[http://www.ubuntu.com/getubuntu/releasenotes/910](http://www.ubuntu.com/getubuntu/releasenotes/910)

and two or maybe three other minor items that I fixed so quickly I've forgotten them.

So other than the severe UUID issue, the upgrade really went very well. Kudos to the Ubuntu team.

Since a horrendous upgrade experience a couple of years ago, I've been doing fresh installs. This upgrade went well enough that I'll be ugrading next time too.

----

Off topic... Do I /like/ Karmic? It's okay. Quite a number of people reported Karmic is faster, which is most welcome after Jaunty and Ibex. I'm sorry to report this speed improvement did not occur on an older machine like mine. (XP 1300, 512 RAM, ATI 9600.)

Perfomance tweaking is complex. It's entirely possible Karmic has improvements that can only show up on faster hardware.

I'd suggest people with older hardware who are happy with Hardy LTS, keep using Hardy. Karmic is definitely slower. If anything it might be even a little more sluggish than Jaunty.

(And yes, you (and I) should really start thinking about upgrading your hardware. It's due.)

### Post by EarlW on 2009-11-16
I was never able to get Ubuntu 9.04 to boot up and run on my Dell Inspiron 7500 laptop.  It would just hang on boot.    But 9.10 installed just fine.  Just one little problem: I couldn't use the TouchPad :sad:.  I plugged in a USB mouse and used that until I found a solution (I don't know exactly which part of this worked, so I'm including everything I did):

```
I installed these:

  sudo apt-get install xserver-xorg-input-synaptics
  sudo apt-get install tpconfig
  sudo apt-get install gsynaptics

I added the **shmconfig.fdi** and **99-x11-synaptics.fdi** files:

---------------------------------
**/etc/hal/fdi/policy/shmconfig.fdi**
---------------------------------
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <device>
    <match key="input.x11_driver" string="synaptics">
      <merge key="input.x11_options.SHMConfig" type="string">on</merge>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>

----------------------------------------
**/etc/hal/fdi/policy/99-x11-synaptics.fdi**
----------------------------------------
synaptics   true   1  2  3   2000   true   true   true   false 
```That did the trick.  Now I have Ubuntu 9.10 running on my Dell 7500. It is pretty responsive too.  Didn't even know that old laptop could be so useful.

Great job Ubuntu developers!

### Post by touchlikefire on 2009-11-16

Unresolved Problems:
[LIST]

[*]network manager just acts silly upon resume from sleep, taking unacceptably long to attach to a network, and often timing out

[*]notifications are now about 200px below the panel, which is quite ugly, I consider this a bug even though it seems to have been done on purpose
[*]SHOWSTOPPER: devicekit or whichever is responsible for recognizing USB device acts sillier than network-manager: refuses to reliably recognize my MP3 player in either MTP or USB mode, and this pisses me off.  Interestingly, Rhythmbox sees it even when nautilus doesn't, but only after about 7 minutes of thinking about it

Resolved Issues:
[list]
[*] Sound popping and crackling - disabled new config option per instructions in forums
[/list]

### Post by The Thug on 2009-11-16
Had been using 9.04 on my laptop for a number of months and decided to upgrade when the beta release of 9.10 came out. Normal updates continued on a daily basis and I had no problems whatsoever.

That evening at home I plugged in my 3G card and managed to connect to the net but the connection bombed out continuously. STRIKE 1. 

The next day I re-connected to our LAN and all of a sudden my settings in Evolution (and passwords) were not recognized. I connect to an Exchange 2003 server through OWA and no matter how I tweaked the settings I could not connect to the server. STRIKE 2.

I then decided to see if my laptop sound (Realteck) was working out of the box or if I had to re-tweak the alsa-base.conf file as I had done with 9.04. Nope not working. Not a major issue but STRIKE 3.

Without the use of 3G and Evolution it was a straight forward decision, dump 9.10 and reinstall 9.04.

Today, I've updated back to 9.10 as I had previously done without any issues. My system is pretty straight forward and I'm running it entirely untweaked (apart from Alsa-base), so why the fresh install didn't work for me, I have no idea.

### Post by mcloide on 2009-11-16
This last upgrade for me feels like a major mistake. At first I have got just some small bugs and a annoying bug. The annoying one was that every time that I have tried to reboot or shut down I was getting the same issue that required a hardware shutdown on my machine.

Today, for the first time, I got a major bug. After rebooting my XServer was fully gone. The terminal was the only thing opened. At this moment, until I find a secondary solution, there is no way I can use my normal user with Xubuntu.

### Post by jeffwrule on 2009-11-16
I was hoping to install 9.10 on a system with a 4x500G sata raid5 / lvm configuration booting off a small 100MB non-raid/non-lvm partition.

booted the live CD/Installer and pre-configured my devices with the following commands.

apt-get mdadm
apt-get lvm2
mdadm -A -s
vgscan

Then used the mdadm command line tool, lvm graphical tool and mke2fs (with a proper stride option), this is key to getting performance on your raid5 disks. 

Proceeded with the Install and it worked perfectly, but after boot, grub could not find my raid / lvm disks.  Apparently it is not configured with support for these devices.

No joy.

Read the install instructions and was pointed to the alternate installer (non-graphical).  Started it last night and went to bed.  This AM it had failed in the install-packages section (I think).  

The tough thing here is that the non-graphical installer just says something went wrong, but does not say exactly what or were to look for the log of the problem.  I am sure that is here somewhere, I will follow-up with that process tonight.

Tried restarting and no joy.

Will try again this evening while I watch it during the entire process.

I would really like it if the raid/lvm pieces were added to the standard installer.  They are quite statable and mature and while not every desktop enthusist uses them I think they would be a nice addition to the standard installer.

I don't need /boot on a raid, but I would like my primary system built on top of a raid device so it can withstand a hard drive failure and not loose any of my data.

LVM makes it easy to manage my disk space when I added new disks/partitions on the system or I want to re-allocate my existing space between partitions.

### Post by andersbk on 2009-11-16
Summary:
Did a fresh install of 9.10. Am now running 9.04.

Details:
Pulled my HD and installed 9.10 fresh on an old 80G sata drive.

FYI, I use ubuntu in a mixed network enviroment, where user/pass are handled by an AD server (over LDAP).

Problem #1 with 9.10: It defaults to a userlist login. This is very very bad if you have:
  * hundreds of users;
  * autofs nfs mounts of users home directories.

This caused the login manager to hang for me, because there were some home dirs that could not be mounted and the login manager tries to automount every single one of them. This is bad.

I was able to solve this by ssh'ing into the machine and setting the login to just a username/pass input field.

So. Point 1, making userlist the default is bad. Or at least during the install process this should be a choice.

Note: For the home user, this should not be a problem.

Problem #2 with 9.10: audio. Pulse audio.

Pulse audio worked for me on this same box in 9.04. OSD audio adjusting with fancy media keyboard worked for me in 9.04.

I did get audio output, but the dumbed down mixer control did not let me adjust volume with any precision.

So, Audio and OSD are a step, not backward, but tangential, since audio has been usable until now with 9.10.

So I pulled the 9.10 drive and am back to a fully functional 9.04.

Show stopper for me was audio and OSD.

### Post by Druegan on 2009-11-17
Upgraded via Update Manager.. the update itself went smooth, with the exception that, after the reboot, my hard drives started going nuts.. all spinning up to top rpm and not slowing for anything..  a further reboot fixed this, hasn't occurred since.

Login worked fine.. but several things are borked.

System monitor now shows that it itself is using 80%+ of my cpu... 

Upgrade broke my flash install on Firefox.. had to manually rip out that 32-bit wrapper and track down and install the 64-bit alpha drivers again.

Sound is funky. Got it working, but now it's got like... nineteen times the volume it used to have, and the various mixers and such are *real* touchy.. a tiny increase sends the volume up to unpleasant levels. Still gotta tweak with that one.

There is some serious problem with some of the innards of Ubuntu.. I'm getting a bunch of Futex_Wait hangups, and every single one of my processes is showing "poll_schedule_timeout" in the "Waiting Channel" bit.. being a noob, I have no clue what that means, but it wasn't there before...

Firefox 3.5.5 is severely unstable..  hits Futex_Wait with a fierceness, crashes to desktop frequently, anything involving Flash is almost unusable.

Parts of the system hang as well.. but not everything. It's kinda weird, and I don't have the "lingua techna" to describe it accurately.. but this release "feels" a lot less stable than previous ones.

At this point I'm just praying for new patches, hoping something fixes the stability probs.

### Post by Shazaam on 2009-11-18
Clean 32 bit ext4 desktop install, using manual partitioning method (didn't like the way Guided was setting up the partitions). Grub install found all distros and they all boot.
Glitches (some cosmetic,and bugs not filed yet)...
1. Dialup issues again. *sigh* No built in driver for Conexant Winmodem; pppconfig didn't work. Had to install wvdial and dependancies to connect.
2. Sound stutters and also clicks to a steady metronone-like beat while playing music. Soundblaster pci card, onboard sound disabled. Codecs not installed yet.
3. Karmic install changed the way Jaunty boots- no more progress bar (text only).
Good stuff...
1. Firefox MUCH faster.
2. No problems installing latest driver from Nvidia (from their website).
3. The new desktop backgrounds are nice.
Oddities (yes I know that I can change them)...
1. Anyone else think the usplash/xsplash(?) progress bar thingee reminds them of Cylons? Lol it needs to be red.
2. White Ubuntu logo on a black background that does nothing then changes to the cylon bar? Eh?
3. System>Administration>Sessions gone. Why?
4. Dark theme is, um, ok. First thing that I changed.
5. Can't confirm that boot time is faster. Faster than pre Jaunty maybe but the same (or slightly slower than Jaunty).
Overall impression is that Karmic is "ok". This could be the first version of Ubuntu that I have installed (starting with Dapper) that may not find a permanent place on my pc.

### Post by bmilner on 2009-11-18
Upgraded, something with gdm crashed during upgrade and had to finish manually. Usually I just update, log into a shell and update my nvidia drivers. This time I was unable to log in, something with gdm re-starting continuously and was unable to enter passwords into login tty. I logged in remotely and then was able to install nvidia drivers for the kernal that was running. Sound doesn't work, not sure why yet.

I've been upgrading linux distributions since SLS 1.05 and I've come to the conclusion that each time I appreciate the upgrade experience less. Other than my first ubuntu install (5.x something ... which was wonderful), I don't think I've had any upgrades without issues. Either it's been network manager wacking my already working connection or my dell monitor no longer being recogonized as a 1600x1200 display or appamor suddenly deciding that my music server no longer has access to mysql or my backlit keyboard no longer staying on. I think for the average user, a new installation works really well. For people who stick with it, it seems that regressions are in your future. Sorry for tone of the message, but this is my upgrade experience. I think the poll should have a new category of "Upgrades sucks, but I'm getting used to it now.".

### Post by beerguzzler on 2009-11-18
3 installs, all with little fixable (probably my fault they were caused) problems.

First off, my mythtv media centre. Was running 8.04 LTS with a couple of Mythtv issues. Mythtv released their new version that isn't compatible with 8.04 so I took the opportunity to install 9.10.

Backed up and also created a Markings file from Synaptic Manager to make my applications installs a little easier. Well that was the theory.

Install done on seperate partitions for / /boot and /home, with the same username and machine name that 8.04 was configured with. During the process I was informed that no profiles other than the main user one. Not really an issue because as stated, Home was on a seperate partition.

Install and subsequent updates went very smoothly. No sound (again) from PCM but install of Alsamixer and subsequent un-muted of the PCM in there cured that.

Previous users re-created and files copied over from the old home folders. No issues.

Tried and failed to load the Markings file in Synaptic Manager, stated that before it could load the applications 6 broken packages needed to be fixed. Attempted to fix said broken packages but to no avail. Oh well, install the old fashioned way in Synaptic.

2nd pc was running 9.04 but because of the new version of mythtv and database updates etc I decided to have 9.10 on there too. Only issue with this one is what appears to be an old bug whereby the wireless adapter has a 1mb/s transfer rate. "sudo iwconfig wlan0 rate 11Mb/s" cures that for the session.

3rd pc was running 8.10 and again no prompts to copy over profiles, so new ones created and files copied over from previous home directory.

One other minor nag that all 3 pc's have is a usplash error message where by it can't read settings above 800x600, but tbh I really can't that bothered spending time on a screen that's viewed for about 2 seconds during boot up!.

So my verdict - better than 8.** by a considerable margin.
Better than 9.04 - not sure, but overall, very impressed. 
Keep it up!

---

### Post by zenlord on 2009-11-18
OK, so I upgraded my uncle's pc from an up-to-date 9.04 two days ago and I couldn't stay until the end of the upgrade process. Of course I got a call that after the reboot, an error occurred and I had to go back.

Problems:
1/ Somehow grub2 was not installed and grub tried to start the 8.04-kernel which of course wasn't available anymore. Weird problem, no?

Nothing a manual edit of the grub-line couldn't fix, after which the /boot/grub/menu.lst was fixed manually.

2/ Because I wanted the menu.lst fully populated, I ran 'update-grub' to see that this command changed all occurences of 'hd(0,5)' to 'hd(1,5)' and left the system again unbootable.

CLI to the rescue :)

3/ The newly bought WiFi usbstick (D-link DWA-140) did not work OOTB - I had read on the forums that I had to blacklist the rt2800usb-module and reboot. After that: no more problems.

So, all in all: minor upgrade problems. I don't know yet if I will manually upgrade to grub2 - I have no experience with it myself and I have heard rumours of it not being stable yet.

So, hth for the devs / others.

Zl.

### Post by Bt00x on 2009-11-18
First off all, thank you all for working so hard to get Ubuntu 9.10 out.

Unfortunately I have to say that, unlike all the other upgrades I've made, this one was a disaster.

One of my laptops got unusable due to problems with Xorg eating 99% of the CPU. I had to downgrade to 9.04.

The other one is working, but some Gnome basic configuration tools just disappeared, I can't get my system the way I want. I've read all about Gnome changes and that now one have to set parameters manually, using low level d-bus tools.

Ever since I first tried Ubuntu, one of my favorite aspects of the distribution is the sensible way that features are included so the end user always get more benefits from one version to the next one. This time looks like something went wrong.

Worst of all is that the overall performance decreased. Firefox hangs, network applications freeze and lots of little losses are collected every day I try the good old features that used to work so fine.

I believe that sticking to a deadline is probably the major source of all those problems. If the bread is not ready, don't take it out of the oven, regardless that the bakery owner is shouting bad words at you. Customers don't like raw bread.

### Post by abickerton on 2009-11-18
After my initial rant a week or so back (and noticing it was quoted all over the place after some exaggeration ) I can confirm that the upgrade while seeming badly broken, was in fact fixable.

Although I have now spent way longer researching on the forums and in launchpad than I ever wish to again.

xorg.conf seemed to be the major cause.
I did some tweaking so that it looks like below (This is an AGP radeon 9600 pro dual head system )

[PHP]Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Configured Screen Device"
	Device	"Configured Video Device"
	SubSection "Display"
		Virtual	2560 1024
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
        Option  "AccelMethod"           "EXA"
        Option  "AccelDFS"           "true"
	Option "MigrationHeuristic" "greedy"
	Option "EnablePageFlip" "true"
	Option "EnableDepthMoves" "true"
        Option  "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
        Option  "VideoOverlay"  "on"
        Option  "ColorTiling"   "true"
	Option "AddARGBGLXVisuals" "true"
        Option "RenderAccel" "true"
        Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "true"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode 0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option "Composite" "Enable"
EndSection[/PHP]

CPU usage is back to normal
Flash performance is acceptable.
Thunderbird & firefox are usable once more.
Still can't do HD video (It's a 5 year old PC, I can live with it)
CIFS Mounts sortof work... Freecom Network Disk (Flaky as hell)

I had to do some clearing out of old packages (some mozilla packages since feisty ) and make sure to remove the compiled from source thunderbird. We'll see how things progress but as I stated previously. If Canonical want the wider populace to adopt Ubuntu or Linux as a whole, upgrading needs to be drastically less involved and more reliable.

### Post by Dennis N on 2009-11-18
Decided to try Karmic, but first in VirtualBox since there seemed to be numerous possible problems.
  
This was my experience:

Live CD session worked fine, leading me to think this install will work! However, trying to reboot after the install completed, I got dropped into a terminal screen with flashing text:

```
Ubuntu 9.10 Elissa ttyl

Log in:

```
(Elissa is the computer's name here)

It is just possible to enter the user name - it seems input is accepted only when the text is on, I was not able to enter a password correctly in numerous tries. Too bad.

This can probably be fixed. I will be looking for a solution as my time permits (there probably is one here someplace) but I am glad I took a cautious approach and did not go ahead and replace my working Intrepid installation!

EDIT - SOLUTION FOUND:
---------------------
CAUTION! Solution for this specific issue: installing/running Ubuntu Karmic in VirtualBox 3.0.10 and:

X fails to start due to vboxvideo module not being found. Launchpad bug 457443

Hit escape (repeatedly) when Ubuntu VM starts so that you can boot into recovery mode in the Virtual Machine.
Enter root shell.
Remove xorg.conf **from the guest OS installation** completely:
```
rm /etc/X11/xorg.conf

```
shut down and start the VM again (or restart).
Now works normally.
I see that VirtualBox created a new xorg.conf for itself.

One problem solved.

EDIT2:

After two days, no further issues found in my Virtual Box installation of Karmic, except that I found slow DNS lookups apparently (from what I read) due to a change making ipv6 address resolution a priority over ipv4 and the ipv6 resolution failing.

EDIT3:

Disable ipv6 in Firefox fixes the slow DNS problem for the most part. Karmic seems to be working well now.

### Post by yester64 on 2009-11-18
> **Dennis N said:**
> Decided to try Karmic, but first in VirtualBox since there seemed to be numerous possible problems.
  
This was my experience:

Live CD session worked fine, leading me to think this install will work! However, trying to reboot after the install completed, I got dropped into a terminal screen with flashing text:

```
Ubuntu 9.10 Elissa ttyl

Log in:

```(Elissa is the computer's name here)

It is just possible to enter the user name - it seems input is accepted only when the text is on, I was not able to enter a password correctly in numerous tries. Too bad.

This can probably be fixed. I will be looking for a solution as my time permits (there probably is one here someplace) but I am glad I took a cautious approach and did not go ahead and replace my working Intrepid installation!

I just like to add, that it is your gfx driver. You need to update the driver in order to log in. That did it for me.
I would download the latest driver from your card manifacture and copy it on either a usb stick or copy it to root and after you reboot get into safemode and drop to command. There you need to execute the driver file and you should be set to log in to the desktop.

Mine was Nvidia.

### Post by Freezewarp on 2009-11-19
I first installed Karmic when it was in Beta. It worked almost flawlessly for me - I had a few problems when upgrading from the beta to the final release (primarily, a graphical problem with fsck), however nothing big. KDE 4.3 also works great, and start-up performance seems to have improved 2-fold (likewise, 9.04 seemed to have improve two-fold from 8.10). It usually takes about 15 seconds on my computer; an 80GB HDD with 1.5GB of RAM and a 1.86GHz Celeron processor. 3D-effects run much smoother in Kwin as well, though Compiz was never a problem to begin with in 9.04, from my experiences. I am not big in graphic-intensive games nor DVD playback, so I can't give an opinion from that aspect, though video in generally seems great as well. Finally, wireless has worked by installing a single package in 8.10 and 9.04 (when I first installed Ubuntu 8.04 on this computer, it was nightmarish), but in 9.10 wireless networking worked straight out of the box.

Also worth noting, I installed Ubuntu, and not Kubuntu, but use KDE by default. I do not dual-boot (though am planning on dual-booting with OpenSuse and Fedora soon), and my computer is an Acer Aspire 4315, which I got about a year and a half ago (and originally dual-booted Vista and Ubuntu 8.04).

So, in summary, I have really no complaints; installed and with no real issues.

### Post by RossendaleIT on 2009-11-20
I installed KK on my main laptop which is a HP6710b with Blista/Ubuntu dual boot, the upgrade failed quite considerably, wouldn't boot fully due to screen issues, I just had two underscore characters displaying. I gave up in the end and booted to an older kernel, copied my .home folder and flattened the partition and installed KK from scratch, which went perfectly. And still runs perfectly.

However, my spare laptop, a HP G7000 was running Jaunty/F11/Debian Lenny and XP. This laptop I tried the upgrade, and it worked flawlessly, and is still running good.

Karmic does seem to have some issues, which for me wasn't too big a problem, the regular web browsing/email sending user may struggle.

My overall feeling toward Karmic is good, it runs smoother than Jaunty IMO. On my 6710b there was no need to enable PAE to allow it to address my 4GB RAM, had to do this on Jaunty.

Terminal services client seems to run nicer too, as a lot of my customers have Windoze based servers it's something I need quite a lot.

Overall, Karmic rules!!! Run Vista for a few hours with several apps open and I'm using 1.8GB RAM/pagefile, run Karmic all day/week/month long and loads of apps open and I still can't get it to use more than 800MB RAM / 0MB swap.

Speaks volumes for me that does!

:)

### Post by pr0cs on 2009-11-20
I had some experience with version 8.X a year ago with a laptop, while I enjoyed how easily it installed I still went back to Windows because a lot of my work demanded that I run Windows specific software.  I was impressed with my experience though.

I finally decided to build a server with some old hardware I had lying around and bought 3 1.5tb drives to try and create a RAID5 server to act as a reliable file server for my home network.   I did have a license for Vista to use but decided to try 9.10 64bit since I enjoyed my previous experience and there seemed to be a lot of help/discussion around using mdadm to build a software RAID.

Issues I encountered:

[LIST]
[*] - upgrading the bios on my hardware caused some really terrible issues, dropped SATA connections, memory failures, lock ups, instability.    Probably nothing that the Ubuntu installation could have warned me about but it was something I fought with for a long time and initially blamed Ubuntu for the instability.  Going back to a previous bios returned the machine to a much more stable state
[*] - I would recommend the installer almost always confirm or show the 'install boot loader" (Advanced) screen.  for some reason it would always selected /dev/hd0 but regularly the machine would not boot unless I had gone into the advanced options and picked the real drive to install the boot loader
[*] - previous installations (due to the instability of the system) caused partial connection/configuration of my RAID5 setup.  This caused some really odd behavior during the sync of the drives in that I could not format them (doing so would lock the machine up).  Starting over and following guides on this forum on how to remove a previous use of mdadm fixed my problem but initially I questioned the reliability of the new drives I bought).  Again probably nothing on the install of 9.10 could be done to remedy this but it was something I fought with for a long time.
[*] - turning on desktop effects caused VNC/remote desktop to be unusable.  It took me a while to figure this out, the connection would be okay but there was no way to actually use the desktop because the screen would not update.  A warning in either the desktop effects screen or on remote desktop in that "having desktop effects on and using remote desktop may make remote desktop unusable"
[*] - I would like the basic options of enabling what sort of software is installed initially.  It's easy enough to uninstall software but I would have liked a generic list to turn things off by default (ie: my file server doesn't really need any games or gimp, etc).
[/LIST]
 
Just thinking off the top of my head on issues that stood out for me.  So far I've been really happy, the amount of help/information that exists out there on how to tweak the system is really exciting.

---

### Post by ParadoxTMA on 2009-11-20
Fresh install would not install GRUB to my SATA drive.  After re-selecting the 'Select and install software" options about six times (kept hanging on post-installation triggers for python) I finally got a bootable system on my IDE drive, but only after unhooking all my other drives so that my IDE was master on cable 0.

Took several tries to get all the updates, because for some reason the update manager kept freezing my network.  Getting the network back required reboot each time.

I now have a usable system, though my USB wireless mouse will randomly get a serious lag, which only corrects after a reboot.

For those just looking to get away from windows, I recommend Mandriva.

Still plugging away at Ubuntu though, because I really need the real-time linux kernel, which requires a lot of tweaking and compiling in Mandriva.

### Post by linux4me on 2009-11-21
> **florus said:**
> Having had minor issues with an upgrade from Jaunty ext3 on my laptop, I did clean installs (ext4) after using gparted to give me separate /home partitions on both the laptop and a desktop. No problems with either, and both now much faster. Very pleased with Karmic, but would like to get rid of the confirm dialogs on restart and shutdown.:D

I ran across this method of getting rid of the confirm dialogs on restart and shutdown by accident. Right-click on the panel and choose "Add to Panel." Select the red button labeled "Shut Down" and click "Add." The resulting button pops up a menu to suspend, shut down, etc., which doesn't require the confirm dialogs. It's sort of like the old shut down dialog from Intrepid and earlier.

### Post by braikar on 2009-11-21
Once installed and all problems fixed, Karmic runs flawlessly, but there were some major bugs that annoyed me with the installer and grub2.

I had windows installed on one hardisk (I use it for games). And wanted to install ubuntu on another harddisk. That was impossible, the installer would not load the second harddisk, it would just allow me to change partition sizes on the windows harddisk. The installer didn't even show the second harddisk... It was obviously a bug in the installer, because the harddisk was clearly visible in GParted.

So I had to unplug the windows harddisk to install ubuntu on the harddisk I wanted.

Then grub2 problems showed up...
Ubuntu was and is running perfectly fine, just grub2 was pretty annoying. The os_probe command wouldn't recognize the win xp harddisk... and there were loads of info on internet on how to set grub2 config to load windows on a partition on the same harddisk as ubuntu. But hardly any information on how to set the config to load windows on a second harddisk. Finally found that there:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/archive/index.php/t-1287490.html](http://ubuntuforums.org/archive/index.php/t-1287490.html)

and since then everything is perfectly fine.
I guess this multiboot problem is not really relevant when I compare it to windows not even detecting any other operating system except microsoft ones!!
Ubuntu has to do loads of things other operating systems don't even care about! So in my opinion: Karmic = a great release :)

### Post by Dexter_Greycells on 2009-11-22
I was actually overwriting an existing Jaunty installation with Karmic. I had quite a few problems. Most were resolved by using temporary/permanent solutions, but one problem is outstanding.

1) When the installation was at 95% and Grub2 was being installed, my PC froze. Then got the Grub2 error 15. Something to do with Grub2 being on one drive and Karmic on the other. Reinstalled grub2 using the instructions at wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2. I think grub2 read the legacy grub installed by Jaunty.

2) My internet connection (through WLAN & ADSL routers) which worked out of the box with Jaunty was not recognized by Karmic (even though it showed 'Auto eth0' active) . Found a solution by using OpenDNS based on similar problems reported in Ubuntuforums. Something with network-manager and may be IPv6 as well. Here was the solution - [https://store.opendns.com/setup/device/ubuntu/](https://store.opendns.com/setup/device/ubuntu/)

3) Karmic recognized my soundcard out of the box (unlike Jaunty where I had to recompile ALSA). But the default settings were incorrect which were resolved by just a few trial -and-error clicks in sound preferences.

4) My ZTE MG880 CDMA1X USB modem was still not recognized by Karmic when I inserted it. Have to run 'sudo modprobe usbserial.vendor=0x19d2 usbserial.product-0xffd' to make Karmic recognize it every time I insert it. At least my service provider is not listed when adding a new network connection (Reliance NetConnect, India).

5) I allocated 2 GB for swap though I had only 1.2 GB RAM for being able to use hibernate. Resuming my PC from hibernate never works. Desktop just gives me a blank screen. This one is still UNRESOLVED.

All in all, it was a learning experience.

### Post by Cavalryman on 2009-11-22
I've installed Koala on 3 computers so far -- one "Ubuntu only" and two dual-boots (one with XP and one with Win7). The only issue I had was that the Ubuntu Software Center didn't work and Synaptic was screwy, but those were pretty easily resolved. Otherwise, I've had no problems. It may be a factor that I carefully selected all my hardware to run reliably under Ubuntu 8.04 a couple of years ago. It seems as if most of the problems people are having are issues of hardware incompatibility which might have been avoided by choosing the hardware initially with Ubuntu in mind. However, I could be wrong about that. Also, I did fresh installs rather than upgrades. My experience with several operating systems has indicated that a fresh install is less problem-prone than an upgrade. It's really not that difficult (in my opinion) to save all my files and just start fresh. To be fair, I've had the same experience with Windows, so it may just be in the nature of an "upgrade" versus a "clean install."

### Post by billd42 on 2009-11-23
Just installed Karmic tonight. I had been using Jaunty but opted for a clean install, as I had been doing upgrades right along starting with Dapper and figured I could probably stand to clean out some of the cruft.

I've been using one form of linux or another since 1993-ish or so.  Slackware, Red Hat, SuSE, and now Ubuntu.  I was kind of nervous after reading all the problem reports here.

I installed karmic to my already-existing LVM setup using the standard install CD.  A lot of the instructions out there on how to do this are way more complicated than they need to be.  It's actually way simpler than it used to be.  Boot from the livecd, then follow the instructions here:  [http://www.debuntu.org/how-to-install-ubuntu-over-lvm-filesystem](http://www.debuntu.org/how-to-install-ubuntu-over-lvm-filesystem) .  Warning:  you do need to be comfortable manipulating disk partitions at a command prompt.  There's no graphical LVM install tool as yet--that's probably the lone item on my wishlist for a future release.

X was the easiest to configure it's ever been on any Linux distro.  It configured itself, pretty much.  The only thing I had to do was tell it to use the nVidia closed-source driver.  (Running an nVidia 9800GTX+ graphics card, and a Samsung 24" 1920x1200 LCD monitor.)

No showstopper bugs.  Nothing that keeps the machine from booting, keeps it from accessing the internet, keeps X from starting, puts my screeen into some weird resolution, or anything like that.  I've found a couple of minor issues that kept things from looking or acting exactly the way I wanted them to but I found good instructions available for fixing things via google and by searching the forums here.  Most of my problems centered around adding back things that aren't installed by default.  mutt/fetchmail/postfix/procmail.  Thunderbird.  Xchat.  I'm trying out Empathy for a while; maybe I'll go back to pidgin, maybe not.

Annoyingly, icons have disappeared from Firefox's menus.  Fix that problem with:
[FONT="Courier New"][size="3"]gconftool-2 --type bool --set /desktop/gnome/interface/menus_have_icons true[/size][/FONT]
at a command prompt.

One nagging problem:  gapcmon--the UPS monitor--doesn't display in the tray area like it did under Jaunty.  Wish I knew what to do to fix that.  But if that's the worst thing that happens then I think I'm doing pretty well.  :D

### Post by Audiosears on 2009-11-23
Had a few major problems after the upgrade (perhaps less major now that I'm aware of how to fix them), but major nonetheless in that it caused our production file server to be unbootable for a few hours, Samba was unavailable for about a day, and GDM/X Server ran into some issues.

Upgraded from 9.04 Intrepid, and updated all packages in the distribution.  Primarily, this server runs LAMP for web service and Samba for windows-compatible file sharing.

First major issue was that menu.lst was not updated with new package information for kernel 2.6.31-14, so this had to be added via the recovery disc shell as system would not boot - threw sreadahead error until this was fixed.  Also, names of package files have always ended with '-server' for server-based versions of Ubuntu, whereas 9.10 changes this to '-generic' and '-generic-pae', the difference being that the pae version allows you to run x86 with > 2 GB memory (and possibly other x64 compatibilities).  This particular server is not x64 capable.

When menu.lst was fixed, machine was able to boot but samba, which updated to 3.4.0, was also broken.  We use a simple workgroup setup where samba username/passwords are used rather than AD, and the samba username / password records were reinitialized such that only the built-in unix accounts had samba accounts - the user accounts that used to be there were no longer present, so these had to be added back in.

I use webmin and VNC to remote-administer this box, and at some point I could no longer log in via GDM (blank screen), no response from mouse / keyboard, same upon several reboots.  Fortunately I was able to remove and reinstall gdm and xserver via webmin's command shell:

apt-get remove xserver-xorg --assume-yes
apt-get remove gdm --assume-yes
apt-get autoremove --assume-yes
apt-get install xserver-xorg gdm --assume-yes

After rebooting, GDM then worked fine, and allowed VNC to remote in again.  I believe this was because some xorg config files may have been corrupted during one my remote sessions somehow.

Just checked for updates again, and updated Kernel-related updates for 2.6.31-15 (among others) without issue.

I highly recommend having webmin onhand as at least a backup method of administration in addition to a remote-desktop tool like VNC.  There have been several times where the X Desktop was unresponsive in the last 4+ years we have run ubuntu, and being able to directly control server functions via a decent GUI and run command shells easily was handy to have.  VNC generally works well as a connectivity tool also, so between both, they will make your life much simpler.

I might also add that solutions to each of these issues were obtained primarily from Ubuntuforums.org, so thanks much to all the folks who posted their problems and especially those who had the answers!

---

### Post by unkilbeeg on 2009-11-24
I've upgraded two machines from Jaunty to Karmic.  Neither went perfectly smoothly.  One is acceptable, the other is not.  Both of these were upgrades, not fresh installs.

My music machine (hooked up to the stereo at home) had problems with the Intel video.  Compiz stopped working, and the resolution dropped from the native resolution of the panel (1024x768 ) to 800x600.  The only thing I was able to do to get it back was to disable the kernel modesetting and the resolution came back.  A minor problem came up at this point -- the system no longer booted from the hard drive.  I was able to boot to an Ubuntu 9.04 CD, and oddly enough, if I choose "boot from hard drive" from the CD boot menu, it worked.  I don't have time to track this down right now, so I'm living with it.  

Many people have had trouble with audio after the upgrade.  I may have had some initial problem, but if so it was easy enough to fix that I just have a vague memory that there was a problem.  :)  This was weeks ago.

This is the acceptable machine.

My netbook has been a disaster.  It also has an Intel graphics system. Compiz stopped here too. The resolution is fine, but it randomly goes blank and the only way to get back is a long press on the power button.  A curse on whoever thought that disabling ctl-alt-bksp was a good idea.

Virtual terminals don't work.  You can shift to them and even see the login prompt (sometimes), but the blinking cursor extends downwards to infinity (a very tall vertical stripe), and nothing you type ever displays.

Suspend to memory doesn't work.  If you assume that it's going to and close the display, you just end up with a very hot netbook and no battery charge left.

Lots of people have had trouble with the Broadcom wireless.  Wonder of wonders, this works fine, with no fiddling.

The new GDM pretty much sucks.  I can live without theme support (it's nice but not necessary) but it's not obvious how to get rid of the "face chooser."  What a bad idea.  Once you apply the non-obvious fix, GDM doesn't load the username prompt, but rather a button that says "log in."  *Then* you get a username prompt. Another bad idea from the Windows world.  At least you don't have to type "ctl-alt-del" to get the username prompt.  Sheesh.

Anyway, aside from the GDM issue, which is just bad design, most of my other problems may just be artifacts of a botched upgrade.  When I get time, I'm going to create a new Karmic bootable USB and do a fresh install.  I'm not going to overwrite my Jaunty USB, since there's a significant chance I'll have to revert to it after all the smoke has cleared.

Fortunately, my customizations of this netbook have been minimal, and all my personal files are on the separate /home partition.

### Post by awd-s on 2009-11-25
Karmic Koala is a big disappointment to me. I upgraded an Asus eeePC 8G from 9.04 to 9.10. It worked but the look and feel is worse; the start-up splash and log-in screens are particularly vile, as are the default desktop background, panels, and icons.

9.10 now has a wizard that insists that it needs all kinds of mobile broadband network information before it can connect through my quad-band GSM Edge telephone. 9.04 just worked, no questions asked.

I tried the 9.10 Live CD on another computer. It refused to connect any of the three wired network ports.

9.10 is considerably slower to start than 9.04 on the eeePC.

I have my doubts about the abolishment of Synaptic Package Manager. 

Appearance of the Mobile Broadband Wizard was a shocking annoyance, so much so that I am in process of overwriting 9.10 with 9.04 on my netbook. Until now I have always upgraded to the latest version of Ubuntu. I won't be so quick to do so from now on. Based on what I have heard and read, I approached this upgrade with caution, which was justified as it turned out in my case. Normally, I would work through any problems, but I don't see why I should flog through this problem when it wasn't a problem at all in the previous version.

I will pass by Karmic Koala and take the 10.04 release for gallop, if it stumbles then I'll stay with 9.04 on my several machines. I have the feeling that there are detrimental changes in the wind for Ubuntu -- I hope I'm wrong.

### Post by Rounin on 2009-11-26
I'm sorry to report this, but for once I'm very disappointed with the new release. Upgrading from 9.04 with apt-get wasn't a good idea – The upgrade was interrupted several times by errors that had to be addressed, and when it had finally finished, GRUB's menu.lst had disappeared.

When the time came to boot the new installation, the system hung on each boot due to a bizarre bug whereby X is somehow started *before* the system has booted, and starts competing with cryptsetup, which is still trying to mount some encrypted disks, for control of the console.

It's one thing that a few bugs managed to slip by the beta testers and wind up in the final release, but that the buggy release hasn't been revoked and replaced by an updated release is very disappointing.

To add insult to injury, now that it's finally up and running it's become infested with Pulseaudio again... And what do you know, no sound.

__________________________________________________

It's now been a few hours, and it looks like the problems are unsolvable for now.

Despite the announced "bootwait" fix, the encrypted partitions now have to be mounted manually rather than at boot time. Listing them in fstab as "bootwait" causes the system to hang differently, but it still hangs.

Audio seems to be absent due to a problem with ALSA, so PulseAudio didn't return from the dead to disable sound after all, just to taunt me.

As the computer is due to be replaced in a few days, it might as well remain that way, but the new computer will probably not be running Ubuntu 9.10.

### Post by LinuxFriend on 2009-11-27
I tried a clean install into a multi-boot environment. I after a few rounds with the a live CD, I decided to use the Kbuntu 9.10 alternate install CD x86 version to see if Kbuntu would allow me to install to the partition I created for it and allow me to choose where to put the boot loader.

I have a master Grub partition that controls all my operating systems. What I found was that when you get to the screen to select an alternate location for the boot loader (other than MBR), that the boot loader is not installed in the partition you specified.

For example, I wanted to install Kbuntu and the boot loader in partition sda7 (hd0,6). Following the examples in the installer, I entered (hd0,6) for the location for the bootloader. Guess where it installed the boot loader? sda6 (hd05). I tried installing this twice with the alternate install cd. Each time it would install the boot loader in the partition previous to the one I picked. Very frustrating.

It overwrote the boot loader in the superblock for one of my other distros (which I was fortunately able to recover).

I believe that the installer is broken when it comes to specifying the boot loader location. So beware.

My work around was to find an example of a menu.lst which included loading a kernel for Ubuntu(since I didn't have any versions of Ubuntu running on any of my machines). I then modified my master grub menu.lst and manually inserted an entry to boot the Kbuntu kernel that I had installed (all my other entries in my master grub menu.lst were chain loading other menus). This allowed me to boot Kbunutu. There were errors that showed up on the screen as it loaded and there was no splash screen, but it did boot and I was able to get into Kbuntu. If I can find the time, I may research the errors further, but I wanted to post something here just in case someone else goes down this path.

---

### Post by iamwhatiseem on 2009-11-27
**Note** I have 12 years experience with *nix systems. I took a different career change, and have been out of the loop for about 8 years. Last platform installed RedHat 7.0 ---------------------------------------   First and foremost, the installation was encouraging. Installing *nix w/o having to hack for hours to get video resolution, sound, NIC's correctly installed/configured???...you have my attention.   First day was nice. Installed Wine with no coding? Mounts the ntfs system out of the box?..now I am truly excited.   Wait...what is up with this "freezing" when my son plays WoW. Screen refresh is horrible. I poke around and see that the video card (Nvidea 9600) is not 'actually' installed. So I install the proprietary drive with a single click...Problem solved.   Wait...I wanted to watch a movie...I get an error...something about "no definition for DVD"...what? I verify that the DVD device is properly installed..yep. I poke around on the net for a good hour, come here..read through threads..get numerous bad advice - and then I happen upon a page about installing dvdreader..after installing - DVD's now play.  Wait..I remember this cool game "tuxracer"..I think I'll play. Crap now what! I get sound, but the screen turns black. After hacking around and going through threads for 45 minutes or so I decided it wasn't worth it.   After 8 years...it is still the same, improved - but the same problem for Linux is still alive and well. It is still faaar from ready for primetime.    [standing by with fire extinguisher]

### Post by movieman on 2009-11-27
Well, this is fun.

Go to upgrade in the update manager. Says 'blah blah', press Upgrade. Asks for my password. Then does nothing.

Try again. And again. And again. Every time it silently exits.

Reboot since I had an outstanding kernel update I hadn't rebooted for. Try again. Still it silently exits.

Discover there's a command-line tool instead. Run it. _NOW_ it actually tells me that it can't upgrade because /tmp is mounted noexec. So I hack /etc/fstab, remount /tmp and the upgrade starts up without exiting.

Except now it tells me it needs several gigabytes of space on / in order to install, and then exits. This machine has 2.5 Terabytes of disk space, but it's mostly a server so it only has 12GB on /. So now I have to delete a gigabyte of stuff just to install, and then restart the upgrade process.

Mother of God, now it wants to download 1.8GB of files in order to upgrade. I'm not sure I have 1.8GB of operating system files installed in total for 9.04... glad I don't have a bandwidth cap.

Guess I'll update this post in a couple of hours when the files have finished downloading.

Edit: Hmm, now 2 1/2 hours of upgrading to do. I'm sure that 8.10 to 9.04 didn't take anywhere near as long; I presume there must be a lot more changes in this version.

Edit: So after the 2 1/2 hours of upgrading, which required deciding whether or not to keep config files about a dozen times so I couldn't just go away and leave it, it spent 10-15 minutes 'searching for obsolete software', listed over 500 packages it wanted to remove, and is now spending over an hour removing them... I hope they're really obsolete and it isn't deleting all the packages it's just installed.

Is it just me, or is 6+ hours to upgrade an operating system perhaps a bit excessive?

Edit: firewall was broken, blocking everything. Samba passwords are gone. IPV6 seems to randomly work or not work, but that may be a Windows problem: right now I can ping from Ubuntu to Windows but not Windows to Ubuntu. MythTV music player doesn't work. xrandr no longer works for setting video modes from the command line, but I presume that's because of the switch to kernel mode setting: of course the only reason why I need that is because the default video mode for my TV is output incorrectly so a quarter of the screen is missing on the right hand side. MythTV user no longer logs in automatically and trying to configure the auto-login from the settings doesn't work.

Edit: music player wasn't working because a) the install replaced the symbolic link at /var/lib/mythtv/music with an empty directory and b) the upgrade deleted my codecs. Xrandr wasn't working because the VGA output suddenly became VGA1. Sensors no longer work. MythTV setup no longer works without a window manager.

Edit: CTRL+ALT+BACKSPACE doesn't work anymore

Edit: SVN server isn't working anymore.

### Post by bambitheman on 2009-11-27
I had to switch from OpenSuse11.1 to Ubuntu 9.04, as the sound on my system died after a kernel upgrade by OpenSuse. So I burned 9.04 to DVD and ran a flawless install, (I've previously played with 6.06 before a hard drive failure and 6.06's inability to correctly format the new drive pushed me to OpenSuse.) I put all my favourite packages, VLC, Vuze etc in my 9.04 install and immediately ran the built in Upgrade to 9.10. To say things went fine is an understatement. 

One problem has subsequently surfaced in as much as WICD will not detect a Belkin wireless USB dongle, and configuring the wired network was a PITA (It didn't initially detect the Ethernet card). Put it this way Solaris 10 was easier to configure for the wired network than WICD and that is saying something...

I'm running a 4 year old Dell E521 with 3GB Ram (DDR2 800) Geforce LE 5300 Graphics card. 

Overall pleased with 9.10, although it has since knocked out my sound card, but managed to rectify that by reverting to Jack...

I did this on the day 9.10 was released, only other comment is that it doesn't pick up the correct name for my USB external HDD, and if you mount and unmount it then changes the name which is a royal PITA. Love the HDD facitlity, just a shame theres no cure for a failing drive with Hard Errors... but its a nice touch. 

overall experience of going from 9.04 to  9.10 I'd say 9/10, other than the network issue, i'm a happy Ubuntu user... (for now...) :p

I'll probably go down the same route when I replace my failing HDD with a new one, tried 9.10 on a laptop (to replace winxp) with horrible results, so bunged 9.04 and then ran the built in upgrade, bingo worked perfick...

---

### Post by bruban on 2009-11-28
I upgraded to 9.10 around 3 weeks ago. I've been using Ubuntu for 4 to 5 years.

Impressions:

Mosts things work fine. The developers have done us proud again.

Three annoying problems though:

- Graphics are still annoying. I have a NVIDIA GeForceMX 400 video card. I have the recurring problem of not being able to use the 'NVIDIA accelerated graphics driver version 96' that has been tested by the Ubuntu developers.

As per usual, when I attempted to apply this driver, I was presented with a blank screen on reboot. I was able to switch to a terminal using CTL-ALT-F1 and set my video driver to 'vesa' in xorg.conf. I then rebooted and disabled the proprietary drivers using the applet via the System>Administration>Hardware Drivers menu.

I'm still using standard drivers, which is a bit disappointing. I'm monitoring the nvidia/ati sticky thread to see what the outcome is.


- The second annoying thing is that the current version is rather unstable. I have been experiencing random crashes that forces the computer to reboot. This did not occur with 9.04 or earlier versions. I have not yet found the cause.


- The third annoying problem is that something appears to have changed with the nfs client. At 9.04, I had no problems going through my (internal) shorewall firewall to a Debian 4 server, hosting nfs shares. Since I ungraded another client to Ubuntu 9.10, it has been unable to access the shares. It is in a home environment and not critical, so I have not chased it down to date.

---

### Post by CoolDreamZ on 2009-11-28

### Post by choochoo22 on 2009-11-28
I wanted  very much to try out Mythbuntu.  I encountered so many fatal problems that the project seems hopeless and I am disapointed and upset about the outcome (any help in booting Vista would be much appreciated). Problems are:

Failure to boot
Failure to support raid
Failure to support video
Failure to support tuner
Failure to support sound
Can't boot Vista.

I bought a Haupage HVR 950Q stick which I had read was supported under 9.10.  My spare computer had died so I decided to try co-installing on my Vista system.  I didn't want to mess with Vista so I moved a 40gb HD over from the defunct computer.  The computer is a Intel C2 Duo on a Gigabyte MOBO with Intel 965G video, ICH8, and raid: [http://www.gigabyte.us/Products/Motherboard/Products_Overview.aspx?ProductID=2659]("http://www.gigabyte.us/Products/Motherboard/Products_Overview.aspx?ProductID=2659")

I installed Mythbuntu 9.04 on the 40gb HD since that was the CD I had.  The resulting system was bootable only using a Super Grub CD for either OS.  The installation partitioner, gparted, and nautilus all failed to recognize Vista or the raid array correctly.  Myth failed to recognize the tuner (expected).  The video could not be set to the correct 1900x1200 mode, max was 1152x768 I believe.

Synaptic was used to upgrade to Karmic.  Karmic dropped the max video mode to 800x600.  An odd boot problem appeared; anytime the system is shut down or re-booted with Karmic, the BIOS freeze on the splash screen when coming back up.  To recover, the power supply must be shut off at the switch for a few seconds then the BIOS can proceed normally.  Myth now recognizes the tuner but I have been unable to get it to tune anything.  It is "asleep", whatever that  means, and has ignored anything I could think of to fix it.  The analog tuner works with TvTime but with no sound.  Sound works OK on DVD playback.  Hours of searching found a patch to fix the sound in TvTime but it is out of synch and needs to be patched each time you open it.  Useless.

All of this is much condensed, many hours were spent.  I can understand some fiddling with the tuner might be necessary but the onboard video, raid, and sound are pretty vanilla and I don't see why they should be such a challenge to Ubuntu.

Initially both Vista and Linux were bootable from Super Grub but not automatically from the MBR.  I tried using Super Grub to fix the MBR.  This proved to be a HUGE mistake with my limited (non-existent?) knowledge.  Now Ubuntu is still bootable only from Super Grub and Vista is unbootable period.  The Vista system and all my files are completely inaccessable.  Help!

---

### Post by TWO on 2009-11-28
I've come back to Linux after a few months away, and was mainly driven by my WinXP deciding it had lost some system file or another.  The whole KDE 3 falling by the wayside and the at the time insufficient KDE 4, drove me away for a bit.

But installing Kubuntu Karmic Koala and playing with it has been a joy! It looks brilliant and is a marked improvement from the last time I used KDE 4 at the .1 stage. That, and Kubuntu in general is behaving a lot better with my aged laptop. I love the progress that has happened!

The only issue I had yesterday was a password one, where the password I was dead certain to have set and was using all evening, suddenly stopped being accepted! :o A visit to the recovery console and a few keystrokes solved that problem. 

I also have the usual battle with trying to enable Japanese input and it never being straightforward. I would love to see a well designed Input Method Editor for (K)Ubuntu/Linux in general. Multi-linguists such as myself are CRYING OUT for an easily configurable one!

I understand that the new Kubuntu/Ubuntu got a lot of bad press on its release, but it's the same story with each new release. Why scores of people are so eager to update at the moment the new version is released- outside of testers- I don't understand. Twice a year, every year it's the same story- the new versions are always intolerably buggy for at least a month after release. 

I have the fortune of having made a fresh install and having made a delayed install. I won't be in a rush to upgrade from Karmic and I just want to say thanks to the great efforts of all in the Ubuntu family and to Canonical! 

It's good to be back!

TWO

### Post by mikechant on 2009-11-29
Second post on this thread; my first one was in relation to my Dell 530 Desktop, this one is for my Asus eeePC 1000.
This netbook was originally supplied with a really crappy Xandros Linux setup; I installed UNR 9.04 which was much better apart from the battery life being very slightly worse and the webcam not working.
Now I've got it dual booting UNR 9.04 and 9.10.

9.10 installed fine (clean install on 8gb sda overwriting all the Xandros crap). I don't fully trust grub2 yet so I installed it to the partition, not to the MBR, and I'm still using legacy grub in 9.04 to dual boot 9.04 and 9.10. Apart from that it was a standard install with ext4. No problems at all. Tested the following so far:
WiFi - Fine, seems to connect quicker  than 9.04.
Display - appears to be using Kernel Mode setting, so screen comes back quicker (near instantly) from suspend
Sound - OK
USB - OK
Boot time: From grub to login prompt 19s (was 20s for 9.04). May improve further as it optimizes itself - it started out at 25s
Webcam - now works 'out of the box'
Desktop - I prefer the new layout
Keyboard/Wireless mouse/Touchpad - All OK.
Generally seems more responsive than 9.04

Given all the problems people have been reporting with 9.10 I have so far been pleasantly surprised with it on both my Desktop and netbook. I guess I have fewer problems than a lot of people because:
a) I use known Linux-friendly hardware
b) I clean-install the new version alongside the previous version and test it while dual booting.
c) I never switch to the new version for normal use until at least a month or two has passed and I've applied all the updates for early serious problems.
d) If a release just doesn't work well for me I stick to the previous release and test the following release when it comes out 6 months later.

Anyhow, I just need to do some more tests and install/set up some more packages and I should be ready to switch to 9.10 on both systems full time.

---

### Post by artships on 2009-11-29
Linux mine 2.6.31-15-generic #50-Ubuntu SMP Tue Nov 10 14:53:52 UTC 2009 x86_64 GNU/Linux

Upgrade from 9.04 was initially grossly disappointing. After reboot I was greeted with all the gnome glory and all my own menu entries were lost.  Discovered some kind soul's hint, ```
echo 'export WINDOW_MANAGER=/usr/bin/fvwm2' > .gnomerc
``` and life looked better.  Until I tried accessing my partitions and disks.

Ironically, several Ubuntu versions ago I abandoned pcmanfm because it wouldn't mount my disks and partitions.  Now, they mount on demand in pcmanfm just like they used to in nautilus, and it's nautilus that prefers to popup: ```
Unable to mount <name-of-disk>
Authentication is required
``` (Nautilus will allow me to access them once pcmanfm has made them available.  Yes, I know it's GVFS that's doing the mounting, but I've no idea why it's only when prodded through pcmanfm that it Does The Right Thing). 

My real surprise is that I'm still surprised that sound is screwed up after an upgrade.  Again. Every time I reboot I have to execute pavucontrol and un-mute Output Devices because it has forgotten that I actually want to hear something from my machine.  Perhaps the line in the scrolling boot log has something to do with it, "Pulseaudio configured for peruser sessions".  I'll try the advice in hidden away in /etc/default/pulseaudio... It got rid of the boot message!  Didn't enable sound.

### Post by Robertjm on 2009-12-01
Been using Karmic for about a week now. Since I have separate partitions for my home and data folders I formatted the normal partitions and installed fresh.

Ran into several issues which I'm still working on.

[COLOR="SeaGreen"]**AMAROK**[/COLOR]:
I tried running the import feature, but nothing worked. Per someone over on Amarok they said to upgrade to v2.2.1, which is not in the repos. I added a repo and did the upgrade. Once that was complete I was able to import my mustic from previous locations that v1.4 used. HOWEVER, as of right now none of my podcasts are showing up in the podcast listings so I cannot run the update podcasts feature till that is resolved. This is a major annoyance (that and the ugly bloated interface I have to now learn).

[COLOR="SeaGreen"]**XINE/Commercial DVD pla**y[/COLOR]
As of right now I'm having issues playing commercial DVDs through XINE. I installed libdvdcss2, etc. which are needed to do so. However, if I right-click on the DVD icon and use the Open with Xine option I get an error that "This player is not compatible with the Region of this disc" and 16 error msgs pop up in succession telling me "the source cannot be read" each one pointing to a different .vob file on the DVD. If I open Xine and then click on the DVD button I don't get the previous error storm, but a single msg that the source seems to be encrypted. 

The same DVD will launch in Totem, though its really choppy to the point of being impossible to watch. VLC also works and is not nearly as choppy as Totem, but there is a little lag here and there that's noticeable. Additionally the color depth seems to be whacked compared to what I had under Intrepid Ibex. Granted, I was using the restricted proprietary drivers with Intrepid, whereas I'm using the open source stuff that Karmic loaded up during the install process. Perhaps some tweaks will fix that, but I haven't been able to devote any time to fixing that.

[COLOR="SeaGreen"]**SOUND SYSTEM ISSUES**[/COLOR]
Another issue that's really bugging me is with my sound system as a whole. I've got a Soundblaster Platinum 5.1+ w/LiveDrive system. When I installed previous versions of Ubuntu (Gutsy, Hardy, Intrepid) the mixer and system installed just fine, which allowed me to control all the inputs for the sound card, as well as the LiveDrive. As of right now, I'm not seeing that anywhere. I'm guessing it has something to do with the whole Alsa vs. Pulseaudio thing, but it's still annoying that its not there as it was out of the box in previous versions of Ubuntu. 

[COLOR="SeaGreen"]**USB connections**[/COLOR]
I also had some issues with my USB drives where they weren't being automounted when I plugged them in (firewire either). It seems like the whole USB subsystem was hosed as my bluetooth USB adapter wasn't flashing. However, that somehow cured itself between powerdowns, and hasn't reappeared since.

[COLOR="Red"]**NOTE TO ANYONE UPGRADING BY REFORMATTING PARTITIONS**[/COLOR]: If you have any repositories that you added for special programs remember to BACKUP the repo file before you blow away the install. I didn't and so had to go hunting for some repos to add back in, which took some time. Totally my fault, but something to consider nonetheless.

On the plus side, I did not run into the X issues I expected to since I have an ATI Radeon X1600 AGP video card, nor have I had any grub2 issues that I've seen other people experiencing. Perhaps its because I chose ext3, instead of ext4? Dont' know.

All in all, I'd have to say this has been the most painless install/upgrade I have experienced since I switched to Ubuntu (been using since the last beta of Dapper Drake). Was it perfect? No, but we're heading in the right direction!!

---

### Post by Mark_in_Hollywood on 2009-12-01
After about 2 months of using Karmic, I still have a few problems. On boot (cold boot or warm boot), the panel applets have to be installed anew. 

I had used Deja Dup to back-up /home before doing a bare-metal install of KK. I used ext 4 on all partitions. That's a separate /home partition, swap and the OS's partition.

On boot (cold or warm boot), after entering the password, the desktop looks "almost normal". The icons on the desktop at the very top of the screen are cut off by the panel. That is resolved by System/Preferences/Appearance/Visual Effects and selecting the "Normal". The OS seeks "drivers" and then give 30 seconds or so to accept the change. If I don't accept nothing changes, obviously, but I must do this daily. Opening Chrome or Firefox, without changing to "normal" makes an unpleasant use. Screen (windows) don't open or close properly, can't be read easily/correctly.

I haven't been able to resolve no icons for Google's Picasa. I have to open that in a terminal.

While boot times are significantly faster, for me, that's a high price to pay for the 100-paper-cuts of a new OS. If it weren't for ext4, I wouldn't be here.

Thank you, community.

Got two machines: 
1: Dual boot Acer laptop (vista and karmic) 32 bit: No problems what so ever (upgraded from Jaunty)
 
2: 64 bit Phenon2 X3 system with asus mb and Nvidia onboard: Fresh install. Small problem with HDMI sound, solved by installing an ALSA-mixer and unmuting a channel. No biggie.
 
The only problem I've got, and have had since going ubuntu, is the Netgear WPN111v2 USB wireless that I want to mount on my 64 bit system. If anyone got a workable solution, please pm me. Having a wire running along my living room floor from my router is rather annoying.

### Post by petitlou60 on 2009-12-02
hello,

i have installed ubuntu 9.10 in dual boot mode with vista

i have resolved some problems

1) automatic partionning make only logical partition, for dual boot from vista you need absolutly a primary partition for /  so i have followed this path

1) from vista reduce my data partition by 10 Go
2) boot from live cd start install, choose language and keyboard then ....
3) choose manual partitioning then ...
     1) create primary ext4 partition for / of 7168Mo
     2) create primary swap partion of remaining free space
4) with advanced otions choose to install grub on /dev/sda3 then leave install to finish
5) reboot vista is starting (because grub is not in mbr)
6) with freeware easy bcd add in vista boot menu a stanza linux ubuntu 9.10 associated with partition marked linux native
7) reboot i have now the two choices, start linux  all is right

note   grub in mbr seems to be able to start vista but after dvd disappears completly ???
         9.10 is the fist linux release which make dvd avilable without coding acpi=off in kernel line

i observe a big bug :KS
1) with users-admin panel create a new user ok
2) attempt to switch user to this new one, system hangs i must start power off  !!!!!!
3) after reboot all seems ok but one users-admin panel only root user appears ??????
4) i can log any of my users but swiching lead always to system hangs !!!!!!!

### Post by Farmer of Bricks on 2009-12-02
I've been with Kubuntu since 7.10, and still loving it. As my monitor, RAM, and graphics card have improved, Kubuntu has taken up the slack admirably. I'm running 9.10 now, with visual effects tuned down to a sane level (nVidia GeForce MX 4000), and the graphics are running better now than they were back on 9.04.

I have only two niggling bugs: SVG icons in dropdown menus and in the taskbar appear much larger than they should be. The Icon doesn't change the size of the menu or taskbar, but instead bleeds of the edge of its defined area, creating an effect that would be rather interesting if it didn't obscure all other icons. The dropdown-menu problem was fixed when I switched to the Bespin style, but the wireless and Kmix icons in the system tray plasmoid are still large. It looks kinda cool for the wireless icon, but the Kmix icon looks plain weird. Images attached. 

Moving on. Most of my settings were carried over from 9.04, except for things that changed from KDE 4.2 to 4.3 : Window decoration, plasma theme compatibility, and theme style. Amarok 1.4 still works, and all my programs were kept.

I backed up my 8 gigs of relevant data to a USB hard drive (3 hours!), but did not end up needing that.

I'm a little miffed by the amount of time necessary to download the upgrade packages, but I can see that 1348 MB of data on a WiFi connection that maxes out at 120kbs is an acceptable reason. 

Overall, I'm happy, especially with the improved package manager and KRunner.

### Post by johnhdsi on 2009-12-02
I did a full install on my Win XP box at work, I put an old 40 gig seagate barracuda drive in and installed it there. Since the install I have had no major issues with anything, the funniest thing is my wireless card. The wireless card I have didn't or wouldn't work in any other version of Ubuntu without all this Ndiswrapper stuff and edits to files, once I installed 9.10 it worked flawless and not only that but it gets a stronger signal for some weird reason than it does when i run XP. he only issue I have not found a fix for at this point is getting Evolution to connect to my hotmail acct. and getting empathy up and running for a yahoo IM acct. I had empathy working when i did a Wui install to try out 9.10 but now it's not, but in the scope of things that's not a bad trade off for what could or has happened before. Overall I have to say that installing 9.10 has been a much easier experience than my previous installs with earlier versions (8.04 and up). On a side note I have to say that the users in the forum have gotten me through so many little hiccups along the way that they have proven invaluable to my overall success using Ubuntu and enjoying my time on it. Good work to all that made this O/S as great as it is!!!!

P.S. Side note #2...My pc runs faster with 9.10 installed on this crappy HD than my Win Xp does on my nice fast HD...Windows .....bleh...

John  :D

[COLOR=Blue]***pull down your pants and slide on the ice***[/COLOR]. (I hope you got long-johns on).

In other words, [COLOR=Orange]_***relax***_[/COLOR], [COLOR=YellowGreen]_**enjoy the Ubuntu Karmic ride.**_[/COLOR]

It's **free**, it's **open**, it's** loved**, **everything** is _**free**_ (just about), and, oh yes, it is _***[COLOR=Blue]FREE[/COLOR]***_.

[COLOR=Sienna]**Karmic Koala**[/COLOR] is a constant work in progress. And It is getting better and better.

Yes, there are a few bumps & dips on the trip to super-OS-dom.

[COLOR=SeaGreen]_***Everything always *[COLOR=DarkOrange]*eventually*[/COLOR]*****gets either **fixed or changed so that it will work.***_[/COLOR]

Many problems are actually**[COLOR=Blue] hardware related[/COLOR]** and not really Ubuntu Karmic's fault.

BIOS settings, hard-drive errors, older PCs, with a none-paid relatively small group of 

volunteers. - (were would Microsoft be today? They can't get it right with 1000s of 

well paid geniuses and billions of dollars spent, which is really pitiful.) -

[COLOR=SandyBrown]_**Ubuntu Karmic**_[/COLOR] is defrag free, mostly spyware & virus free and usually very forgiving.

Some problems in Ubuntu are actually permission related, not program related at all.

It would be good to know were all the configurations are located and have an easy way 

to recover from a critical error. No OS is perfect!

*[COLOR=Blue]We may need a [COLOR=Lime]**Ubuntu Karmic Koala for Dummies**[/COLOR] Book[/COLOR]* (electronic of course).

An *[COLOR=Blue]**[COLOR=Navy]update[/COLOR]** reversal script[/COLOR]* or program would be nice too. There are probably already 

_***[COLOR=Blue]Linux command line solutions[/COLOR]***_, for most problems, the Linux masters just need to 

_**[COLOR=Navy]patiently[/COLOR]**_ explain it to the former Microsoft group that are realizing the superior Linux- 

Ubuntu-Karmic-OS, it is far better than DOS or VISTA but it takes some hands-on work,

a Linux mindset, and knowledge of how Karmic works. (for instance, Xorg.conf is not 

necessary in Karmic, it may just get one in trouble.)

I've got **[COLOR=Black]*Vista*[/COLOR]** :( on one hard-drive (and rarely use it), **[COLOR=Blue]Karma[/COLOR]** **[COLOR=Blue]Koala[/COLOR]** ;) on the second 

HDD, _**[COLOR=Red]Lucid Lynx alpha[/COLOR]**_ :p on a reported bad HDD (according to SMART). Guess what, 

Vista refused to install or work on that HDD, [COLOR=Orange]_***Lucid works great.***_[/COLOR] I like it better than 

Windows 7 (which, honestly, should be called Vista SP3 alpha-beta rip-off, because 

people are paying Microsoft for the privilege of debugging their beta version.)

And, oh yes,[COLOR=SandyBrown]** _Karmic works well with_**[/COLOR] ***[COLOR=Navy]old[/COLOR]*** printers, scanners, mice, Monitors, Video 

cards, sound cards, and [COLOR=RoyalBlue]**WINE**[/COLOR] lets one run ones favourite Windows programs, that 

refuse to work on Vista or Windows 7, because Microsoft wants to force everyone to get 

rid of older, perfectly good hardware or software, and buy expensive newer stuff.

And, oh yes, [COLOR=RoyalBlue]**WINE**[/COLOR] is another**[COLOR=Blue] FREE[/COLOR]** piece of software running under Karmic. 

Sure, **[COLOR=RoyalBlue]WINE[/COLOR]** is still in development, and not all Windows software works with it, but one 

can find _**[COLOR=DarkOrange]even better Linux software to replace Microsoft with[/COLOR][COLOR=DarkOrange]![/COLOR]**_ -

[COLOR=Black]One thing one might want to see in Karmic & WINE is an [COLOR=Teal][B][I]add-on that assigns letters 

to HDDs[/I][/B][/COLOR][/COLOR][COLOR=Teal]*** & Disks, Music players, flash drives, etc.***[/COLOR] for compatibility purposes - Maybe 

"Magic Mongoose" or whatever appropriate name the Ubuntu masters will come up with

for the next version of Linux, will have a way to deal with software that's looking for a 

lettered mount volume. -

Well, here is to _**[COLOR=Teal]Ubuntu Karmic Koala & Lucid Lynx,[/COLOR]**_[COLOR=Black] great innovations[/COLOR], for the truly 

smart people of the world who appreciate the wisdom of [COLOR=DarkOrange]**UBUNTU-KARMA-LINUX**[/COLOR].

[COLOR=Black]**BavarianPH**, enjoy the international adventure of **[COLOR=Blue]Karmic Koala,[/COLOR]**

_**[COLOR=Blue]UBUNTU FOREVER!!![/COLOR]**_

### Post by can8dnSix on 2009-12-03
Installing Karmic Koala was simple and easy. I installed it to an R61i Thinkpad w/ 4GB of RAM. I used the 32 bit version of Ubuntu and did "sudo apt-get install linux-generic-pae" to allow me to see all the memory.
uname -r -> 2.6.31-15-generic-pae

I further paired w/ my LG Incite and it asked me if I wanted to share the Internet Access on the phone with the notebook. Now this is a feature that has been lacking in the past, but here, without any configuration worked right out of the box. 

I'm happy to say, my name is L, and "I'm Linux."
