### Post by markallenneil on 2009-12-03
Horrible experience, my Ubuntu is borked and I've no idea what to do now.

1) Did an in-situ upgrade... went to some appointments while it worked.
2) Came home 3 hours later... a message said "8 more minutes."
3) 20 minutes later still said "8 more minutes."  Obviously stuck.  Rebooted computer.
4) Boot was dying with mounting errors... couldn't mount any of my disks.
5) After reading a lot of web posts...
  a) Manually mounted disks and swap.
  b) Did a "sudo dkpg --configure -a".
  c) Did "apt-get -f install" and "apt-get upgrade".
6) Lots of stuff happened, it really seemed to be finishing up the install.
7) Finally rebooted... had the 9.04 Ubuntu boot screen... then a really pretty new boot screen which I suppose is 9.10.
8) New login method... kind of like WinXP.
9) Then booted into my desktop.  Hurray!

Well, so I thought.  60 seconds later I had a black screen and a blinking cursor.

10) Reboot.  Same behavior.
11) Reboot again.  Don't log-in this time.  60 seconds and a black screen.

I'm guessing something is being loaded in the background and failing miserably... enough to kill something important.

So I don't know WTF to do now.  I'm no computer idiot, but this really shouldn't be this hard... what with the command-line and mysterious black screens and what-not.

Is it possible to burn a CD and somehow repair this borked system?  I had important files and databases and had configured things nicely.  I can probably get the files back, but must I *really* start from scratch again?

Now I'm just plain scared of ever updating Ubuntu again.  From the poll, it seems 35% of folks out there are in the same boat (serious issues they cannot resolve).

Help, anyone?

### Post by dettaglio on 2009-12-04
Upgraded a month ago. Sound became an issue like before 9.04: OpenArena doesn't work with player running, sound engine causes high cpu load or something, anyway it break sound in OA and makes the game rather a slideshow.

Sound in Wine games starts poping, and resetting it 5-20 times helps. (fortunately in Live for speed there's a keystroke for that). Why sound breaks is still a puzzle for me: sometimes apparently because of high CPU load, another time because of Skype, Flash working in the browser, etc.

Empathy is a thowaway compared to Pidgin becuase of bad ICQ functionality, and poor interface in general. Pidgin isn't perfect either, it's like a wooden toy, but at least it works and shows users correctly. Empathy shows ugly UINs, renaming is impossible or hidden, can't read profiles, etc.

The few good things can be backported to 9.04: new versions of many apps. Turning off USB devices is nice, but not worth all the 9.10 sacrifices.

Very dissapointed.

### Post by IBadget on 2009-12-04
I installed Karmic, replacing Windows. The problem is that I had to disable ACPI (acpi=off) to install and to get Karmic booted up. At first, I was nervous because I had no clue how to tell Karmic not to use ACPI. I booted from the LiveCD to get on the Internet to research on how to disable ACPI. I found that I had to add "acpi=off" to the end of the relevant boot command, after "quiet splash". After temporarily disabling ACPI by pressing e to edit the boot command, Karmic worked. Then I researched on how to permanently disable ACPI and found that I had to edit /etc/default/grub to add the "acpi=off" option after the "quiet splash" and used the update-grub command. Somewhat painful to get it to work, but it finally works.

### Post by Lyleb on 2009-12-04

### Post by sboisen on 2009-12-05
I started with a working 8.10, upgraded over the network to 9.04 (seemed to work) and then 9.10, which did not work and rendered the system unusable. It would start up and give me a login prompt, but then never get beyond trying to start the window manager. 

So i gave up the upgrade altogether (there was no data or environment i cared about keeping: this is an older box, and not my main computer), downloaded the ubuntu-9.10-desktop-i386.iso, and burned it to disk. 

My initial attempt at installing failed: i did a total HD rewrite, but at about 80% "configuring apt", it stopped making progress, so after waiting about 20 minutes, i rebooted and started all over again. I had a wired network connection, so this time i pulled that first. 

The second attempt succeeded (again, i rewrote the HD: this time it recognized the 9.10 install, but i figured it was safer to just start over). I've been able to log in, Firefox works ... so now i'm just downloading a whole raft of updates before i start reloading the few applications i had on 8.04.

Also, i was disappointed to find that boxee doesn't support this distribution yet: too new i guess, and my bad for not checking first. 

Overall, i'm not very happy with the experience: i wasted a lot of time with the failed network upgrade, couldn't find solutions on the forum (i looked), and i'm back a square zero kind of wishing i'd stuck with 8.10. I'm not grousing, just providing the feedback that i believe this thread is asking for.

### Post by erniej on 2009-12-05
[FONT=Arial][SIZE=2][FONT=Arial]Worst experience of my 20+ year computing existence. And then the best.

Let me set the scene: after 2 weeks trying to upgrade to Win 7, then discovering I had a defective disk (MS confirmed it), then finally installing 7 only to discover Home Premium has been deliberately crippled to force you to upgrade to Pro (for a mere $90!) I realized I had to escape from Redmond forever or risk becoming a danger to myself and others.

After leaving the frying pan I merrily entered the fire, downloading ver 9.10, burning to a disk and attempting to install it on a Dell 1520 laptop (1G/120GB). For the next 2 weeks (it may have been more, but I'm not counting the screaming episodes or the catatonic states) I attempted to get 9.10 installed.

I say attempted because I never succeeded, even with abundant help from hardened Ubuntu veterans in newsgroups and joining this forum to seek further assistance.

First off, it allegedly installed, then informed me that it couldn't run because there were 298-307 bad sectors on the HDD. Since I had checked out the disk using two disk tools beforehand, and used FDISK and FORMAT to wipe it clean, I couldn't see how it could be true. After trying 3 disk management programs, including the HDD manufacturer's own setup and analysis program, I knew it wasn't true.

I was then urged by legions of well-wishers and evangelists to install 8.04/8.10 (I tried both)[/FONT][/SIZE][/FONT][FONT=Arial][SIZE=2][FONT=Arial] which, I was assured was so bulletproof and easy to use it could be installed on a typewriter if necessary. Each time I wiped the drive and re-F'd it to level the playing field.

Again it pretended to install, and I was assured that it was all set up and ready to go, and that the HDD was flawless. Except nothing worked, not even the wireless network, preventing me from doing much. 

When I say nothing worked, I mean N-O-T-H-I-N-G worked. Everything I tried to do involved *sudo* command lines, until I began to think I had found out where DOS went when it died. All the command line entries asked for my password, but didn't recognize it, and either I didn't have permission to do anything or the command line entry was dead or frozen, or sat there quietly mocking me and making rude symbols whenever it thought I wasn't looking. 

Back on the GUI side, I tried to get the network set up manually, which I can do, and have done in my sleep (that's another story). But either the info would appear in the little windows but the Apply button was greyed out, or it wouldn't accept any input at all. Not even a blinking cursor.

I'll leave out the succeeding 8-12-hour days over the next week+ (I'm retired, have far more persistence than average and I HATE to be beaten by machinery). Suffice it to say that nothing ever worked, and the problem seemed to be the Broadcom network card installed in the Dell. I then discovered that there were no drivers for our Linksys-N home net and internet connections, which I still find hard to believe, they being the biggest outfit of its kind.

At that point I struck my colors, retired from the field, and wept myself to sleep under my good blanky in my favorite closet. Ubuntu was not for me.

Three days elapse, and I realize I don't need the second hard drive in my main desktop Dell anymore, having a 64GB flash drive to use for backup now. So, madcap, devil-may-care creature that I am, I took my CD disk already loaded with the 9.10 and tried to install it on that now-empty and formatted D: drive. 

It worked flawlessly. I needed only one or two command-line entries, and I'm using it now to write this. The only glitch is that it won't find our Canon MP560 wireless printer, even with Linux drivers from Japan. (Canon doesn't make Linux drivers either) Apart from that I am as happy as the proverbial marine shellfish, and am learning hourly about all the neat things I can do now. 

And it hasn't crashed once!!

As soon as I'm comfortable doing it, I'll repartition the main HDD and copy a disk image of D: to it. I am a very happy Ubuntu user. :D


[/FONT][/SIZE][/FONT]

---

### Post by edantes on 2009-12-06
My Vista moment with Ubuntu was 9.04 -- problems with ATI's end of support for my graphics card and wifi support which depended on an old madwifi release.   

This time (9.10 Karmic) around everything went well, thanks.  I did a fresh install to upgrade my filesystem to ext4.  The opensource ATI and wifi support improved greatly and cured all 

I am running the AMD64 version and for the first time sun-java has full 64-bit support including the Firefox plugin.  The only remaining 64-bit headache is Flash.  I am running the Adobe alpha 64-bit version which is buggy even for Flash standards.

Overall, Karmic has been a worthy upgrade.  The adjustments I had to make are mostly related with updating some outside apps with older versions in the Ubuntu repositories.

### Post by cabotcat on 2009-12-06
I am certain my experience is not unique. I was running WIndows XP and Ubuntu 9.04 in a dual boot configuration on an Asus 1000H netbook. I upgraded to 9.10 UNR using the Wubi installer (huge mistake). After the upgrade I essentially had two boot loader programs upon every boot. The recent upgrade to the 2.6.31-15 kernel left me at the grub prompt like many so many others. With the help of this board I was able to repair my set up but continued with the "2 boot loader" scenario. Yesterday the 16 kernel was available so I downloaded and installed it and the grub prompt issue returned.

At this point I was frustrated so I decided to go with the ful blown version of 9.10. I tried to remove 9.10 UNR through the Windows add or remove programs application to no avail. At this time I downloaded the Wubi uninstaller program which also did not do the trick. I modified the boot.ini file and was able to revert to a single Windows XP boot scenario. I downloaded the full desktop ISO version of 9.10 and loaded it to a USB drive. I booted off the drive and started a full blown install of 9.10. I was able to resize and create partitions for 9.10, swap and program and document space, all while maintaining the integrity of Windows XP. Upon completion of the install a reboot loaded the proper boot loader and all is right with the world. The full version of 9.10 runs so much better and faster on its own partition without any of the pieces necessary for a Wubi install of UNR. 

Moral of the story? I was to the point of turning my back on Ubuntu. With a little perseverance and much help from this board I am ecstatic with my current configuration. Hopefully after some time spent with Ubuntu I can reach the point of removing XP completely. 

Thanks to all and I am glad I am able to share this success story.

---

### Post by atboom on 2009-12-06
I finally got my Win7 free upgrade disc from HP (didn't want to install Ubuntu only to have to do Win7 right after) and began working on my dual boot setup of Win7 and Ubuntu 9.10. Well to start I had problems with partitioning. Not because of Ubuntu, but because HP ships their new PCs with one main partition and one Recovery partition; both primary partitions. I was going to set up a Win7 partition, a large data partition, an Ubuntu partition, a small swap and leave the recovery partition. But you an only have 4 primary partitions, so I had to delete the Recovery partition (hehe). (Not to mention that Win7 tried to not let me partition it as small as I wanted from Windows, but I used gparted then rebooted to Win7 to repair it)
So I finally get to installing. Using the LiveCD I can install my wireless card from restricted drivers already on the CD. Installation goes smooth and I reboot into my new Ubuntu desktop. First thing to do, install the wireless. Click Harware drivers, select one, hit Activate...... a few seconds later the system locks up entirely, forcing a hard reboot. After I'm back at the desktop I try to finish installing the drivers, only now I've picked up the bug that prevents you from installing/uninstalling anything (always says installArchives failed). Spent about an hour looking up things to try from here and elsewhere, then just said to heck with it, I hadn't done anything yet, so I reinstalled (not a great start lol). 2nd go around no crash and all is well!

### Post by n4pgw on 2009-12-06
My experience is probably unique in that I am both a newbie to Linux and Ubuntu but I have installed Ubuntu several dozen times now.  I am a windoze user from way, way, back.  ("fantatic" meant I upgraded my 4.77 Mhz PC to an 8 Mhz XT.)  I am still in the steep learning curve phase and just now getting to know my way around.

In the last few weeks, I started experimenting with Linux flavors looking for one I would like.  I played with 31 flavors before deciding on Ubuntu.  I first tested Ubuntu 8.04.02 desktop.  By the time I made my decision, 9.10 was out so I have been working with it.  

I first installed Ubuntu on an external USB drive with my internal HDD carefully turned off so as not to lose access to Windoze.  I did not always like the configuration so I kept installing it on the external hdd until I was comfortable with it.  All the installs went well, I was still experimenting at the time.  However, the drive became flaky, so I purchased another.  I have installed it several more times.  I then started installing it on a flash drive.  After installing it on three flash drives as though it were a main drive, I learned that I would quickly destroy the flash drives so I decided to install it on internal drives.

I have a new laptop with Vista, recovery dvd and nothing installed yet, so I figured that I had nothing to lose installing Ubuntu and they could share the same hdd.  The install went nearly flawlessly.  I had to let vista run the scandisk after the partition was resized, and I had to connect the laptop to the wired network to do the first pass of updates.  After that, it was able to recognize the wireless adapter.  

The laptop is cheep Dell Inspiron that was on sale at Walmart a few months back for $400.  There are two drivers for the wireless network that Ubuntu finds, one is proprietary and one is not.  Only the proprietary version works.  The laptop (I am on now) has no problems that I have seen in the last week in which I have been toying with it.   

The version I installed on the 500GB external HDD seems to work well no matter which computer I plug it into.  It even adapted to the various monitors on the computers I plugged it into, two different laptops and two different desktops.  It even adapted immediately to my new wide screen when I connected it to my desktop.

The two hardest tasks I had, so far with one exception, has been to get it to play DVDs and how to install it on flash drives. 

I reinstalled Ubuntu over a dozen times just trying to get DVDs to play.  I had some interesting experience with Mplayer.  I had some movies that would work well, some would not be recognized, some would play audio only and some would show video and have no audio.  I finally managed to get VLC media player to cooperate with all my movies.  Also, finding the sites with accurate, specific information on watching DVDs is difficult, at best.  There are very many hits when searching.  Most are forum posts and/or older versions.  Some do not give enough information, "install libdvd2css" and others are overkill having you install a dozen packages so you can play videos of various formats, audio formats, and internet videos with divx encryptions as well as "useful tools".  While the site with the overkill works, I still do not know what the minimum requirements are to watch encrypted DVDs--But I have enjoyed watching movies since installing everything. 

After having such great success with Ubuntu installed on a USB hdd, I decided to install it on a USB flash drive.  I have a 2GB and a 4GB Sandisk and a PNY 8GB.  I went thru the first set of instructions I found and installed it on the 2GB first.  It worked just like the CD version.  The only problem was that, just like the CD version, everthing was reset when the system was rebooted.  I tried installing it on the 8GB and had no luck.  It could not format the drive and would crash in the install so I tried the 4GB Sandisk.  It installed successfully on the 4GB so i  tried once again on the PNY.  I had to take the PNY thru three configurations to clean it so Ubuntu would install on it.  I do not remember the exact order of things, but I had to use Windoze to delete the partition, Ubuntu booted from an HDD install and Ubuntu booted from the CD-ROM to ultimately delete the partition data and get Ubuntu to install.  In the process of doing this, I came to a point where Windoze recognized it as a 2.4 Terabyte drive.  :)  Of course, Ubuntu recognized it as an 8GB blank.  (Victory at last.)  I then booted Ubuntu from a CD and installed the OS as though it were a hard drive.  Once I was successful in that, I read that the life expectancy of using the flash drive like that was less than a year.  I tried several times to follow the process of adding a persistent partition for the 4GB flash drive, but I gave up before becoming successful and went on to installing Ubuntu onto my laptop.

Installing Ubuntu onto my laptop was pretty much flawless.  It resized the NTFS partition, installed itself and a swap partition on part of the unused drive, created a Grub boot loader that works well.  It was on this laptop that I followed the overkill instructions to get the DVDs to play.  I did have to connect the network up through a wire to get the original updates.  After that, Ubuntu found two drivers to use with my wireless adapter.  Only the proprietary driver worked.  

After having such success with the laptop, I figured it would be a cinch to install Ubuntu on my main desktop.  I installed a 120GB hdd in my desktop and booted Ubuntu CD to install it.  I partitioned a section at the end for the swap and another partition with NTFS for data leaving 60 GB available for Ubuntu.  After the install, before booting, I set swapon to the last partition, deleted the swap partition it created, and stretched the Ubuntu partition to fill the original partition back.  I did a shut down and removed the cd.  When I booted, I got "GRUB _".  That is still all I get.

Although I had many good installs, leave it to Murphy to crash the one most important drive.

NOTICE: I will be posting my problems to the appropriate forums for tech support. Please do not offer help in reply to this post.  Thank you

As far as the basic install of Ubuntu 9.10, almost all of my installs have gone flawlessly.  My only real failure was with a computer running XP-Pro.  I am glad to have chosen Ubuntu as my Linux.

Buck

---

### Post by hawthornso23 on 2009-12-06
Ubuntu Upgrade. 

After reading the forums expected possible problems with my ATI video driver, so made sure I had the latest version installed and the installation file handy.

The upgrade process stalled out when downloading files and made no progress for a day or so on my slowish connection, being stuck on file 1648 out of 1879. Was able to figure out that the file it choked on was a large nexuiz data file. Decided to uninstall nexuiz to clear the blockage and reinstall it after the upgrade. Recommend if you have nexuiz you consider this.

The upgrade asked me about several modified config files mostly related to video. It was not clear what the ideal choice was in these cases and indeed I probably did it wrong as after the reboot ubuntu complained about video and came back up in limited graphics mode. A reinstallation of the graphics driver cleared the problem. 

Next reboot it complained about a missing swap partition. Checking in /etc/fstab and in /dev/disk/by-uuid I noticed that the UUID of my swap partition had apparently changed. Edited the fstab to put in the new uuid for the swap partition. 

Next reboot was fine.

Everything now seems to work. My customised applications menu got a little disarrayed, but that is easily fixed.  Went to medibuntu and added the repositary there to upgrade medibuntu applications. Reinstalled nexuiz.

Sweet.

### Post by artisan on 2009-12-08
Just done the upgrade for a workstation.

Firefox passwords lost. Export of passwords was done before upgrade.
Firefox will not accept the passwords back. But, I have a copy anyway.

So backup your firefox passwords if you have a lot.


Apart from that...
The upgrade looks very good.
:)

Edit:
I ended up with a blank password folder and Firefox would not let me import my saved passwords.
Firefox would not remember my old passwords.
Turns out that somewhere during the upgrade, the default user account was corrupted.
If this happens create a new profile for your self and your new profile will accept you old passwords.

### Post by martin.sullivan on 2009-12-08
This is only my first posting on this forum, so bear with me.   I've been a long-time user of firstly pilot-link, then jpilot and thence gnome-pilot to synchronise a variety of PIM data to a Palm Tungsten E. Physically, I use USB and I've found that the 'usb:' interface works best and doesn't appear to require to much magic, for example:  pilot-dlpsh -p usb: -i  This has worked in just about all versions of Ubuntu, until recently that is. It would appear that Karmic (9.10) doesn't seem to have such a working system. I've done a bit of googling and indeed searched this and other forum. It would appear that I may not be alone in this, but most of the replies feature pedagogic explanations of the 'visor' module and 'serial' interfaces on USB, which I consider deprecated.  I have some time on my hands and I'm prepared to characterise this problem further, the necessary hooks seem to be in the kernel although a tcpdump-like USB protocol analyser will have to be acquired.   So please e-mail your encouragement, or if I'm completely wrong and I'm missing some minor novice-type trick please post a solution as a reply here.  At the moment the workaround is to keep one PC back on Intrepid (9.04).  I've also made a similar posting at the Brighthand (PDA) forum.  Martin (ms09 at zois in the 'co uk' domain).

### Post by gbtitus on 2009-12-09
The upgrade to Karmic itself went without a hitch, but I do have one nagging problem: /usr/bin/X is leaking memory (in the heap, according to both pmap and /proc/`pidof /usr/bin/X`/maps).  Oddly, the Aisle Riot solitaire game seems to drive the leak the worst, adding on the order of 10 mb of /usr/bin/X heap per game.  The system has a dual-core 3 gHz Pentium 4 and 1 gb of memory, with an Nvidia GeForce4 MX 440, and runs the Nvidia driver (v 96.43.14, from the Nvidia web site).  If I stay logged in for long enough I'll eventually find /usr/bin/X requiring so much memory the system becomes unusable.  Once, memorably, it took 1/2 hour to log out; this was before I figured out what to watch for.  This leak didn't occur with Jaunty.  I haven't put any significant effort into chasing down the leak yet.  It's easy enough to log out and back in.

Oh, and WMV files won't play in firefox; it says it lacks a decoder.  I haven't tried chasing that down either.  This is more of a work machine than a play machine.

Neither of these problems is causing me great difficulty, and overall usability seems nicer.  On the whole I'm pleased.  Good work!

### Post by biglarryk56 on 2009-12-09
After two months of experience with Jaunty (x86) and Karmic (amd64), I've given up on Ubuntu and gone back to Windows 7 Ultimate...here's why:
[LIST]
[*]I could never get my webcam to work...I have a builtin Ricoh WDM webcam, and the r5u870 drivers would not work in Jaunty, and there's no 64-bit driver available for Karmic.
[*]I do basic video editing, and none of the Linux-based video editors (LIVES, Kdenlive, Pitivi) work as easily and seamlessly as good old Windows Movie Maker.
[*]No Linux-based DRM removal software (I use Tunebite 7, and it won't install under WINE).
[*]Amazon doesn't have a Linux-based Unbox Player available (however, they do have a Linux-based MP3 downloader)
[*]Rhapsody and iTunes won't run under WINE, there's no native Linux port.
[*]Since my home network has a host-based all-in-one (Dell A940), I can't do any printing whatsoever from my laptop's wireless connection.
[*]Samba gives me fits...sometimes my Windows-based devices show up, most of the time they don't.
[*]The nVidia 185 drivers (recommended) were a hit-and-miss when installed.
[/LIST]On the plus side:
[LIST]
[*]WinFF is far superior in converting video formats.
[*]mimms makes it extremely easy to download MMS video streams.
[/LIST]In short, it's a lack of native Linux applications for what I use that keeps me from staying with Ubuntu. I'll stay informaed about new developments, and perhaps I'll go back someday, but for now, I'll just have to stick with Win7.
 
HP Pavilion dv9005us
AMD Turlon 64 X2 TL-50
2GB DDR2 RAM
nVideo GeForce Go 6150
80GB Fujitsu SATA HD

### Post by stingx on 2009-12-09
Clean installation of 9.04 and then upgrading to 9.10 works just fine... But, one dissapointment.. video drivers... Amd has recently abandoned support for many "old" hardware, such as my "ATI x1400". So, two possible solutions: stick with old driver (wich works perfectly... many-many sleep/wakeup) or open source driver. After upgrade, ubuntu starts with "vesa" driver and proprietary driver manager give me no choice, so manual editing of xorg.conf solved situation.
Open source driver works. opengl accelerated, but there a sleep/wakeup problems. When trying to send system to sleep, it seems like i was ignored, but after 20-40 seconds it "suddenly" going into sleep (less than a half-second from monitor blanks to blinking power led).
After that, system normally awakes (but with display backlight slightly dimmed). No other sleeps are possible. When trying to send system to sleep 2-nd time, it's simply shows random color bars on display(some of them are blinking) and totally hangs (even Alt+SysRq+B won't work. I think, kernel panic).

So it will be very helpful to identify that situations at drivers update. So, fglrx driver packages should be split to current version and "old". So, i will never be asked with driver upgrade and everything will just work (but there should be some job done to support proprietary driver compatible with each new kernel interface.)

### Post by martin.sullivan on 2009-12-11
This has been solved.

Strace(8) has been used to discover that there were some permission
problems when an 'ordinary' user attempted to work with
pilot-dlpsh. After permissions were granted by causing the ordinary
user to be in plugdev, adm, admin, and dialout via the /etc/group
file. pilot-link, jpilot and gnome-pilot worked. It is unclear what
the exact combination that is required for use, but simply putting the
user in plugdev was insufficient.

The failing permissions seemed to be from an ioctl call, and there was
nothing in the normal file-space based set that indicated anything out
of the ordinary.

Did it work flawlessly ? YES
Did you get problems ? NOT at install on any 3 machines.
Did you manage to solve them ? NOT yet
if yes how ?
Problems with EXT4 partitions on one pc.
My disk was fine with EXT3 but on EXT4 it looses files and I do not know if this is a 
EXT4 problem or a bad disk. I will re-install karmic on ext3 as a friend at work has had the same problem. Is this common?
Karmic is pretty cool. Shame dontzap is not supported anymore. CTRL, ALT, Backspace though not needed now is nice to have.

### Post by zieglerj on 2009-12-12
Upgraded from 9.04. For some reason the kernel didn't install and this reeked havoc with my NVIDIA. 

sudo apt-get install linux-headers-2.6.31* 

then delete /boot/grub/menu.lst

sudo update-grub

allowed me to manually reinstall the NVIDIA driver. Aside from very minor sound issues, it's worked great since. I just want to know how to get into the login screen settings. The new menu for them sucks compared with the options we used to have.

### Post by Purple Mouse on 2009-12-14
Hi all, I'm a noob to the world of Linux and going through a few learning curves in the process. I have to say I've had positive experience so far with few issues. I downloaded the 9.10 NBR iso file and created a bootable USB. Installed on a clean SSD. Most things appears to work. I managed to crash the system using the wrong dongle info but found the commands to undo the damage and it still works fine! I'm using the local cafe for wireless connection. It just works!
Among many others Trakerjon provided good basic info [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1310795](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1310795)
**Skype** I am still seeking info on how to setup 590 Plantronics bluetooth headset, it is recognised but I'm not able to use it for skype. I must be missing something here...maybe its drivers or something...I've asked ASUS for info on the Sound & Video cards so I can source the drivers (am I going down the right path here?)
**Music** I've been a heavy user of Windows Media Player & iTunes as I have a large music collection and like many of the features. I understood Amarok to be a good open source alternative however nothing plays. I've scanned my collection several times and see the files but nothing plays. Any suggestions?
**Desktop** Yeah yeah so I'm still moving in my mind from Windows to Linux however I'm stumped as to why the first thing I see is the 'Home' screen when I've tried to setup a desktop. Widgets dont stick to the desktop and the desktop is 'buried' /home/rich/Desktop. Am I missing something here?

The good stuff is that the basic 9.1 NBR just works. This is so refreshing and encouraging for those seeking an alternative computing world without walls, gates and windows.

### Post by lian1238 on 2009-12-15
> **Purple Mouse said:**
> 
**Desktop** Yeah yeah so I'm still moving in my mind from Windows to Linux however I'm stumped as to why the first thing I see is the 'Home' screen when I've tried to setup a desktop. Widgets dont stick to the desktop and the desktop is 'buried' /home/rich/Desktop. Am I missing something here?

Not 100% sure what you mean, but try running (Alt+F2) gconf-editor and go to /apps/nautilus/preferencs/ and uncheck desktop_is_home_dir.

Edit: then refresh (F5) the desktop.

### Post by cmarslett on 2009-12-17
[B][B]I had no trouble with my upgrade other than a few video codecs stopped working and I could not use either of my printers (Canon MF4270 and Samsung CLP-500).

The ubuntuforums.com thread [/B]"Issues installing Canon ip1900 due to libcupsys2 superseded by libcups2" showed me the way to edit the Canon common.deb file to fix the dependency issue for that driver (had to change the dependency on libcupsys2 to one on libcups2 since the transition packakge libcupsys2 is gone, or so I think).

Just reinstalling the Samsung drivers worked for the CLP-500.

I'm still working in background mode to find out why the codecs don't work.  That's a very minor issue though.  The printers were what mattered to me.
[/B]

### Post by rhardie on 2009-12-18

### Post by youngmix on 2009-12-19
I upgraded from 9.04 to 9.10. As expected, I lost numerous programs/features. (Like my fish bowl in compiz fuzion :() But, the system's genuinely fast and smooth. Some programs didn't function too well, either, and weren't being properly maintained by myself or synaptic. So, I edited my sources to include the "ultimate edition" repository and grabbed a copy of ultimatix, which I used to upgrade the programs which needed it. Problems solved. The only problems I still have are editing my login screen and grub splash. Can't do it properly yet and I really miss kgrubeditor. It always worked like a charm for me. At the moment, I'm having too much fun with Nautilus/Gnome/Xfce to really care. Getting everything running the way I wanted only took 7 hours of playing with about 2 hours of work.

### Post by kaligus on 2009-12-20
first *upgrade* machine no problems.  Mostly headless play toy webserver.
* pentium 4, 750MB RAM, 450mhz dual 80g drives, 4 PATA, no audio, onboard vga-ish graphics... noah used this bad boy once upon a time.


second machine *sort of clean* (4 drives one of which is /home since edgy or so, overwrite /etc /var /usr because I do not have space to install new)
* AMD 64, 6GB RAM, nvidia 6600, 1.2TB hard drives 80% full until I get a project archived off, gigabyte ma770-ud3 main board, onboard sweet audio (at least on live cd and 9.04), scsi (adaptec 2940) without the scanner plugged in, 3 total PATA ports, 6 total SATA.

2) as soon as I install nvidia drivers I boot to a psychedelic flashing screen tty1 which can not be typed on.

3) had several kernel screen flashes first boot after install finished.  never repeated since that I have seen.

### Post by ScottinSoCal on 2009-12-20
I had 9.04 with a clean install, then my hard drive started failing. Rather than back everything up, I just backed up my data files, replaced the hard drive, and did a clean install of 9.10.

I'm running a Dell D810 laptop, 1920x1200 display, 2 GB of RAM, new 320 GB HDD, 2.1 GHz processor. It has bluetooth, modem, Intel wireless NIC, etc. The only issue is minor, and it seems to be across many laptops, not just mine. Suspend doesn't work reliably unless I trigger it manually. Everything else works flawlessly, and much better than it did under WinXP. My wireless connection would go down at least once a day, and I'd have to reboot to get it back. Something with the driver, obviously, but it was annoying, and reinstalling the driver didn't seem to help. I couldn't let it run without rebooting for more than a few days, or it got flaky. All of that has gone away. I've permanently upgraded away from Windows.

### Post by basmooyman on 2009-12-27
Installed Karmic on my new Asus eeepc T91. Worked out of the box, but i did reinstall a few times before the installation worked the way I wanted. 
What I did now was ;

1) insert the extra 16Gb sd in the side slot, 

2) I kept the XP installation in place and made it dual boot. The first 16 Gb devided equaly between XP and Karmic and the home dir on the additional 16Gb card.

3) After the install I got all the upgrades using the Upgrade Manager.

4) Then I installed evtouch, using Synaptics, and calibrated the touchscreen using the instructions by jcole (thanks); [http://setupguides.blogspot.com/2009/11/touchscreen.html](http://setupguides.blogspot.com/2009/11/touchscreen.html).

5) Afterwards I added the Poulsbo repositorys using the method described at:
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/HardwareSupportComponentsVideoCardsPoulsbo/](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/HardwareSupportComponentsVideoCardsPoulsbo/)


After this the resolution was fine and reaction was less sluggish. The touchsreen worked and was alligned.

wifi, bluetooth worked out of the box. I did install blueman to be able to go online using my phone.

For now it works OK. But I am still looking for a few solutions;
 - suspend and hibernate dont work.
 - Camera doesnt work
 - some fn buttons dont work(backlight) wifi toggle does work.
 - rotate screen button doesnothing, would be nice to be able to use it.
 - there seems to be a problem with USB stick. I run moinmoin from it but it gets me errors. Probably to do with write permissions.


I am pleased with the installation, the machine works fine and i will stick to it and hope the minor issues get solved in later upgrades.

Thanks guys

---

### Post by lnx on 2009-12-28
I recently upgraded 3 laptops from 9.04 to 9.10.

In 9.04, Mobile Broadband worked superbly.

In 9.10, Mobile Broadband is fatally buggy.
         /etc/resolv.conf is no longer updated with any nameservers
         for Mobile Broadband (Swedish networks "Telia" and "3")
         The correct data is in /etc/ppp/resolv.conf
         After every reconnect, /etc/resolv.conf data is wiped out,
         and I have to copy the information manually.
         Making a symbolic link does not work - the link is removed
         at the next network connection.

Horrible! I have seen that many others have problems with 9.10 and
         NetworkManager, but no bug fix seems to appear.
         How can such a serious functional bug slip through release
         testing?

         I will have to downgrade to 9.04 if at all possible (I have
         hacked various Linux distributions since 1995), or install
         from scratch.

### Post by palenekim on 2009-12-29
I am a new member to the Linux community and I recently downloaded Karmic (64-bit) to my laptop. I did a little bit of homework first. 

When I first bought my Laptop it had Windows XP pre-installed and I remained using it for many years. I have and had never had any problems with the setup so it's not like I have anything bad to say against windows. My computer has a sticker on it which says that it is designed for XP but is capable of Windows Vista. Unfortunately, I heard a lot of bad stories about Vista so I chose not to install it. After the years passed I was satisfied with XP. 

I was considering getting Windows 7, then I discovered about Linux while being online and how it is free to download and setup. This intrigued me so I decided to investigate. After understanding the capabilities of my laptop I chose to install (64-bit) Karmic.  My laptop has an intel t5500 processor with 2Gb Ram with nvidia hd graphics card so I new I was able to install 64-bit Karmic. Additionally, my laptop has 2 hard drives so the installation and subsequent partitioning of the second hard drive which was exclusive to Linux made the download very easy (I leave the first hard drive exclusive to Windows XP which I can access through "dual boot" by means of "Grub."  

Funnily enough I initially installed the wrong version of Ubuntu which was meant for "Network" computers. I eliminated this install and tried again with the correct version. After I installed the correct version I input all the necessary codecs and commands to download the updates so my computer could play video. The only issue I had was with the of Adobe Flash, I was not able to watch Hulu or Youtube correctly. Fortunately that was taken care of with the instructions left on another thread to install a prerelease version of Flash 10 for a 64-bit setup. Hopefully Adobe will rectify the problem that way it can be part of Karmic's regular updates. 

Other than that little problem, everything else was a cinch. 

(For people who want to know about the thread which helped me install a pre-release version of 64-bit Adobe Flash... read very carefully the first post of this thread:   [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1358591&highlight=hulu+problems+64-bit+Karmic](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1358591&highlight=hulu+problems+64-bit+Karmic) )

### Post by silvagroup on 2010-01-11
My two cents....
I have to say that my upgrades went very well. I have upgraded two of my five systems so far. One HP laptop w/Intel 64 and one desktop w/aAMD 64. Did them two weeks ago and I have yet to see any major issues with my upgrade.
The one problem I have is not a show stopper and it started later on in 9.04 and has carried over to 9.10. Again it's just an inconvenience and probably a simple config or version issue with java and adobe, I think.

Endolith it's your money, but I would first try a fresh install of 9.10 making sure you use a good download, they do get corrupted sometimes in the download process. Also you may want to try some other *nix version which maybe better suited for your system specs, although Ubuntu should do, minimum system requirements are:
 700 MHz x86 processor
 384 MB of system memory (RAM)
 8 GB of disk space
 Graphics card capable of 1024x768 resolution
 Sound card
 A network or Internet connection
If you don't have these mins you can still install 9.10 using alternate install cd at some performance loss. But there also are other *nix versions that which have lesser systems requirements if need be.

### Post by mlnease on 2010-01-11
Hello All,

In my ninth week now using Karmic exclusively--works great after a few minor bumps initially with the torrent-downloaded ISO.  I reinstalled with the Canonical disk and it's been smooth sailing ever since, consistent with my experience of dozens of installs of Dapper and Jaunty on many different computers and I'm no one's idea of a Linux hacker--just a typical ex-MS GUI user with minimal command line skills.  I suppose this somehow makes me a 'resident shill'--so be it.

All of that said, I hit a snag this morning and could use a little help troubleshooting.  Update Manager froze during a typical (though large) update.  I gave it fifteen minutes or so (with minimal CPU activity per System Monitor) and finally force-quit and attempted to reboot--couldn't get beyond the GRUB screen.

So I restarted with Lucid Alpha 1 Live CD (for the hell of it--seems to work well too) and will try to troubleshoot from here.  Think I'll go to my most recent Sbackup backup in var/backup and try to restore, but receive the error message, "You do not have the permissions necessary to view the contents of "backup"."  So I opened gksudo Nautilus, but couldn't find my file system there.  I did find a link there called 'Access-Your-Private-Data' but it appears dead.  So per the 'Read Me' in the same directory, in a terminal I entered 'ecryptfs-mount-private' but received 'ERROR: Encrypted private directory is not setup properly'.

I remember accessing my files from a live cd before but don't recall how to to it--any help would be much appreciated.

Many thanks as always to the developers and the Ubuntu community for making participation in the evolution of this great OS such a pleasure--well, with realistic expectations and a modicum of patience anyway. 

Cheers,

mike

---

### Post by HappyFeet on 2010-01-11
> **mlnease said:**
> 
I remember accessing my files from a live cd before but don't recall how to to it--any help would be much appreciated.


Just go to Places>Computer and you should see your hard drive.

### Post by bcrowell on 2010-01-15
I've been very disappointed with this upgrade. Sound is even more broken than it was in Jaunty. I also ran into this new bug in xsplash [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xsplash/+bug/504403](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xsplash/+bug/504403) , which causes every login session to hang for 15 seconds. The reason is that xsplash has a 15-second delay hardcoded into it, in a function whose name in the source code is (I'm not making this up) temporary_hack_for_initial_fade(). I ended up recompiling xsplash with the 15 changed to a 1, and it fixed the problem. But...yeesh...the reason I use ubuntu is that it's supposed to be the distro where you don't *have* to waste time modifying and compiling software that's broken :-(

### Post by Genius314 on 2010-01-15
Intel Core 2 Duo E8500 (3.16 GHz)
Nvidia GeForce 8800 512
4 Gigs of DDR2 memory

Graphire 3 tablet

It's a nightmare to deal with.  Just look at all the posts on this forum.  Thousands of people posting continuously, trying to figure out why their computers aren't working right.  I think Ubuntu advocates sugar-coat their reviews because they believe in the idealism of Ubuntu more than the practicality of it.  When their machine crashes or locks up, they inwardly blame it on the hardware manufacturer or something so that they can continue telling people that Ubuntu itself works well. ("It's sooo much better than that proprietary Windows crap")

> It is an impossibility for ubuntu to work on every computer in the world, considering they only have 700mb of data to work with.These aren't hardware incompatibility bugs (though those are a huge problem, too).  They're problems with the software being written and maintained poorly.  Stuff that used to work, but no longer works, because someone made a stupid mistake or added a poorly-tested new feature, and the "many eyes" didn't catch the problem.

> I built a computer with linux in mind because computing freedom matters a lot to me. It may not mean as much to youSo you agree that people who advocate linux do so for ideological reasons, not because it actually works well?  :)

If I like it after a few months of use, I'll probably give up on Ubuntu.  They've added all the nice little things that I liked about Ubuntu, while Ubuntu has wasted many hours of my life dealing with problems that couldn't be fixed in time for the release, instead of using my computer to get things done.  I'd gladly pay money for a desktop linux OS that actually worked, but if Ubuntu is the best desktop Linux, I guess there's no such thing.

I like the idea of open source, and it works quite well for some things, but it doesn't seem to work well for a desktop OS.

### Post by bug67 on 2010-01-19
My experience:

*_**Install - worked but had few things to fix, nothing serious though**_*

Actually, on one *real* issue if you can call it that.  I had the sound clicking problem noted in this bug report:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/alsa-driver/+bug/456022](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/alsa-driver/+bug/456022)

I fixed it by changing the last line in the file, "/etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base.conf" from this:

```
options snd-hda-intel power_save=10 power_save_controller=N
```

to this:

```
#options snd-hda-intel power_save=10 power_save_controller=N
```  

and rebooting. :D

Only other thing is a little shadow stays behind on the desktop when I open a drawer I have in the panel.  Didn't happen in 9.04.  MINOR ANNOYANCE.

Other than that, I had to install all the requisite medibuntu, universal and restricted repositories.  Also the Ubuntuzilla stuff so I could keep Firefox and Thunderbird current.   

When Lucid gets here, I will be installing it (clean) and sticking with it for LTS.

### Post by growlf on 2010-01-19
The upgrade went well, and a few glitches in hardware (wireless for example) evaporated nicely.  All was well until I found that I had no way to turn the user list login screen back to my prompted/more secure one short of the shell prompt.  The GUI is now devoid  of these features.  It seems this is an issue upstream from Gnome and a solution may be long in coming.  Certainly not soon enough for many folx - particularly in business venues where the login sound needs to be disabled, the restart buttons should also be disabled and the login prompt should replace the user list style for security reasons.

Our solution:  we created GDM2Setup and have posted it on Launchpad.  You can find it [here]("https://launchpad.net/gdm2setup") if you are interested.  Please file bug reports if you find anything wrong and let us know if you find it useful.

### Post by t3chnique on 2010-02-02
The installation process of 9.10 was smooth. The first and only problem so far that I experienced with 9.10 was that it didn't correctly detect my monitor's screen resolution. The only resolution it can produce was 800x600. It was pretty frustrated for a newb like me and took me several days of searching around and asking questions here. Finally i got it fixed by creating a xorg.conf.new and added the appropriate horizontal sync, vertical refresh and mode line. I then saved it as xorg.conf in /etc/X11. Restarted gdm and it worked :D. I was so happy. 

Thank you Joregon and Stonage for helping me solve the issue. And this thread too
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1324239](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1324239)

### Post by DawieS on 2010-03-05
I have 3 desktops on a home network, and decided to go for a full install rather than upgrade.

On 2 of them, the installation went flawless. The third desktop does not have a CD/DVD ROM and was installed via USB. For some reason, the Grub menu was bypassed on this one at boot-time, and it booted directly into the Ubuntu desktop. This was a quick fix due to good advice from these forums.

My Epson Stylus Photo 915 printer worked flawlessly out of the box.

I had to add a driver file to get an old flat-bed scanner (Genius ColorPage-Vivid 1200 XE) working. This was quickly fixed after a bit of hunting for advice on the Internet.

Overall, I am quite impressed. I find Karmic to be fast and rock solid, without a single glitch on applications over the past 2 months.:grin:

### Post by sjmay1973 on 2010-03-06
Yeah, I had a similar problem. The GRUB 2 is in BETA and it had a problem - it conflicted with GRUB 1.5 already on the system. It made my system unbootable by freezing at Grub 1.5 error code 15. I sought help on here, and it came back as "Put Windows XP/Vista/etcetc... disc in the drive and use the 'fixmbr' utility. Futile! The best thing to do, which was advised somewhere else on Ubuntu Forums, was to use the UBuntu Live CD to bring up the OS, download supergrub.iso (google it), install supergrub onto a USB stick, boot up from there and do it that way.

Karmic is by far the best UBUNTU kernel so far. Flawless! Love it! Kudos to the team that created it.!

### Post by Lechatelier on 2010-03-11
I have been using ubuntu and installing every version since 4.10 without many problems. With 9.10 install was satisfactory too. 

What I did  not like was some pieces of software.

1.- The default install is EXT4, but I was not informed that I would not be able to backup my system partition easily. I used fsarchiver but this  is a software you have to study (not just read instructions) so it is ok for a system managers who will do routine backups on may machines but not for the final user. 

2.- Neither my microphone nor the sound recorder work. This is not particular to  9.10 but applies to 9.04 too but not to the last LST which manages the microphone ok at least from the live installation disk. This appears to be a pervading problem since there are lots of references in the internet. I tried quite a few things without being able to solve the problem.

3.- Grub 2 is quite slow in my system. It is cute but again, you have to study to configure it. This is not a big deal and I am sure will be solved and I do not boot the system quite often.

The problem with EXT4 backups is new, can be worked around and is to be expected in new software but the problem with the microphone is old and unsolved. it remembers me of the trouble I got involved when I tried to use Ubuntu with an USB modem; there were lots of things about it in the internet but after some time I just started to use Windows until I could change to a supplier without that requirement.

I will wait and see what happens with 10.04. If it solves the problems with the mike I will keep using it as long as it is maintained, otherwise I will start trying new distros. I ham becoming old so fighting problems is no longer as fun as it used to be.

### Post by jn2010 on 2010-03-13
(1) I'm having problems setting up the following: boot (ext3) on RAID1, root (ext4) and swap on LUKS RAID5. I was able to boot degraded array (by yanking out 1 of 3 drives) without encryption on RAID5, with RAID5 seamlessly resyncing when all 3 drives back online. However, I have yet to figure out how to reinstall with encryption on RAID5. Runs fine with all 3 drives online, but when simulating disk failure, I don't make it to LUKS password prompt. I get dropped to Busybox intramfs prompt. It might be that I didn't properly install grub to each of the drives, update initramfs . . ? I did an apt-get update and apt-get install mdadm. Also tried the dpkg-mdadm --reinstall (or something to that effect) and last I recall boot-degraded=TRUE. Tried apt-get install grub-pc too. Any help would be appreciated. Here is a link to my issue: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1427766&highlight=raid5+lvm+encrypted](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1427766&highlight=raid5+lvm+encrypted)

(2) Also had some issues running Nautilus/Samba to connect to shared folders on laptop running Vista x86. (3) Played music from shared folder on Windows laptop and ran into audio problems (no sound). Panicked, updated, installed various media players etc etc and finally realized there is another system volume control icon in the upper right-hand corner next to the network icon.

### Post by mikechant on 2010-03-14
9.10 new install for eeePC 1000:

Seemed OK at first - everything working, faster than 9.04, but in everyday use wireless connection was annoyingly flaky at times (after being rock solid in 9.04), and it would crash sometimes on return from suspend (suspect new video driver with KMS). If it was my PC I might put up with this and work on it, but it's my wife's and it's not fair to her to cause too much disruption, so I reverted to 9.04 (simple reboot and change grub default back - this is why I *always* do side-by-side installs rather than upgrade the existing install). I'm not going to bother trying to troubleshoot this because 10.04 is out next month, I'll try again then.

### Post by chris.willis on 2010-03-21
I recently installed Kubuntu after using Ubuntu on an Acer Aspire One. I am a beginner and had to find out a few tweaks which I'll include here if anybody in the future browses through:

Fonts:
the fonts are too large on the AAO screen. To go System Settings -> change Font to 96 points.

Browser: Swiftfox
Add repository by going to System Settings -> Add and remove Software
- Edit Software Sources
- Tick everything on that page, then click 'Other Software' tab
- Tick the first two options, then click add. Enter:
- $ deb [http://getswiftfox.com/builds/debian](http://getswiftfox.com/builds/debian) unstable non-free
- Close, then reload
- Search for and install Swiftfox. Runs better and quicker than Firefox with almost identical functionality

Wireless network: wicd
(wicd is much better than the standard adapter on the AAO. The radio button is intermittent. Installing wicd really improves things)
- Use Konsole
- $ sudo apt-get install wicd
- After install, press Alt-F2 and type wicd on the command. Select the icon when it comes up, and run it
- The icon will appear in the menu and change in the notification area. Set up your wireless.
- Important: the first one or two times to connect will frustrate you, but after that, it's perfect.

Setup: VLC - issues on the AAO after installation.
(I predominantly use xvid aac video. The following makes a big difference in Kubuntu)
- Test a video in VLC. If you can't hear sound, do the following:
- Right click the volume control icon in the notification  area
- Left click the volume control, put the slider all the way up to max for Master and PCM
- Right click the icon. Select Master Chanel &#8594; Master &#8594; OK
- Play a video in VLC
- Tools &#8594; Preferences &#8594; Audio
- Click 'Save volume on exit'
- Under 'Type', select 'ALSA'
- Click 'Headphone surround effect' and 'volume normaliser'


Setup: OpenOffice Writer
- Tools &#8594; Options &#8594; Memory
- Steps: 20
- Graphics cache: 128mb
- Memory per object: 20mb
- Number of objects: 20
- Java &#8594; uncheck 'use Java environment'

Setup: Kontact with gmail using imap
Incoming:
- Server: imap.gmail.com
- Port: 993
- Login: full, including @gmail.com (ie, [email]myname@gmail.com[/email])
- Click: store imap password, include manual check, enable interval check: 10 mins
- Security &#8594; Use SSL

Accounts sending
- Name: without @gmail.com (ie, just Joe Bloggs)
- Outgoing server: smtp.gmail.com
- Port: 465 and tick SSL
Advanced
- Tick 'server requires authentication'
- Login: full, including @gmail.com (ie, [email]myname@gmail.com[/email])


Touchpad issue:
The AAO is frustrating to use with Kubuntu. In my Ubuntu distro, there are specific settings. when typing, sometimes I brush the touchpad with my palm or it clicks on its own. I do a lot of typing, but the Fn-F7 on the AAO disables the keypad without additional software

Apart from the above, I just enjoyed customising the system. Despite the touchpad problem which is driving me nuts, I don't think I'll be switching back to Ubuntu, but hey, what a fantastic choice we Linux users have!

Overall, no issues on the Acer Aspire One. It doesn't suspend when the lid is closed, but it's only a mouseclick away. Installation was easy using unetbootin and a USB - a little too easy for a long-time Windows user!

- Adobe Flash is crappy and stutters. Tried every tweak on the internet, but no luck. Even without Compiz or other effects enabled.
- Can't get 480p or 720p mkv to play without hanging.
- Connection to XP shared folders keep disconnecting (tried fstab and pysdm)
